cases.title: Ear Tugging and Fussiness After a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Right ear pain and irritability for one day after a week of cold symptoms.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for one day of right ear tugging, fussiness, and poor sleep. He had a runny nose, cough, and low-grade fevers for the past week that were improving until yesterday, when he became inconsolable at night and pointed to his right ear. He has had two prior ear infections, the last about eight months ago, both treated with oral antibiotics. He attends daycare full time. He is otherwise developing normally and is up to date on vaccinations, including pneumococcal conjugate. He takes no daily medications. His mother gave acetaminophen 160 mg last night with partial relief. There are no known drug allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; his father smokes outside the home. There is no family history of immunodeficiency. He is eating and drinking somewhat less than usual but is making wet diapers and has had no vomiting, neck stiffness, or rash. He has not had ear surgery or tympanostomy tubes.
cases.exam: Alert but clingy toddler, consolable in mother's arms. Right tympanic membrane is bulging, erythematous, and opaque with loss of the normal landmarks and light reflex; pneumatic otoscopy shows markedly reduced mobility. No air-fluid level or visible perforation. Left tympanic membrane is pearly gray, mobile, with a normal cone of light. No mastoid swelling, tenderness, or auricular protrusion. No tragal tenderness. Oropharynx mildly erythematous; clear rhinorrhea. Neck supple without adenopathy of concern. Lungs clear; no respiratory distress. Skin without rash.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most strongly establishes the diagnosis of acute otitis media rather than a simple viral upper respiratory infection?
options: Erythema of the tympanic membrane alone
A bulging tympanic membrane with reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy
Clear rhinorrhea and a low-grade fever
Ear tugging in a fussy toddler
stages.explanation: A bulging tympanic membrane with impaired mobility is the most specific sign of acute otitis media and reflects a middle-ear effusion under pressure. Erythema alone is unreliable because crying, fever, or cerumen removal can redden the membrane without infection. Clear rhinorrhea and low-grade fever are nonspecific and accompany ordinary viral URIs. Ear tugging is common in toddlers and poorly predicts AOM. Diagnostic criteria require either moderate-to-severe bulging (or new otorrhea not from otitis externa) OR mild bulging with recent-onset ear pain or intense erythema. This child's bulging, opaque, immobile membrane plus acute otalgia satisfies the definition.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his age and presentation, what is the most appropriate management decision regarding antibiotics?
options: Observation for 48-72 hours with analgesia and follow-up, since he has unilateral disease
Start oral antibiotics now because he meets criteria for treatment
Prescribe topical ofloxacin otic drops without oral therapy
Refer urgently for tympanostomy tube placement
stages.explanation: Immediate antibiotics are recommended for children 6-23 months with unilateral or bilateral AOM, and for any child with severe symptoms (toxic appearance, otalgia ≥48 hours, fever ≥39 °C, or otorrhea). At 3 years with confirmed AOM and clear bulging, treatment is appropriate; the observation option is intended mainly for non-severe AOM in children ≥2 years with reliable follow-up, but he is already symptomatic with a markedly bulging drum, making treatment reasonable. Topical otic drops do not treat an intact-membrane middle-ear infection. Tympanostomy tubes are for recurrent AOM or persistent effusion, not a first acute episode.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic for this child with no penicillin allergy and no amoxicillin in the past 30 days?
options: Azithromycin
Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
Ciprofloxacin orally
stages.explanation: High-dose amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg/day) is first-line for AOM in a child without recent amoxicillin exposure, concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, or penicillin allergy; the high dose overcomes intermediate-resistance pneumococci. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is preferred when amoxicillin was used in the prior 30 days or when conjunctivitis suggests Haemophilus. Azithromycin has poor pneumococcal coverage and rising resistance, reserved for severe penicillin allergy. TMP-SMX has inadequate streptococcal coverage and is not recommended. Oral ciprofloxacin is not a first-line agent for AOM and is generally avoided in young children for this indication.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: His mother asks what to watch for. Which finding would most concern you for a suppurative complication requiring re-evaluation?
options: Persistent clear nasal discharge for several more days
Posterior auricular swelling pushing the ear forward with fever
A brief return of fever to 38 °C on day two
Mild decreased hearing on the affected side
stages.explanation: Postauricular swelling that displaces the auricle anteriorly, with erythema and fever, suggests acute mastoiditis, the most common suppurative complication of AOM, requiring imaging, IV antibiotics, and possible drainage. Persistent clear rhinorrhea reflects the resolving viral URI and is benign. A transient low fever in the first 48 hours is expected and does not signal failure unless symptoms worsen or persist beyond 48-72 hours. Mild conductive hearing loss is universal during AOM from the effusion and improves as it clears; it warrants follow-up only if it persists for months, suggesting otitis media with effusion.

pearls: Diagnose AOM by a bulging, immobile tympanic membrane, not erythema alone—crying reddens normal drums.
High-dose amoxicillin (80-90 mg/kg/day) is first-line unless amoxicillin was used in the past 30 days or conjunctivitis is present, which favors amoxicillin-clavulanate.
Children <6 months and those with severe disease always get antibiotics; observation is an option for non-severe AOM in children ≥2 years with reliable follow-up.
Postauricular swelling with anterior displacement of the auricle signals mastoiditis and needs urgent evaluation.

cases.title: Failed Hearing Screen and 'Selective' Listening
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report she seems not to hear well and failed a school hearing screening.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl is brought in after failing a kindergarten hearing screening in her right ear. Her parents say that for the past two to three months she turns the television up loud, frequently asks 'what?', and seems to ignore them unless they face her. She had several ear infections as a toddler but none recently and has had no ear pain, fever, or drainage in months. She had a cold about ten weeks ago. She has no balance problems, no speech regression, and is meeting developmental and academic milestones, though her teacher notes she is inattentive during reading circle. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with both parents; her mother smoked during the home's first year but the household is now smoke-free. There is no family history of childhood deafness. She breathes through her mouth at night and snores occasionally but has no apneic pauses. Birth history was unremarkable, and newborn hearing screening was normal.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, cooperative child in no distress and afebrile. Otoscopy shows bilateral retracted tympanic membranes that are dull and amber, with visible air-fluid levels and bubbles behind the right drum; no bulging, erythema, or otorrhea. Pneumatic otoscopy reveals markedly reduced mobility bilaterally. Weber test (512 Hz) lateralizes to the right ear; Rinne shows bone conduction greater than air conduction on the right, consistent with a conductive pattern. Nasal turbinates mildly congested; oropharynx clear with 2+ tonsils. No cervical adenopathy. Cranial nerves intact; gait and balance normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The otoscopic findings of dull, retracted membranes with air-fluid levels and no erythema or pain most suggest which diagnosis?
options: Acute otitis media
Otitis media with effusion
Acute otitis externa
Cholesteatoma
stages.explanation: Middle-ear fluid without signs of acute infection (no bulging, marked erythema, otalgia, or fever) defines otitis media with effusion. Air-fluid levels, retraction, dullness, and reduced mobility on a non-painful, afebrile child are classic. Acute otitis media requires a bulging, inflamed drum with acute symptoms, absent here. Otitis externa causes a painful, tender canal with discharge and a normal middle ear. Cholesteatoma produces a retraction pocket with keratin debris and chronic foul drainage—not seen here—and would be unusual as a first presentation of bilateral effusions following a cold.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY & TYMPANOMETRY
stages.content:
Tympanometry: Type B (flat) tracing bilaterally, normal canal volume
Pure-tone audiometry: bilateral conductive hearing loss, air-bone gap ~25-30 dB
Speech reception thresholds mildly elevated bilaterally
Otoacoustic emissions: absent bilaterally (consistent with middle-ear fluid)

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Her tympanogram shows a Type B (flat) tracing with normal canal volume. What does this indicate?
options: A tympanic membrane perforation
A patent tympanostomy tube
Middle-ear effusion with an intact, immobile tympanic membrane
Normal middle-ear function
stages.explanation: A Type B tympanogram with normal ear-canal volume indicates fluid behind an intact, non-compliant tympanic membrane—the hallmark of otitis media with effusion. A flat tracing with abnormally large canal volume instead suggests a perforation or a patent tube, because the probe is measuring the middle-ear space too. A Type A tracing is normal, and a Type C tracing (negative peak pressure) indicates eustachian tube dysfunction with retraction but not frank effusion. Her flat curve plus normal volume confirms an intact drum with trapped fluid.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given bilateral effusions present for under three months with documented mild conductive loss, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate bilateral tympanostomy tube placement
Watchful waiting with repeat exam and audiometry in about 3 months
A course of high-dose amoxicillin
Daily intranasal and oral antihistamines
stages.explanation: Most effusions after a URI resolve spontaneously, so guidelines recommend watchful waiting for about three months from onset (or diagnosis) in a child without risk factors for developmental delay, with documentation of hearing. Tympanostomy tubes are indicated for effusion persisting ≥3 months with hearing loss, recurrent AOM with effusion, or structural/at-risk concerns—not yet met here. Antibiotics do not improve long-term OME outcomes and are not recommended for OME alone. Antihistamines, decongestants, and steroids have not shown benefit for OME and carry side effects, so they are explicitly not recommended.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: At the 3-month recheck, effusions persist with a 30 dB conductive loss and her teacher reports worsening attention. What is the best next step?
options: Continue observation for another 3 months
Prescribe a prolonged antibiotic course
Refer for bilateral tympanostomy tube placement
Start oral corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Bilateral OME persisting ≥3 months with documented hearing loss and functional impact (classroom difficulty) meets criteria for tympanostomy tube referral, which restores hearing and reduces effusion recurrence. Continued observation risks ongoing hearing loss during a critical period for language and learning. Prolonged antibiotics offer no durable benefit for chronic effusion and promote resistance. Oral or intranasal steroids do not provide lasting resolution of OME and are not recommended. Adenoidectomy may be added, especially if there are nasal obstructive symptoms or repeat tube placement is needed.

pearls: A Type B tympanogram with normal canal volume = effusion behind an intact drum; with large volume, suspect perforation or a patent tube.
OME is fluid without acute inflammation; it commonly follows a URI or resolving AOM and usually self-resolves within 3 months.
Antibiotics, antihistamines, decongestants, and steroids are not recommended for OME—watchful waiting with hearing documentation is the mainstay.
Refer for tympanostomy tubes when effusion persists ≥3 months with hearing loss or in at-risk children, to protect speech and learning.

cases.title: Painful Ear After a Summer of Swimming
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of worsening right ear pain and itching after frequent swimming.
cases.history: A 19-year-old competitive swimmer presents with two days of progressively severe right ear pain, itching, and a sense of fullness. He has been training in an outdoor pool twice daily through the summer and often uses cotton swabs to dry his ears afterward. He now notices scant whitish discharge and pain when chewing or touching the ear. He has no fever, no hearing loss beyond mild muffling, and no prior ear surgery. He is healthy with no diabetes, takes no regular medications, and has no allergies. He drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. There is no history of tympanic membrane perforation, tubes, or recent air travel. He denies vertigo, facial weakness, or severe headache. He tried over-the-counter acetaminophen with minimal relief and a few drops of hydrogen peroxide last night without improvement. He is sexually active and otherwise reports no systemic symptoms or recent illnesses.
cases.exam: Right auricle: marked tenderness on tragal pressure and pain with pinna traction. External auditory canal is diffusely erythematous and edematous with whitish-to-clear debris partially occluding the lumen; the canal skin is macerated. The tympanic membrane is only partially visualized but appears intact and non-bulging behind the edema, without perforation. No periauricular or postauricular swelling, no granulation tissue, and no facial nerve weakness. Hearing is mildly reduced on the right consistent with canal obstruction. Left ear is normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Cranial nerves intact.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which examination finding best distinguishes acute otitis externa from acute otitis media in this patient?
options: A bulging, immobile tympanic membrane
Pain on tragal pressure and pinna traction with a swollen, erythematous canal
Fever above 39 °C
An air-fluid level behind the eardrum
stages.explanation: Tenderness with tragal pressure or pinna traction plus a diffusely inflamed, edematous canal localizes disease to the external auditory canal, defining acute otitis externa. A bulging, immobile drum and middle-ear air-fluid levels indicate disease of the middle ear (otitis media), where the canal is typically normal and the pinna non-tender. High fever is uncommon in uncomplicated otitis externa and would prompt concern for a deeper or systemic process. This patient's classic risk factor (frequent water exposure with canal trauma from swabs) and canal findings confirm swimmer's ear.

stages.label: MICROBIOLOGY
stages.question: Which organism is the most common cause of acute otitis externa in this setting?
options: Streptococcus pneumoniae
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Candida albicans
Moraxella catarrhalis
stages.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the leading pathogen in acute (bacterial) otitis externa, especially with water exposure, followed by Staphylococcus aureus. The warm, moist canal and disrupted skin barrier favor Pseudomonas. Streptococcus pneumoniae and Moraxella catarrhalis are typical of acute otitis media, not external canal infection. Candida and Aspergillus cause otomycosis, usually after prolonged antibiotic drop use or in chronic cases, presenting with itching and visible fungal elements rather than the acute bacterial picture seen here. Empiric topical therapy is chosen to cover Pseudomonas and Staph.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: Assuming an intact tympanic membrane, what is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral ciprofloxacin
Topical antibiotic otic drops (e.g., ciprofloxacin-containing) with canal cleaning and water precautions
Topical aminoglycoside-containing drops regardless of membrane status
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated acute otitis externa is treated with topical antibiotic drops (often with a steroid), achieving far higher local concentrations than oral therapy, plus gentle aural toilet and keeping the ear dry. Fluoroquinolone drops cover Pseudomonas and Staph and are safe even if the membrane status is uncertain. Oral antibiotics are reserved for extension beyond the canal, immunocompromise/diabetes, or inability to deliver topicals. Aminoglycoside drops are ototoxic if the membrane is perforated, so they should be avoided when integrity is unconfirmed. If canal edema blocks drop entry, a wick is placed to deliver medication.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point will most reduce his risk of recurrence?
options: Routinely clean the canal with cotton swabs after each swim
Instill acidifying/drying ear drops after swimming and avoid inserting objects into the canal
Use prophylactic oral antibiotics during swim season
Irrigate the ears daily with tap water
stages.explanation: Acidifying and drying agents (e.g., dilute acetic acid or isopropyl alcohol drops) after water exposure restore the canal's protective acidic environment and inhibit Pseudomonas, while avoiding cotton swabs prevents the skin microtrauma that lets bacteria invade. Cotton swabs are a major cause of otitis externa and should be discouraged. Prophylactic oral antibiotics are unnecessary, drive resistance, and do not address the local environment. Daily tap-water irrigation keeps the canal moist and macerated, worsening risk. Earplugs or a swim cap and thorough drying also help.

pearls: Pain on tragal pressure or pinna traction localizes disease to the canal—acute otitis externa, not otitis media.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common pathogen; topical fluoroquinolone drops plus drying and aural toilet are first-line.
Avoid aminoglycoside otic drops when the tympanic membrane may be perforated, due to ototoxicity risk; place a wick if canal edema blocks drops.
Prevent recurrence with post-swim acidifying/drying drops and by never inserting cotton swabs into the canal.

cases.title: Relentless Ear Pain and Drainage in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of severe right ear pain and drainage now with new facial droop.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (last A1c 10.4%) presents with three weeks of deep, boring right ear pain that is worse at night and out of proportion to exam findings, plus persistent purulent drainage. He was treated by his primary doctor for 'swimmer's ear' with topical drops without improvement. Over the last two days he noticed the right corner of his mouth drooping and difficulty fully closing his right eye. He has chronic kidney disease stage 3 and hypertension. Medications include metformin 1000 mg BID, glipizide 10 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and aspirin 81 mg. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired mechanic, former smoker (40 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), and drinks rarely. He denies vertigo, but reports decreased hearing on the right. He has had subjective fevers and fatigue. No recent trauma, no head/neck cancer history, and no recent travel. He uses a cotton swab in his ears occasionally.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, uncomfortable elderly man. Right external auditory canal is edematous with copious purulent discharge and a prominent mass of granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction of the floor of the canal. The tympanic membrane is obscured. Marked tenderness over the tragus and periauricular region; tenderness on palpation over the mastoid and temporomandibular area. Right-sided peripheral facial nerve palsy (House-Brackmann grade III) with incomplete eye closure and flattened nasolabial fold. Other cranial nerves intact; no nystagmus. No meningismus. Glucose by fingerstick 268 mg/dL. Left ear normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy noted.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of features most strongly points to necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa rather than ordinary otitis externa?
options: Itching and mild canal erythema in a healthy young swimmer
Severe nocturnal pain out of proportion, granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, and cranial neuropathy in a diabetic
A bulging tympanic membrane with fever
Bilateral canal erythema that improved with topical drops
stages.explanation: Necrotizing otitis externa is an invasive skull-base osteomyelitis seen in elderly diabetics or immunocompromised patients. The hallmarks are severe, unremitting pain out of proportion to exam (worse at night), granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction of the canal, failure of topical therapy, and cranial nerve involvement—most often the facial nerve (CN VII) as infection tracks through the stylomastoid foramen. A simple swimmer's ear is self-limited, lacks granulation tissue at the junction, and never causes cranial palsies. A bulging drum indicates otitis media. Improvement with drops argues against invasive disease.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory test and the most useful imaging study to confirm the diagnosis?
options: CBC alone and a plain skull radiograph
ESR/CRP plus CT of the temporal bone to assess bony erosion (with MRI for soft-tissue/intracranial extension)
Blood cultures alone and ultrasound of the neck
Lumbar puncture and chest radiograph
stages.explanation: An elevated ESR and CRP support the diagnosis and are tracked to monitor response. CT of the temporal bone best demonstrates bony erosion of the skull base and is the standard initial cross-sectional study; MRI better defines soft-tissue and intracranial extension and dural/marrow involvement. Plain radiographs lack sensitivity for early skull-base osteomyelitis. Blood cultures are often negative and neck ultrasound does not assess the skull base. Lumbar puncture is not indicated without meningeal signs and could be hazardous. Technetium/gallium scintigraphy can help confirm osteomyelitis and follow resolution. Culture of canal granulation tissue guides therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL
ESR 92 mm/hr
CRP 11.4 mg/dL
HbA1c 10.4%
Glucose 268 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline CKD3)
CT temporal bone: erosion of the bony external auditory canal floor extending toward the skull base; opacification of mastoid air cells
Canal tissue culture: pending
MRI: enhancing soft tissue at the skull base with early clival marrow signal change

stages.label: PATHOGEN & THERAPY
stages.question: Cultures are pending. Which pathogen is most likely, and what is the most appropriate empiric systemic therapy?
options: Staphylococcus aureus; oral cephalexin
Pseudomonas aeruginosa; prolonged systemic antipseudomonal therapy (e.g., IV/oral fluoroquinolone or antipseudomonal beta-lactam)
Streptococcus pneumoniae; oral amoxicillin
Aspergillus species; oral fluconazole
stages.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa causes the overwhelming majority of necrotizing otitis externa, so empiric therapy must be antipseudomonal—commonly a systemic fluoroquinolone (e.g., ciprofloxacin) or an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftazidime, or cefepime), continued for a prolonged course (often 6-8 weeks) guided by clinical and inflammatory-marker response. Oral cephalexin and amoxicillin lack Pseudomonas coverage. Aspergillus is a less common cause, considered chiefly in immunocompromised patients or when bacterial therapy fails, and would require antifungals such as voriconazole, not fluconazole. Strict glucose control and ENT involvement for debridement of granulation tissue are essential adjuncts.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Besides antibiotics, which intervention is most critical to improve outcome in this patient?
options: Aggressive topical aminoglycoside drops as monotherapy
Strict glycemic control plus ENT consultation for debridement and close follow-up of inflammatory markers
Immediate radical mastoidectomy in all cases
Empiric systemic corticosteroids to reduce facial nerve swelling
stages.explanation: Optimizing glycemic control reverses the immune dysfunction that allows skull-base invasion, and ENT consultation provides debridement of granulation tissue, deep cultures, and serial monitoring (exam plus ESR/CRP, and often nuclear imaging) to guide duration of therapy. Topical drops alone are inadequate for invasive osteomyelitis. Surgery is reserved for debridement or complications, not routine radical mastoidectomy in every patient. Corticosteroids are not standard; the facial palsy here results from infectious nerve involvement and is managed by treating the infection, not by steroids, which could worsen glucose control and immune suppression.

cases.diagnosis: Necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa with facial nerve palsy
pearls: Suspect necrotizing otitis externa in an elderly diabetic or immunocompromised patient with severe nocturnal otalgia out of proportion, granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, and failure of topical drops.
Cranial neuropathies (CN VII first, then IX-XII at the jugular foramen) signal skull-base osteomyelitis and a worse prognosis.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the dominant pathogen; treat with prolonged systemic antipseudomonal therapy and biopsy/culture granulation tissue.
ESR/CRP plus CT (bone) and MRI (soft tissue) confirm and monitor disease; tight glucose control and ENT debridement are essential.

cases.title: Years of Smelly Ear Drainage and Declining Hearing
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of foul-smelling drainage and gradual hearing loss in the left ear.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents with chronic, foul-smelling drainage from her left ear that waxes and wanes over the past year, along with gradually worsening hearing on that side. She recalls frequent ear infections as a child and had tympanostomy tubes placed twice. The drainage is scant but persistent and does not fully clear with the over-the-counter drops she has tried intermittently. She denies severe pain, but reports occasional mild pressure and, in the last month, brief episodes of dizziness when the ear is manipulated or when she blows her nose hard. She has no fevers, facial weakness, or severe headaches. She is otherwise healthy, takes only an oral contraceptive, and has no allergies. She works as a teacher, does not smoke, and drinks occasionally. There is no family history of hearing loss or ear tumors. She has not had recent water exposure beyond normal showering and denies prior ear surgery other than the childhood tubes.
cases.exam: Left ear: scant malodorous, mucopurulent debris in the canal. After cleaning, there is a retraction pocket in the pars flaccida (attic) of the tympanic membrane containing whitish, keratinous (pearly) debris; the surrounding margin shows a small amount of granulation tissue. The remaining membrane is dull and partially retracted. Weber (512 Hz) lateralizes to the left; Rinne shows bone conduction greater than air conduction on the left (conductive pattern). Right ear is normal. No facial nerve weakness; smile and eye closure symmetric. No spontaneous nystagmus; fistula test reproduces mild transient dizziness with tragal pressure. No postauricular swelling or tenderness.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An attic retraction pocket filled with pearly keratin debris and chronic foul otorrhea most likely represents which diagnosis?
options: Otitis media with effusion
Cholesteatoma
Otomycosis
Glomus tympanicum tumor
stages.explanation: Cholesteatoma is an abnormal collection of keratinizing squamous epithelium in the middle ear/attic, typically arising from a pars flaccida retraction pocket, especially in patients with a history of chronic ear disease or tubes. The pearly keratin debris, chronic foul (often scant) otorrhea unresponsive to drops, conductive hearing loss, and associated granulation tissue are classic. Otitis media with effusion causes fluid without keratin debris or foul drainage. Otomycosis shows fungal hyphae/spores and itching, not a keratin-filled retraction pocket. A glomus tympanicum is a vascular red retrotympanic mass with pulsatile tinnitus, not malodorous keratin debris.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why is cholesteatoma dangerous despite often causing only mild symptoms?
options: It is a malignant tumor that metastasizes early
It erodes surrounding bone via enzymatic and pressure effects, destroying ossicles and adjacent structures
It always causes immediate, complete sensorineural deafness
It resolves spontaneously once drainage stops
stages.explanation: Although benign and non-neoplastic, cholesteatoma progressively erodes bone through pressure and the release of osteolytic enzymes, destroying the ossicular chain (causing conductive loss) and potentially the labyrinth (vertigo, sensorineural loss from a labyrinthine fistula), facial canal (facial palsy), or tegmen (intracranial extension, abscess, meningitis). It does not metastasize—it is not a cancer. It does not cause immediate total deafness; loss is usually gradual and conductive initially. It does not resolve on its own; ongoing growth makes definitive surgical removal necessary. This patient's positive fistula sign warns of erosion toward the labyrinth.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiometry (left): conductive hearing loss with ~35 dB air-bone gap
Word recognition: preserved
Tympanometry (left): reduced compliance
High-resolution CT temporal bone: soft-tissue mass in Prussak's space (attic) with scutum erosion and partial erosion of the long process of the incus; no obvious tegmen or facial canal dehiscence

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given CT showing an attic soft-tissue mass with scutum and ossicular erosion, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Long-term suppressive topical and oral antibiotics
Surgical removal via tympanomastoidectomy
Tympanostomy tube placement
Observation with serial audiograms
stages.explanation: Cholesteatoma is a surgical disease: definitive treatment is complete removal, typically tympanomastoidectomy, often with staged ossicular reconstruction to address the conductive loss. Surgery eradicates the keratin matrix, prevents continued bony erosion, and avoids complications like labyrinthine fistula, facial palsy, and intracranial spread. Antibiotics (topical/oral) may temporarily control superinfection and drainage but cannot remove the keratin matrix or halt growth. Tympanostomy tubes treat effusion, not cholesteatoma, and would not address the erosive disease. Observation allows progressive destruction and is inappropriate once a cholesteatoma with bony erosion is identified.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding during follow-up would most urgently suggest an intracranial complication?
options: Mild conductive hearing loss on audiogram
New high fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness
Scant recurrence of ear drainage
Tinnitus in the affected ear
stages.explanation: Fever, severe headache, and meningismus suggest meningitis or a brain abscess from intracranial extension—the most feared cholesteatoma complications—requiring emergent imaging, neurosurgical/ENT involvement, and IV antibiotics. Mild conductive loss is expected from ossicular involvement and is not an emergency. Scant recurrent drainage suggests local disease or residual/recurrent cholesteatoma needing ENT re-evaluation but is not immediately life-threatening. Tinnitus is common with chronic ear disease and nonspecific. Other danger signs include facial palsy, vertigo with a positive fistula test (labyrinthine erosion), and a postauricular fluctuant swelling (subperiosteal abscess).

pearls: Chronic foul otorrhea unresponsive to drops plus an attic (pars flaccida) retraction pocket with keratin debris = cholesteatoma until proven otherwise.
Cholesteatoma is benign but locally destructive—it erodes ossicles and can cause labyrinthine fistula, facial palsy, and intracranial abscess/meningitis.
A positive fistula test (vertigo/nystagmus with tragal pressure) signals erosion toward the labyrinth.
Definitive treatment is surgical (tympanomastoidectomy); antibiotics only temporize drainage and cannot remove the keratin matrix.

cases.title: Sudden Muffled Hearing After a Shower
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt fullness and muffled hearing in the right ear after showering this morning.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with sudden right-sided ear fullness and muffled hearing that began right after his morning shower. He says he has felt mild buildup for weeks and sometimes uses cotton swabs to clean his ears, which he feels has 'packed things in.' He denies pain, drainage, fever, vertigo, or tinnitus, though he notes his own voice sounds louder in that ear. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia, controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily, and takes aspirin 81 mg. He wears hearing aids in both ears for age-related hearing loss. No known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, non-smoker, and drinks one glass of wine nightly. He has no history of tympanic membrane perforation, ear surgery, or tubes. He denies recent water sports or swimming. There is no facial weakness, severe headache, or balance disturbance. He used over-the-counter drops once without relief and is worried his hearing aid stopped working.
cases.exam: Right external auditory canal is completely occluded by a firm, dark-brown plug of cerumen; the tympanic membrane cannot be visualized. The visible canal skin around the plug is intact without erythema, swelling, or discharge, and the tragus is non-tender. Left ear shows a small amount of cerumen with a visible, intact, mobile tympanic membrane and normal landmarks. Weber (512 Hz) lateralizes to the right; Rinne shows bone conduction greater than air conduction on the right, indicating a conductive pattern on that side. No facial nerve weakness, no nystagmus, normal gait. No postauricular tenderness or swelling.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Weber test lateralizes to the right and Rinne shows bone > air conduction on the right. How should this be interpreted given the occluding plug?
options: Right sensorineural hearing loss
Right conductive hearing loss consistent with the occluding cerumen
Normal hearing bilaterally
Left conductive hearing loss
stages.explanation: In a conductive loss, the Weber lateralizes to the affected (worse-conducting) ear because that ear is shielded from competing ambient sound and detects bone-conducted sound better, while a negative Rinne (bone > air) confirms impaired air conduction on that side. Here the occluding cerumen blocks air conduction in the right ear, producing exactly this pattern. A sensorineural loss would lateralize the Weber away from the affected ear with a normal (air > bone) Rinne. Normal hearing would give a midline Weber and air > bone bilaterally. The findings are not on the left, which conducts normally.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before attempting irrigation to remove the impaction, what is the most important thing to confirm or ask?
options: Whether he prefers warm or cold water
Whether there is any history of tympanic membrane perforation, ear surgery, or tubes
His most recent cholesterol level
Whether he has eaten recently
stages.explanation: Irrigation is contraindicated when the tympanic membrane may not be intact—prior perforation, ear surgery (e.g., mastoidectomy), or tympanostomy tubes—because forcing water into the middle ear risks infection, pain, and vertigo. A history of recurrent otitis externa, diabetes, or immunocompromise also raises caution and may favor manual removal or referral. Water temperature should be body-temperature to avoid caloric-induced vertigo, but that is secondary to ensuring it is safe to irrigate at all. Cholesterol level and recent meals are irrelevant to this minor procedure. Confirming an intact membrane (or using cerumenolytics and curette/suction instead) is the priority.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: He confirms no perforation, surgery, or tubes. Which removal approach is appropriate and safe?
options: Aggressive deep cotton-swab removal
Cerumenolytic drops to soften the plug, then gentle body-temperature irrigation or manual removal under direct visualization
Oral antibiotics to dissolve the wax
Forceful irrigation with cold tap water
stages.explanation: Standard care is to soften the plug with a cerumenolytic (e.g., carbamide peroxide, mineral oil, or saline), then remove it with gentle body-temperature irrigation or by manual extraction (curette/suction) under direct visualization. Body-temperature water prevents caloric vertigo, and gentleness avoids canal trauma or perforation. Cotton swabs push wax deeper and abrade the canal, the very cause of impaction—they should be avoided. Antibiotics have no role in dissolving cerumen. Cold, forceful irrigation can induce vertigo, pain, and trauma. After removal, the canal and drum should be inspected to confirm clearance and integrity.

stages.label: OUTCOME
stages.question: After successful removal, his hearing returns to baseline and the tympanic membrane appears intact. What is the best counseling to prevent recurrence?
options: Use cotton swabs daily to keep wax out
Avoid inserting objects into the canal; cerumen is usually self-clearing, and use periodic softening drops if prone to buildup
Schedule monthly professional irrigation indefinitely for everyone
Begin chronic prophylactic antibiotic drops
stages.explanation: Cerumen is protective and normally migrates out on its own, so the best advice is to avoid inserting cotton swabs or other objects that push wax inward and disrupt the canal—exactly the behavior that caused his impaction. Patients prone to buildup, especially hearing-aid users, can use periodic softening drops (mineral oil or over-the-counter cerumenolytics) and seek removal as needed. Routine monthly irrigation for everyone is unnecessary and can cause trauma; it is reserved for select patients with recurrent impaction. Prophylactic antibiotic drops have no role and promote resistance and otomycosis.

cases.diagnosis: Cerumen impaction (right ear) causing conductive hearing loss
pearls: An occluding cerumen plug causes conductive loss: Weber lateralizes TO the affected ear and Rinne shows bone > air on that side.
Never irrigate if there is possible tympanic membrane perforation, prior ear surgery, or tubes—use manual removal instead.
Use body-temperature water for irrigation to avoid caloric-induced vertigo, and soften hard plugs with cerumenolytics first.
Counsel against cotton swabs—they push wax deeper and abrade the canal; cerumen is normally self-clearing.

cases.title: Brief Spinning Spells When Rolling Over in Bed
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden brief episodes of room-spinning when she rolls over in bed or tilts her head back.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman reports several days of sudden, brief spinning sensations triggered by position changes—rolling over in bed, lying down, looking up to a high shelf, or bending forward. Each spell lasts under a minute and resolves when she holds still, though she feels mildly unsteady and nauseated afterward. She denies hearing loss, tinnitus, ear fullness, headache, or any continuous vertigo between episodes. There is no recent head trauma, though she had a minor fall a few weeks ago without losing consciousness. She has hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and takes no other medications. No known allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. She works as a librarian. She denies double vision, slurred speech, limb weakness, numbness, or trouble walking apart from the brief positional spells. There is no family history of vestibular disorders. She is anxious that this could be a stroke. She has had no recent viral illness or ear infection.
cases.exam: General and cardiovascular exams unremarkable; no orthostatic drop. Otoscopy normal bilaterally; tympanic membranes intact. Cranial nerves intact; no spontaneous nystagmus on primary gaze. Hearing grossly normal; Weber midline and Rinne air > bone bilaterally. Gait steady; Romberg negative; finger-to-nose and rapid alternating movements normal; no dysmetria or dysarthria. Dix-Hallpike maneuver to the right provokes, after a few seconds' latency, transient upbeating-torsional nystagmus and reproduction of her vertigo lasting about 20 seconds, then fatiguing with repetition. Left Dix-Hallpike is negative. No focal neurologic deficits.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Dix-Hallpike maneuver provokes latent, fatigable, upbeating-torsional nystagmus with brief positional vertigo. What is the diagnosis?
options: Central (cerebellar) positional vertigo
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo of the posterior canal
Meniere disease
Vestibular neuritis
stages.explanation: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) of the posterior semicircular canal produces brief, positionally triggered vertigo with a characteristic Dix-Hallpike response: a few seconds of latency, transient (typically <60 s) upbeating-torsional nystagmus toward the affected (down) ear, and fatigue on repetition. Central positional vertigo causes nystagmus that is immediate, non-fatiguing, often purely vertical (e.g., downbeat) or direction-changing, with other neurologic signs. Meniere disease causes spontaneous vertigo lasting minutes to hours with hearing loss, tinnitus, and fullness—not positional triggering. Vestibular neuritis causes acute, continuous (not positional) vertigo with unidirectional spontaneous nystagmus.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying mechanism of posterior-canal BPPV?
options: Endolymphatic hydrops from excess endolymph
Displaced otoconia (canaliths) from the utricle entering the semicircular canal
Viral inflammation of the vestibular nerve
A vestibular schwannoma compressing CN VIII
stages.explanation: BPPV results from otoconia (calcium carbonate crystals, 'canaliths') that dislodge from the utricular macula and migrate into a semicircular canal, most often the posterior canal. With head position changes, these particles move within the canal and abnormally deflect the cupula, producing brief vertigo and the classic nystagmus. Endolymphatic hydrops is the mechanism of Meniere disease. Viral inflammation of the vestibular nerve underlies vestibular neuritis. A vestibular schwannoma causes progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss and imbalance, not brief positional spells with a positive Dix-Hallpike. Recognizing the canalith mechanism explains why repositioning maneuvers work.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the most effective initial treatment for her right posterior-canal BPPV?
options: Daily meclizine indefinitely
The Epley (canalith repositioning) maneuver
Oral corticosteroids
Surgical labyrinthectomy
stages.explanation: The Epley canalith repositioning maneuver is first-line and highly effective: it uses sequential head positions to guide the displaced otoconia out of the posterior canal back into the utricle, resolving symptoms in most patients, often after one or two sessions. Vestibular suppressants like meclizine only blunt symptoms briefly, do not cure BPPV, and chronic use impairs central compensation and causes sedation, so they are not recommended for ongoing management. Corticosteroids treat vestibular neuritis or sudden sensorineural hearing loss, not BPPV. Surgical procedures (e.g., canal occlusion) are last-resort for rare, intractable cases, never first-line.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which finding during evaluation would argue against BPPV and prompt urgent workup for a central cause?
options: Brief vertigo that fatigues with repeated Dix-Hallpike
Direction-changing or downbeat nystagmus that does not fatigue, with gait ataxia
Mild nausea after a positional spell
A normal otoscopic exam
stages.explanation: Non-fatiguing, direction-changing, or purely vertical (especially downbeat) nystagmus, particularly with truncal/gait ataxia, dysarthria, or other neurologic signs, suggests a central (cerebellar or brainstem) lesion and warrants urgent imaging (MRI). Brief, fatigable vertigo on repeated Dix-Hallpike is the expected, reassuring BPPV pattern. Mild post-spell nausea is common and benign. A normal otoscopic exam is expected in BPPV and does not point to a central cause. Distinguishing peripheral from central vertigo at the bedside prevents missing a posterior-circulation stroke masquerading as 'dizziness.'

cases.diagnosis: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (posterior canal, right)
pearls: BPPV gives brief (<1 min), positionally triggered vertigo with latent, fatigable, upbeating-torsional nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike—no hearing loss or tinnitus.
The mechanism is displaced otoconia (canaliths) in a semicircular canal, usually the posterior canal.
Treat with the Epley canalith repositioning maneuver; avoid chronic vestibular suppressants, which delay central compensation.
Non-fatiguing, direction-changing or downbeat nystagmus, or gait ataxia, suggests a central cause and needs MRI.

cases.title: Recurrent Vertigo Attacks with a Roaring Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: Repeated episodes of severe spinning lasting hours with fullness and roaring in the left ear.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man reports recurrent episodes of intense vertigo over the past four months. Each attack comes on spontaneously, lasts roughly two to three hours, and is accompanied by nausea, vomiting, a low-pitched roaring tinnitus, and a sensation of pressure or fullness in the left ear. He notices that his hearing in the left ear worsens during attacks, sounding muffled and distorted, then partially recovers between episodes, though he feels his baseline left-ear hearing is slowly declining. He has no headache, photophobia, or visual aura, and no continuous vertigo between attacks. He denies recent infection, head trauma, or new medications. He has well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily and no other medical problems. No known allergies. He works in sales, drinks coffee heavily, eats a lot of salty take-out food, and drinks alcohol on weekends; he does not smoke. There is no family history of similar episodes. He is frustrated that attacks strike without warning and disrupt his work.
cases.exam: Between attacks, the patient appears well and is in no distress. Otoscopy is normal bilaterally with intact tympanic membranes. Weber (512 Hz) lateralizes to the right ear; Rinne shows air > bone bilaterally, consistent with a left sensorineural pattern. No spontaneous or positional nystagmus at rest; Dix-Hallpike negative bilaterally. Cranial nerves intact, including normal facial sensation and movement. Gait steady, Romberg negative, no dysmetria or dysdiadochokinesia. Cardiovascular exam normal without bruits. No neck stiffness. The remainder of the neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The combination of episodic vertigo lasting hours, fluctuating hearing loss, low-pitched tinnitus, and aural fullness in one ear is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
Meniere disease (endolymphatic hydrops)
Vestibular migraine
Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)
stages.explanation: Meniere disease is defined by recurrent spontaneous vertigo episodes lasting 20 minutes to 12 hours, fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss (classically low-frequency), tinnitus, and aural fullness in the affected ear. This patient's hours-long attacks with a roaring tinnitus, fullness, and fluctuating then progressively declining left-ear hearing fit the criteria. BPPV causes brief positional spells without hearing symptoms. Vestibular migraine causes episodic vertigo often with headache, photophobia, or aura and usually lacks progressive cochlear loss. An acoustic neuroma causes slowly progressive unilateral sensorineural loss and imbalance, not discrete vertigo attacks with fullness and fluctuation.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiometry (left): low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (up-sloping), ~35 dB at 250-500 Hz improving at higher frequencies
Pure-tone audiometry (right): within normal limits
Word recognition (left): reduced
Tympanometry: Type A bilaterally (normal middle-ear pressure)
MRI internal auditory canals (to exclude retrocochlear pathology): no vestibular schwannoma

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: His left-ear audiogram shows a low-frequency sensorineural loss and MRI excludes a schwannoma. How does this fit the diagnosis?
options: It refutes Meniere disease, which causes high-frequency loss only
Early Meniere disease classically produces a fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural loss, and a normal MRI excludes a retrocochlear mass
It indicates noise-induced hearing loss
It confirms otosclerosis
stages.explanation: Early Meniere disease characteristically causes a fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (an up-sloping audiogram), which can become broader and more permanent over time—exactly the pattern seen here. MRI of the internal auditory canals is obtained to rule out retrocochlear pathology such as a vestibular schwannoma; a normal study supports a primary inner-ear (hydropic) process. Noise-induced loss classically produces a high-frequency notch around 4 kHz, not low-frequency loss. Otosclerosis causes conductive (not sensorineural) loss, often with a Carhart notch, and a normal tympanogram with stapes fixation, which does not match this sensorineural, fluctuating picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line, long-term management to reduce the frequency of his attacks?
options: Daily high-dose meclizine indefinitely
Dietary sodium restriction and a diuretic (e.g., HCTZ), plus reducing caffeine and alcohol
Immediate intratympanic gentamicin ablation
Surgical labyrinthectomy now
stages.explanation: First-line maintenance therapy for Meniere disease targets endolymphatic fluid balance: a low-sodium diet plus a diuretic, with reduction of caffeine, alcohol, and other triggers, lowers attack frequency for many patients. Vestibular suppressants like meclizine and antiemetics help abort acute attacks but are not appropriate for daily long-term prophylaxis and impair central compensation. Intratympanic gentamicin (chemical ablation) and surgical labyrinthectomy are reserved for medically refractory, disabling disease because they can worsen hearing/balance; intratympanic steroids are a less destructive escalation. Lifestyle/diuretic therapy is the standard initial step before escalating.

stages.label: ACUTE ATTACK
stages.question: During a severe acute attack with vertigo and vomiting in the office, what is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Epley repositioning maneuver
A vestibular suppressant/antiemetic such as meclizine or an antihistamine, with supportive care
Empiric IV antibiotics
Oral sodium loading
stages.explanation: Acute Meniere attacks are managed symptomatically with vestibular suppressants and antiemetics (e.g., meclizine, dimenhydrinate, promethazine, or a benzodiazepine), antinausea support, and hydration until the episode passes. The Epley maneuver treats BPPV, a different, positional disorder, and would not help hydropic vertigo. Antibiotics have no role because Meniere is not infectious. Sodium loading is exactly the wrong move—high salt worsens endolymphatic hydrops; patients are counseled to restrict sodium. Short courses of suppressants for acute attacks are acceptable, but chronic daily use is avoided to allow central vestibular compensation.

pearls: Meniere = episodic vertigo (minutes to hours) + fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss + tinnitus + aural fullness in one ear.
Early disease shows a fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural loss; obtain MRI to exclude a vestibular schwannoma.
First-line maintenance is low-sodium diet plus a diuretic, with caffeine/alcohol reduction; escalate to intratympanic steroids/gentamicin or surgery only if refractory.
Treat acute attacks with vestibular suppressants and antiemetics; avoid chronic suppressant use, which delays central compensation.

cases.title: Sudden Constant Spinning After the Flu
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt, severe, continuous vertigo with vomiting that began this morning.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman presents to the ED with sudden onset of severe, continuous room-spinning vertigo that started this morning and has persisted for several hours, with nausea and repeated vomiting. The vertigo is constant—present even when she lies still—though head movement makes it worse. She had an upper respiratory viral illness with sore throat and congestion about a week ago. She denies hearing loss, tinnitus, or ear fullness, and has no headache, double vision, slurred speech, facial droop, limb weakness, or numbness. She has no prior similar episodes. She has no significant medical history, takes only an oral contraceptive, and reports no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. There is no history of head trauma. She is frightened she is having a stroke. She cannot walk without assistance because of the vertigo and feels she veers to one side. There is no recent travel, no neck pain or manipulation, and no family history of stroke or migraine.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, lying still with eyes closed, vomiting intermittently. Spontaneous horizontal nystagmus with a torsional component, fast phase beating to the right, suppressed by visual fixation. Otoscopy normal; hearing grossly intact bilaterally, Weber midline, Rinne air > bone bilaterally. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; no dysarthria, no facial asymmetry. Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin without dysmetria; no limb ataxia out of proportion. Gait markedly unsteady with a tendency to fall toward the left; unable to walk unaided. No long-tract signs. HINTS exam performed at the bedside (head impulse, nystagmus, test of skew) as detailed below.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with acute continuous vertigo, what is the single most important initial task?
options: Immediately perform the Epley maneuver
Distinguish a peripheral vestibular cause from a central (stroke) cause
Order an urgent audiogram
Start empiric IV antibiotics for labyrinthitis
stages.explanation: The crucial first step in acute, continuous vertigo (acute vestibular syndrome) is to determine whether the cause is peripheral (benign, e.g., vestibular neuritis) or central (dangerous, e.g., posterior-circulation stroke), because management and disposition differ entirely. The Epley maneuver treats BPPV, which causes brief positional—not continuous—vertigo, so it is inappropriate here. An audiogram is not the priority in the acute setting and would not exclude stroke. Antibiotics are not indicated unless there is bacterial labyrinthitis (e.g., with concurrent suppurative ear disease). The HINTS exam and neurologic assessment are the tools used to make this peripheral-versus-central distinction.

stages.label: HINTS EXAM
stages.content:
Head Impulse test: positive (corrective saccade) when the head is rapidly turned toward the LEFT — abnormal vestibulo-ocular reflex on the left
Nystagmus: unidirectional, horizontal-torsional, fast phase to the right, suppresses with fixation; does not change direction with gaze
Test of Skew: no vertical skew deviation on alternate cover testing (negative)
Finger rub hearing: symmetric, no acute hearing loss

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Her HINTS exam shows a positive (abnormal) head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus, and no skew. What does this pattern indicate?
options: A central cause (stroke), requiring urgent MRI
A peripheral cause consistent with vestibular neuritis
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
Meniere disease
stages.explanation: A reassuring 'peripheral' HINTS pattern is: abnormal (positive) head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus that does not change direction, and absent skew deviation—this points to vestibular neuritis. Paradoxically, in acute vestibular syndrome a NORMAL head impulse test, direction-changing nystagmus, or a positive skew (any one) suggests a central/stroke cause and is more concerning than a peripheral pattern. Her abnormal left head impulse (impaired VOR on the affected side), single-direction right-beating nystagmus, and negative skew are classic for a peripheral vestibular lesion. BPPV gives brief positional vertigo with a positive Dix-Hallpike, and Meniere includes hearing loss/tinnitus/fullness—neither fits.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the post-viral onset, continuous vertigo, peripheral HINTS pattern, and preserved hearing, what is the diagnosis?
options: Labyrinthitis
Vestibular neuritis
Cerebellar stroke
Vestibular schwannoma
stages.explanation: Vestibular neuritis is acute, continuous vertigo from inflammation of the vestibular nerve, often following a viral illness, with a peripheral HINTS pattern and—importantly—preserved hearing, because only the vestibular division is affected. Labyrinthitis presents similarly but includes hearing loss and/or tinnitus from cochlear involvement, which she lacks. A cerebellar stroke would typically show a central HINTS pattern (normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew) and/or other neurologic signs; her exam is reassuringly peripheral. A vestibular schwannoma causes slowly progressive unilateral sensorineural loss and imbalance, not an acute continuous attack.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for her vestibular neuritis?
options: Long-term daily vestibular suppressants to keep symptoms fully controlled
Short-course symptomatic relief (antiemetics/vestibular suppressants) for a few days, then early vestibular rehabilitation; consider corticosteroids early
Immediate IV thrombolysis
Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics
stages.explanation: Vestibular neuritis is managed with brief symptomatic treatment—antiemetics and vestibular suppressants (e.g., meclizine, antihistamines, benzodiazepines) for the first few days—followed by early mobilization and vestibular rehabilitation exercises to promote central compensation; prolonged suppressant use actually delays recovery. Corticosteroids are sometimes started early and may speed recovery of vestibular function. Thrombolysis is for ischemic stroke, which her peripheral HINTS pattern argues against. Antibiotics are not indicated because the process is viral/post-viral inflammation of the nerve, not a bacterial infection. Most patients improve substantially over days to weeks as compensation occurs.

pearls: Acute vestibular syndrome = sudden, continuous vertigo; the first task is peripheral vs central (stroke), using the HINTS exam.
Peripheral/reassuring HINTS = abnormal head impulse + unidirectional nystagmus + no skew; a NORMAL head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew suggests stroke.
Vestibular neuritis spares hearing; labyrinthitis adds hearing loss/tinnitus from cochlear involvement.
Treat with short-course suppressants/antiemetics, early vestibular rehab (avoid prolonged suppressants), and consider early corticosteroids.

cases.title: One-Sided Deafness That Came On in Hours
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapid loss of hearing in the left ear over several hours with ringing and fullness.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man comes to the ED after noticing that over the course of this morning his left ear went from normal to profoundly muffled, now with loud ringing (tinnitus) and a sense of fullness. He first noticed it when he could not hear his phone on that side. He has mild constant unsteadiness but no true spinning, no headache, no facial weakness, no vision changes, and no limb symptoms. He denies recent loud-noise exposure, barotrauma, ear trauma, or ototoxic drug use, and had no preceding ear pain or drainage. He had a mild cold two weeks ago. He has hypertension and prediabetes; medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and aspirin 81 mg. No known allergies. He works in finance, does not smoke, and drinks socially. No family history of early hearing loss or autoimmune disease. He is very anxious because the hearing has not improved over several hours. He has no fever, no neck stiffness, and no rash. He has not flown recently and denies scuba diving.
cases.exam: External ears and canals normal; tympanic membranes intact, mobile, and pearly bilaterally with no effusion, perforation, or cerumen impaction. There is markedly reduced hearing to finger rub and whispered voice on the left. Weber (512 Hz) lateralizes to the RIGHT (away from the affected left ear); Rinne shows air > bone in both ears. No spontaneous nystagmus; gait mildly cautious but able to walk. Cranial nerves intact including normal facial movement and sensation; no dysarthria or dysmetria. No facial palsy, no meningismus, no focal weakness. Cardiovascular and remaining neurologic exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Weber lateralizes away from the affected ear and both ears have air > bone on Rinne. What type of hearing loss does this indicate?
options: Conductive hearing loss in the left ear
Sensorineural hearing loss in the left ear
Conductive hearing loss in the right ear
Normal hearing with malingering
stages.explanation: When the Weber lateralizes to the BETTER (unaffected) ear and Rinne remains air > bone bilaterally, the loss is sensorineural in the worse (left) ear. In sensorineural loss, sound localizes to the good ear and air conduction still exceeds bone conduction because the conductive apparatus is intact. A conductive loss would instead lateralize the Weber toward the affected ear with a negative Rinne (bone > air) on that side. The intact, mobile tympanic membranes and clear canals exclude cerumen, effusion, or perforation as a cause, supporting a sensorineural process rather than a mechanical/conductive one or malingering.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given rapid unilateral sensorineural hearing loss developing over hours with a normal ear exam, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Cerumen impaction
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL)
Otitis media with effusion
Otosclerosis
stages.explanation: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss is defined as a sensorineural drop of at least 30 dB across three contiguous frequencies developing within 72 hours, usually unilateral, often idiopathic (frequently presumed viral/vascular). A normal otoscopic exam with a sensorineural Weber/Rinne pattern over hours is classic and constitutes an otologic emergency. Cerumen impaction and otitis media with effusion cause conductive—not sensorineural—loss and would show abnormal otoscopy and a Weber lateralizing toward the affected ear. Otosclerosis causes a slowly progressive conductive loss with a Carhart notch, not an acute sensorineural drop. Prompt recognition is vital because treatment is time-sensitive.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY & WORKUP
stages.content:
Urgent pure-tone audiometry (left): sensorineural loss of ~45 dB across 1000, 2000, and 4000 Hz
Audiometry (right): within normal limits
Word recognition (left): markedly reduced
Tympanometry: Type A bilaterally (normal middle ear)
Basic labs: CBC, glucose, and metabolic panel unremarkable
Plan: MRI of internal auditory canals/brain to evaluate for retrocochlear pathology (e.g., vestibular schwannoma)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Audiometry confirms a 45 dB sensorineural loss and he presents within the treatment window. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Reassurance and routine outpatient follow-up in 6 weeks
Prompt corticosteroids (oral high-dose, with intratympanic steroids as an option/salvage)
Empiric IV antibiotics for presumed labyrinthitis
Immediate cochlear implantation
stages.explanation: Idiopathic SSNHL is treated urgently with corticosteroids—high-dose oral prednisone (or equivalent), with intratympanic steroid injection used as primary therapy in some patients or as salvage if oral steroids fail. The treatment window is narrow: starting within about two weeks (ideally sooner) gives the best chance of hearing recovery, so this is an emergency, not a routine follow-up. Antibiotics are unnecessary because there is no bacterial infection; otoscopy is normal and there is no suppurative process. Cochlear implantation is not an acute intervention and is considered only for permanent, severe-to-profound loss after medical therapy fails. MRI is obtained to exclude a retrocochlear mass.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Why is MRI of the internal auditory canals recommended in idiopathic SSNHL?
options: To detect cerumen in the canal
To evaluate for retrocochlear pathology such as a vestibular schwannoma
To diagnose otitis media
To confirm a tympanic membrane perforation
stages.explanation: A minority of patients with apparently idiopathic SSNHL harbor retrocochlear pathology—most importantly a vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma)—so MRI of the internal auditory canals/brain (or, less ideally, auditory brainstem response) is recommended to evaluate for a tumor or other central lesion. Cerumen and tympanic membrane perforation are diagnosed by simple otoscopy, not MRI, and cause conductive rather than sensorineural loss. Otitis media is also an otoscopic/clinical diagnosis. Identifying a schwannoma changes management and surveillance, which is why imaging is part of the standard workup even when steroids are started empirically for presumed idiopathic SSNHL.

pearls: Weber lateralizing AWAY from the bad ear with air > bone bilaterally = sensorineural loss; a normal ear exam over hours points to SSNHL.
SSNHL = ≥30 dB sensorineural loss over ≥3 contiguous frequencies within 72 hours—an otologic emergency.
Start corticosteroids promptly (oral high-dose ± intratympanic); the recovery window is roughly two weeks, so do not defer treatment.
Obtain MRI of the internal auditory canals to exclude retrocochlear pathology (vestibular schwannoma) in idiopathic SSNHL.

cases.title: Toddler with Drooling and Refusal to Eat
cases.chiefComplaint: Her parents report sudden drooling, fussiness, and refusal to eat for the past 3 hours.
cases.history: An 18-month-old previously healthy girl is brought to the ED by her parents after they found her crying and drooling in the living room about 3 hours ago. She had been playing unsupervised near the coffee table, where the family keeps a TV remote and several loose hearing-aid and watch batteries. The parents are unsure whether she swallowed anything but note an open, empty battery compartment on a small remote. Since then she has refused her bottle and a cracker, gags when she attempts to swallow, and points to her chest. There has been no vomiting, no fever, and no respiratory distress. She has been developing normally, is up to date on immunizations, and takes no medications. There are no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and an older sibling; no smokers in the home. Family history is unremarkable. The parents deny any prior choking episodes or known food allergies. They estimate the ingestion window to be less than 4 hours and brought the remote and a spare battery to show the triage nurse.
cases.exam: The child is alert but fussy and consolable in her mother's arms. She is drooling pooled saliva and gags with swallowing attempts. There is no stridor, no retractions, and no wheeze; air entry is symmetric. The oropharynx shows no visible foreign body, no burns, and no erythema. There is no neck swelling, crepitus, or tenderness. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and non-tender with normal bowel sounds. There is no perioral cyanosis. Capillary refill is brisk and she is well perfused. No drooling of blood and no hematemesis are noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the history of a possible swallowed disc-shaped battery and acute drooling, what is the single most important immediate step?
options: Reassure the parents and arrange outpatient follow-up since she has no respiratory distress
Obtain emergent biplane (AP and lateral) radiographs of the neck, chest, and abdomen
Give activated charcoal to bind the ingested object
Induce emesis with ipecac to expel the foreign body
stages.explanation: Emergent AP and lateral radiographs from the neck through the abdomen are critical to localize a suspected battery and distinguish it from a coin. A button battery in the esophagus is a true emergency because mucosal injury begins within 2 hours. Reassurance and outpatient follow-up is dangerous given the time-sensitive caustic injury. Activated charcoal does not bind a metal battery, can obscure imaging, and risks aspiration. Inducing emesis is contraindicated for any caustic or foreign-body ingestion because it risks re-exposure of the esophagus and aspiration. Biplane films also let you assess the orientation and the characteristic step-off, guiding urgency of endoscopic removal.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
AP neck/chest radiograph: 20-mm radiopaque disc in the proximal esophagus at the level of the thoracic inlet, with a peripheral lucent halo producing a 'double-ring' (halo) sign.
Lateral radiograph: same disc shows a visible step-off in profile.
No pneumomediastinum, no subcutaneous emphysema, no free air under the diaphragm.
Chest: lungs clear, no consolidation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The radiographic 'double-ring' sign on the AP film and the step-off on lateral view most specifically indicate which diagnosis?
options: Esophageal coin ingestion
Esophageal button (disc) battery ingestion
Aspirated foreign body in the right mainstem bronchus
Esophageal food impaction
stages.explanation: The 'double-ring' (halo) sign on the AP view and a step-off/bilaminar appearance on the lateral view are classic for a button battery, reflecting its bilayer construction; this distinguishes it from a coin, which appears as a single uniform disc with smooth edges. The position in the esophagus rather than the airway, plus the lack of wheeze and the symmetric breath sounds, argues against bronchial aspiration. Food impaction would not be radiopaque and would not produce these rings. Recognizing the battery is essential because, unlike a coin, it cannot be safely observed and requires emergent removal.

stages.label: TEMPORIZING MEASURE
stages.question: While mobilizing the team for endoscopic removal, which evidence-based intervention can be given to mitigate esophageal injury in a child this age with a battery lodged for under 12 hours?
options: Administer oral honey (10 mL every 10 minutes) up to 6 doses if the child can swallow and removal is not immediate
Have the child drink large volumes of water to flush the battery into the stomach
Administer intravenous proton pump inhibitor as the primary neutralizing therapy
Give oral sodium bicarbonate to neutralize the alkaline current
stages.explanation: National Capital Poison Center guidance supports early oral honey (in children >12 months, within 12 hours of ingestion, if the battery is esophageal and the airway is protected) to coat the battery and buffer the local alkaline hydrolysis that drives liquefactive necrosis; sucralfate serves the same role in-hospital. Forcing large water boluses does not reliably dislodge the battery and risks aspiration. IV PPI reduces gastric acid but does not address the local hydroxide generation at the negative pole and is not the temporizing measure. Oral bicarbonate is alkaline and would worsen, not neutralize, the injury. Honey is adjunctive and never delays endoscopic removal.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive next step in management for this esophageal button battery?
options: Admit for observation and repeat radiograph in 24 hours to confirm passage
Schedule outpatient endoscopy within 48 hours
Emergent endoscopic removal in the operating room, ideally within 2 hours
Glucagon administration to relax the lower esophageal sphincter and allow passage
stages.explanation: An esophageal button battery is a surgical emergency: emergent endoscopic removal (rigid or flexible) in the OR, ideally within 2 hours of presentation, is the standard of care because mucosal burns progress rapidly and can lead to perforation, tracheoesophageal fistula, or fatal exsanguination from aortoesophageal fistula. Observation is acceptable only for batteries that have already passed into the stomach in an asymptomatic child, never for esophageal lodgement. Delaying to outpatient endoscopy risks irreversible necrosis. Glucagon is used for esophageal food impaction, not batteries, and would dangerously delay removal. After removal, the mucosa is inspected and the patient is monitored for delayed complications.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two weeks after successful removal, the same child returns with massive hematemesis and shock. Which life-threatening complication of the original injury is most likely?
options: Mallory-Weiss tear from vomiting
Aortoesophageal (or other major vessel) fistula from delayed transmural necrosis
Esophageal candidiasis with bleeding
Peptic ulcer disease
stages.explanation: Delayed massive hemorrhage after esophageal button battery injury is most often due to erosion through the esophageal wall into a major vessel, classically the aorta, producing an aortoesophageal fistula days to weeks later; this carries very high mortality and can occur even after the battery is removed because necrosis continues. A Mallory-Weiss tear causes self-limited bleeding, not exsanguinating hemorrhage in this context. Esophageal candidiasis causes odynophagia and rarely massive bleeding, and is unrelated to caustic injury timeline. Peptic ulcer disease does not fit an 18-month-old with a recent caustic esophageal burn. This is why prolonged post-removal monitoring and serial imaging are required.

cases.diagnosis: Esophageal button (disc) battery ingestion with double-ring sign requiring emergent endoscopic removal
pearls: The 'double-ring/halo' sign on AP and a step-off on lateral radiograph distinguish a button battery from a coin.
An esophageal button battery is a true emergency: remove endoscopically within 2 hours; injury begins within 2 hours of contact.
Early oral honey (>12 months, <12 hours) or in-hospital sucralfate buffers the alkaline injury but never delays removal.
Delayed complications (TEF, esophageal stricture, aortoesophageal fistula) can present days to weeks later, even after removal.

cases.title: Preschooler with Mouth Pain After a Household Spill
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother reports he screamed after putting his mouth to a bottle of drain cleaner and now has mouth pain and drooling.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the ED roughly 45 minutes after his mother heard him scream in the laundry room. She found him next to an open bottle of crystalline drain cleaner (sodium hydroxide) with white granules on his lips and a wet shirt. He immediately spat, cried, and refused to drink. Since then he has been drooling, complaining of mouth and throat pain, and has vomited once with no blood. There has been no stridor or voice change, but he is reluctant to swallow. He is otherwise healthy, fully immunized, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He lives with his mother and grandmother; the cleaner was stored under the sink. The mother did not induce vomiting and gave a small sip of water at home. There is no prior history of ingestions or developmental concerns. Family history is noncontributory. She brought the product container, which lists sodium hydroxide as the active ingredient and is labeled strongly alkaline.
cases.exam: The child is crying but consolable and protecting his airway. The lips and anterior tongue show white-gray pseudomembranous burns with surrounding erythema. The oropharynx is erythematous with patchy white plaques on the posterior pharyngeal wall and soft palate. He is drooling and refuses to swallow. The voice is clear and there is no stridor, hoarseness, or drooling of blood. Neck is supple without crepitus or swelling. Lungs are clear with no retractions. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the epigastrium, non-distended, with normal bowel sounds. No skin burns beyond the perioral area.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After ensuring airway, breathing, and circulation in this child with a strong-alkali ingestion, which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Administer a weak acid (dilute vinegar) to neutralize the alkali
Give activated charcoal and induce emesis to clear residual cleaner
Provide nothing by mouth, give IV fluids, and consult for early endoscopy and poison control
Perform immediate blind nasogastric tube placement to lavage the stomach
stages.explanation: For caustic ingestion the priorities are airway assessment, NPO status, IV hydration, analgesia, and early involvement of poison control and the endoscopy/surgical team. Attempting chemical neutralization with an acid is contraindicated because the exothermic reaction generates heat and worsens tissue injury. Activated charcoal and induced emesis are contraindicated: charcoal does not bind caustics and obscures endoscopy, while emesis re-exposes the esophagus to the corrosive and risks aspiration. Blind NG placement or gastric lavage risks perforating already-weakened tissue. Supportive care plus timely endoscopy is the cornerstone, allowing injury grading that guides prognosis and treatment.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TIMING
stages.question: This child has clear oropharyngeal burns and symptoms. When should diagnostic upper endoscopy be performed?
options: Within 12-24 hours of ingestion (and before 48 hours)
Immediately at the bedside within the first hour
Only after 1 week to allow inflammation to settle
Endoscopy is unnecessary because oral burns reliably predict esophageal injury
stages.explanation: Endoscopy is best performed between 12 and 24 hours after ingestion (and avoided after 48 hours): early enough to grade injury and guide management, but late enough for the burn to declare its depth. Endoscopy within the first hour underestimates injury depth because the burn has not fully evolved. Waiting a week crosses the window of maximal wall weakening (roughly days 5-15), when scope-induced perforation risk peaks. Critically, the presence or absence of oral burns does NOT reliably predict esophageal injury: significant esophageal burns can occur without oral findings and vice versa, which is precisely why endoscopy is indicated in symptomatic children.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy at 18 hours:
Proximal esophagus: circumferential whitish exudate with deep ulceration (Zargar grade 2b).
Mid esophagus: scattered hemorrhage and ulceration, near-circumferential.
Stomach: patchy erythema, no deep ulceration.
No full-thickness necrosis or perforation visualized.
