Vitals stable during procedure.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Given grade 2b circumferential esophageal injury, which complication is this child at highest long-term risk for?
options: Achalasia
Esophageal stricture formation
Barrett esophagus within weeks
Zenker diverticulum
stages.explanation: Circumferential grade 2b (and grade 3) caustic injuries carry a high risk of esophageal stricture as the deep burn heals by fibrosis and contraction over weeks to months; these children need surveillance and often serial dilation. Achalasia is a primary motility disorder of the lower esophageal sphincter, not a sequela of caustic burns. Barrett esophagus results from chronic acid reflux over years and would not appear within weeks. Zenker diverticulum is a posterior pharyngeal pulsion diverticulum related to cricopharyngeal dysfunction, unrelated to alkali burns. Long-term, caustic strictures also confer an increased lifetime risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, warranting future surveillance.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which post-endoscopy management strategy is most appropriate for this grade 2b circumferential injury?
options: Routine high-dose corticosteroids for all caustic burns to prevent stricture
Supportive care, proton pump inhibitor, nutritional support, and planned surveillance for stricture with dilation as needed
Immediate esophagectomy to prevent malignancy
Discharge home today with oral antibiotics and no follow-up
stages.explanation: Management of a grade 2b injury centers on supportive care: NPO initially then graded nutrition (sometimes enteral access), acid suppression with a PPI, analgesia, and structured follow-up to detect and dilate strictures as they form. Routine high-dose steroids for all burns are not recommended; evidence is mixed and they may mask perforation and increase infection, though selected protocols use them for grade 2b only in specialized centers. Immediate esophagectomy is reserved for full-thickness necrosis/perforation, not stable grade 2b. Discharge with antibiotics and no follow-up is unsafe given high stricture risk and the need for monitoring. Surveillance for delayed malignancy is also part of long-term care.

cases.diagnosis: Alkaline (sodium hydroxide) caustic ingestion with grade 2b circumferential esophageal injury
pearls: Never neutralize, dilute aggressively, induce emesis, or give charcoal in caustic ingestion; secure airway and keep NPO.
Endoscopy at 12-24 hours grades injury; oral burns do NOT reliably predict esophageal involvement.
Alkali causes liquefactive necrosis with deep penetration; circumferential grade 2b/3 burns lead to strictures.
Caustic strictures carry an increased lifetime risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma; long-term surveillance is needed.

cases.title: Woman with Sudden Tongue and Lip Swelling
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports her tongue and lower lip swelled rapidly over the past 2 hours.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents to the ED with progressive swelling of her tongue and lower lip that began about 2 hours ago while she was watching television. She denies any new food, medication, insect sting, or detergent exposure. She has no hives, no itching, and no rash. She reports mild difficulty articulating words because of her swollen tongue but denies fever, sore throat, or chest tightness. Her history includes hypertension, for which she started lisinopril 20 mg daily about 8 months ago, and type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily. She also takes atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. She has no known drug allergies and no prior episodes of swelling. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and has no family history of recurrent angioedema. She has never required intubation or epinephrine. She notes she has had an occasional dry cough since starting her blood pressure medicine, which she attributed to allergies. She has not taken any NSAIDs recently.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious but speaking in full sentences with a slightly muffled voice. There is marked non-pitting swelling of the tongue and lower lip; the tongue is enlarged but mobile and does not yet obstruct the oropharyngeal view. There is no urticaria, no facial flushing, and no periorbital swelling. There is no stridor at rest, and lungs are clear bilaterally with good air entry. No wheezing. The uvula is midline and mildly edematous. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy. There is no drooling. Cardiovascular exam shows a regular rhythm without murmur. No skin rash anywhere on the body.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given isolated tongue and lip swelling without urticaria or pruritus in a patient on lisinopril, which mechanism best explains her presentation?
options: IgE-mediated (histaminergic) anaphylaxis to a food allergen
Bradykinin-mediated angioedema from ACE inhibitor therapy
Hereditary angioedema due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency presenting for the first time at 58
Cellulitis of the lip and tongue
stages.explanation: The absence of urticaria and pruritus, the isolated mucosal swelling, and the chronic use of lisinopril point to bradykinin-mediated angioedema from ACE inhibitor therapy; ACE inhibitors block kininase II, allowing bradykinin to accumulate, and this can occur months to years after starting the drug. IgE-mediated anaphylaxis typically includes urticaria, pruritus, flushing, or bronchospasm and a clear trigger. Newly presenting hereditary angioedema at 58 is very unlikely; it usually manifests in childhood/adolescence with recurrent attacks and a family history. Cellulitis would cause warmth, erythema, tenderness, and often fever, not acute symmetric non-erythematous swelling. The dry cough she noted is also a classic ACE-inhibitor clue.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate priority in this patient?
options: Continuous airway assessment and monitoring in a setting prepared for difficult airway management
Immediate administration of a large fluid bolus
Obtaining a CT of the neck before any intervention
Sending the patient home with a prescription for an antihistamine
stages.explanation: The single greatest danger of angioedema involving the tongue is airway compromise, which can progress unpredictably; the priority is close, continuous airway monitoring in a monitored setting with difficult-airway equipment and personnel ready, and a low threshold for early intubation while it is still feasible. A fluid bolus does not address airway edema and is not the priority in a normotensive-to-hypertensive patient. CT neck wastes time and removes the patient from monitoring; the diagnosis is clinical. Discharge with an antihistamine is unsafe because bradykinin-mediated edema can worsen and does not respond to antihistamines. Stopping the offending ACE inhibitor permanently is essential but secondary to airway safety.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is given IM epinephrine, an antihistamine, and IV corticosteroids empirically, but her tongue swelling continues to progress over 30 minutes. What does this most strongly suggest?
options: She needs a higher dose of antihistamine
The edema is bradykinin-mediated and unlikely to respond to standard anaphylaxis therapy; secure the airway
She has refractory anaphylaxis requiring repeat epinephrine indefinitely
The diagnosis must be infectious and antibiotics should be started
stages.explanation: Lack of response to epinephrine, antihistamines, and steroids is characteristic of bradykinin-mediated (ACE-inhibitor) angioedema, which does not respond to histamine-directed therapy; persistent progression mandates definitive airway protection. Escalating antihistamines will not help a bradykinin process. Labeling it refractory anaphylaxis and giving endless epinephrine ignores the mechanism and exposes her to cardiac risk. An infectious etiology is inconsistent with the rapid, afebrile, non-erythematous presentation. While standard anaphylaxis drugs are reasonable empirically because mechanism is not always certain at the bedside, failure to respond should pivot management toward airway control and consideration of targeted agents (e.g., icatibant or C1-inhibitor concentrate where used).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After her airway is stabilized and swelling resolves, what is the most important long-term instruction at discharge?
options: Restart lisinopril once swelling resolves since it was effective
Switch to a different ACE inhibitor at a lower dose
Permanently discontinue all ACE inhibitors and document the reaction; choose an alternative antihypertensive class
Carry an epinephrine auto-injector and continue lisinopril
stages.explanation: ACE-inhibitor angioedema is a class effect, so the drug must be permanently discontinued, the reaction documented in the chart, and an alternative class (e.g., calcium channel blocker, thiazide) selected; angiotensin receptor blockers carry a small but real cross-reactivity risk and are used cautiously if at all. Restarting lisinopril risks recurrent, potentially fatal episodes. Switching to another ACE inhibitor does not help because the mechanism is shared across the class. Prescribing an epinephrine auto-injector and continuing lisinopril is wrong on both counts: epinephrine does not reliably reverse bradykinin edema and the offending drug must be stopped. Clear documentation prevents future inadvertent re-exposure.

cases.diagnosis: ACE inhibitor-induced (bradykinin-mediated) angioedema of the tongue and lip
pearls: ACE-inhibitor angioedema is bradykinin-mediated: no urticaria or pruritus, and it can begin months to years after starting the drug.
It does not reliably respond to epinephrine, antihistamines, or steroids; the airway is the priority.
Tongue and laryngeal involvement can progress rapidly; intubate early while the airway is still accessible.
Permanently discontinue all ACE inhibitors (class effect) and document the reaction; avoid re-exposure.

cases.title: Young Woman with Noisy Breathing and Shortness of Breath
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports progressive noisy breathing and exertional shortness of breath over the past 3 months.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents to the ENT clinic with 3 months of progressively noisy breathing and dyspnea on exertion. She first noticed difficulty during workouts and now hears noise both when inhaling and exhaling even at rest. She was hospitalized in the ICU 6 months ago for pneumonia and ARDS requiring intubation for 11 days, followed by a brief noninvasive ventilation course. Since extubation she has had a mild change in voice and intermittent throat tightness. She denies fever, weight loss, hemoptysis, sinus symptoms, nasal crusting, hematuria, or joint pains. She has been treated twice for presumed asthma with inhalers and a steroid course with no improvement. Her PMH is otherwise unremarkable; she takes only an albuterol inhaler with no benefit and has no allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, works as a teacher, and drinks socially. Family history is negative for autoimmune disease. She has no history of reflux symptoms, no prior neck surgery, and no recent upper respiratory infection. She is increasingly anxious because the noise worsens when she climbs stairs.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable at rest but audibly stridulous, with stridor on both inspiration and expiration (biphasic) that intensifies with deep breathing. Voice is near-normal with mild roughness. There is no drooling. Flexible laryngoscopy shows normal, symmetrically mobile vocal cords with a normal glottis, but the view below the cords suggests narrowing of the subglottis. The oropharynx is normal, the neck is supple without masses or thyromegaly, and there is no stridor-associated retraction at rest. Lungs are clear bilaterally without wheeze on auscultation away from the neck. Nasal mucosa is normal with no crusting or septal lesions. No saddle-nose deformity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Biphasic stridor with normal vocal cord mobility in a patient with recent prolonged intubation localizes the obstruction to which site?
options: Supraglottis (above the vocal cords)
Glottis (vocal cord level)
Subglottis/upper trachea (a fixed lesion at or below the cricoid)
Lower trachea and bronchi
stages.explanation: Biphasic stridor with normally mobile cords indicates a fixed, narrowed segment at or just below the glottis, i.e., the subglottis or upper trachea; her prolonged intubation is a classic cause of acquired subglottic stenosis. Supraglottic lesions typically produce inspiratory stridor and often a muffled 'hot-potato' voice. Glottic problems usually cause hoarseness with inspiratory stridor and are excluded here by normal cord mobility. Lower tracheal or bronchial obstruction tends to cause expiratory wheeze/stridor and often a monophonic wheeze, not fixed biphasic stridor. The fixed extrathoracic-to-subglottic location explains why the noise is present in both phases of respiration.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond flexible laryngoscopy, what is the most informative next step to characterize the airway lesion?
options: Pulmonary function testing with a flow-volume loop and CT of the neck/chest, with operative endoscopy for definitive grading
Empiric escalation of asthma inhalers and reassessment in 3 months
Methacholine challenge testing
Sleep study (polysomnography)
stages.explanation: A flow-volume loop showing flattening of both inspiratory and expiratory limbs supports a fixed central airway obstruction, while CT imaging and especially direct operative endoscopy (microlaryngoscopy/bronchoscopy) define the length, severity, and grade (e.g., Cotton-Myer) of stenosis to guide treatment. Escalating asthma therapy has already failed and delays diagnosis of a mechanical lesion. A methacholine challenge tests for bronchial hyperreactivity (asthma), which this fixed lesion is not, and could be hazardous. A sleep study evaluates obstructive sleep apnea, unrelated to daytime fixed stridor. Misdiagnosis as asthma is the classic pitfall in subglottic stenosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Flow-volume loop: flattening of both inspiratory and expiratory limbs (fixed central airway obstruction).
CT neck/chest: circumferential subglottic narrowing ~1.5 cm below the cords, ~50-60% luminal compromise, ~2 cm in length.
c-ANCA/PR3: negative. p-ANCA/MPO: negative.
Urinalysis: no hematuria, no red cell casts. ESR 12 mm/hr, CRP 3 mg/L (normal).
Chest CT: no nodules or cavitary lesions.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ANCA panel is negative, urinalysis is bland, and there are no sinonasal or pulmonary lesions. Combined with her intubation history, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) with subglottic involvement
Post-intubation (acquired) subglottic stenosis
Relapsing polychondritis
Laryngeal amyloidosis
stages.explanation: Her 11-day intubation, focal circumferential subglottic narrowing, negative ANCA serology, bland urinalysis, normal inflammatory markers, and absence of sinonasal/pulmonary disease point to post-intubation acquired subglottic stenosis, a common cause from mucosal ischemic injury under the endotracheal cuff. GPA can cause subglottic stenosis but is supported by positive PR3-ANCA, sinonasal disease, pulmonary nodules/cavities, or glomerulonephritis, all absent here. Relapsing polychondritis features recurrent auricular and nasal cartilage inflammation and saddle-nose deformity, not seen here. Laryngeal amyloidosis usually presents with submucosal deposits and a different endoscopic appearance and is rare. Idiopathic subglottic stenosis remains on the differential but the clear intubation history makes acquired stenosis most likely.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For a symptomatic, short-segment (~2 cm) subglottic stenosis, which initial treatment is most appropriate?
options: Permanent tracheostomy as first-line therapy
Long-term systemic corticosteroids alone
Endoscopic intervention (e.g., balloon dilation with or without radial CO2 laser incisions and steroid injection)
Observation with repeat imaging in 1 year
stages.explanation: For short-segment, low-grade symptomatic subglottic stenosis, endoscopic management, balloon dilation often combined with radial laser incisions and adjunctive steroid injection, is the preferred initial, airway-preserving approach; open laryngotracheal resection is reserved for recurrent or longer/higher-grade lesions. Up-front permanent tracheostomy bypasses the obstruction but is reserved for failed reconstruction or critical airways, not first-line. Systemic steroids alone do not durably reverse mature fibrotic stenosis and carry side effects. Observation for a year is inappropriate for a patient with biphasic stridor and exertional dyspnea, which signals significant compromise. Patients should be counseled that endoscopic dilation often requires repeat procedures.

cases.diagnosis: Post-intubation acquired subglottic stenosis (idiopathic and GPA excluded)
pearls: Biphasic stridor with mobile vocal cords localizes a fixed obstruction to the subglottis/upper trachea.
A flow-volume loop flattened in both limbs signals fixed central airway obstruction; do not mistake it for asthma.
Always screen for GPA (PR3-ANCA, urinalysis, sinonasal/pulmonary disease) when evaluating subglottic stenosis.
Short-segment stenosis is first managed endoscopically (balloon dilation, laser, steroid injection); resection is for refractory disease.

cases.title: Ventilated Patient with Sudden Respiratory Distress
cases.chiefComplaint: The bedside nurse calls because the patient suddenly became agitated, hypoxic, and is in respiratory distress through his tracheostomy.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is in the ICU recovering from a prolonged hospitalization for COPD exacerbation and aspiration pneumonia. He underwent a surgical tracheostomy 9 days ago and was weaned to a tracheostomy collar this morning. Over the past hour the nurse noted thick, dried secretions at the stoma and progressively labored breathing; the patient is now acutely agitated, diaphoretic, and clutching his throat. He has not had a fever and his last suctioning was several hours ago because the inner cannula has not been routinely cleaned on this shift. His PMH includes COPD, type 2 diabetes, and prior tobacco use (40 pack-years, quit 5 years ago). Medications include scheduled bronchodilators, inhaled corticosteroid, insulin, and DVT prophylaxis. He has no known drug allergies. He lives with his wife and was independent before admission. There is no recent bleeding from the stoma and no chest trauma. The tracheostomy is a standard cuffed tube with a disposable inner cannula. The respiratory therapist is en route.
cases.exam: The patient is in obvious respiratory distress with suprasternal and intercostal retractions and accessory muscle use. He is agitated and unable to speak. The tracheostomy tube appears in place at the stoma, but there is crusted, tenacious secretion visible at the cannula opening. Auscultation reveals markedly diminished air movement through the tracheostomy with no audible air at the mouth or nose. There is no bleeding, no peristomal swelling, and no subcutaneous emphysema. Lungs have poor air entry bilaterally without focal consolidation appreciable through the distress. Capillary refill is 3 seconds; he is centrally cyanotic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Following the emergency tracheostomy/laryngectomy algorithm, what is the correct first set of actions in this distressed patient?
options: Immediately remove the entire tracheostomy tube and attempt orotracheal intubation
Call for help, apply high-flow oxygen to BOTH the face and the stoma, and assess tube patency
Inject saline down the tube and clamp it to loosen secretions
Start chest compressions
stages.explanation: Per the emergency tracheostomy algorithm, you call for help, deliver oxygen to both the face and the stoma (in case the patient still has a patent upper airway), and then assess tube patency and breathing. Immediately yanking the whole tube and attempting oral intubation skips the simpler, faster patency-restoring maneuvers and may not be possible in some patients. Injecting saline and clamping the tube is not a recognized emergency step and clamping a tube in obstruction is harmful. Chest compressions are premature in a patient with a pulse and a circulation; the problem is ventilation/obstruction. Oxygenation by two routes buys time while you troubleshoot the tube.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Oxygen is applied to both sites. The next step in the obstructed-tracheostomy algorithm is to assess patency. What should you do first to address a suspected mucus plug?
options: Remove and inspect the inner cannula, then pass a suction catheter through the tube
Immediately deflate the cuff and remove the outer tube
Perform emergency cricothyroidotomy
Bag-mask ventilate over the mouth with the stoma left open
stages.explanation: The first patency step is to remove the inner cannula (a common, rapidly reversible site of plugging) and then attempt to pass a suction catheter through the tube; if the catheter passes and suctioning clears the plug, ventilation is restored. Removing the entire outer tube is the next step only if removing the inner cannula and suctioning fail to relieve obstruction, because full decannulation risks losing the airway in a fresh tract. Cricothyroidotomy is a last-resort rescue, not the first move when a simple plug is likely. Bagging over the mouth may fail because air escapes through the stoma and the upper airway may be unreliable; address the tube first.

stages.label: BEDSIDE ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Inner cannula removed: lumen is occluded by a thick, dried mucus plug.
Suction catheter does NOT pass beyond the tip of the outer tube initially.
After inner cannula removal and instillation/suctioning, a large plug is cleared and the suction catheter now passes freely.
SpO2 begins to rise to 94% with bag ventilation via the tube.
Capnography returns a normal waveform through the tracheostomy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After clearing the plug and confirming a normal capnography waveform through the tube, what does the returning waveform confirm and what is the next priority?
options: It confirms esophageal placement; remove the tube
It confirms patent tube ventilation; resume oxygenation, suction as needed, and prevent recurrence with humidification and inner-cannula care
It indicates a tracheoinnominate fistula; call vascular surgery
It is unreliable in tracheostomy patients and should be ignored
stages.explanation: A normal capnography waveform confirms that the tube is patent and ventilating the lungs; the immediate crisis is resolved, so you continue oxygenation, suction as needed, and prevent recurrence with adequate humidification, regular inner-cannula cleaning, and scheduled suctioning, the very lapses that caused this plug. Capnography reflects exhaled CO2 from the lungs, so it does not indicate esophageal placement; a flat/absent waveform would suggest that or a displaced tube. A tracheoinnominate fistula presents with bleeding, not an isolated mucus plug, and is not suggested by a good waveform. Capnography is in fact useful and reliable for confirming tracheostomy patency. Humidified air and bundle care prevent recurrent plugging.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If removing the inner cannula and attempting suction had NOT relieved the obstruction and the patient remained hypoxic, what is the correct next escalation?
options: Continue suctioning the same tube indefinitely
Remove the outer tracheostomy tube and ventilate via the stoma or the upper airway (oral intubation), considering a smaller tube or bougie-guided exchange
Increase the FiO2 and wait 30 minutes
Sedate and paralyze the patient before doing anything else
stages.explanation: If inner-cannula removal and suctioning fail, the algorithm escalates to removing the entire (outer) tracheostomy tube and ventilating by whichever route works, oral bag-mask/intubation if the upper airway is patent, or directly via the stoma, with options like a smaller tube or bougie/airway-exchange catheter to re-establish a controlled airway. Endless suctioning of an obstructed tube wastes time during hypoxia. Simply raising FiO2 and waiting does nothing if the tube is occluded and the patient cannot ventilate. Sedation and paralysis before securing the airway removes the patient's respiratory drive and is dangerous in an unresolved obstruction. The guiding principle is to restore a patent conduit for ventilation as quickly as possible.

cases.diagnosis: Acute tracheostomy tube obstruction from a mucus plug (tracheostomy emergency)
pearls: In tracheostomy distress: call for help and oxygenate BOTH face and stoma, then assess tube patency.
Remove and inspect the inner cannula first, then pass a suction catheter; escalate to removing the outer tube if unresolved.
A normal capnography waveform confirms a patent, ventilating tube; a flat waveform suggests obstruction or misplacement.
Prevent mucus plugging with humidification, scheduled inner-cannula care, and regular suctioning.

cases.title: Bleeding Around a Recent Tracheostomy
cases.chiefComplaint: The nurse calls because bright red blood appeared around the tracheostomy and inside the tube, then stopped.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man is in the ICU 3 weeks after a surgical tracheostomy placed during a prolonged ventilator course for severe necrotizing pneumonia and ARDS. He has been slowly weaning. Over the past day the team noted intermittent oozing at the stoma attributed to granulation tissue. This morning the nurse observed a brisk pulsation of bright red blood from around and within the tracheostomy tube lasting about 30 seconds before stopping spontaneously; roughly 15 mL was collected in the tubing. He is hemodynamically stable now and denies pain. His PMH includes hypertension, obesity, and obstructive sleep apnea. He has required relatively high cuff pressures, and his tracheostomy sits low. Medications include antihypertensives, DVT prophylaxis (enoxaparin), and a proton pump inhibitor. He has no known drug allergies, quit smoking years ago, and has no bleeding diathesis or family history of coagulopathy. The bedside team is concerned because this is the second small bleed in 12 hours. There is no melena or hematemesis. He is alert and following commands.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and in no acute distress at the moment. The tracheostomy is in place and low-lying; a small amount of fresh clot is present at the stoma and within the proximal tube. There is no active bleeding at this instant, no peristomal cellulitis, and no purulent discharge. There is mild granulation tissue at the stoma margin. Breath sounds are present bilaterally without new wheeze. There is no neck mass, no expanding hematoma, and no bruit appreciated. Cardiovascular exam is unremarkable. Abdomen is soft and non-tender. There is no hematemesis, no oral bleeding source, and no epistaxis. The patient denies lightheadedness.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A brisk, self-limited 'sentinel' bleed from a tracheostomy 3 weeks postoperatively, in a patient with a low-lying tube and high cuff pressures, should raise immediate concern for which diagnosis?
options: Bleeding from granulation tissue at the stoma
Tracheoinnominate artery fistula (TIF)
Posterior epistaxis tracking down the airway
Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage
stages.explanation: A pulsatile, self-limited 'sentinel' bleed days to weeks after tracheostomy, especially with a low tube and high cuff pressures eroding the anterior tracheal wall, is the hallmark warning of a tracheoinnominate artery fistula, which can precede catastrophic exsanguination. Granulation tissue causes minor oozing, not a brisk pulsatile sentinel bleed, and must never be assumed without excluding TIF. Posterior epistaxis would present with blood from the nose/nasopharynx, not pulsing from the stoma. Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage presents with diffuse infiltrates, hypoxemia, and blood throughout the airways, not a focal pulsatile peristomal bleed. Any tracheostomy bleed beyond the first 48 hours must be treated as a TIF until proven otherwise.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which combination of factors most increases this patient's risk for a tracheoinnominate fistula?
options: High tracheostomy placement and low cuff pressures
A low-lying tracheostomy (at or below the 3rd-4th tracheal ring), high cuff pressures, and prolonged cannulation
Recent extubation and use of a proton pump inhibitor
Obesity alone with normal tube position
stages.explanation: Classic TIF risk factors are a low tracheostomy stoma (at or below the 3rd-4th tracheal rings, near where the innominate artery crosses the trachea), excessive cuff or tube-tip pressure causing anterior wall ischemia/erosion, and prolonged cannulation, all present here. High placement with low cuff pressures is the opposite of the risk profile. PPI use and recent extubation are unrelated to arterial erosion. Obesity alone is not sufficient; it is the mechanical relationship between the tube/cuff and the artery that matters. Recognizing these factors helps anticipate and prevent the fistula, and heightens suspicion when a sentinel bleed occurs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While preparing the patient for the operating room, he suddenly has massive bright red hemorrhage gushing from the tracheostomy. What is the correct immediate bedside maneuver?
options: Deflate the cuff and remove the tracheostomy tube to look for the source
Hyperinflate the tracheostomy cuff; if bleeding continues, perform the Utley maneuver (orotracheal intubation, then digital compression of the artery against the sternum through the stoma)
Pack the stoma tightly with gauze and apply a pressure dressing
Administer tranexamic acid and observe
stages.explanation: Emergency control of TIF hemorrhage starts with overinflating the tracheostomy cuff to tamponade the bleed; if that fails, the Utley maneuver is performed, orotracheal intubation to secure the airway distal to the bleed (the cuff sealing the lower airway), then removal of the tracheostomy tube and digital compression of the innominate artery anteriorly against the posterior sternum/manubrium through the stoma, while rushing to the OR. Removing the tube to 'look' removes the tamponade and floods the airway with blood. External gauze packing cannot compress a deep arterial bleed and risks asphyxiation. Tranexamic acid and observation is grossly inadequate for arterial exsanguination. Definitive repair (ligation/sternotomy) follows in the OR.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The bleeding is temporarily controlled with cuff overinflation and digital compression. What is the definitive next step?
options: Transfuse and continue cuff tamponade indefinitely in the ICU
Emergent transport to the operating room for sternotomy with ligation/repair of the innominate artery (with vascular/cardiothoracic surgery)
Bedside cauterization of the stoma granulation tissue
CT angiography first, then reassess in several hours
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of a tracheoinnominate fistula is emergent operative repair, typically median sternotomy with ligation or repair of the innominate artery, performed with vascular/cardiothoracic surgery while temporizing measures hold; mortality is extremely high without immediate surgery. Indefinite cuff tamponade in the ICU is only a bridge and will eventually fail. Cauterizing 'granulation tissue' at the bedside dangerously mistakes the lethal source and wastes time. Obtaining CT angiography before intervening is acceptable only in a stable patient with a small sentinel bleed and a controlled airway; in active massive hemorrhage it must not delay the OR. Endovascular options exist at some centers but operative control remains standard.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: After successful management, the team reviews how to reduce TIF risk in future tracheostomy patients. Which practice is most effective?
options: Routinely place tracheostomies as low as possible in the trachea
Maintain appropriate cuff pressures (≈20-30 cmH2O), avoid excessively low stoma placement, and minimize tube-tip/cuff trauma
Keep cuff pressures maximally high to ensure a seal
Avoid all suctioning to prevent mucosal irritation
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on limiting anterior tracheal wall pressure necrosis: maintain cuff pressures in the safe range (about 20-30 cmH2O, below capillary perfusion pressure), avoid stomas placed too low near the innominate artery, and minimize tube angulation/tip trauma. Routinely placing tubes as low as possible increases proximity to the innominate artery and raises TIF risk. Maximal cuff pressures cause mucosal ischemia and are a primary driver of erosion. Avoiding all suctioning leads to plugging and infection without preventing arterial erosion. Proper tube selection, position, and cuff management are the cornerstones of prevention, along with prompt investigation of any sentinel bleed.

cases.diagnosis: Tracheoinnominate artery fistula presenting with a sentinel bleed
pearls: Any tracheostomy bleed after the first 48 hours is a tracheoinnominate fistula until proven otherwise; a sentinel bleed precedes exsanguination.
Risk factors: low stoma (3rd-4th ring or below), high cuff pressures, and prolonged cannulation.
Emergency control: overinflate the cuff, then the Utley maneuver (orotracheal intubation + digital compression of the artery against the sternum).
Definitive treatment is emergent sternotomy with innominate artery ligation/repair.

cases.title: Newborn with Color Changes That Improve When She Cries
cases.chiefComplaint: The NICU team is called for a term newborn with episodes of cyanosis that resolve when she cries.
cases.history: A term newborn girl, born at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal delivery, is noted in the first hours of life to have recurrent episodes of perioral cyanosis and respiratory distress that occur at rest and during feeding attempts but dramatically improve when she cries. Apgar scores were 8 and 9. There was no maternal infection, no meconium, and a reassuring prenatal course, though a third-trimester ultrasound suggested a possible cardiac anomaly. The infant struggles to coordinate breathing with breastfeeding and becomes dusky when the mouth is closed around the nipple. There is no fever, and birth weight is appropriate. Family history is unremarkable. The mother took prenatal vitamins and no other medications and denies alcohol or tobacco use. The nurse attempting routine nasal suctioning could not pass a catheter through either nostril. On crying, the infant pinks up immediately as she breathes through her mouth. There is no stridor and no drooling. The team is evaluating for associated anomalies given the prenatal cardiac concern.

vitals.SpO2: 88% at rest, rising to 97% when crying

cases.exam: The infant is in mild respiratory distress at rest with perioral cyanosis that resolves promptly with crying (mouth breathing). A 6-French catheter cannot be passed through either nostril into the nasopharynx. The oropharynx is patent. There is a soft systolic murmur. Examination notes possible iris colobomata bilaterally and small, low-set ears. The chest is clear with symmetric air entry once mouth breathing. Heart rate is regular. The abdomen is soft. There is no cleft lip or palate. External nares appear patent but no airflow is detected on the mirror test at the nostrils. No facial dysmorphism beyond the noted ear and eye findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cyclical cyanosis at rest that resolves with crying, plus inability to pass a catheter through either nostril, is the classic presentation of which condition?
options: Laryngomalacia
Bilateral choanal atresia
Pierre Robin sequence
Tracheoesophageal fistula
stages.explanation: Neonates are obligate nasal breathers, so bilateral choanal atresia (bony or membranous blockage of the posterior nasal apertures) produces cyanosis at rest that paradoxically improves with crying, when the infant breathes through the mouth, and failure to pass a catheter through either nostril is the classic bedside clue. Laryngomalacia causes inspiratory stridor that worsens with crying/feeding, not relief with crying, and a catheter passes freely. Pierre Robin sequence features micrognathia, glossoptosis, and cleft palate with airway obstruction, but the catheter passes. Tracheoesophageal fistula presents with choking, coughing, and inability to pass an orogastric tube into the stomach, not nasal obstruction. The cyclical pattern is pathognomonic for bilateral choanal atresia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate intervention to stabilize this newborn's airway?
options: Emergent surgical repair of the atresia in the delivery room
Place an oral airway (e.g., McGovern nipple or oral airway) to maintain mouth breathing
Initiate continuous positive nasal airway pressure (nasal CPAP)
Begin prostaglandin E1 infusion
stages.explanation: The immediate, life-saving step is to establish an oral airway so the obligate-nasal-breathing neonate can breathe through the mouth, classically a McGovern nipple (a modified nipple with the tip cut off, taped in place) or an oral airway, which stabilizes the infant until definitive surgery. Emergent delivery-room surgery is not how this is managed; the airway is first secured and surgery planned electively/semi-electively. Nasal CPAP is futile because the nasal passages are physically obstructed. Prostaglandin E1 is used for ductal-dependent cardiac lesions, not for choanal atresia airway obstruction; while a cardiac workup is warranted, it does not relieve the nasal obstruction. The oral airway directly addresses the obligate-nasal-breathing problem.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Fine-cut CT of the nasal cavity and skull base: bilateral bony posterior choanal atresia with a thickened, medially bowed vomer and narrowed posterior nasal apertures.
Echocardiogram: tetralogy of Fallot.
Ophthalmology: bilateral iris colobomata.
Hearing screen: referred (failed) bilaterally.
Growth/genitourinary exam: noted for follow-up; karyotype and CHD7 testing pending.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
stages.question: Given colobomata, a cardiac defect, choanal atresia, ear/hearing abnormalities, and the catheter findings, which syndrome must be evaluated?
options: VACTERL association
CHARGE syndrome
DiGeorge (22q11.2 deletion) syndrome alone
Treacher Collins syndrome
stages.explanation: CHARGE syndrome, Coloboma, Heart defects, Atresia of the choanae, Retardation of growth/development, Genitourinary anomalies, and Ear abnormalities/deafness, fits this infant with colobomata, tetralogy of Fallot, bilateral choanal atresia, and ear/hearing findings; it is often caused by CHD7 gene mutations. VACTERL involves vertebral, anal, cardiac, tracheoesophageal, renal, and limb anomalies but not choanal atresia or colobomata as defining features. Isolated DiGeorge syndrome features conotruncal cardiac defects, hypocalcemia, and immune deficiency but not the coloboma/choanal-atresia constellation, though it overlaps with conotruncal lesions. Treacher Collins is a mandibulofacial dysostosis without choanal atresia or colobomata as core features. Recognizing CHARGE prompts coordinated multidisciplinary evaluation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After airway stabilization and workup, what is the definitive treatment for bilateral choanal atresia?
options: Lifelong oral airway with no surgery
Transnasal endoscopic repair (choanal opening) with removal of the atretic plate and posterior vomer, with or without stenting
Tracheostomy as first-line definitive therapy
Repeated balloon dilation of the nares only
stages.explanation: Definitive management of bilateral choanal atresia is surgical repair, most commonly transnasal endoscopic resection of the atretic (often bony) plate and posterior vomer to create patent choanae, sometimes with temporary stenting and later dilation for restenosis. A permanent oral airway with no surgery is not a durable solution for an obligate nasal breather and impairs feeding. Tracheostomy is reserved for complex cases or when repair must be delayed, not first-line definitive therapy. Balloon dilation of the nares alone does not address the posterior atretic plate that causes the obstruction. Postoperative restenosis is common, so close follow-up and possible revision are anticipated, and associated CHARGE anomalies are managed concurrently.

cases.diagnosis: Bilateral choanal atresia (bony), in the setting of CHARGE syndrome
pearls: Neonates are obligate nasal breathers: bilateral choanal atresia causes cyanosis at rest that improves with crying.
Inability to pass a catheter through either nostril is the classic bedside sign; confirm with fine-cut CT.
Immediate management is an oral airway (e.g., McGovern nipple); definitive treatment is transnasal endoscopic repair.
Always evaluate for CHARGE syndrome (Coloboma, Heart, Atresia choanae, Retardation, GU, Ear) and CHD7.

cases.title: Man with Nasal Whistling and Persistent Crusting
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports a whistling sound when he breathes through his nose and recurrent nasal crusting and bleeding.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents to the ENT clinic with several months of a whistling sound during nasal breathing, recurrent crusting inside the nose, and intermittent epistaxis. He notes the symptoms have slowly worsened and that his nose sometimes feels obstructed and dry. He underwent a septoplasty for a deviated septum about 2 years ago and recalls a period of nasal packing afterward. He also admits, when asked privately, to intranasal cocaine use 'on and off' over the past several years, most recently a few months ago. He denies fever, weight loss, night sweats, sinus pain, joint pains, hematuria, or cough. His PMH is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no regular medications, occasionally uses oxymetazoline spray, and reports no allergies. He drinks socially and works in construction with dust exposure. Family history is noncontributory. He denies prior nasal trauma other than surgery and denies using nasal steroid sprays long-term. He is concerned the whistling is embarrassing and the crusting causes daily bleeding.
cases.exam: Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a perforation of the cartilaginous (anterior) nasal septum, roughly 1 cm in diameter, with heaped crusting along the margins and a small amount of fresh blood. The edges show granulation tissue but no overt mass. There is no saddle-nose deformity, no external nasal collapse, and no septal hematoma. The remainder of the nasal mucosa is dry with scattered crusts. There is no sinus tenderness. The oral cavity and oropharynx are normal; the palate is intact. Neck exam shows no lymphadenopathy. There are no skin lesions, no oral ulcers, and no signs of systemic vasculitis on general exam. Lungs are clear.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Nasal whistling with anterior septal perforation and crusting in this patient is most directly explained by which finding?
options: Turbulent airflow through a hole in the nasal septum
Nasal polyposis obstructing the airway
Allergic rhinitis with mucosal edema
Nasal valve collapse from prior rhinoplasty
stages.explanation: A septal perforation creates turbulent airflow as air passes through the hole, producing the characteristic whistling sound, along with crusting and recurrent bleeding at the perforation margins. Nasal polyposis causes obstruction, hyposmia, and a boggy appearance but not a whistling perforation. Allergic rhinitis produces clear rhinorrhea, sneezing, and pale boggy turbinates, not a hole in the septum. Nasal valve collapse causes dynamic inspiratory obstruction but not a septal perforation with whistling. Recognizing the perforation as the source directs the workup toward its many possible causes, both local (surgery, trauma) and systemic.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given prior septoplasty AND cocaine use, which workup is most appropriate to identify the cause and exclude dangerous mimics of septal perforation?
options: No workup is needed; attribute it to prior surgery and observe
A targeted evaluation including ANCA serologies, syphilis (RPR/treponemal) and other infectious testing, and biopsy of the perforation margin if a mass, atypia, or non-healing lesion is suspected
Empiric long-term oral antibiotics without testing
Immediate surgical closure without identifying the cause
stages.explanation: Septal perforation has a broad differential, prior surgery, cocaine/intranasal drug use, vasculitis (especially GPA), infections (syphilis, TB), malignancy, and overuse of vasoconstrictor sprays, so a targeted workup is warranted: ANCA serologies (PR3/MPO) for GPA, infectious testing including RPR/treponemal for syphilis, and biopsy of the margin when malignancy, granulomatous disease, or a non-healing/atypical lesion is suspected. Attributing it to surgery without testing risks missing GPA or malignancy. Empiric antibiotics treat nothing specific and delay diagnosis. Surgical closure before identifying the cause is inappropriate, especially with active cocaine use or untreated systemic disease, which would doom the repair. Note that cocaine-induced midline lesions can produce a positive atypical ANCA pattern, complicating interpretation.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
c-ANCA/PR3: negative. p-ANCA/MPO: negative (atypical perinuclear pattern not detected).
RPR: nonreactive. Treponemal antibody: negative.
ESR 14 mm/hr, CRP 4 mg/L (normal).
CBC and renal function normal; urinalysis bland.
Biopsy of perforation margin: inflammation and necrosis without granulomas, vasculitis, or malignancy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With negative vasculitis and infectious workup, a bland urinalysis, and a biopsy lacking granulomas or malignancy, what is the most likely cause of this perforation?
options: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Local mucosal injury from cocaine use and/or prior septal surgery
Nasal squamous cell carcinoma
Tertiary syphilis
stages.explanation: After excluding vasculitis, infection, and malignancy, the perforation is best attributed to local mucosal injury, here the combination of cocaine's intense vasoconstriction/ischemia and prior septoplasty (especially if there was a mucosal tear or postoperative hematoma/infection). GPA is unlikely with negative ANCA, normal inflammatory markers, bland urinalysis, and no granulomas on biopsy. Squamous cell carcinoma is excluded by a benign biopsy without atypia. Tertiary syphilis is excluded by negative RPR and treponemal testing, and classically causes a bony (posterior) perforation or saddle nose rather than this anterior cartilaginous defect. Identifying a local cause guides management and emphasizes cessation of the offending exposure.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for this symptomatic septal perforation?
options: Immediate surgical flap closure regardless of ongoing cocaine use

Counsel strict cessation of cocaine and nasal vasoconstrictors, and start conservative care (saline irrigation, emollients/humidification, treat crusting); consider a septal button or surgical repair once the mucosa heals and use has stopped
Cauterize the entire perforation margin to seal it
Long-term oral corticosteroids
stages.explanation: First-line management is conservative and addresses the cause: counsel strict cessation of cocaine and overused vasoconstrictor sprays, and provide saline irrigation, emollients/ointments, and humidification to control crusting and bleeding; a silicone septal button can relieve whistling and crusting, and surgical closure (mucosal flaps) is considered only after the mucosa has healed and exposure has stopped, since active cocaine use causes high failure rates. Immediate surgical closure despite ongoing cocaine use is doomed to fail. Cauterizing the margins can enlarge the perforation and worsen bleeding. Long-term systemic steroids are not indicated for a local, non-vasculitic perforation. Conservative care plus cause-removal sets the stage for any later definitive repair.

cases.diagnosis: Anterior nasal septal perforation due to local injury (cocaine use and prior septoplasty), with GPA, syphilis, and malignancy excluded
pearls: Nasal whistling with crusting and epistaxis suggests a septal perforation creating turbulent airflow.
Workup must exclude GPA (ANCA), infection (syphilis/TB), malignancy (biopsy), and drug/spray-induced injury.
Cocaine-induced midline destruction can mimic GPA and may produce atypical ANCA patterns, complicating diagnosis.
Manage conservatively first (saline, emollients, septal button) and stop the offending exposure before any surgical closure.

cases.title: Facial Sweating While Eating After Prior Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports flushing and sweating over her cheek whenever she eats, starting a few months after surgery.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman returns to the ENT clinic reporting that, for the past 2 months, she develops flushing, warmth, and beads of sweat over her left cheek and preauricular area whenever she eats, particularly with tart or flavorful foods. She underwent a left superficial parotidectomy 8 months ago for a benign pleomorphic adenoma; her recovery was otherwise uneventful and her facial nerve function is intact. The gustatory sweating is socially bothersome and predictable, occurring within seconds of eating and resolving when she finishes. She denies pain, swelling, fever, or discharge from the incision. She has no dry mouth, no eye symptoms, and no weight loss. Her PMH includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has no diabetes. She has no known drug allergies, does not smoke, and drinks wine occasionally. Family history is unremarkable. She specifically notes the affected skin overlies her surgical scar and that the reaction is reproducible with every meal. She has not tried any treatment and wants to know why this is happening.
cases.exam: There is a well-healed left preauricular and cervical parotidectomy scar without erythema, tenderness, or fluctuance. The overlying cheek skin appears normal at rest. Facial nerve function is intact in all branches (symmetric forehead, eye closure, smile). There is no parotid mass or recurrence palpable, and no cervical lymphadenopathy. The contralateral face is normal. There is no trismus and the oral cavity is moist with normal salivary flow from Stensen's duct. When a slice of lemon is given during the visit, the patient develops visible flushing and beads of sweat over the scar region within seconds, confirming the reproducible gustatory response. Eyes are not affected.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Gustatory sweating and flushing over the cheek developing months after a parotidectomy is characteristic of which condition?
options: Frey syndrome (auriculotemporal syndrome)
Salivary fistula
Recurrent pleomorphic adenoma
First-bite syndrome
stages.explanation: Frey syndrome (auriculotemporal syndrome) is gustatory sweating and flushing over the cheek/preauricular skin that appears months after parotid surgery, caused by aberrant regeneration of severed parasympathetic (secretomotor) fibers of the auriculotemporal nerve onto the sweat glands and cutaneous blood vessels of the overlying skin. A salivary fistula produces leakage of saliva from the wound, not sweating. Recurrent pleomorphic adenoma presents as a new mass, not gustatory sweating, and her exam shows no recurrence. First-bite syndrome causes intense pain in the parotid region with the first bite of each meal that eases with subsequent bites, not sweating. The reproducible sweat response to a sialogogue confirms Frey syndrome.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which mechanism best explains Frey syndrome?
options: Sympathetic overactivity causing vasoconstriction of facial vessels
Aberrant reinnervation: postganglionic parasympathetic fibers (from the auriculotemporal nerve) misdirect to sweat glands and skin vessels
Compression of the facial nerve by scar tissue
Autoimmune destruction of the parotid gland
stages.explanation: Frey syndrome results from aberrant regeneration: after parotidectomy severs the postganglionic parasympathetic secretomotor fibers (carried via the auriculotemporal nerve to the parotid), these regrowing cholinergic fibers misconnect to the now-exposed cholinergic sweat glands and cutaneous vessels of the overlying skin, so a salivary stimulus triggers sweating and flushing instead of salivation. Sympathetic overactivity causing vasoconstriction is the opposite of the cholinergic, vasodilatory response seen here. Facial nerve compression by scar would cause weakness, not gustatory sweating, and her nerve is intact. Autoimmune parotid destruction describes Sjogren-type pathology with dry mouth/eyes, not this localized cutaneous phenomenon. Because the fibers are cholinergic, anticholinergic and botulinum toxin therapies are effective.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: Which simple in-office test can objectively confirm and map the area of gustatory sweating?
options: Schirmer test
Minor starch-iodine test (iodine applied to skin, then starch; sweating turns the area blue-black during a gustatory stimulus)
Sialography
Tensilon (edrophonium) test
stages.explanation: The Minor starch-iodine test confirms and maps Frey syndrome: iodine is painted on the cheek and allowed to dry, starch powder is dusted over it, and a gustatory stimulus is given; areas of sweating turn blue-black, delineating the affected skin and guiding therapy such as botulinum toxin injection. The Schirmer test measures tear production for dry-eye/lacrimal evaluation, not sweating. Sialography images the salivary ductal system and is irrelevant to mapping cutaneous sweating. The Tensilon/edrophonium test evaluates myasthenia gravis. The starch-iodine test is inexpensive, visual, and directly demonstrates the cholinergic gustatory sweating pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient finds the gustatory sweating socially distressing. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Reassurance only, as no treatments exist
Topical antiperspirant/anticholinergic (e.g., aluminum chloride or topical glycopyrrolate) and/or intradermal botulinum toxin injection to the affected skin
Revision parotidectomy
Systemic high-dose corticosteroids
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for bothersome Frey syndrome is medical: topical agents such as aluminum chloride antiperspirant or topical anticholinergics (e.g., glycopyrrolate), and especially intradermal botulinum toxin injections, which block the aberrant cholinergic transmission and provide durable relief mapped by the starch-iodine test. Reassurance alone is reasonable for mild cases but inadequate for a patient who is distressed and seeking treatment. Revision surgery is invasive, risks the facial nerve, and is not first-line for this functional symptom. Systemic high-dose steroids are not indicated and carry unjustified risk. Botulinum toxin is the most effective and commonly used option, with repeat injections as effects wane; preventive interposition grafts at the original surgery can reduce incidence.

cases.diagnosis: Frey syndrome (auriculotemporal/gustatory sweating) following superficial parotidectomy
pearls: Frey syndrome is gustatory sweating/flushing over the cheek months after parotid surgery due to aberrant parasympathetic reinnervation of sweat glands.
The culprit nerve is the auriculotemporal nerve carrying postganglionic parasympathetic fibers.
Confirm and map it with the Minor starch-iodine test.
First-line treatment is intradermal botulinum toxin (and topical anticholinergics/antiperspirants); surgery is rarely needed.

cases.title: Itchy, Blocked Ear After Weeks of Ear Drops
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports an intensely itchy, blocked ear with debris despite using antibiotic ear drops for several weeks.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents to the clinic with 3 weeks of an intensely itchy, full-feeling right ear that has not improved, and in fact worsened, despite using antibiotic-steroid ear drops prescribed for presumed swimmer's ear. He notes dull discomfort, decreased hearing on that side, and occasional thin discharge. He is an avid swimmer and frequently uses cotton swabs. He denies fever, severe pain, vertigo, or facial weakness. He recently finished the prolonged course of topical antibiotic drops without relief. His PMH is unremarkable; he is not diabetic and not immunocompromised. He takes no systemic medications and has no allergies. He works as a lifeguard, swims daily, and lives alone. There is no family history of ear disease. He reports the itching is the most prominent and distressing symptom and that cleaning the ear with swabs only briefly helps before the fullness returns. He has not had ear surgery and uses no hearing aids. He has noticed small flecks of dark material on his cotton swabs.
cases.exam: Otoscopy of the right ear reveals a canal filled with damp, cottony white debris interspersed with black/dark fungal elements resembling 'wet newspaper,' classic for fungal colonization. The canal skin is mildly erythematous and edematous but not severely tender, and the discharge is non-purulent. The tympanic membrane, where visible after gentle cleaning, appears intact without perforation or middle-ear effusion. There is no mastoid tenderness, no postauricular swelling, and no granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction. Pneumatic otoscopy shows a mobile, intact drum. The left ear is normal. Cranial nerves, including the facial nerve, are intact. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy and no severe pain on tragal pressure.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An intensely itchy ear with cottony white debris and black fungal elements after prolonged antibiotic drops is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Bacterial otitis externa (Pseudomonas)
Otomycosis (fungal otitis externa)
Malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa
Cholesteatoma
stages.explanation: Intense itching, a sensation of fullness, and cottony debris with visible black fungal hyphae after a course of antibiotic drops point to otomycosis, fungal otitis externa, most often caused by Aspergillus (classically Aspergillus niger giving the black 'pepper' appearance) or Candida; prolonged topical antibiotics promote fungal overgrowth by suppressing normal flora. Bacterial Pseudomonas otitis externa typically causes severe pain and tenderness with purulent discharge rather than itching and fungal elements, and would usually respond to the antibiotic drops. Malignant otitis externa occurs in elderly diabetics/immunocompromised patients with severe pain and granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, absent here. Cholesteatoma is a keratin sac in the middle ear/attic with chronic drainage, not canal fungal debris.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which factor most contributed to this patient developing otomycosis?
options: Prolonged use of topical antibiotic ear drops together with frequent water exposure and a moist canal
A history of childhood ear infections
A perforated tympanic membrane
Use of a hearing aid
stages.explanation: Otomycosis is favored by a warm, moist canal and by prolonged topical antibiotic (and steroid) drops, which disrupt the normal protective flora and allow fungal overgrowth; this swimmer/lifeguard with daily water exposure and weeks of antibiotic drops has exactly this profile. A history of childhood ear infections is not a major risk factor for adult otomycosis. A perforated tympanic membrane is a consideration that affects which treatments are safe but is not the inciting risk factor here, and his drum is intact. Hearing aid use can promote canal occlusion and moisture but is absent in this patient. Recognizing the antibiotic/moisture combination guides both treatment and prevention counseling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of this patient's otomycosis?
options: Continue the same antibiotic-steroid drops for another 2 weeks
Thorough aural toilet (microsuction/debridement) to remove fungal debris, then a topical antifungal (e.g., clotrimazole) and keep the ear dry
Start oral antifungal therapy first-line for all cases
Prescribe systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of otomycosis treatment is meticulous cleaning, microsuction or debridement to physically remove the fungal mass, followed by a topical antifungal such as clotrimazole (or acidifying/drying agents like acetic acid in select cases), plus strict dry-ear precautions. Continuing the antibiotic-steroid drops perpetuates the problem by further suppressing normal flora. Oral (systemic) antifungals are reserved for refractory, extensive, or invasive disease, not routine first-line therapy. Systemic antibiotics target bacteria and would worsen fungal overgrowth. Caution is needed if the tympanic membrane is perforated, since some preparations are ototoxic; here the drum is intact. Debridement plus a topical antifungal and dryness is curative for most patients.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point will best help this lifeguard prevent recurrence?
options: Use prophylactic antibiotic drops before every swim
Keep the ears dry (use earplugs/drying after swimming), avoid cotton-swab trauma, and avoid unnecessary topical antibiotics
Irrigate the ears daily with tap water
Apply petroleum jelly deep in the canal weekly
stages.explanation: Prevention focuses on removing the moist, flora-disrupting environment: keep ears dry with earplugs and post-swim drying (e.g., a towel, hair dryer on low, or acidifying/drying drops), avoid cotton-swab trauma that damages protective cerumen and skin, and avoid unnecessary topical antibiotics that predispose to fungal overgrowth. Prophylactic antibiotic drops before swimming would actually promote recurrent otomycosis by suppressing normal flora. Daily tap-water irrigation reintroduces moisture and may carry contaminants, worsening the problem. Packing petroleum jelly deep in the canal traps moisture and debris and is not recommended. Dryness, gentle canal hygiene, and antibiotic stewardship are the key preventive measures for this water-exposed patient.

cases.diagnosis: Otomycosis (fungal otitis externa), most commonly Aspergillus
pearls: Intense itching plus cottony/black debris ('wet newspaper') after prolonged antibiotic drops suggests otomycosis (often Aspergillus niger).
Prolonged topical antibiotics and a moist canal predispose to fungal overgrowth.
Treat with thorough debridement/microsuction plus a topical antifungal (clotrimazole) and dry-ear precautions.
Check for tympanic membrane perforation before using potentially ototoxic preparations; reserve oral antifungals for refractory disease.

cases.title: One-Sided Ringing and Trouble on the Phone
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradual hearing loss and ringing in the right ear over the past year.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 12-month history of progressive right-sided hearing loss and a continuous high-pitched ring in the same ear. She notes she can no longer use the phone with her right ear because voices sound 'garbled and distant,' even when loud enough. Over the last few months she has had occasional unsteadiness, described as a vague swaying when turning quickly, but no true spinning vertigo and no falls. She denies otalgia, otorrhea, facial weakness, facial numbness, headache, or visual changes. PMH is notable only for hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No prior ear surgery, noise exposure, or ototoxic drug use. Medications: levothyroxine, a daily multivitamin. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional wine, works as an accountant, no recreational drug use. Family history: mother with age-related hearing loss; no family history of neurofibromatosis, early-onset deafness, or tumors. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no dysphagia, hoarseness, or limb weakness.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing woman in no distress. Otoscopy: both external canals patent; tympanic membranes pearly, mobile, with normal landmarks and no effusion or perforation. Weber tuning fork (512 Hz) lateralizes to the LEFT ear; Rinne is positive (air > bone) bilaterally, consistent with a right sensorineural pattern. Facial sensation is intact to light touch in all three trigeminal divisions; corneal reflex present bilaterally. Facial nerve function is symmetric (House-Brackmann I). No nystagmus at rest or on gaze. Romberg negative; gait steady. Remaining cranial nerves intact; no cerebellar dysmetria on finger-to-nose. Neck supple, no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test best characterizes this patient's hearing complaint and guides further workup?
options: Tympanometry alone
Pure-tone and speech audiometry (formal audiogram)
Otoacoustic emissions screening only
Electronystagmography (ENG)
stages.explanation: A formal audiogram with pure-tone thresholds and speech testing is the essential first step for any asymmetric hearing loss; it confirms the sensorineural pattern suggested by tuning forks, quantifies asymmetry, and crucially measures word recognition. Disproportionately poor speech discrimination relative to pure-tone loss is a classic retrocochlear red flag. Tympanometry assesses middle-ear/TM mobility and would be normal here, missing the sensorineural problem. Otoacoustic emissions screen cochlear outer hair cells but do not localize a retrocochlear lesion or quantify speech understanding. ENG evaluates vestibular function and may be abnormal but is not the initial diagnostic test for asymmetric SNHL and does not characterize hearing. The audiogram drives the decision to image.

stages.label: AUDIOGRAM
stages.content:
Right ear: moderate-to-severe high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss; pure-tone average (PTA) 52 dB
Left ear: normal hearing; PTA 12 dB
Word recognition score (WRS): right 44% (poor), left 96%
Tympanometry: type A bilaterally (normal middle-ear pressure and compliance)
Acoustic reflexes: absent on the right, present on the left

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given asymmetric SNHL with disproportionately poor right word recognition, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: MRI of the internal auditory canals with gadolinium
Non-contrast CT of the temporal bones
Reassurance and repeat audiogram in 1 year
Begin a high-dose oral prednisone taper
stages.explanation: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI focused on the internal auditory canals (IACs) and cerebellopontine angle is the gold standard for asymmetric SNHL with poor speech discrimination, the classic presentation of a retrocochlear lesion such as vestibular schwannoma. It detects even small intracanalicular tumors. CT temporal bones is excellent for bone (cholesteatoma, otosclerosis, fracture) but poorly visualizes the membranous IAC contents and small soft-tissue tumors. Reassurance/repeat audiogram is unsafe when retrocochlear red flags are present and risks missing a growing tumor. A prednisone taper treats sudden idiopathic SNHL, not a chronic progressive asymmetric loss, and would delay diagnosis.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI IAC with gadolinium: 14 mm enhancing mass centered at the right porus acusticus, extending into the IAC with a small cerebellopontine-angle component ('ice-cream-cone' appearance)
No brainstem compression; fourth ventricle and cerebellum normal
No additional lesions; no bilateral masses
Facial nerve and remaining IAC structures preserved

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: From which structure do these tumors most commonly arise, explaining the imaging and symptoms?
options: Cochlear (auditory) division of CN VIII
Facial nerve (CN VII)
Schwann cells of the vestibular division of CN VIII
Trigeminal nerve (CN V) ganglion
stages.explanation: Vestibular schwannomas arise from Schwann cells of the vestibular division of the eighth nerve, typically near the Scarpa ganglion at the porus acusticus, hence the location seen on MRI. Despite the vestibular origin, hearing loss and tinnitus dominate clinically because tumor growth compresses the adjacent cochlear nerve and its blood supply within the confined IAC; frank vertigo is uncommon due to gradual central compensation. The cochlear division is affected secondarily, not the origin. Facial-nerve schwannomas exist but are rare and usually present with facial weakness/twitching. Trigeminal schwannomas arise more medially and cause facial numbness, not isolated SNHL.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 50-year-old with a 14 mm tumor, no brainstem compression, and serviceable contralateral hearing, which management options are most appropriate to discuss?
options: Immediate radical mastoidectomy
Observation with serial MRI, stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgical resection
Whole-brain radiation therapy
Empiric chemotherapy with bevacizumab
stages.explanation: Management of vestibular schwannoma is individualized among three accepted options: active surveillance with serial MRI (appropriate for small/slow-growing tumors, especially in older or asymptomatic patients), stereotactic radiosurgery (good local control for tumors generally up to ~2.5-3 cm without significant brainstem compression), and microsurgical resection (favored for larger tumors, brainstem compression, or younger patients). A 14 mm tumor without brainstem compression fits all three discussions. Radical mastoidectomy treats chronic otitis/cholesteatoma, not IAC tumors. Whole-brain radiation is for diffuse metastatic disease, not a benign focal schwannoma. Bevacizumab is reserved for NF2-related tumors, not sporadic unilateral schwannomas.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Because this is a unilateral sporadic tumor in a 50-year-old with no family history, which statement about further evaluation is most accurate?
options: She requires genetic testing and screening for neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) before any treatment
Bilateral vestibular schwannomas would have suggested NF2, but her single unilateral tumor and negative history make sporadic disease far more likely
She needs immediate screening of all first-degree relatives with MRI
A unilateral schwannoma at this age is pathognomonic for NF2
stages.explanation: BILATERAL vestibular schwannomas are the hallmark of neurofibromatosis type 2; a single unilateral tumor in a 50-year-old with no family history and no other findings (cafe-au-lait macules, other CNS tumors, lens opacities) overwhelmingly represents sporadic disease, the most common scenario. Routine genetic testing/NF2 screening is not mandated before treating a typical sporadic unilateral tumor, especially at this age (NF2 usually presents younger). Screening all first-degree relatives is not indicated without features suggesting a hereditary syndrome. A unilateral schwannoma is not pathognomonic for NF2; it is in fact the typical sporadic presentation, whereas bilateral tumors define NF2.

pearls: Asymmetric SNHL with word recognition disproportionately worse than pure-tone thresholds is a retrocochlear red flag mandating MRI IAC with gadolinium.
Vestibular schwannomas arise from the vestibular division of CN VIII but present with hearing loss and tinnitus because of cochlear nerve compression.
Absent acoustic reflexes and poor speech discrimination on the affected side support a retrocochlear localization.
Three management pathways exist: serial MRI surveillance, stereotactic radiosurgery, and microsurgery, chosen by tumor size, growth, hearing, and patient age.

cases.title: Worsening Hearing That Got Worse During Pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradually worsening hearing in both ears, noticeably worse since becoming pregnant.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman, 28 weeks pregnant, reports several years of slowly progressive hearing loss, left worse than right, that accelerated during this pregnancy. She finds she hears better in noisy restaurants than her companions do, a phenomenon she finds odd. She has a soft, low-pitched buzzing tinnitus but denies vertigo, otorrhea, otalgia, or aural fullness. There is no history of recurrent ear infections, head trauma, noise exposure, or ototoxic medications. She notes she tends to speak softly because her own voice sounds loud to her. PMH: otherwise healthy; uncomplicated pregnancy. Medications: prenatal vitamins with iron, folic acid. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker, no alcohol during pregnancy, works as a teacher. Family history is striking: her mother and maternal aunt both needed 'an ear operation' for hearing loss in their 30s-40s. Review of systems negative for fevers, weight change, neurologic symptoms, or visual disturbance. No blue sclerae or bone fragility.
cases.exam: Well-appearing pregnant woman in no distress. Otoscopy: external canals clear; both tympanic membranes are intact, mobile, and translucent without perforation, retraction, or effusion. A faint reddish hue is not appreciated behind either drum. Weber (512 Hz) lateralizes to the LEFT (worse-hearing) ear; Rinne is NEGATIVE bilaterally (bone > air), left more pronounced than right, indicating a conductive pattern. Facial nerve function symmetric (House-Brackmann I). No spontaneous nystagmus. Pneumatic otoscopy shows normal drum mobility. No postauricular scars or masses. Remainder of head and neck exam unremarkable; no cervical lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The tuning fork findings (Weber to the worse ear, bilateral Rinne bone > air) indicate which type of hearing loss?
options: Sensorineural hearing loss
Conductive hearing loss
Mixed loss with predominant sensorineural component
Functional (nonorganic) hearing loss
stages.explanation: Weber lateralizing to the worse-hearing ear plus a Rinne in which bone conduction exceeds air conduction defines a conductive hearing loss. In conductive loss the affected ear is shielded from ambient noise and transmits bone-conducted sound more efficiently, so Weber localizes there. A sensorineural loss would lateralize Weber to the BETTER ear with a positive Rinne (air > bone). A predominantly sensorineural mixed loss would still show air > bone. Functional loss yields inconsistent, non-physiologic responses and would not produce this reproducible, internally consistent conductive pattern with a clear family and pregnancy history.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a young woman with progressive conductive loss worsened by pregnancy, a normal mobile eardrum, and a family history of similar surgery, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Cholesteatoma
Chronic serous otitis media
Otosclerosis
Ossicular discontinuity from prior trauma
stages.explanation: Otosclerosis is the classic cause of progressive conductive loss in a young to middle-aged adult with a normal, mobile tympanic membrane and a positive family history; it is often autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance and frequently accelerates during pregnancy due to hormonal effects on abnormal bony remodeling at the stapes footplate. Cholesteatoma usually shows otorrhea and an abnormal drum (retraction pocket, keratin debris). Chronic serous otitis would show a dull, immobile drum with effusion on pneumatic otoscopy, absent here. Ossicular discontinuity requires a trauma or infection history and typically causes a large air-bone gap with a hypermobile or abnormal drum, not this picture.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiometry: bilateral conductive hearing loss with air-bone gaps (left ~35 dB, right ~25 dB)
Bone-conduction thresholds: dip at 2000 Hz (Carhart notch)
Tympanometry: type A bilaterally (normal compliance)
Acoustic (stapedial) reflexes: absent bilaterally
Word recognition: 100% bilaterally when sound is delivered above threshold

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The 2000 Hz bone-conduction dip on the audiogram (Carhart notch) is an artifact related to what underlying pathology?
options: Cochlear hair cell loss from noise exposure
Fixation of the stapes footplate at the oval window
Eustachian tube dysfunction with negative middle-ear pressure
Demyelination of the cochlear nerve
stages.explanation: The Carhart notch is an artifactual depression of bone-conduction thresholds around 2000 Hz caused by stapes footplate fixation, which alters the inner ear's mechanical resonance and lowers measured bone conduction; it typically resolves after successful stapes surgery, confirming it is mechanical rather than true sensorineural loss. Noise-induced loss classically produces a 4000 Hz notch and is sensorineural, not a conductive picture with absent reflexes. Eustachian tube dysfunction causes negative pressure and a type C or B tympanogram, not a type A tracing with a Carhart notch. Cochlear nerve demyelination would cause sensorineural/retrocochlear findings, not stapes fixation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She wishes to avoid surgery during pregnancy. What is the most appropriate definitive and interim management plan?
options: Immediate stapedectomy during the current pregnancy
Lifelong oral fluoride and calcium with no role for surgery or amplification
Hearing aid now, with stapedectomy/stapedotomy offered electively after delivery
Tympanostomy tube placement to ventilate the middle ear
stages.explanation: For otosclerosis, the two definitive options are amplification with a hearing aid (which works well because the cochlea is intact) and stapes surgery (stapedotomy/stapedectomy with prosthesis). Elective stapes surgery is reasonable to defer until after delivery to avoid operative and anesthetic risks in pregnancy, so fitting a hearing aid now and planning surgery later is ideal. Operating during pregnancy is not indicated for an elective, non-emergent condition. Sodium fluoride may be considered for active cochlear otosclerosis but is not first-line and does not preclude amplification or surgery. Tympanostomy tubes treat middle-ear effusion, which she does not have.

pearls: Suspect otosclerosis in a young adult with progressive conductive loss, a normal mobile eardrum, and family history; pregnancy can accelerate it.
Weber lateralizes to the worse ear and Rinne shows bone > air in conductive loss.
The Carhart notch (2 kHz bone-conduction dip) is a mechanical artifact of stapes fixation that improves after surgery.
Definitive options are a hearing aid or stapedotomy/stapedectomy with a prosthesis; absent stapedial reflexes support the diagnosis.

cases.title: Can't Follow Conversations at Family Dinners
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasing difficulty understanding speech, especially in noisy settings, over several years.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man is brought in by his daughter for several years of gradually worsening hearing. He says people 'mumble' and complains his television is too quiet, though family insists it is blaring. He struggles most at restaurants and family gatherings, finding it hardest to pick out a single voice amid background noise.
