High-pitched sounds like the doorbell and his grandchildren's voices are particularly hard to hear. There is no asymmetry, no vertigo, no otorrhea, and only intermittent mild bilateral tinnitus. He denies prior loud-noise occupational exposure beyond ordinary yardwork. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly; no aminoglycosides, loop diuretics at high dose, or chemotherapy. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: retired bookkeeper, never smoker, rare alcohol, lives with his wife. Family history: hearing loss in both parents in old age. ROS: no facial weakness, no neurologic deficits, no recent infections.
cases.exam: Pleasant elderly man who watches the examiner's lips and asks for repetition in the noisy hallway but does better in the quiet room. Otoscopy: minimal cerumen, easily cleared; both tympanic membranes intact, mobile, with normal landmarks and no effusion. Weber (512 Hz) does not lateralize (midline); Rinne positive (air > bone) bilaterally, consistent with a symmetric sensorineural pattern. Facial nerve function symmetric. No nystagmus; gait and Romberg normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy or masses. Cognitive screen grossly intact for orientation and recall when questions are clearly heard.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this man's symmetric, gradual, high-frequency hearing loss?
options: Presbycusis (age-related hearing loss)
Cerumen impaction
Acoustic neuroma
Ménière disease
stages.explanation: Presbycusis is the most common cause of symmetric, bilateral, gradual, high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in older adults, classically with disproportionate difficulty understanding speech in background noise. The midline Weber and bilateral positive Rinne confirm a symmetric sensorineural process. Cerumen impaction causes conductive loss and was excluded on otoscopy. Acoustic neuroma causes unilateral/asymmetric SNHL, not symmetric bilateral loss. Ménière disease produces episodic vertigo, fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss, aural fullness, and roaring tinnitus, none of which fit this steady, painless, high-frequency, non-vertiginous presentation.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiometry: bilateral symmetric sloping high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (mild at 1000 Hz worsening to moderate-severe at 4000-8000 Hz)
Speech reception thresholds mildly elevated and symmetric
Word recognition: 78% right, 80% left (mildly reduced, symmetric)
Tympanometry: type A bilaterally
No significant interaural asymmetry

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line intervention to improve his communication?
options: Cochlear implantation
Bilateral hearing aids with auditory rehabilitation counseling
Oral corticosteroids
Cerumen softening drops only
stages.explanation: Bilateral hearing aids are first-line for symmetric presbycusis causing communication difficulty, and outcomes improve with counseling on realistic expectations, an adjustment period, and assistive strategies. Cochlear implantation is reserved for severe-to-profound loss with poor benefit from hearing aids, not mild-to-moderate presbycusis like this. Oral corticosteroids are used for sudden sensorineural hearing loss, not chronic age-related loss, and would not help. Cerumen drops address impaction, which was already cleared and is not the cause of his deficit; they would not address the sensorineural loss.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point about hearing aids and his overall health is most accurate and high-yield?
options: Hearing aids restore hearing completely to normal
He should wait until the loss is profound before trying any device
Hearing aids are unnecessary if he simply turns up the television volume
Untreated hearing loss is associated with social isolation, depression, and accelerated cognitive decline, so amplification and communication strategies matter
stages.explanation: Untreated hearing loss in older adults is linked to social isolation, depression, reduced quality of life, and accelerated cognitive decline; addressing it with amplification and communication strategies (facing the speaker, reducing background noise, using captions) is therefore important beyond hearing alone. Hearing aids improve audibility and clarity but do not fully restore normal hearing, especially word understanding in noise. Waiting for profound loss is harmful, as earlier intervention yields better adaptation and outcomes. Relying on louder TV volume does not address speech-in-noise difficulty and isolates him further from conversation.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Several months later he reports persistent trouble in restaurants despite well-fit aids. What is the most appropriate next recommendation?
options: Order an MRI of the brain to rule out a tumor
Recommend assistive listening devices (e.g., remote microphone) and communication strategies in addition to his hearing aids
Stop using the hearing aids since they are not helping
Initiate a trial of betahistine
stages.explanation: Even with well-fit hearing aids, speech-in-noise understanding remains challenging in presbycusis; remote/directional microphones and other assistive listening devices, plus communication strategies and aural rehabilitation, meaningfully improve performance in difficult environments. MRI is unnecessary given symmetric, stable, audiometrically confirmed presbycusis without red flags. Abandoning hearing aids would worsen audibility and isolation; the goal is to augment, not discard, them. Betahistine is used (mainly outside the US) for Ménière disease vertigo and has no role in presbycusis.

pearls: Presbycusis is symmetric, gradual, high-frequency SNHL with hallmark difficulty understanding speech in noise.
Bilateral hearing aids plus counseling are first-line; cochlear implants are reserved for severe-to-profound loss with poor aid benefit.
Untreated hearing loss is linked to isolation, depression, and accelerated cognitive decline, so early intervention matters.
Assistive listening devices and communication strategies extend benefit beyond hearing aids in noisy settings.

cases.title: A Factory Worker's Yearly Hearing Check
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine occupational hearing screen with new difficulty hearing coworkers and ringing after shifts.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents to the occupational health clinic for his annual hearing surveillance. He has worked 18 years on a metal stamping line where machinery is loud enough that coworkers must shout to be heard. He admits he 'sometimes' wears the foam earplugs provided but often skips them because they are uncomfortable and make it hard to hear instructions. He notices a high-pitched ringing in both ears that is worse at the end of shifts and gradual difficulty understanding speech, especially with background noise. He denies vertigo, otorrhea, otalgia, asymmetry, or sudden change. He also has a recreational history of attending loud concerts and using firearms for hunting without ear protection. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: smokes half a pack per day for 20 years, social alcohol. Family history: no hereditary hearing loss. ROS: no facial weakness, no neurologic symptoms, no recent infection or trauma.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing man, conversant with occasional requests to repeat. Otoscopy: canals clear; both tympanic membranes intact, mobile, normal landmarks, no perforation or effusion. Weber (512 Hz) midline; Rinne positive (air > bone) bilaterally, consistent with a symmetric sensorineural pattern. Facial nerve symmetric. No spontaneous nystagmus; gait and Romberg normal. No postauricular tenderness or masses; no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiometry: bilateral sensorineural dip centered at 4000 Hz ('noise notch')
Thresholds at 4000 Hz: 45 dB bilaterally; relative recovery at 8000 Hz
Lower frequencies (250-2000 Hz): near normal
Tympanometry: type A bilaterally
Word recognition: 90% bilaterally

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The audiogram shows a 4000 Hz notch with recovery at 8000 Hz. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Presbycusis
Otosclerosis
Noise-induced hearing loss
Ménière disease
stages.explanation: A symmetric sensorineural notch at 3000-6000 Hz (classically 4000 Hz) with relative recovery at 8000 Hz is the signature of noise-induced hearing loss, reflecting maximal cochlear injury at the basal turn from chronic loud-noise exposure. Presbycusis produces a downsloping high-frequency loss that worsens progressively toward 8000 Hz rather than recovering, and it usually affects older patients. Otosclerosis causes conductive loss with a Carhart notch and absent reflexes, not a sensorineural picture with normal type A tympanometry. Ménière disease causes episodic vertigo with fluctuating low-frequency loss, not a fixed high-frequency notch.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important intervention to prevent further hearing loss?
options: Begin oral antioxidant supplements
Consistent and correct use of hearing protection plus engineering controls to reduce noise exposure
Start a hearing aid immediately
Schedule annual MRI surveillance
stages.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss is permanent but entirely preventable; the cornerstone is reducing exposure through consistent, correctly fitted hearing protection (earplugs/earmuffs) and engineering/administrative controls (machine modification, enclosures, limiting time in loud areas) per OSHA hearing-conservation standards. Antioxidants have shown no proven benefit and are not standard. A hearing aid may help his existing deficit later but does not prevent ongoing damage, which is the priority in a 45-year-old with continued exposure. MRI is unnecessary for a symmetric, classic noise notch without asymmetry or red flags.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which additional counseling point is most appropriate for this worker?
options: Once a 4000 Hz notch appears, no further protection is worthwhile
He should also protect his ears during recreational noise (concerts, firearms) because that exposure adds to the damage
Smoking has no relationship to hearing health
His hearing loss will reverse if he simply switches jobs
stages.explanation: Recreational noise such as concerts and firearm use is a significant, often overlooked contributor to noise-induced loss; protecting against it (earplugs/muffs, especially for impulse noise from guns) is essential alongside workplace protection. Continued protection remains worthwhile even after a notch appears, because further exposure causes additional, cumulative permanent damage. Smoking is actually associated with increased risk and progression of hearing loss, so cessation counseling is appropriate. Established noise-induced hearing loss does not reverse with job change; only prevention of further exposure can halt progression.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should his occupational hearing surveillance proceed going forward?
options: Discontinue annual testing since damage is done
Continue annual audiometric monitoring, document a standard threshold shift if it occurs, and reinforce the hearing-conservation program
Refer for immediate cochlear implant evaluation
Test hearing only if he develops vertigo
stages.explanation: OSHA-mandated hearing-conservation programs for noise-exposed workers require ongoing annual audiometric monitoring to detect a standard threshold shift early, trigger protective interventions, and document occupational injury. Stopping surveillance abandons early detection and worker protection. Cochlear implantation is for severe-to-profound loss with poor hearing-aid benefit and is not indicated for a moderate high-frequency notch. Limiting testing to vertigo onset would miss progressive, often asymptomatic threshold shifts, defeating the purpose of surveillance.

pearls: A symmetric 4000 Hz sensorineural notch with recovery at 8000 Hz is the classic signature of noise-induced hearing loss.
Noise-induced loss is permanent but preventable; consistent hearing protection and engineering controls are the cornerstone.
Recreational noise (concerts, firearms) and smoking add to occupational risk and should be addressed.
OSHA hearing-conservation programs require annual audiometric surveillance to catch a standard threshold shift early.

cases.title: Ear Pain After Getting Hit
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden ear pain and decreased hearing after being slapped across the ear.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department two hours after being struck open-handed across the left ear during an altercation. He felt immediate sharp pain, a 'pop,' and muffled hearing on that side, accompanied by transient ringing and brief dizziness that has largely resolved. He noticed a small amount of bloody drainage from the ear canal. He denies persistent vertigo, severe imbalance, facial weakness, facial numbness, clear watery drainage, severe headache, loss of consciousness, neck pain, or visual changes. He has had no prior ear surgery, ear infections, or hearing problems. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol (had two drinks tonight), nonsmoker, works in construction with occasional loud machinery exposure. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: otherwise negative; no nausea or vomiting, no other injuries reported, no nasal or oral trauma, and no prior episodes of ear bleeding or discharge. He denies use of cotton swabs and has never had ear tubes placed.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative man, mildly uncomfortable. Left ear: small amount of dried blood at the canal entrance; canal otherwise without active brisk bleeding or foreign body. Otoscopy of the left tympanic membrane shows a sharp-edged perforation in the posteroinferior quadrant with surrounding small blood clots; no purulent discharge and no visible cholesteatoma. Weber (512 Hz) lateralizes to the LEFT (affected) ear; Rinne shows bone > air on the left, positive (air > bone) on the right, a conductive pattern on the left. Facial nerve function symmetric (House-Brackmann I). No spontaneous nystagmus. No Battle sign, no raccoon eyes, no clear rhinorrhea/otorrhea. Right ear normal. Neck nontender with full range of motion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis explaining his pain, hearing loss, and otoscopic finding?
options: Acute otitis media with effusion
Cholesteatoma
Traumatic tympanic membrane perforation
Otitis externa
stages.explanation: A sudden slap to the ear creates a rapid pressure wave that ruptures the tympanic membrane; the sharp-edged perforation with blood clots, immediate pain, a 'pop,' and conductive hearing loss after blunt aural trauma is diagnostic of a traumatic TM perforation. Acute otitis media presents with fever, otalgia, and a bulging erythematous drum with effusion, not an acute traumatic tear after a slap. Cholesteatoma is a chronic process with keratin debris and a retraction pocket or marginal perforation, not acute trauma. Otitis externa causes canal edema, tenderness on tragal traction, and discharge with an intact drum, which does not match a fresh perforation.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which examination finding would be most concerning for inner-ear (perilymph fistula or ossicular) injury requiring urgent ENT involvement?
options: Mild conductive hearing loss alone
Persistent severe vertigo with sensorineural hearing loss and nystagmus
A small amount of dried blood at the canal
Transient tinnitus that resolves
stages.explanation: Persistent severe vertigo with sensorineural hearing loss and nystagmus suggests inner-ear injury (e.g., perilymphatic fistula or labyrinthine/ossicular disruption transmitting force to the inner ear) and warrants urgent ENT evaluation, as it may need surgical exploration. A small conductive loss is expected with a simple perforation and usually heals spontaneously. Dried blood at the canal is common with a traumatic perforation and not alarming by itself. Transient, self-resolving tinnitus is also typical of an uncomplicated perforation and does not signal inner-ear damage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His vertigo has resolved and there is no purulent drainage. What is the most appropriate management of the perforation?
options: Immediate surgical tympanoplasty tonight
Keep the ear dry (water precautions), expectant management, and ENT follow-up, as most traumatic perforations heal spontaneously
Irrigate the ear canal vigorously to remove all blood
Start oral antibiotics and ear drops routinely for all dry perforations
stages.explanation: Most traumatic tympanic membrane perforations heal spontaneously within weeks to a few months; management is conservative with strict dry-ear precautions (no water entry, no swimming) and outpatient ENT follow-up to confirm healing and document hearing. Immediate tympanoplasty is unnecessary up front and reserved for perforations that fail to heal or have ossicular injury. Vigorous irrigation is contraindicated because forcing water or debris through the perforation risks introducing infection and inner-ear injury. Routine antibiotics are not needed for a clean, dry perforation; topical antibiotic drops are added mainly if there is contamination (e.g., dirty water) or signs of infection.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most important discharge instruction for this patient?
options: Use cotton swabs daily to keep the canal clean
Strictly avoid getting water in the ear and return for any worsening hearing, drainage, fever, vertigo, or facial weakness
Resume swimming immediately to test hearing
Instill over-the-counter hydrogen peroxide drops nightly
stages.explanation: The key discharge counseling is strict dry-ear precautions (no water in the ear, use a cotton ball coated in petroleum jelly while showering) and clear return precautions: worsening or sudden sensorineural hearing loss, purulent drainage or fever (suggesting infection), persistent vertigo, or facial weakness (suggesting deeper injury). Cotton swabs can traumatize the canal and enlarge the perforation and should be avoided. Swimming introduces water through the perforation and risks otitis media. Hydrogen peroxide or other drops should not be instilled through an open perforation without ENT guidance, as fluid can reach the middle/inner ear.

pearls: A slap or blast to the ear causes a pressure-wave rupture of the TM with acute pain, conductive loss, and a sharp-edged perforation.
Most traumatic perforations heal spontaneously with dry-ear precautions and ENT follow-up; avoid swabs and irrigation.
Persistent severe vertigo, sensorineural loss, or nystagmus suggests inner-ear injury and needs urgent ENT evaluation.
Reserve antibiotic drops for contamination or infection; counsel strict water avoidance and clear return precautions.

cases.title: Drooping Face With a Painful Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: New left-sided facial droop with severe ear pain and a rash in the ear.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman presents with three days of severe, deep left ear pain followed by progressive drooping of the left side of her face since yesterday. She reports the affected eye will not close fully and that food collects in her left cheek. She also notes altered taste, sensitivity to loud sounds on the left (hyperacusis), and a few painful 'blisters' she felt in her ear canal. Over the past day she has had mild dizziness and a muffled, ringing left ear. She denies limb weakness, slurred speech, vision loss, or headache with neck stiffness. PMH: type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.1%), hypertension. Medications: metformin 1000 mg BID, lisinopril 20 mg daily. Allergies: sulfa (hives). Social: retired, nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. She had chickenpox as a child and has not received the shingles vaccine. ROS: no fever currently, no recent travel or tick exposure, no chest symptoms.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable woman holding her left ear. Left facial exam: complete left facial weakness involving the FOREHEAD (cannot raise left eyebrow or wrinkle forehead), incomplete left eye closure with Bell phenomenon, and flattened nasolabial fold (House-Brackmann grade IV). Ear: vesicles on an erythematous base on the left external auditory canal and concha; tympanic membrane intact without effusion. Weber lateralizes to the right; Rinne air > bone bilaterally (left sensorineural pattern). Mild horizontal nystagmus on left gaze. Remaining cranial nerves intact; no limb weakness, normal coordination, no neck stiffness. Oral mucosa without vesicles; no parotid mass.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Facial palsy with ear-canal vesicles, otalgia, and sensorineural symptoms in this patient is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus)
Bell palsy
Acute otitis media with facial involvement
Stroke involving the facial motor cortex
stages.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome is reactivation of varicella-zoster virus in the geniculate ganglion, producing the triad of peripheral facial palsy, severe otalgia, and vesicles in the ear canal/auricle (zoster oticus); it often involves the eighth nerve as well, causing the hearing loss, tinnitus, and vertigo seen here. Bell palsy is idiopathic and lacks vesicles and prominent SNHL/vertigo. Acute otitis media shows a bulging, inflamed drum with effusion, absent here, and rarely causes this picture. A central (cortical) stroke spares the forehead because of bilateral upper-face innervation, whereas this patient has complete forehead involvement, indicating a peripheral lesion.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: The fact that the forehead is involved (she cannot raise the left eyebrow) tells you the lesion is located where?
options: Upper motor neuron / contralateral motor cortex
Lower motor neuron / peripheral facial nerve (CN VII)
Internal capsule
Cerebellum
stages.explanation: Forehead involvement localizes the lesion to the lower motor neuron (peripheral facial nerve). The forehead receives bilateral cortical input, so an upper motor neuron (central) lesion spares the forehead and weakens only the lower face on the opposite side. Complete unilateral weakness including the forehead indicates a peripheral CN VII lesion, as in Ramsay Hunt or Bell palsy. Internal capsule lesions cause contralateral upper-motor-neuron facial weakness with forehead sparing. Cerebellar lesions cause ataxia and coordination deficits, not isolated facial weakness, and would not explain ear vesicles.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment regimen for Ramsay Hunt syndrome?
options: Antiviral therapy alone (acyclovir or valacyclovir)
Oral antibiotics (amoxicillin) only
Corticosteroids plus antiviral therapy, with eye protection
Observation, as it resolves without treatment
stages.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome is treated with combination corticosteroids plus antivirals (acyclovir or valacyclovir), ideally started early (within ~72 hours), which improves the chance of facial recovery; meticulous eye protection (lubricating drops, ointment, taping/patching at night) prevents exposure keratitis from incomplete eye closure. Antiviral monotherapy is suboptimal compared with combined therapy for facial outcomes. Antibiotics target bacterial infection and have no role in a viral zoster process. Observation alone is inappropriate: Ramsay Hunt has a worse prognosis than Bell palsy, and prompt treatment plus eye care meaningfully affects recovery and prevents corneal injury.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Compared with Bell palsy, what should you tell her about prognosis and complications?
options: Recovery is essentially always complete and faster than Bell palsy
Facial recovery is generally LESS complete than Bell palsy, and she is at risk for persistent hearing loss and postherpetic neuralgia
There is no risk to her eye
The vesicles indicate the infection is no longer contagious
stages.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome carries a poorer prognosis for facial recovery than Bell palsy, with a higher rate of incomplete recovery and synkinesis; patients are also at risk for persistent sensorineural hearing loss, balance disturbance, and postherpetic neuralgia. Recovery is therefore neither always complete nor faster. The eye is at significant risk for exposure keratopathy because of incomplete closure, so lubrication and protection are essential. Active zoster vesicles contain infectious virus and can transmit varicella to susceptible (e.g., unvaccinated, immunocompromised, pregnant) contacts, so she remains contagious until lesions crust.

pearls: Ramsay Hunt = peripheral facial palsy + otalgia + vesicles in the ear canal/auricle from VZV reactivation in the geniculate ganglion.
Forehead involvement localizes to a lower motor neuron (peripheral) lesion; a central lesion spares the forehead.
Treat with corticosteroids PLUS antivirals early, and protect the eye from exposure keratitis.
Prognosis is worse than Bell palsy, with higher risk of incomplete recovery, hearing loss, and postherpetic neuralgia.

cases.title: Woke Up With Half My Face Not Working
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden weakness of one side of the face since this morning.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents with weakness of the right side of his face that he noticed on waking this morning, about 10 hours ago. He could not drink his coffee without it dribbling, and his right eye feels dry and will not blink fully. He reports the symptoms came on rapidly over hours, and he has mild pain behind the right ear, slightly altered taste on the front of his tongue, and a sense that sounds are uncomfortably loud on the right. He denies limb weakness, numbness, slurred speech, difficulty walking, double vision, vesicular rash, fever, headache with neck stiffness, or recent tick bite. He had a mild upper respiratory infection last week. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: software developer, nonsmoker, social alcohol, no recent travel to Lyme-endemic areas. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: no joint pains, no rash, no recent dental procedures, no prior similar episodes.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man, anxious. Facial exam: complete right facial weakness INVOLVING the forehead (cannot raise right eyebrow, cannot fully close right eye, flattened right nasolabial fold, droop of right corner of mouth); House-Brackmann grade III-IV. Bell phenomenon present (eye rolls up on attempted closure). Ear: external canal and auricle WITHOUT vesicles; tympanic membrane normal and mobile bilaterally. Hearing grossly symmetric; Weber midline, Rinne air > bone bilaterally. No nystagmus. Remaining cranial nerves intact; extraocular movements full; tongue midline; palate elevates symmetrically. Motor strength 5/5 in all limbs, normal coordination and gait, no sensory deficit. No neck stiffness, no parotid mass, no rash.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Acute unilateral facial weakness involving the forehead, no rash, normal hearing, and an otherwise normal neurologic exam most likely represents what?
options: Ischemic stroke
Ramsay Hunt syndrome
Bell palsy (idiopathic peripheral facial palsy)
Myasthenia gravis
stages.explanation: Bell palsy is an acute, idiopathic peripheral (lower motor neuron) facial palsy presenting over hours to days with complete unilateral weakness INCLUDING the forehead, often with postauricular pain, altered taste, and hyperacusis, and a normal remaining neurologic exam; it is a clinical diagnosis. A central stroke spares the forehead and usually has other deficits. Ramsay Hunt requires vesicles and typically prominent otalgia with hearing loss/vertigo, absent here (no rash, normal hearing). Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable, often bilateral ocular/bulbar weakness without the acute, complete, unilateral forehead-involving pattern and without taste or hyperacusis changes.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which feature would prompt you to question the diagnosis of Bell palsy and pursue further workup or imaging?
options: Forehead involvement on the weak side
Gradual onset over many weeks, other cranial nerve deficits, or limb weakness
Postauricular pain
Altered taste on the anterior tongue
stages.explanation: Red flags arguing against simple Bell palsy include slowly progressive onset over weeks (suggesting a mass/tumor), involvement of other cranial nerves, limb weakness or other CNS signs (suggesting stroke or central pathology), bilateral palsy, or recurrent/non-resolving palsy, any of which warrant imaging and further evaluation. Forehead involvement is EXPECTED in Bell palsy and confirms a peripheral lesion, not a red flag. Postauricular pain and altered taste (chorda tympani) and hyperacusis (stapedius) are typical accompanying features of Bell palsy reflecting the nerve segments involved, not warning signs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He presents within 72 hours of onset. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Antibiotics alone
Oral corticosteroids started promptly, with eye protection
Surgical facial nerve decompression now
No treatment; all cases resolve fully
stages.explanation: Prompt oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone/prednisolone) started within 72 hours improve the likelihood and completeness of recovery in Bell palsy and are first-line; meticulous eye protection (artificial tears during the day, lubricating ointment and taping/patching at night) prevents exposure keratitis from incomplete closure. Antivirals may be added for severe (complete) palsy but steroids are the core therapy; antibiotics have no role in idiopathic Bell palsy. Surgical decompression is not standard initial care and is controversial/rarely used. While many patients recover, treatment improves outcomes, so doing nothing is inappropriate, and not all cases recover completely.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because his right eye does not fully close, what is the most important protective measure to prevent a serious complication?
options: Topical antibiotic drops to prevent conjunctivitis
Lubricating artificial tears by day plus ointment and taping/patching the eye closed at night to prevent exposure keratitis/corneal ulceration
Atropine drops to dilate the pupil
A pressure patch worn continuously for two weeks without lubrication
stages.explanation: Incomplete eye closure (lagophthalmos) leaves the cornea exposed and at risk for drying, abrasion, exposure keratitis, and ulceration; protection with frequent lubricating drops during the day and thicker ointment plus taping or patching the eye shut at night is essential to prevent corneal injury and vision loss. Routine antibiotic drops do not address the underlying exposure/drying problem. Atropine dilates the pupil and offers no corneal protection. A continuous pressure patch without lubrication can itself abrade the cornea and does not keep it moist; the goal is lubrication plus protected closure, especially overnight.

pearls: Bell palsy is acute idiopathic peripheral CN VII palsy with forehead involvement; a central lesion spares the forehead.
Start oral corticosteroids within 72 hours; add antivirals for severe/complete palsy.
Aggressive eye protection (drops by day, ointment and taping at night) prevents exposure keratitis.
Slowly progressive onset, other cranial-nerve or limb deficits, or recurrence are red flags warranting imaging.

cases.title: A Toddler's Ear That Won't Get Better
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening ear pain with swelling behind the ear and a pushed-out earlobe.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother for worsening left ear pain, fever, and new swelling behind the ear. He was diagnosed with a left ear infection about 10 days ago but the family did not fill the antibiotic prescription, hoping it would resolve. Over the last two days he became more irritable, febrile, and stopped eating well; today his mother noticed his left ear looks 'pushed forward' and the area behind it is red, swollen, and tender. He has had no neck stiffness, no seizures, no vomiting, and no altered mental status, though he is fussy. PMH: recurrent acute otitis media (3 episodes in the past year); full-term birth; immunizations up to date. Medications: none currently; acetaminophen at home. Allergies: none. Social: lives with parents, attends daycare. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: decreased appetite, poor sleep due to pain; no rash, no respiratory distress, no ear trauma.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, fussy but consolable toddler. Left ear: the auricle is displaced anteriorly and inferiorly (protruding); the POSTAURICULAR area is erythematous, swollen, fluctuant, and tender, with loss of the normal postauricular crease. Otoscopy of the left ear shows a bulging, erythematous, opacified tympanic membrane with purulent middle-ear effusion; landmarks obscured. Right ear normal. No facial weakness (House-Brackmann I). Neck supple without meningismus; no Kernig/Brudzinski signs. No nystagmus. Neurologic exam nonfocal; alert and interactive between bouts of crying. No cervical lymphadenitis distinct from the postauricular swelling.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Protruding auricle with postauricular erythema, swelling, and tenderness following untreated acute otitis media most likely indicates what?
options: Cellulitis of the auricle only
Acute mastoiditis
Postauricular lymphadenopathy
Parotitis
stages.explanation: Acute mastoiditis is a suppurative complication of acute otitis media in which infection spreads to the mastoid air cells, producing postauricular erythema, swelling, tenderness, and the classic anteriorly/inferiorly displaced (protruding) auricle with loss of the postauricular crease, exactly as seen here in a child with untreated AOM. Simple auricular cellulitis would not push the ear forward or originate from the middle ear. Postauricular lymphadenopathy causes a discrete mobile node, not diffuse mastoid swelling with a protruding pinna. Parotitis causes swelling over the angle of the jaw/cheek that lifts the earlobe outward but is anterior/inferior to the ear, not in the mastoid region, and is not preceded by AOM.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to confirm mastoiditis and evaluate for complications?
options: CT of the temporal bone with contrast
Plain skull radiographs
Ultrasound of the postauricular area
No imaging; treat empirically and discharge
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the temporal bones is the imaging of choice to confirm mastoiditis (coalescence/opacification of mastoid air cells, bony septal breakdown) and to detect complications such as subperiosteal abscess, intracranial extension, or sinus thrombosis that would change management. Plain radiographs lack sensitivity and detail for the mastoid and complications. Ultrasound is not adequate to assess bony mastoid involvement or intracranial spread. Discharging without imaging or admission is unsafe in an ill, febrile child with a suppurative complication; mastoiditis requires admission, IV antibiotics, and often imaging to guide possible surgical drainage.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
CRP 92 mg/L
Blood culture: pending
CT temporal bone with contrast: opacification and coalescence of left mastoid air cells with breakdown of bony septa; small subperiosteal abscess
No intracranial abscess or sinus thrombosis identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given coalescent mastoiditis with a subperiosteal abscess, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral antibiotics and discharge with follow-up
Admission for IV antibiotics, ENT consultation, and surgical drainage (myringotomy ± mastoidectomy/abscess drainage)
Topical antibiotic ear drops alone
Observation with antipyretics only
stages.explanation: Coalescent mastoiditis with a subperiosteal abscess requires inpatient admission, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent ENT consultation, and surgical drainage; myringotomy (with possible tympanostomy tube) drains the middle ear and obtains cultures, and the subperiosteal abscess/coalescent disease often needs incision and drainage or mastoidectomy. Oral antibiotics with discharge are inadequate for a drained-abscess-level complication. Topical drops cannot treat deep mastoid/abscess infection. Observation with antipyretics alone neglects a potentially serious infection that can extend intracranially. Prompt antibiotics plus source control are essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication of untreated mastoiditis is most important to monitor for in this child?
options: Allergic rhinitis
Intracranial extension (meningitis, epidural/brain abscess) or sigmoid/lateral sinus thrombosis
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
Tympanosclerosis
stages.explanation: Mastoiditis can extend beyond the mastoid to cause serious complications including meningitis, epidural or brain abscess, and lateral/sigmoid sinus thrombosis, as well as facial nerve palsy and labyrinthitis; vigilance for headache, altered mental status, neck stiffness, new neurologic deficits, or persistent spiking fevers is critical, which is why CT (and sometimes MRV) and close monitoring are used. Allergic rhinitis is unrelated. BPPV is a benign positional vertigo unrelated to acute mastoid infection. Tympanosclerosis is a chronic scarring change of the TM, not an acute life-threatening complication of mastoiditis.

pearls: Mastoiditis is a suppurative complication of AOM: postauricular erythema/swelling/tenderness with a protruding, anteroinferiorly displaced auricle.
Contrast CT of the temporal bone confirms coalescent disease and detects subperiosteal abscess or intracranial spread.
Treat with admission, IV antibiotics, ENT consult, and surgical drainage (myringotomy ± mastoidectomy) when coalescent or abscessed.
Watch for intracranial extension (meningitis, brain abscess) and sigmoid/lateral sinus thrombosis.

cases.title: Bleeding From the Ear After a High-Speed Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Head injury with blood behind the eardrum and hearing loss after a motorcycle crash.
cases.history: A 25-year-old man is brought by EMS after a motorcycle crash at highway speed in which he was wearing a helmet that struck the pavement on the right side. He had a brief loss of consciousness at the scene and now reports right-sided hearing loss, blood from the right ear, dizziness, and a severe headache. He denies neck pain (but is collared), limb weakness, or chest/abdominal pain on initial survey. He is awake and answering questions but slightly confused about the event. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol; denies drugs; reports drinking earlier today. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: nausea, no vomiting, no vision loss, no seizure, no clear watery nasal drainage, and no extremity numbness or weakness. Primary survey: airway patent, breathing unlabored, hemodynamically stable; GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6). C-spine immobilized pending clearance, and a FAST exam and secondary survey are unremarkable for other significant injuries.
cases.exam: Young man on a backboard in a cervical collar, mildly confused but cooperative. HEENT: ecchymosis over the RIGHT mastoid process (Battle sign); right otoscopy shows blood behind an intact, bluish tympanic membrane (hemotympanum). No clear watery rhinorrhea appreciated yet; a drop of bloody fluid is at the right canal. Pupils equal and reactive; no raccoon eyes currently. Facial nerve: right facial movements appear symmetric to forehead, eye closure, and smile on initial testing (House-Brackmann I), though exam is limited by patient cooperation. Weber lateralizes to the LEFT; right Rinne air > bone (right sensorineural pattern). Neuro: moving all extremities, no focal limb deficit. Neck immobilized, no obvious deformity. Remainder of trauma survey unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After ATLS primary survey and stabilization, which finding triad (Battle sign, hemotympanum, hearing loss) should make you suspect what injury?
options: Isolated external ear laceration
Temporal bone fracture
Mandibular fracture
Simple concussion only
stages.explanation: Battle sign (postauricular ecchymosis), hemotympanum, and hearing loss after significant head trauma are classic signs of a temporal bone (skull base) fracture. The temporal bone houses the otic capsule and facial nerve, so fractures commonly cause hemotympanum, CSF otorrhea, hearing loss, and possible facial palsy. An external ear laceration would be visible superficially and would not cause hemotympanum or these basilar signs. A mandibular fracture causes malocclusion, jaw pain, and trismus, not hemotympanum or Battle sign. A simple concussion can cause confusion but does not produce hemotympanum, Battle sign, or focal hearing loss, which indicate structural skull-base injury.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to evaluate the suspected fracture and its complications?
options: MRI brain without contrast
High-resolution non-contrast CT of the head and temporal bones
Plain skull radiographs
Carotid ultrasound
stages.explanation: High-resolution non-contrast CT of the head and temporal bones is the study of choice in trauma to detect skull-base/temporal bone fractures, classify them (e.g., otic-capsule sparing vs. violating), and identify associated intracranial injury (hemorrhage), making it ideal in this acutely injured patient. MRI is excellent for soft tissue and later facial nerve/CSF evaluation but is slow, less available emergently, and inferior for acute bony detail and hemorrhage in the trauma bay. Plain radiographs lack the resolution to characterize temporal bone fractures. Carotid ultrasound does not evaluate the fracture (CT angiography would be used if vascular injury is suspected).

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head/temporal bone: longitudinal fracture of the right temporal bone, otic-capsule sparing
Opacification of right middle ear and mastoid air cells (consistent with hemotympanum)
No intracranial hemorrhage; no midline shift
Small amount of pneumocephalus adjacent to the fracture
Ossicular chain appears intact

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Why is documenting facial nerve function early and serially (onset and progression) critically important in temporal bone fracture?
options: It determines antibiotic choice

Immediate-onset complete facial paralysis suggests nerve transection/impingement and may warrant urgent surgical exploration, whereas delayed/incomplete onset usually reflects edema and is managed conservatively
Facial nerve status predicts the need for a cervical collar
It has no impact on management
stages.explanation: Timing of facial palsy guides management: immediate, complete paralysis at the time of injury suggests anatomic disruption or bony impingement of the facial nerve and may require urgent imaging directed at the nerve and surgical decompression/repair, whereas delayed-onset or incomplete weakness usually results from nerve edema and is managed conservatively (often with steroids) with a good prognosis. This is why onset (immediate vs. delayed) and progression must be documented before sedation/intubation obscures the exam. Facial status does not determine antibiotic choice or the need for a cervical collar, and it clearly does impact management decisions, so saying it has no impact is incorrect.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: There is concern for a CSF leak (clear fluid from the ear/nose). What is the most appropriate initial management of a traumatic CSF otorrhea in this setting?
options: Immediate surgical dural repair in the ED
Conservative management with head-of-bed elevation and observation, as most traumatic CSF leaks resolve spontaneously; avoid packing the ear and avoid routine prophylactic antibiotics
Tightly pack the ear canal to stop the leak
Start high-dose corticosteroids to seal the leak
stages.explanation: Most traumatic CSF leaks from temporal bone fractures close spontaneously with conservative measures: head-of-bed elevation, activity/strain precautions, and close observation; surgery is reserved for persistent leaks or those with complications. The ear should NOT be packed, as obstructing flow can promote retrograde infection and meningitis, and routine prophylactic antibiotics for CSF leak are not recommended by current guidance because they do not clearly reduce meningitis and risk resistant organisms. Immediate ED dural repair is not indicated for a fresh leak that usually resolves. Corticosteroids do not seal CSF leaks. The priority is observation and meningitis vigilance.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The CT showed a longitudinal, otic-capsule-SPARING fracture. Compared with a transverse, otic-capsule-VIOLATING fracture, what is the expected clinical pattern?
options: Otic-capsule-violating (transverse) fractures more often cause profound sensorineural hearing loss, vertigo, and facial palsy, whereas otic-capsule-sparing (longitudinal) fractures more often cause conductive loss and CSF otorrhea
Otic-capsule-sparing fractures always cause complete deafness and dense facial paralysis
Both fracture types are clinically identical and the distinction does not matter
Transverse fractures spare the inner ear and only cause external ear bruising
stages.explanation: Otic-capsule-violating (often transverse) fractures cross the inner ear and carry the highest rates of profound sensorineural hearing loss, vertigo, facial nerve injury, and intracranial complications. Otic-capsule-sparing (often longitudinal) fractures, like this patient's, more commonly cause conductive hearing loss (ossicular/middle-ear involvement, hemotympanum) and CSF otorrhea, with lower rates of severe SNHL and facial palsy, hence his preserved facial function and SNHL pattern that may partly reflect concussive injury. Otic-capsule-sparing fractures do NOT always cause complete deafness or dense paralysis. The distinction is clinically important for prognosis and complication risk, and transverse fractures involve, not spare, the inner ear.

cases.diagnosis: Right temporal bone fracture (longitudinal, otic-capsule sparing) with hemotympanum
pearls: Battle sign, hemotympanum, and hearing loss after head trauma signal a temporal bone (skull-base) fracture.
Non-contrast CT of the head and temporal bones is the emergent imaging of choice.
Document facial nerve onset/progression early: immediate complete palsy suggests transection and possible surgery; delayed palsy is usually edema treated conservatively.
Manage traumatic CSF otorrhea conservatively (HOB elevation, observation); do not pack the ear, and avoid routine prophylactic antibiotics.

cases.title: A Ringing That Never Stops
cases.chiefComplaint: Constant ringing in both ears for several months that is interfering with sleep.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with several months of persistent ringing in both ears. He describes it as a steady high-pitched tone, present most of the day, more noticeable at night and in quiet rooms, and it is disrupting his sleep and concentration. He has some difficulty hearing in noisy settings but no sudden change. He denies pulsatile quality, vertigo, otorrhea, otalgia, or facial weakness. He has a long history of recreational and occupational noise exposure (years of woodworking and concerts). He drinks several cups of coffee daily and recently increased his use of ibuprofen for knee pain. PMH: hypertension, osteoarthritis, mild anxiety. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, ibuprofen 600 mg up to three times daily, occasional aspirin. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (quit 5 years ago), social alcohol, retired carpenter. Family history: age-related hearing loss in parents. ROS: no neurologic deficits, no headache, no unilateral symptoms, low mood related to poor sleep.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, somewhat fatigued man. Otoscopy: canals clear; both tympanic membranes intact, mobile, normal landmarks, no effusion. Weber (512 Hz) midline; Rinne air > bone bilaterally (symmetric, no conductive component). No spontaneous nystagmus. Auscultation over the neck, periauricular region, and orbits reveals NO bruit; no audible pulsatile sound. Facial nerve symmetric; remaining cranial nerves intact. Gait and Romberg normal. No carotid bruit; no lymphadenopathy or neck masses. Cardiac exam regular without murmurs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His tinnitus is bilateral, non-pulsatile, and continuous. What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Audiometry and a thorough history including medication review
MRI of the brain with contrast immediately
Carotid angiography
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: For chronic subjective tinnitus, the initial workup is a careful history (including ototoxic/contributing medications and noise exposure) and a formal audiogram, since tinnitus is most often associated with underlying hearing loss; this guides management and identifies treatable contributors. Immediate contrast MRI is reserved for red-flag features such as UNILATERAL or pulsatile tinnitus or asymmetric hearing loss, not symmetric bilateral non-pulsatile tinnitus. Carotid angiography is for suspected vascular/pulsatile causes after appropriate stepwise evaluation, not first-line here. Lumbar puncture is not part of the routine tinnitus workup and would be considered only for specific indications (e.g., suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension with pulsatile tinnitus and papilledema).

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiometry: bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with a noise-notch component
Symmetric thresholds (no significant interaural asymmetry)
Tympanometry: type A bilaterally
Word recognition: 88% bilaterally
Tinnitus pitch match: high-frequency tone corresponding to region of hearing loss

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which feature, if present, would change your approach and prompt dedicated MRI (IAC) imaging?
options: Bilateral symmetric tinnitus with symmetric hearing loss
Unilateral tinnitus and/or asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss (or pulsatile tinnitus)
Tinnitus worse in quiet rooms
A history of noise exposure
stages.explanation: Unilateral tinnitus, asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss, or pulsatile tinnitus are red flags that warrant dedicated imaging: gadolinium MRI of the internal auditory canals for asymmetric/unilateral findings (to rule out vestibular schwannoma), and MRI/MRV or vascular imaging for pulsatile tinnitus. Bilateral symmetric tinnitus with symmetric hearing loss, as this patient has, does not require routine MRI. Tinnitus being more noticeable in quiet rooms is typical of subjective tinnitus and not a red flag. A history of noise exposure explains the symmetric loss and tinnitus but is not itself an indication for MRI.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His audiogram is symmetric with no red flags. What is the most appropriate management for his bothersome subjective tinnitus?
options: Long-term benzodiazepines as first-line therapy
High-dose oral steroids
Patient education/reassurance, hearing amplification or sound therapy (sound enrichment/maskers), and cognitive behavioral therapy; review and minimize contributing factors
Surgical labyrinthectomy
stages.explanation: Evidence-based management of chronic subjective tinnitus centers on education and reassurance, addressing associated hearing loss (hearing aids/amplification, which often reduce tinnitus perception), sound therapy/sound enrichment or maskers, and cognitive behavioral therapy for distress, sleep, and coping; reducing contributing factors (excess NSAIDs/aspirin, caffeine, noise exposure) is also reasonable. There is no proven pharmacologic cure. Long-term benzodiazepines are not first-line and carry dependence risk. Steroids treat sudden sensorineural hearing loss, not chronic stable tinnitus. Labyrinthectomy is a destructive procedure for intractable vertigo (e.g., Ménière), not a treatment for subjective tinnitus, and would not help.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which additional advice is most appropriate to reduce his symptom burden?
options: Increase caffeine to stay alert despite poor sleep
Limit excessive NSAID/aspirin and caffeine use, protect hearing from further noise, and use background/ambient sound at night to ease tinnitus and improve sleep
Stop all of his prescribed medications immediately
Avoid all sound, including hearing aids, to 'rest' the ears
stages.explanation: Practical counseling includes minimizing potential aggravators (excessive NSAIDs/aspirin and caffeine), protecting hearing from further noise exposure, and using background or ambient sound (white noise, a fan, sound generators) at night, since tinnitus is most bothersome in quiet and sound enrichment plus good sleep hygiene reduce its impact. Increasing caffeine can worsen sleep and is counterproductive. Abruptly stopping prescribed medications (e.g., antihypertensive) is unsafe and should not be advised broadly. Avoiding all sound is the opposite of effective management; silence accentuates tinnitus, and amplification/sound therapy generally helps rather than harms.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic subjective tinnitus (associated with noise-induced/age-related hearing loss)
pearls: Subjective tinnitus is usually linked to underlying hearing loss; initial workup is history (with med review) plus audiometry.
Red flags (unilateral, asymmetric SNHL, or pulsatile tinnitus) warrant dedicated MRI IAC or vascular imaging.
There is no cure; manage with education, hearing amplification, sound therapy, and CBT, and minimize contributors (NSAIDs/aspirin, caffeine, noise).
Sound enrichment at night and hearing aids reduce tinnitus perception, which is worst in quiet environments.

cases.title: Whooshing in the Ear That Won't Quit
cases.chiefComplaint: Rhythmic whooshing sound in the right ear for three months.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents with a three-month history of a rhythmic 'whooshing' noise in her right ear that beats in time with her heartbeat. It is worse when she lies down and when she exerts herself, and she can sometimes quiet it by pressing on the right side of her neck. She also reports daily morning headaches and intermittent blurring of vision with brief 'grey-outs' when she stands. She denies hearing loss, vertigo, otorrhea, or facial weakness. PMH: obesity (BMI 38), polycystic ovary syndrome, and migraine. Medications: combined oral contraceptive, and she recently started a course of doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for acne. Allergies: none. Social: works as an accountant, non-smoker, rare alcohol, no recreational drugs. Family history: hypertension in both parents. She is anxious because an online search suggested the sound could mean a 'brain tumor.' She has gained roughly 25 pounds over the past year and reports the headaches and noise both worsened during this weight gain.
cases.exam: Alert, obese woman in no distress. Otoscopy: both tympanic membranes are normal with no retrotympanic mass, fluid, or pulsation behind the drum. There is no tenderness over the mastoid. A soft bruit is audible on auscultation over the right temporal region and posterior to the ear; it diminishes with light compression of the right internal jugular vein. Tuning fork (Weber/Rinne) is symmetric with no conductive or sensorineural loss. Cranial nerves are intact. Dilated fundoscopy reveals bilateral blurring of the optic disc margins. No focal motor or sensory deficit. Neck supple, no thyromegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single feature on history and exam most strongly distinguishes pulsatile (objective/vascular) tinnitus from ordinary non-pulsatile tinnitus and directs the workup?
options: The tinnitus is louder in a quiet room
The sound is rhythmic, synchronous with the heartbeat, and an audible bruit changes with jugular compression
The patient also has migraine headaches
The tinnitus is unilateral
stages.explanation: Pulsatile tinnitus that beats with the pulse and is accompanied by an auscultable bruit modifiable by jugular/carotid compression points to a vascular source and mandates vascular imaging rather than routine reassurance. A quieter room amplifies all tinnitus and is non-specific. Migraine is common and does not localize the source. Unilaterality raises concern for a retrocochlear/structural lesion but, on its own, does not establish a vascular/pulsatile etiology; the pulse-synchronous bruit does. Recognizing true pulsatile tinnitus is the pivotal step because it reroutes the patient toward CT/MR angiography and venography rather than a standard audiogram-only pathway.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the obesity, daily headaches, transient visual obscurations, bilateral disc blurring, and recent doxycycline use, which diagnosis best unifies the picture and what is the most appropriate confirmatory test sequence?
options: Glomus tympanicum — obtain a temporal bone CT
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension — obtain MRI/MRV, then lumbar puncture with opening pressure
Carotid artery dissection — obtain carotid ultrasound only
Vestibular migraine — start a triptan and reassure
stages.explanation: An obese woman of childbearing age on a tetracycline with headaches, transient visual obscurations, papilledema, and pulsatile tinnitus fits idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH). The correct sequence is neuroimaging with venography to exclude a mass or venous sinus thrombosis, then lumbar puncture documenting elevated opening pressure with normal CSF constituents. Glomus tympanicum would show a retrotympanic mass behind an intact drum, absent here. Carotid dissection usually presents with neck pain and Horner syndrome, and ultrasound alone is inadequate. Vestibular migraine does not cause papilledema or pulse-synchronous bruit; treating empirically would miss vision-threatening raised pressure.

stages.label: IMAGING & LP
stages.content:
MRI brain: no mass, normal ventricles, empty sella, flattening of the posterior globes
MRV: patent dural venous sinuses, no thrombosis, focal transverse sinus stenosis bilaterally
CTA head/neck: no dural arteriovenous fistula, no aneurysm
Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 32 cm H2O (normal <25)
CSF: protein 22 mg/dL, glucose 60 mg/dL, WBC 1/µL, normal cytology
Formal visual fields: enlarged blind spots bilaterally

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The opening pressure is 32 cm H2O with normal CSF and bilateral papilledema. Besides weight loss counseling, what is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy and an immediate medication change?
options: Start acetazolamide and stop the combined oral contraceptive and doxycycline
Start a calcium channel blocker and continue current medications
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation for venous stenosis
Place a ventriculoperitoneal shunt immediately
stages.explanation: First-line medical therapy for IIH is acetazolamide, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces CSF production, combined with weight loss. Tetracyclines (doxycycline) and, less consistently, estrogen-containing contraceptives are associated with raised intracranial pressure and should be discontinued. A calcium channel blocker treats migraine/hypertension, not CSF pressure. Anticoagulation treats venous sinus thrombosis, which the MRV excluded; the transverse sinus stenosis here is a downstream effect of high pressure, not a clot. Surgical CSF diversion or optic nerve sheath fenestration is reserved for medical failure or rapidly progressive vision loss, not as the initial step in a patient who has not yet been treated medically.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient instead had a normal opening pressure but the CTA had revealed a dural arteriovenous fistula at the transverse-sigmoid junction, what would be the definitive treatment and the main feared complication of leaving it untreated?
options: Observation only; the main risk is hearing loss
Endovascular embolization; the main risk of cortical venous reflux is intracranial hemorrhage
Acetazolamide; the main risk is optic atrophy
Mastoidectomy; the main risk is facial palsy
stages.explanation: A dural arteriovenous fistula (DAVF) is a classic vascular cause of pulsatile tinnitus, often arising at the transverse-sigmoid junction and producing a pulse-synchronous bruit. Definitive treatment is catheter-based endovascular embolization to obliterate the abnormal shunt (occasionally open surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery in selected cases). The most feared complication of high-grade DAVFs with cortical venous reflux is intracranial hemorrhage from venous hypertension, which is what drives the urgency of treatment. Observation is unacceptable for lesions demonstrating cortical reflux. Acetazolamide treats raised CSF pressure in IIH, not a structural arteriovenous shunt. Mastoidectomy addresses middle-ear and mastoid disease and would not obliterate a dural fistula; facial palsy is a mastoid surgery risk, not the principal danger of an untreated DAVF.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She starts acetazolamide and weight loss and stops the offending medications. What follow-up plan most reliably protects against the worst outcome of her IIH?
options: Serial formal visual field and acuity testing with ophthalmology, escalating to surgery (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunt) if vision deteriorates
Repeat lumbar puncture monthly regardless of symptoms with no eye exams
Reassurance and discharge with no scheduled follow-up
Brain MRI every week to watch the transverse sinus stenosis
stages.explanation: The sight-threatening complication of IIH is progressive papilledema leading to optic atrophy and permanent vision loss, so the cornerstone of follow-up is serial formal visual field perimetry and acuity testing coordinated with ophthalmology, with surgical escalation (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF diversion) if vision worsens despite medical therapy. Routine monthly LPs without monitoring vision miss the actual endpoint that matters and are not how disease is tracked. Discharging without follow-up risks silent, irreversible visual decline. Weekly MRI of a downstream venous stenosis adds cost and radiation/contrast without guiding management; the stenosis is a consequence of pressure, not the monitoring target.

cases.diagnosis: Pulsatile tinnitus due to idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH)
pearls: Pulse-synchronous tinnitus with an auscultable bruit that changes with jugular compression is vascular until proven otherwise and warrants angiographic/venographic imaging.
IIH classically affects obese women of childbearing age; tetracyclines, vitamin A derivatives, and estrogen can precipitate it.
Diagnosis requires neuroimaging (with MRV to exclude sinus thrombosis) plus a lumbar puncture opening pressure >25 cm H2O with normal CSF.
First-line therapy is weight loss plus acetazolamide; escalate to surgical CSF diversion or optic nerve sheath fenestration only for vision-threatening or refractory disease.

cases.title: A Pulsing Sound and a Red Spot Behind the Eardrum
cases.chiefComplaint: Pulsatile ringing in the left ear with mild hearing loss for six months.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman reports six months of a 'heartbeat' sound in her left ear accompanied by gradually worsening hearing on that side and a sense of fullness. The whooshing is constant, beats with her pulse, and is occasionally accompanied by a faint clicking. She denies vertigo, otorrhea, facial weakness, dysphagia, or hoarseness. PMH: well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Allergies: none. Social history: lifelong non-smoker, social alcohol, retired schoolteacher. Family history: a maternal aunt had 'tumors in the neck' requiring surgery, details unknown. She has tried over-the-counter decongestants without relief. She notes that the hearing seems muffled as if 'underwater,' and the pulsing is loud enough to interfere with sleep. There has been no preceding upper respiratory infection, head trauma, or barotrauma. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no palpitations, weight loss, flushing, or episodic hypertension.
cases.exam: Otoscopy of the left ear reveals a well-circumscribed reddish-blue mass behind the inferior portion of an intact tympanic membrane, appearing to arise from the promontory. Pneumatic otoscopy with positive pressure causes the mass to blanch (Brown sign). The right ear is normal. Tuning fork testing shows Weber lateralizing to the left and Rinne negative on the left (bone > air), consistent with left conductive hearing loss. There is no facial nerve weakness, no nystagmus, and no palpable neck mass. Cranial nerves IX through XII are intact. No carotid bruit. Remainder of the head and neck exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A reddish retrotympanic mass that blanches with pneumatic positive pressure (Brown sign) behind an intact drum, with conductive hearing loss and pulsatile tinnitus, is most consistent with which lesion?
options: Cholesteatoma
Glomus tympanicum (tympanic paraganglioma)
High-riding/dehiscent jugular bulb
Aberrant internal carotid artery
stages.explanation: A vascular, reddish mass on the cochlear promontory behind an intact membrane that blanches with pneumatic pressure (Brown sign) and produces pulsatile tinnitus with conductive loss is the textbook glomus tympanicum. Cholesteatoma appears as a pearly-white keratin mass, usually with retraction or perforation, and is not pulsatile. A dehiscent jugular bulb and an aberrant carotid are vascular mimics, but they sit in characteristic locations (jugular bulb posteroinferior/bluish; aberrant carotid anteroinferior/reddish coursing across the promontory) and are vital structures—distinguishing them from a paraganglioma before any biopsy is the entire point, because biopsy of a vascular variant causes catastrophic hemorrhage.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before any intervention, which imaging study is most important to characterize the lesion, define bony involvement, and exclude vascular variants masquerading as a paraganglioma?
options: Plain mastoid radiographs
High-resolution temporal bone CT with contrast (plus MRI/MRA as needed)
Office biopsy of the retrotympanic mass
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: High-resolution temporal bone CT is the key first study: it precisely defines the lesion's location, ossicular and bony involvement, and the integrity of the jugular plate and carotid canal, thereby distinguishing a promontory-confined glomus tympanicum from a glomus jugulare or a dangerous vascular variant; MRI/MRA then helps assess soft-tissue extent and reveals the classic 'salt-and-pepper' flow voids of a paraganglioma. Plain mastoid films are obsolete and lack the resolution for any of these structures. Office biopsy is strictly contraindicated until a vascular variant has been excluded—biopsying an aberrant carotid artery or a high/dehiscent jugular bulb can cause catastrophic, potentially fatal hemorrhage. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial carotid flow but cannot characterize a middle-ear mass or delineate its bony margins.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
High-resolution temporal bone CT: well-defined soft-tissue mass confined to the middle ear overlying the cochlear promontory; intact jugular plate and intact carotid canal; ossicles intact; no bony erosion of the otic capsule
MRI: avidly enhancing middle-ear mass with internal flow voids ('salt-and-pepper' appearance)
No evidence of extension to the jugular foramen
24-hour urine metanephrines: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging shows a mass confined to the middle ear with an intact jugular plate and carotid canal. What is the diagnosis and why does the normal urine metanephrine screen still matter?
options: Glomus jugulare; metanephrines confirm it is benign
Glomus tympanicum; a minority of paragangliomas are catecholamine-secreting or multicentric, so biochemical and syndrome screening is prudent
Meningioma; metanephrines are irrelevant
Schwannoma; metanephrines exclude malignancy
stages.explanation: A mass confined to the cochlear promontory with an intact jugular plate and carotid canal is a glomus tympanicum, not a glomus jugulare (which centers on and erodes the jugular foramen and can involve cranial nerves IX–XII). Although head-and-neck paragangliomas are usually non-secretory, a small fraction secrete catecholamines, and germline mutations (notably the succinate dehydrogenase SDHx genes) confer multicentric and hereditary disease with pheochromocytoma risk—particularly relevant here given the family history of neck tumors—so urinary or plasma metanephrines and genetic counseling are appropriate before and after treatment. Meningioma and schwannoma do not show the avid salt-and-pepper flow voids or blanch with pneumatic pressure, and metanephrine screening is specific to paraganglioma and pheochromocytoma biology, not to those other tumor types.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this small glomus tympanicum confined to the middle ear in an otherwise healthy 55-year-old, what is the preferred definitive management?
options: Surgical excision (transcanal/tympanotomy resection)
Office incisional biopsy followed by chemotherapy
Empiric long-term antibiotics
Systemic beta-blockade alone with no further treatment
stages.explanation: A small, surgically accessible glomus tympanicum is best treated by complete surgical excision via a transcanal or tympanotomy approach, which is typically curative for promontory-confined disease. Incisional office biopsy risks brisk bleeding and is unnecessary once imaging is diagnostic; paragangliomas are not chemosensitive, so chemotherapy has no role here. Antibiotics treat infection, which this is not. Beta-blockade is used to control catecholamine effects in secretory tumors perioperatively but is not a standalone treatment for the mass. Radiotherapy is an alternative chiefly for larger jugulare lesions or poor surgical candidates, not for a small tympanicum tumor in a healthy patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given the family history of 'neck tumors' and the SDHx association, what long-term plan is most appropriate after successful resection?
options: No further follow-up is needed once the tumor is removed
Annual catecholamine screening for life regardless of genetics
Refer for genetic counseling/SDHx testing and arrange surveillance for multicentric/recurrent paraganglioma in the patient and at-risk relatives
Start lifelong beta-blockade prophylactically
stages.explanation: Because head-and-neck paragangliomas can be hereditary (notably SDHx mutations) and multicentric—a possibility specifically suggested here by the family history of neck tumors—appropriate long-term care includes referral for genetic counseling and testing plus clinical and imaging surveillance for additional or recurrent paragangliomas, extending screening to at-risk relatives when a germline mutation is identified. Declaring that no follow-up is needed ignores the real risk of recurrence and metachronous (second-site) tumors that can appear years later. Indiscriminate lifelong catecholamine screening in every patient is not the correct framework; biochemical testing and surveillance should be risk-stratified by genetic results and clinical findings. Prophylactic beta-blockade is unwarranted in a confirmed non-secretory tumor and does nothing to address the central need for genetic surveillance.

pearls: Brown sign—blanching of a retrotympanic mass with pneumatic positive pressure—is highly suggestive of a glomus tumor.
Never biopsy a vascular-appearing middle-ear mass in the office until temporal bone CT excludes an aberrant carotid or dehiscent/high jugular bulb.
Glomus tympanicum is confined to the promontory with an intact jugular plate; glomus jugulare erodes the jugular foramen and may involve cranial nerves IX–XII.
Screen for catecholamine secretion and SDHx-related hereditary/multicentric paraganglioma, especially with a suggestive family history.

cases.title: Swollen, Tender Ear After a Match
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful swelling of the outer ear after a wrestling match.
cases.history: A 21-year-old collegiate wrestler presents to the emergency department about six hours after a takedown in which his right ear was repeatedly ground against the mat and an opponent's headgear. He noticed progressive swelling and throbbing pain of the outer ear over the past several hours. He denies hearing loss, dizziness, drainage of blood or fluid from the ear canal, neck pain, loss of consciousness, or visual changes. PMH: none. Medications: none; he denies anabolic steroids, NSAID overuse, or blood thinners. Allergies: none. Social history: NCAA wrestler with a prior 'minor' ear injury two seasons ago that was 'left alone' and now leaves that ear slightly thickened. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol. Immunizations up to date including tetanus within five years. Family history: noncontributory. He is eager to return to competition this weekend and asks whether the ear can simply be left to heal on its own as his last one was.
cases.exam: The right auricle is diffusely swollen, tense, and tender with a bluish, fluctuant collection over the anterolateral surface that effaces the normal contours of the helix and antihelix. There is no overlying laceration, no active bleeding, and no purulence. The external auditory canal is patent without blood or otorrhea; the tympanic membrane is intact and mobile. There is no Battle sign, hemotympanum, or CSF leak. Hearing is grossly intact and the tuning fork exam is symmetric. The contralateral ear shows a firm, irregular, fibrotic deformity (old 'cauliflower' change). No facial weakness; cranial nerves intact.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis, and what is the underlying pathophysiology that makes prompt treatment important?
options: Auricular cellulitis; antibiotics alone suffice
Relapsing polychondritis; immunosuppression is needed
Auricular (subperichondrial) hematoma; blood between perichondrium and cartilage strips the cartilage of its blood supply
Preauricular abscess; needs incision and packing
stages.explanation: A tense, fluctuant, bluish collection effacing auricular contours after blunt shear trauma is a subperichondrial hematoma. Because auricular cartilage is avascular and depends on the overlying perichondrium for nutrition, blood accumulating in this plane separates cartilage from its blood supply, risking necrosis and neocartilage formation ('cauliflower ear'). Cellulitis is a spreading skin infection without a fluctuant blood collection and would not follow shear trauma this way. Relapsing polychondritis is a recurrent autoimmune chondritis, typically bilateral and sparing the lobule, not a post-traumatic single-ear fluctuance. A preauricular abscess is purulent and located anterior to the tragus, not a diffuse hematoma of the pinna.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management of this fresh auricular hematoma?
options: Reassurance and oral antibiotics; allow it to resorb
Incision/needle evacuation of the hematoma followed by a compressive bolster dressing and reevaluation
Ice and elevation only, with return in two weeks
Immediate surgical reconstruction of the auricle
stages.explanation: Treatment is prompt evacuation of the hematoma (needle aspiration for small/acute collections or, more reliably, incision and drainage) followed by a compressive dressing—through-and-through bolster sutures, a conforming splint, or a pressure dressing—to obliterate the dead space and reappose perichondrium to cartilage, preventing reaccumulation. Simply observing or icing allows the cartilage to be deprived of perfusion and to deform. Antibiotics are an adjunct (cartilage infection risk), not the primary treatment. Reconstruction is reserved for established cauliflower deformity, not an acute, drainable hematoma. Close follow-up within 24–48 hours ensures the collection has not recurred.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After successful drainage and bolster placement, which adjunctive measure is most appropriate, and what organism most concerns you in cartilage infection?
options: No antibiotics are ever indicated for drained auricular hematomas
A topical antifungal only
High-dose corticosteroids to reduce swelling
Anti-pseudomonal coverage (e.g., a fluoroquinolone) given Pseudomonas aeruginosa as the key cause of auricular perichondritis
stages.explanation: Because drainage breaches the perichondrium and auricular cartilage is poorly vascularized, prophylactic antibiotics covering Pseudomonas aeruginosa—the classic and most feared cause of auricular perichondritis and chondritis—are commonly given, typically an oral fluoroquinolone in adults, to prevent a devastating cartilage infection that can itself destroy the framework. Saying antibiotics are never indicated ignores the real, recognized risk of post-procedural chondritis after instrumenting the perichondrial space. A topical antifungal is irrelevant, since fungi are not the relevant pathogens in acute traumatic auricular infection. Systemic corticosteroids neither prevent cartilage infection nor treat the hematoma and could impair healing and host defense; residual swelling is managed by adequate evacuation and continued compression, not steroids.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What counseling and follow-up plan is most appropriate for this athlete?
options: Clear him to wrestle this weekend with no restrictions or follow-up
Arrange ENT/wound recheck in 24–48 hours, keep the bolster, hold contact sport until healed, and stress protective headgear to prevent recurrent cauliflower deformity
Refer only for cosmetic surgery in several months
Discharge with no instructions since hematomas always resolve
stages.explanation: Auricular hematomas readily reaccumulate after drainage, so the patient needs a structured plan: a wound and ear recheck within 24–48 hours to confirm the collection has not refilled, retention of the compressive bolster or splint until the perichondrium has reapposed, temporary avoidance of contact sport until fully healed, and emphatic counseling about wearing protective headgear going forward—his contralateral cauliflower ear vividly demonstrates what happens without timely treatment and ongoing protection. Clearing him to compete immediately invites reaccumulation, infection, and permanent deformity. Deferring everything to delayed cosmetic surgery ignores the acute, reversible problem that must be addressed now. Discharging without instructions is unsafe, because untreated or recurrent hematomas are precisely the mechanism by which cauliflower ear forms.

pearls: Auricular cartilage is avascular and depends on the perichondrium; a subperichondrial hematoma must be evacuated promptly to prevent cartilage necrosis and cauliflower ear.
Treatment is drainage plus a compressive bolster/splint to obliterate dead space, with a 24–48 hour recheck because hematomas reaccumulate.
