Cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa (e.g., a fluoroquinolone) after drainage, the classic cause of auricular perichondritis.
Counsel athletes to wear protective headgear; prevention is far easier than reconstructing an established cauliflower deformity.

cases.title: A Newborn With an Underdeveloped Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: Malformed right external ear noted at birth.
cases.history: A full-term newborn boy is evaluated in the NICU after the delivery team noted an abnormally small, malformed right external ear with no obvious ear canal opening. Pregnancy was uncomplicated; mother denies gestational diabetes, alcohol, or known teratogen exposure, though she took no specific medications and had limited prenatal care. The infant was born at 39 weeks by spontaneous vaginal delivery with Apgars of 8 and 9. He is feeding adequately. There is no maternal family history of childhood deafness, but a paternal cousin reportedly 'had ear surgery as a child.' On parental report, the left ear looks normal. The parents are anxious about whether their son will be able to hear and whether this signals a broader 'syndrome.' Examination by the neonatology team raised concern for additional subtle facial asymmetry. The newborn hearing screen has not yet been performed. There is no respiratory distress and no feeding difficulty suggestive of cleft palate.
cases.exam: Right ear: a small, disorganized auricular remnant (peanut-shaped cartilage) with absence of a normal helix/antihelix and no patent external auditory canal (aural atresia). Left ear: normal pinna with a patent canal and a visible, intact tympanic membrane on otoscopy. Facial exam shows mild right-sided micrognathia and subtle flattening of the right malar region; the eyes have a slight downslant. Oral exam reveals an intact palate with normal suck. No skin tags beyond the auricular remnant, no preauricular pits on the left. Cardiac exam normal, no murmur. No limb anomalies. Tone and reflexes appropriate for age.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For a newborn with unilateral microtia and aural atresia, what is the single most important early audiologic priority?
options: Reassure the parents that one normal ear guarantees normal development and defer all testing
Confirm hearing in the contralateral (normal-appearing) ear with objective newborn testing to ensure at least one hearing ear
Schedule auricular reconstruction in the first week of life
Begin hearing aids for the atretic ear immediately
stages.explanation: With microtia/atresia the affected ear has a maximal conductive loss, so the overriding early priority is to confirm that the contralateral ear hears normally—because intact hearing in at least one ear permits normal speech and language development. This is verified objectively (e.g., otoacoustic emissions and/or automated auditory brainstem response). Simply reassuring without testing is unsafe, since microtia can be bilateral or the 'normal' ear can have occult loss. Auricular reconstruction is deferred for years (until cartilage growth and emotional readiness). Hearing aids for the atretic ear are considered later (often a bone-conduction device), not as the first newborn step before audiometric confirmation.

stages.label: HEARING SCREEN
stages.content:
Right ear: no otoacoustic emissions; automated ABR shows no response (consistent with atresia/conductive block)
Left ear: otoacoustic emissions present; automated ABR PASS at normal levels
Diagnostic ABR pending for thresholds
Renal ultrasound: ordered as part of anomaly screen

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The left ear passes and the right fails. Given micrognathia, malar flattening, and downslanting eyes, which syndrome screen and embryologic theme best fits, and what evaluations are warranted?
options: Isolated microtia; no further workup needed
A first/second branchial (pharyngeal) arch disorder spectrum (e.g., craniofacial microsomia / Treacher Collins features); evaluate with genetics, ophthalmology, cardiac and renal screening
Trisomy 21; order only a karyotype and stop
Congenital CMV; treat with antivirals empirically without testing
stages.explanation: Microtia/atresia with mandibular hypoplasia, malar flattening, and downslanting palpebral fissures points to a first/second pharyngeal arch developmental spectrum (craniofacial microsomia, and overlapping Treacher Collins features), and the external/middle ear derive from these arches. Appropriate workup is multidisciplinary: clinical genetics, ophthalmology, and screening for associated cardiac and renal anomalies (the latter because ear anomalies cluster with renal malformations). Calling it isolated ignores the dysmorphic features. Trisomy 21 has a different phenotype (upslanting fissures, hypotonia) and a karyotype-only approach is too narrow. Congenital CMV causes sensorineural loss and is diagnosed by testing, not treated empirically without confirmation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding habilitation and the timing of treatment for the conductive loss and the auricular deformity, which plan is most appropriate?
options: Immediate surgical canal reconstruction and pinna reconstruction in the neonatal period
Do nothing until the child is a teenager
Ensure aided hearing for the conductive component (e.g., bone-conduction hearing device when indicated), enroll in early intervention, and defer auricular reconstruction until childhood
Recommend cochlear implantation of the atretic ear now
stages.explanation: Management prioritizes hearing access and development: ensure adequate hearing (the normal ear here suffices for language, but a bone-conduction device is used for bilateral atresia or to optimize binaural hearing), provide early-intervention/audiology follow-up, and defer auricular reconstruction (autologous rib graft or prosthesis) and any atresiaplasty until later childhood when the cartilage framework has grown and the child can participate. Neonatal canal/pinna surgery is not done. Doing nothing risks missing evolving hearing or developmental needs. Cochlear implantation treats severe sensorineural loss, not a purely conductive atresia, so it is inappropriate here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before any future surgical atresiaplasty is contemplated, which imaging study is essential and why?
options: Plain skull radiograph to confirm the diagnosis
High-resolution temporal bone CT to assess middle/inner ear anatomy, ossicular development, facial nerve course, and atresia plate—key surgical candidacy factors
Brain MRI to look for a tumor
No imaging is needed before atresia surgery
stages.explanation: High-resolution temporal bone CT is essential before considering atresiaplasty: it delineates the middle ear space, the ossicular mass and mobility, the degree of mastoid pneumatization, the thickness of the atretic bony plate, and—critically—the frequently aberrant course of the facial nerve, all of which determine surgical candidacy (formalized in the Jahrsdoerfer grading scale) and operative risk. Plain radiographs lack the spatial resolution to show any of these fine structures and are inadequate. Brain MRI is not the appropriate study to evaluate a purely conductive atresia and is instead reserved for suspected sensorineural or central pathology. Proceeding to surgery without this CT roadmap would expose the child to unacceptable risk, most importantly iatrogenic facial nerve injury from its anomalous position.

cases.diagnosis: Microtia with congenital aural atresia (first/second pharyngeal arch spectrum)
pearls: In microtia/atresia, urgently confirm normal hearing in the contralateral ear—one hearing ear allows normal speech and language development.

External and middle ear structures arise from the first and second pharyngeal arches; associated micrognathia, malar hypoplasia, and downslanting fissures should prompt a syndrome and multi-organ (cardiac, renal, ophthalmologic, genetic) workup.
Defer auricular reconstruction and atresiaplasty to later childhood; manage hearing early with bone-conduction devices when indicated.
Obtain high-resolution temporal bone CT (e.g., Jahrsdoerfer assessment) before any atresia surgery to map ossicles and the facial nerve.

cases.title: Persistent Ear Drainage With Headache and Spiking Fevers
cases.chiefComplaint: Weeks of ear discharge now with severe headache and high spiking fevers.
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl is admitted with three weeks of left ear drainage that was treated as 'swimmer's ear,' now accompanied by five days of worsening headache, intermittent vomiting, and high fevers that spike and remit. Her parents note she has been increasingly drowsy and complains of double vision. She has a long history of recurrent otitis media and a chronically draining left ear. PMH: recurrent acute otitis media as a toddler, no prior surgery, no immunodeficiency. Medications: she completed amoxicillin and then ofloxacin drops without improvement. Allergies: none. Immunizations up to date. Social: attends third grade, swims competitively. Family history: noncontributory. Over the past two days the headaches have become constant and she has had two episodes of vomiting without diarrhea. There is no neck stiffness reported at home, no seizures, and no recent travel. The drainage is foul-smelling. She is irritable and prefers a dark, quiet room.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, irritable child. Left ear: foul, mucopurulent otorrhea filling the canal; the tympanic membrane cannot be fully visualized but there is a posterosuperior perforation with granulation tissue and white debris suggestive of cholesteatoma. Marked tenderness and slight swelling over the mastoid with mild postauricular erythema. Neurologic exam: photophobia, mild neck stiffness, and a left abducens (CN VI) palsy with horizontal diplopia on left gaze. Fundoscopy shows early bilateral disc edema. No focal limb weakness; gait cautious. Right ear normal. Remainder of cranial nerves intact. No rash. Kernig sign equivocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a child with chronic suppurative otitis media now showing spiking ('picket-fence') fevers, headache, and a CN VI palsy, which intracranial complication should top the differential and drive imaging?
options: Benign tension headache
Lateral (sigmoid/transverse) sinus thrombosis with otitic hydrocephalus from contiguous middle-ear/mastoid infection
Migraine with aura
Viral labyrinthitis
stages.explanation: Chronic ear infection plus swinging ('picket-fence') fevers, headache, raised-intracranial-pressure signs, and an abducens (CN VI) palsy is the classic presentation of lateral (sigmoid/transverse) venous sinus thrombosis, often accompanied by 'otitic hydrocephalus'—raised intracranial pressure from impaired cerebral venous drainage. The picket-fence fever pattern reflects intermittent septic emboli from septic thrombophlebitis of the infected sinus. A CN VI palsy is a classic false-localizing sign of elevated intracranial pressure and does not indicate a brainstem lesion here. Tension headache and migraine do not cause fever, papilledema, or otorrhea. Viral labyrinthitis causes vertigo and hearing change but not high fevers, papilledema, or a foul-draining cholesteatomatous ear. This infectious-vascular complication must therefore be imaged urgently to confirm the clot and source.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate imaging strategy, and what caution applies regarding lumbar puncture?
options: Lumbar puncture first to relieve pressure, then imaging
Contrast-enhanced CT of the temporal bones/brain and CT or MR venography; defer LP until imaging excludes a mass/obstruction and herniation risk
Plain mastoid films only
MRI of the lumbar spine
stages.explanation: Cross-sectional imaging is obtained first: contrast CT (and ideally MRI) of the brain and temporal bones plus CT/MR venography demonstrates mastoid/middle-ear disease, the 'empty delta' sign of sinus thrombosis, abscess, and the patency of venous sinuses. Lumbar puncture is deferred until imaging excludes an intracranial mass/obstructing lesion and assesses herniation risk; performing LP first in a patient with focal signs and papilledema risks herniation. Plain mastoid films are inadequate. Lumbar spine MRI is irrelevant to an intracranial venous and otologic process. Venography is the key sequence to confirm the clot.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.4 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
CRP 142 mg/L
Blood cultures: pending (later grew anaerobes and streptococci)
Contrast CT temporal bones: opacified left mastoid air cells with bony septal erosion (coalescent mastoiditis) and soft tissue consistent with cholesteatoma
MR venography: filling defect (empty delta sign) within the left sigmoid and transverse sinus = lateral sinus thrombosis
Brain MRI: no parenchymal abscess; mildly small ventricles with signs of raised intracranial pressure
Ear culture: mixed flora including Pseudomonas and anaerobes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms coalescent mastoiditis with cholesteatoma and sigmoid sinus thrombosis. What is the cornerstone of management?
options: Oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up
Anticoagulation alone without surgery
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics plus urgent surgical source control (mastoidectomy with removal of cholesteatoma and drainage), with anticoagulation considered case-by-case
Antiviral therapy and observation
stages.explanation: The infected source must be removed: management combines broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Pseudomonas and anaerobes with urgent mastoidectomy to eradicate the coalescent mastoiditis/cholesteatoma and drain the perisinus infection (sometimes with sinus exploration). Anticoagulation for the septic sinus thrombosis is considered on a case-by-case basis (often used when thrombus propagates or symptoms persist), but it is adjunctive—not a substitute for source control. Oral antibiotics or outpatient management are grossly inadequate for an intracranial complication. Antibiotics alone without surgery leave the nidus (cholesteatoma) in place. This is a bacterial, not viral, process, so antivirals have no role.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Even after antibiotics and surgery, she has persistent headache and worsening papilledema from otitic hydrocephalus. What is the principal therapeutic goal to prevent permanent harm?
options: Lower the elevated intracranial pressure (e.g., acetazolamide and/or serial CSF drainage) and monitor vision to prevent optic nerve injury and blindness
Accept the papilledema as harmless
Stop all antibiotics immediately
Begin high-dose vitamin A
stages.explanation: Otitic hydrocephalus is raised intracranial pressure from impaired venous drainage; untreated papilledema threatens the optic nerves and vision, so the goal is to reduce ICP—using carbonic anhydrase inhibitors such as acetazolamide and, when needed, serial lumbar punctures or CSF diversion—while serially monitoring visual fields and acuity. Ignoring papilledema risks irreversible optic atrophy and blindness. Stopping antibiotics prematurely would let the infection relapse. Vitamin A is not a treatment for intracranial hypertension and (as a teratogen-class agent) can itself raise ICP. The unifying principle is aggressive pressure control to protect vision.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She improves after mastoidectomy and IV antibiotics. Which broader principle best explains why this complication occurred and how to prevent it in similar children?
options: Acute otitis media in children is never dangerous and needs no follow-up

Untreated/inadequately treated chronic suppurative otitis media—especially with cholesteatoma—can erode bone and spread intracranially, so it requires definitive surgical management and audiologic follow-up, not just repeated drops
Swimming alone caused the intracranial clot and avoiding water is fully protective
The complication was unrelated to the ear disease and was purely coincidental
stages.explanation: The key lesson is that chronic suppurative otitis media with cholesteatoma is a surgical disease: the keratinizing matrix erodes bone and can extend to the mastoid, venous sinuses, and intracranium, producing complications like sinus thrombosis, meningitis, and abscess. Prevention hinges on recognizing chronic/foul otorrhea that fails topical therapy, obtaining timely otologic referral, and performing definitive surgery (tympanomastoidectomy) with audiologic follow-up—rather than cycling through ear drops. Claiming childhood ear infection is never dangerous is false. Swimming may contribute to external/middle-ear infection but did not solely cause an intracranial clot, and water avoidance is not 'fully protective.' The complication was a direct, not coincidental, extension of the ear disease.

cases.diagnosis: Lateral (sigmoid) sinus thrombosis with otitic hydrocephalus complicating cholesteatomatous chronic suppurative otitis media/coalescent mastoiditis
pearls: Picket-fence (swinging) fevers with headache and a false-localizing CN VI palsy in a chronically draining ear suggest lateral sinus thrombosis and raised intracranial pressure.
Obtain contrast CT/MRI with venography (look for the empty delta sign); defer LP until imaging excludes mass effect and herniation risk.
Treat with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Pseudomonas and anaerobes plus urgent mastoidectomy for source control; anticoagulation is case-by-case.
Otitic hydrocephalus threatens vision—lower intracranial pressure (acetazolamide, CSF drainage) and monitor the optic nerves closely.

cases.title: Seasonal Sneezing and Itchy, Watery Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent sneezing, clear runny nose, and itchy eyes every spring.
cases.history: A 24-year-old graduate student presents with bouts of sneezing, clear watery nasal discharge, nasal itching, and congestion that recur every spring and worsen on days with high pollen counts or after she mows the lawn. She also has itchy, watery eyes and an itchy palate. Symptoms have occurred each spring since her teens and resolve in winter. She denies fever, facial pain, purulent discharge, tooth pain, or unilateral symptoms. PMH: mild intermittent asthma and childhood eczema. Medications: albuterol inhaler as needed; she has tried over-the-counter pseudoephedrine with some relief but it keeps her awake. Allergies: penicillin (rash as a child). Social: nonsmoker, lives with a cat, no occupational dust exposure. Family history: mother with allergic rhinitis, father with asthma. She is frustrated that symptoms interfere with studying and sleep, and she has been rubbing her nose upward frequently. She asks whether she needs antibiotics, having taken them for 'sinus problems' in the past without clear benefit.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young woman, frequently sniffling. Anterior rhinoscopy shows pale, boggy, bluish nasal turbinates with thin clear secretions and no purulence or unilateral mass. There is a horizontal crease across the lower nasal dorsum (nasal salute sign). The eyes show conjunctival injection with watery discharge and mild lower-lid darkening (allergic shiners). The oropharynx has a 'cobblestone' posterior wall from lymphoid hyperplasia; no exudate. Lungs are clear to auscultation with no wheeze at rest. Ears: tympanic membranes normal. No sinus tenderness on palpation. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which constellation best supports allergic rhinitis over a viral upper respiratory infection or bacterial sinusitis?
options: Purulent discharge, facial pain, and fever
Unilateral bloody discharge and a nasal mass
High fever with tender, swollen turbinates
Seasonal, recurrent nasal/ocular/palatal itching with sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, pale boggy turbinates, and allergic shiners
stages.explanation: Allergic rhinitis is characterized by the itch-sneeze-clear-rhinorrhea-congestion tetrad, seasonal recurrence with identifiable triggers (pollen, mowing the lawn, animal dander), prominent ocular and palatal itching, pale boggy bluish turbinates, allergic shiners, and the horizontal nasal-crease 'salute' from chronic upward rubbing. A strong personal and family history of atopy (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis) further reinforces the diagnosis. By contrast, purulent discharge with facial pain and fever suggests bacterial sinusitis. Unilateral bloody discharge with a discrete nasal mass raises concern for neoplasm or a foreign body and warrants a different, focused evaluation. High fever with tender, swollen turbinates is not typical of an allergic process. The classic itching plus clear discharge in a reproducible seasonal pattern are the discriminating features that point firmly to allergic rhinitis here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy for her moderate, persistent-during-season symptoms?
options: An intranasal corticosteroid as the cornerstone, with a second-generation oral antihistamine for itch/sneeze
A 10-day course of oral antibiotics
Long-term oral decongestant (pseudoephedrine) daily
Topical decongestant spray (oxymetazoline) daily for the whole season
stages.explanation: Intranasal corticosteroids are the single most effective first-line therapy for allergic rhinitis, simultaneously addressing congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, and itch by suppressing the local inflammatory response; a non-sedating second-generation oral antihistamine is a reasonable adjunct, especially for the itching, sneezing, and ocular symptoms that bother her. Antibiotics treat bacterial infection, which she does not have, and have caused her no benefit in the past. Chronic daily oral pseudoephedrine causes insomnia (which she already experienced) along with hypertension and other cardiovascular effects, so it is not an appropriate maintenance strategy. Topical oxymetazoline used for more than a few consecutive days causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa) and must be avoided for ongoing seasonal control, making the intranasal steroid the clear cornerstone of therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond medication, which intervention is most appropriate to recommend?
options: Begin systemic corticosteroids for the season
Allergen avoidance/environmental control and, for confirmation or refractory cases, allergy testing with consideration of immunotherapy
Order a CT of the sinuses now
Start prophylactic antibiotics each spring
stages.explanation: Allergen avoidance and environmental control—limiting outdoor pollen exposure on high-count days, showering after mowing, and managing the household cat dander—are foundational and address the root cause rather than just the symptoms. When the offending trigger is unclear or symptoms remain refractory despite optimized pharmacotherapy, allergy testing (skin prick or serum specific IgE) identifies the relevant sensitizations and opens the door to allergen immunotherapy, the only intervention that can durably modify the disease long-term. Systemic corticosteroids carry too many cumulative adverse effects to be used for routine seasonal control. Sinus CT is unnecessary in straightforward allergic rhinitis without red flags such as unilateral or bloody symptoms. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role whatsoever in an allergic, non-infectious process and would only promote resistance.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She returns having used oxymetazoline spray several times daily for two weeks for congestion and now feels more blocked than ever. What happened and what is the fix?
options: She has developed bacterial sinusitis; start antibiotics
Rhinitis medicamentosa from rebound congestion; stop the topical decongestant and bridge with an intranasal corticosteroid
She has a deviated septum requiring surgery
Increase the oxymetazoline frequency
stages.explanation: Prolonged use of topical decongestants causes rhinitis medicamentosa—rebound nasal congestion and tachyphylaxis driven by downregulation of mucosal adrenergic receptor responsiveness, so the nose becomes more blocked the more spray is used. The fix is to discontinue the offending spray entirely and bridge the rebound congestion with an intranasal corticosteroid (with or without a short oral steroid taper and saline irrigation) until the mucosa recovers over the following weeks. It is not bacterial sinusitis, so antibiotics are wrong and unhelpful. A deviated septum causes fixed structural obstruction unrelated to her recent two weeks of spray use and does not explain a symptom that began precisely after overusing the decongestant. Increasing the oxymetazoline frequency only deepens the very downregulation that caused the problem.

pearls: The itch-sneeze-clear rhinorrhea-congestion pattern with pale boggy turbinates, allergic shiners, and a nasal salute crease defines allergic rhinitis.
Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective first-line therapy; add a second-generation antihistamine for itch/sneeze and ocular symptoms.
Topical decongestants used beyond a few days cause rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion)—treat by stopping the spray and bridging with an intranasal steroid.
For refractory or unclear cases, pursue allergen avoidance, allergy testing, and consider immunotherapy as disease-modifying therapy.

cases.title: A Cold That Got Worse Instead of Better
cases.chiefComplaint: Facial pressure and yellow-green nasal discharge that have worsened after an initial cold.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents on day 11 of a respiratory illness. It began as a typical cold with sore throat, congestion, and clear runny nose; he started improving around day 5 but then developed worsening symptoms over the past four days, with thick yellow-green nasal discharge, pressure and pain over his right cheek and upper teeth, and a low-grade fever. He notes the pain increases when he leans forward. He denies vision changes, eye swelling, severe headache, neck stiffness, or confusion. PMH: well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily; no immunodeficiency. Medications: lisinopril; acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, office worker, no recent travel. Family history: noncontributory. He has been using saline rinses and a brief course of oxymetazoline without lasting relief. He wants antibiotics because the colored discharge and persistence make him worried about a 'sinus infection.' He has had no dental procedures recently and no prior episodes of sinusitis.
cases.exam: Comfortable, in mild discomfort with facial pressure. Anterior rhinoscopy shows erythematous, edematous mucosa with purulent (mucopurulent) discharge in the right middle meatus. There is tenderness to palpation and percussion over the right maxillary sinus and the right maxillary teeth without a discrete dental abscess. No periorbital edema, proptosis, chemosis, or extraocular movement limitation. Cranial nerves intact; no meningismus. Oropharynx with mild posterior drainage, no peritonsillar swelling. Ears: tympanic membranes normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical pattern best distinguishes acute bacterial rhinosinusitis from an uncomplicated viral upper respiratory infection?
options: Any colored nasal discharge automatically means bacterial infection
Symptoms persisting ≥10 days without improvement, OR severe symptoms (high fever ≥39°C with purulence) for ≥3–4 days, OR 'double worsening' after initial improvement
Symptoms lasting fewer than 5 days
Isolated clear rhinorrhea for 2 days
stages.explanation: Bacterial rhinosinusitis is suggested by one of three guideline patterns: (1) persistent symptoms lasting at least 10 days without any improvement, (2) severe onset with high fever (≥39°C) plus purulent discharge or facial pain lasting 3–4 consecutive days at the start of illness, or (3) 'double sickening'—a worsening of symptoms after an initial viral improvement, which is exactly what this patient describes. Colored nasal discharge alone is not diagnostic, because uncomplicated viral infections commonly produce thick, purulent-appearing mucus as well. Symptoms lasting under 5 days or brief clear rhinorrhea indicate a self-limited viral course. This patient's day-11 worsening after early improvement around day 5 fits the double-worsening criterion squarely, making likely bacterial infection the best-supported diagnosis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: In this otherwise healthy adult meeting clinical criteria, is sinus imaging indicated, and how should the diagnosis be made?
options: Obtain a CT sinus on every patient to confirm
Diagnose clinically; reserve imaging for suspected complications or alternative diagnoses
Obtain plain sinus radiographs routinely
Order an immediate MRI of the brain
stages.explanation: Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis is fundamentally a clinical diagnosis based on the symptom pattern and physical exam; routine imaging is explicitly not recommended for uncomplicated cases because it cannot reliably distinguish viral from bacterial mucosal inflammation and only adds cost and radiation exposure without changing management. Cross-sectional imaging (CT, or MRI when intracranial or orbital complications are suspected) is reserved for red-flag features—orbital signs, severe or worsening headache, neurologic changes, immunocompromise, or failure to respond to appropriate therapy. Plain sinus radiographs are insensitive and outdated and have no role here. An immediate brain MRI would be appropriate only if a complication such as intracranial extension were suspected, which it is not in this otherwise well-appearing, neurologically intact adult.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He meets criteria for acute bacterial rhinosinusitis. What is the most appropriate initial management in this otherwise healthy, non-penicillin-allergic adult?
options: Immediate broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone
Topical oxymetazoline continuously for two weeks
Either watchful waiting with symptomatic care, or first-line amoxicillin-clavulanate if antibiotics are chosen, plus saline irrigation and analgesia
Oral corticosteroids alone
stages.explanation: For uncomplicated acute bacterial rhinosinusitis, current guidelines support either watchful waiting with symptomatic care (many cases resolve spontaneously) or, when antibiotics are warranted, first-line amoxicillin-clavulanate, alongside supportive measures such as saline irrigation, analgesics, and intranasal corticosteroids for symptom relief. Respiratory fluoroquinolones are reserved for patients with penicillin allergy or treatment failure, given their adverse-effect profiles, black-box warnings, and resistance concerns, so they are not the immediate choice in this non-allergic patient. Continuous oxymetazoline beyond a few days causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa). Oral corticosteroids alone are not a primary treatment for acute bacterial sinusitis. Thus amoxicillin-clavulanate—not a fluoroquinolone—is the preferred agent when a decision is made to treat, with watchful waiting an equally acceptable alternative.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which new finding would signal a complication requiring urgent imaging and likely hospitalization rather than continued outpatient care?
options: Mild persistent cheek pressure
Yellow nasal discharge
A mild low-grade fever
Periorbital swelling with proptosis and painful or limited eye movements, or altered mental status/severe headache
stages.explanation: Orbital extension (periorbital or orbital cellulitis with proptosis, painful or restricted extraocular movements, or any change in vision) and intracranial spread (severe headache, altered mental status, meningismus, or focal neurologic deficits) are emergencies that require urgent contrast CT or MRI, intravenous antibiotics, and frequently surgical drainage of an abscess. In contrast, mild cheek pressure, colored nasal discharge, and a low-grade fever are expected features of sinusitis itself and do not signal a complication, so they should not trigger escalation on their own. Recognizing the specific eye and neurologic red flags is precisely what separates routine outpatient sinusitis from a potentially vision- or life-threatening complication, and the ethmoid sinuses' proximity to the orbit makes the eye the most common site of dangerous spread.

pearls: Suspect bacterial rhinosinusitis with symptoms ≥10 days without improvement, severe onset ≥3–4 days, or 'double worsening' after initial improvement.
Colored nasal discharge alone does not differentiate viral from bacterial disease; diagnosis is clinical and imaging is reserved for complications.
First-line antibiotic (when treating) is amoxicillin-clavulanate; watchful waiting is acceptable in uncomplicated cases.
Orbital signs (proptosis, painful eye movements) or neurologic changes mandate urgent imaging, IV antibiotics, and surgical consultation.

cases.title: Losing the Sense of Smell With a Constantly Blocked Nose
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of nasal blockage and near-total loss of smell.
cases.history: A 46-year-old man reports four to five months of progressive bilateral nasal obstruction, postnasal drip, and a near-complete loss of smell and taste. He breathes mostly through his mouth and snores. He denies facial pain, fever, or bloody discharge. He has a history of asthma that has been harder to control this year, and he recalls a severe reaction—wheezing and facial flushing—after taking ibuprofen for a headache a few months ago. PMH: asthma, prior 'nasal congestion' episodes. Medications: inhaled corticosteroid/long-acting beta-agonist, albuterol as needed. Allergies: NSAIDs (respiratory reaction). Social: nonsmoker, accountant. Family history: father with asthma. He has used over-the-counter antihistamines and decongestants with minimal benefit. He notes that his asthma and nasal symptoms seem to flare together. The anosmia is the most distressing symptom, as he can no longer smell food or detect smoke. He has had no prior sinus surgery and no recent upper respiratory infection preceding this protracted course.
cases.exam: Mouth-breathing, hyponasal speech. Anterior rhinoscopy and nasal endoscopy reveal multiple pale, glistening, grape-like polypoid masses arising from the middle meatus bilaterally, with mucoid secretions and edematous mucosa; the masses are insensate to gentle touch and do not bleed readily. No purulence, no unilateral mass, and no bloody discharge. Septum midline. Eyes: no proptosis or periorbital swelling; extraocular movements full. Lungs: scattered end-expiratory wheezes. Oropharynx with cobblestoning from postnasal drainage. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Cranial nerves intact, including no visual deficit.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Bilateral pale, insensate, grape-like masses in the middle meatus with anosmia and obstruction are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Unilateral inverted papilloma
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps
Acute viral rhinitis
stages.explanation: Bilateral, pale, glistening, insensate polypoid masses arising from the middle meatus, combined with hyposmia or anosmia and chronic nasal obstruction, define chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, an eosinophilic inflammatory disease of the sinonasal mucosa. Inverted papilloma is by contrast typically unilateral, more vascular and friable, and carries malignant potential, so any unilateral mass warrants biopsy and imaging rather than empiric medical therapy. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma presents with a nasopharyngeal mass, epistaxis, unilateral otitis media with effusion, or a neck node, not with bilateral benign-appearing polyps. Acute viral rhinitis is short-lived and shows erythematous, swollen mucosa rather than chronic grape-like polyps. The bilateral, painless, anosmia-dominant picture in an asthmatic is therefore classic for polypoid chronic rhinosinusitis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given his asthma and the NSAID reaction, which associated condition should you specifically recognize, and what counseling follows?
options: Cystic fibrosis; start pancreatic enzymes
Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (Samter triad: asthma, nasal polyps, NSAID/aspirin sensitivity); counsel strict avoidance of nonselective NSAIDs
Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis; start antifungals
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis; start cyclophosphamide
stages.explanation: Asthma plus nasal polyps plus NSAID/aspirin-induced respiratory reactions is aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease, classically called the Samter triad. Recognizing it matters because nonselective NSAIDs and aspirin can trigger severe, even life-threatening bronchospasm and nasal symptoms, so strict avoidance must be counseled, with formal aspirin desensitization considered in select motivated patients. Cystic fibrosis can cause nasal polyps but presents in childhood with pulmonary and gastrointestinal disease, not first in a 46-year-old. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis causes asthma with central bronchiectasis and markedly elevated IgE and Aspergillus sensitivity, not this specific triad. Granulomatosis with polyangiitis causes destructive sinonasal disease (crusting, septal perforation, saddle-nose deformity) with systemic and renal involvement, not bland bilateral polyps, and its immunosuppressive treatment is unrelated to this benign inflammatory presentation.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Nasal endoscopy: bilateral middle-meatal polyps with eosinophilic-appearing mucin
CT sinuses (coronal): bilateral pansinus mucosal thickening and polypoid opacification, patent skull base, no bony destruction
CBC: eosinophils mildly elevated
Pulmonary function: mild obstructive pattern with bronchodilator response
Serum total IgE: mildly elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms bilateral polypoid pansinus disease without bony destruction. What is the appropriate first-line medical management?
options: Long-term broad-spectrum antibiotics as monotherapy
Intranasal corticosteroids (high-volume) and saline irrigation, with short courses of oral corticosteroids for flares
Immediate radical surgery as the only option
Topical decongestants used continuously
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps is topical, often high-volume, intranasal corticosteroids combined with saline irrigation to deliver anti-inflammatory medication throughout the sinonasal cavity, supplemented by short courses of oral corticosteroids to control significant flares and shrink bulky polyps. Long-term antibiotic monotherapy is not the cornerstone for this predominantly inflammatory, eosinophilic, type 2 disease, since it is not primarily an infection. Functional endoscopic sinus surgery is reserved for medically refractory disease and is not the mandatory first step before optimized medical therapy is tried. Continuous topical decongestants cause rhinitis medicamentosa and worsen obstruction. Modern care increasingly adds biologics targeting type 2 inflammation for refractory cases, but topical corticosteroids plus saline irrigation remain the appropriate first-line foundation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His polyps and symptoms persist despite optimized topical steroids, irrigations, and steroid bursts. What are the appropriate next options?
options: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery and/or a type 2-targeted biologic (e.g., anti-IL-4/13 or anti-IL-5) for refractory disease
Continue the same regimen indefinitely with no change
Lifelong oral corticosteroids
Radiation therapy to the sinuses
stages.explanation: For medically refractory chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, the appropriate next steps are functional endoscopic sinus surgery to physically remove the polyps and open the obstructed sinuses, and/or initiation of a monoclonal antibody targeting type 2 inflammation (such as dupilumab targeting IL-4/13, mepolizumab targeting IL-5, or omalizumab targeting IgE), which reduce polyp burden and the ongoing need for surgery and systemic steroids. Simply repeating an already-ineffective medical regimen unchanged is inappropriate and wastes time. Chronic systemic corticosteroids cause unacceptable cumulative toxicity, including bone, metabolic, and ocular harm, and cannot be used as a long-term strategy. Radiation has no role in this benign inflammatory polyp disease. Surgery and biologics together constitute the guideline-endorsed escalation pathway for refractory disease.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (with aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease/Samter triad)
pearls: Bilateral pale, insensate, grape-like middle-meatal masses with anosmia indicate chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps; unilateral lesions demand biopsy/imaging to exclude neoplasm.
Asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID/aspirin sensitivity = Samter triad (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease); counsel NSAID avoidance.
First-line therapy is intranasal corticosteroids plus saline irrigation, with short oral steroid courses for flares.
Refractory disease is managed with functional endoscopic sinus surgery and/or type 2-targeted biologics, not chronic systemic steroids.

cases.title: Black Crusting in the Nose of a Critically Ill Diabetic
cases.chiefComplaint: Facial pain and a black spot in the nose in a patient admitted for diabetic ketoacidosis.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man admitted to the ICU for diabetic ketoacidosis develops worsening right-sided facial pain, nasal congestion, and a bloody-then-blackish nasal discharge over 24 hours. Nursing notes new right facial numbness and eyelid swelling. He has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (recent A1c 12.4%), frequently missing insulin doses. PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, prior pyelonephritis. Medications (home): metformin and insulin glargine, inconsistently taken; lisinopril. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, lives alone. Family history: diabetes. On admission he was profoundly acidotic and hyperglycemic and is being treated with an insulin drip and fluids. Over several hours he becomes more somnolent and the facial findings progress, with decreased sensation over the cheek and difficulty fully opening the right eye. The team is concerned the rhinologic findings represent something more dangerous than ordinary sinusitis given his immunocompromised, acidotic state.
cases.exam: Ill, lethargic man. Right periorbital edema with mild proptosis and partial ophthalmoplegia; pupil sluggish. Right facial hypoesthesia in V1–V2 distribution. Nasal endoscopy: a black, necrotic-appearing eschar on the right middle turbinate with surrounding pale, insensate mucosa that does not bleed when probed. No bleeding on debridement of the necrotic area (poor mucosal bleeding is an ominous sign of tissue infarction). The hard palate shows a small area of mucosal discoloration. Neck supple. Lungs with tachypnea, clear. Neurologic exam notable for somnolence and the cranial neuropathies above. Remainder limited by clinical status.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A black nasal eschar with insensate, non-bleeding mucosa in a diabetic in DKA with progressing facial/orbital signs is most concerning for which diagnosis?
options: Uncomplicated viral sinusitis
Allergic rhinitis
Acute invasive fungal rhinosinusitis (mucormycosis)
Nasal polyposis
stages.explanation: Acute invasive fungal rhinosinusitis—classically mucormycosis caused by Rhizopus and related Mucorales—presents in immunocompromised or diabetic ketoacidotic/acidotic patients with rapidly progressive facial pain, a black necrotic eschar, insensate and poorly bleeding (infarcted) mucosa, and angioinvasive spread to the orbit and brain that causes proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and cranial neuropathies. The low-pH, iron-rich, hyperglycemic environment of DKA specifically favors fungal proliferation and impairs neutrophil function. Viral sinusitis and allergic rhinitis do not cause tissue necrosis or angioinvasion and would not produce these findings. Nasal polyps are pale and benign without any eschar or infarction. The combination of an acidotic diabetic host, frank tissue necrosis, and orbital extension makes invasive mucormycosis the urgent diagnosis to confirm and treat without delay.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step to confirm the diagnosis without delaying care?
options: Wait for fungal blood cultures before any action
Urgent endoscopic biopsy of the necrotic tissue for frozen-section histopathology (looking for broad, non-septate, right-angle-branching hyphae) plus fungal stains/culture, with concurrent imaging
Treat empirically with antibacterials only and observe
Obtain allergy testing
stages.explanation: Confirmation is by urgent biopsy of the necrotic tissue with frozen-section histopathology, which demonstrates the characteristic broad, ribbon-like, pauciseptate (sparsely septated) hyphae branching at wide (~90°) angles and invading blood vessels; fungal stains, cultures, and cross-sectional imaging (CT and MRI to map orbital, sinus, cavernous sinus, and intracranial involvement) proceed in parallel rather than sequentially. Crucially, antifungal therapy and surgical debridement should not await culture results, which are slow and frequently negative even in true disease. Blood cultures are usually unhelpful for Mucorales because the organism is angioinvasive locally rather than reliably fungemic. Antibacterials alone miss the fungus entirely and waste critical time. Allergy testing is completely irrelevant in this emergent, life-threatening angioinvasive infection and would dangerously delay definitive care.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Glucose 392 mg/dL
Bicarbonate 12 mEq/L, anion gap elevated; beta-hydroxybutyrate elevated (DKA)
WBC 15.6 ×10³/µL
Frozen section: broad, pauciseptate hyphae with wide-angle branching, vascular invasion and tissue necrosis
CT sinus/orbit: right maxillary/ethmoid opacification with bony erosion and infiltration of orbital fat
MRI brain/orbit: enhancement extending to the orbital apex; early cavernous sinus involvement

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Frozen section confirms invasive mucormycosis with orbital extension. What is the cornerstone of management?
options: Oral fluconazole and discharge
Emergent wide surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue PLUS systemic liposomal amphotericin B AND aggressive correction of the underlying DKA/hyperglycemia
Systemic corticosteroids to reduce orbital swelling
Antibacterials alone with delayed surgery
stages.explanation: Survival depends on a three-pronged emergent approach delivered simultaneously: aggressive surgical debridement of all devitalized tissue (repeated as often as necessary because the infection keeps advancing), systemic antifungal therapy with liposomal amphotericin B as the first-line agent (with isavuconazole or posaconazole as step-down or alternative agents), and reversal of the predisposing condition by correcting the acidosis and hyperglycemia and minimizing any immunosuppression. Fluconazole has essentially no meaningful activity against Mucorales and would be a critical error. Corticosteroids worsen immunosuppression and are contraindicated as treatment here. Antibacterials do not treat the fungus at all, and any delay in surgery allows lethal angioinvasive spread to the orbit and brain. Source control plus amphotericin plus metabolic correction together form the lifesaving triad.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite treatment, his ophthalmoplegia and somnolence worsen and MRI shows progression toward the cavernous sinus and orbital apex. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Switch to oral fluconazole and continue current surgical plan unchanged

Repeat aggressive surgical debridement (including orbital exenteration if the orbit is non-salvageable), continue/optimize liposomal amphotericin B, and intensify metabolic and multidisciplinary critical care
Stop antifungals because the infection has spread beyond cure
Begin high-dose corticosteroids to reduce the orbital and cavernous sinus inflammation
stages.explanation: Progressive angioinvasive mucormycosis demands re-operation: repeat debridement of all newly necrotic tissue, and orbital exenteration when the orbit is non-salvageable, in order to halt spread toward the cavernous sinus and brain, while continuing and optimizing liposomal amphotericin B and tightening metabolic control under full ICU and multidisciplinary support. Switching to oral fluconazole is wrong because Mucorales are intrinsically resistant to it and disease would continue unchecked. Abandoning antifungals concedes a potentially survivable infection; aggressive surgical and medical escalation, not therapeutic nihilism, is what is indicated even when the disease is advancing. Corticosteroids would deepen the immunosuppression and are contraindicated, since the worsening here represents ongoing angioinvasive infection rather than a steroid-responsive inflammatory process. Timely, coordinated escalation offers the only realistic chance of survival and orbital salvage.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What overarching principle most determines this patient's prognosis and ongoing care?
options: Mucormycosis is self-limited; supportive care suffices
Speed and completeness of debridement, sustained antifungal therapy, and durable control of the immunocompromising state (glycemic control); ongoing multidisciplinary monitoring for progression
A single dose of antifungal cures it
Outpatient oral antifungals are adequate from the start
stages.explanation: Prognosis in invasive mucormycosis hinges on early and complete surgical debridement, sustained systemic antifungal therapy, and durable correction of the underlying immunocompromise—here meaning tight glycemic and acid-base control to reverse the DKA—coordinated across ENT, infectious disease, endocrinology, ophthalmology, and neurosurgery, with serial reassessment because the infection can progress rapidly and frequently requires repeat trips to the operating room. The disease is fulminant and frequently fatal if undertreated: it is never self-limited, is not cured by a single antifungal dose, and cannot be managed with outpatient oral therapy from the outset. Aggressive, multidisciplinary, and sustained care—rather than any single intervention—is ultimately what saves lives and salvages orbits in these critically ill patients, and survival improves markedly the earlier all three arms are instituted.

pearls: Suspect invasive mucormycosis in diabetics in DKA or immunocompromised patients with facial pain and a black necrotic, insensate, non-bleeding nasal eschar.
Angioinvasion causes orbital/cavernous-sinus extension (proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, cranial neuropathies); biopsy shows broad pauciseptate hyphae branching at wide angles.
Do not wait for cultures—start treatment immediately based on frozen section and clinical suspicion.
Management is the triad of emergent surgical debridement, liposomal amphotericin B, and correction of the underlying condition (acidosis/hyperglycemia).

cases.title: Stubborn Nasal Congestion With Thick, Tenacious Mucus
cases.chiefComplaint: Long-standing nasal obstruction with thick mucus and facial fullness in a young allergic patient.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman with a history of asthma and allergic rhinitis presents with more than a year of bilateral nasal obstruction, thick tenacious nasal discharge, and a sense of facial pressure and fullness. She reports recurrent 'sinus infections' partially treated with multiple antibiotic courses without lasting improvement. She denies black eschar, severe acute pain, visual changes, or systemic toxicity. PMH: atopic asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema. Medications: inhaled corticosteroid, cetirizine, intranasal steroid used intermittently. Allergies: dust mite and mold sensitization on prior testing. Social: nonsmoker, teacher. Family history: atopy. She is immunocompetent and not diabetic. She notes the discharge is sometimes described by clinicians as thick and 'peanut-buttery,' and a prior outside CT reportedly showed 'opacified sinuses with bright material.' She has had progressive but painless facial changes, with a sense that one cheek looks slightly fuller. The course has been chronic and indolent rather than fulminant.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, immunocompetent young woman. Nasal endoscopy: edematous mucosa with polypoid change and thick, inspissated, eosinophilic-appearing mucin (greasy, tan-brown 'peanut-butter' character) filling the middle meatus bilaterally; no black necrotic eschar and the mucosa bleeds normally when touched (no tissue infarction). Mild outward bowing/fullness of the left medial canthal region without acute inflammation. No proptosis at rest, extraocular movements full, vision intact. No facial numbness. Oropharynx with postnasal mucus. Lungs with occasional wheeze. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No signs of systemic toxicity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An atopic, immunocompetent young woman with chronic polypoid sinusitis, thick eosinophilic 'peanut-butter' mucin, and expansile sinus changes—without necrosis—is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Acute invasive fungal sinusitis (mucormycosis)
Bacterial maxillary sinusitis
Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis
Squamous cell carcinoma of the sinus
stages.explanation: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis occurs in immunocompetent, atopic patients and features chronic nasal polyposis with characteristic thick, eosinophilic 'allergic (peanut-butter) mucin,' often with unilateral or asymmetric, expansile opacification that can remodel bone. The host is immunocompetent and there is no tissue invasion or necrosis—contrasting sharply with invasive mucormycosis, which strikes immunocompromised/DKA hosts with black eschar and angioinvasion. Bacterial sinusitis is more acute and lacks eosinophilic mucin and expansile remodeling. Sinonasal squamous cell carcinoma can erode bone but presents with a friable mass, bleeding, and often pain/cranial neuropathy rather than allergic eosinophilic mucin. The atopic host plus non-invasive expansile eosinophilic mucin fits allergic fungal disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which combination of findings best supports the diagnosis (the diagnostic criteria) of allergic fungal rhinosinusitis?
options: Tissue invasion by hyphae on biopsy with vascular thrombosis
Type I hypersensitivity (atopy), nasal polyposis, characteristic CT findings, eosinophilic mucin with non-invasive fungal hyphae, and positive fungal stain—without tissue invasion
Positive bacterial culture only
Negative allergy testing with normal sinuses
stages.explanation: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis is supported by the Bent and Kuhn-type criteria: type I hypersensitivity/atopy, nasal polyposis, characteristic CT findings (heterogeneous hyperattenuation/expansion), eosinophilic 'allergic mucin' containing fungal hyphae on stain but without tissue invasion, and a positive fungal stain/culture. The hallmark is fungus and eosinophilic mucin WITHOUT invasion of tissue—an allergic/hypersensitivity response, not an infection of the tissue itself. True tissue invasion with vascular thrombosis defines invasive fungal disease, a different entity. A positive bacterial culture indicates bacterial infection, and negative allergy testing with normal sinuses excludes this atopic, polypoid process.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
CT sinuses: heterogeneous, hyperattenuating material expanding the left ethmoid/maxillary sinuses with bony remodeling/thinning and mild medial orbital wall bowing; no aggressive bony destruction
MRI: central signal void within the inspissated mucin with peripheral mucosal enhancement
Histopathology: eosinophilic 'allergic' mucin with Charcot-Leyden crystals and scattered non-invasive fungal hyphae; NO tissue/vascular invasion
Total serum IgE: markedly elevated
Fungal stain: positive for hyphae within mucin

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Pathology confirms non-invasive eosinophilic mucin with hyphae. What is the cornerstone of treatment for allergic fungal rhinosinusitis?
options: Systemic liposomal amphotericin B and radical orbital exenteration
Endoscopic sinus surgery to remove all allergic mucin/polyps and open the sinuses, followed by topical (and tapering systemic) corticosteroids and ongoing medical/allergy management
A single short antibiotic course alone
Observation with no treatment
stages.explanation: Treatment of allergic fungal rhinosinusitis is functional endoscopic sinus surgery to evacuate the allergic mucin and polyps and re-establish sinus drainage, combined with postoperative corticosteroids (topical, with a tapering systemic course for refractory/extensive disease) and long-term medical and allergy management (saline irrigation, control of atopy; immunotherapy and adjunctive antifungals are debated). Because the disease is non-invasive and the host is immunocompetent, the aggressive regimen for invasive mucormycosis (systemic amphotericin, exenteration) is inappropriate and overtreats this allergic process. A single antibiotic course does not address the fungal/eosinophilic immunology, and observation allows progressive expansion with bony remodeling and orbital encroachment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her preoperative CT shows the expansile mucin bowing and thinning the medial orbital wall with mild proptosis. What does this signify, and how does it change management priorities?
options: It means the disease has become invasive mucormycosis and requires amphotericin

It reflects bony remodeling/expansion from chronic pressure (mass effect), not tissue invasion; it raises the urgency of surgical clearance to relieve orbital compression and preserve vision, while remaining a non-invasive allergic process
It indicates malignant transformation requiring biopsy for carcinoma and radiation
It is an incidental finding that requires no change in plan or timing
stages.explanation: In allergic fungal rhinosinusitis the inspissated eosinophilic mucin can expand sinuses and remodel/thin bone, producing proptosis or visual compromise by pressure (mass effect), not by tissue invasion—so this finding signifies expansile, compressive disease and heightens the urgency of endoscopic surgical clearance to decompress the orbit and protect vision, while the disease remains non-invasive in an immunocompetent host. It is not a conversion to invasive mucormycosis (which occurs in immunocompromised/DKA hosts with necrosis and angioinvasion), so amphotericin is not indicated. It is benign allergic disease, not carcinoma, so biopsy-for-malignancy and radiation are wrong. Orbital encroachment is never merely incidental—it accelerates, rather than leaves unchanged, the surgical plan.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After surgery she does well but is at high risk for what outcome, and what reduces it?
options: Cure is permanent; no follow-up needed
Recurrence is common; reduce it with diligent postoperative corticosteroid/anti-inflammatory therapy, saline irrigation, allergy control, and regular endoscopic surveillance
Conversion to invasive mucormycosis is expected; start lifelong amphotericin
Malignant transformation is certain; plan radiation
stages.explanation: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis has a high recurrence rate, so long-term control depends on diligent postoperative anti-inflammatory therapy (topical corticosteroids, sometimes systemic tapers for flares), saline irrigation, optimization of the underlying atopy/allergy (with immunotherapy and adjunctive measures in selected cases), and regular endoscopic surveillance to detect and treat recurrent mucin/polyps early. It does not 'cure' with surgery alone, so dismissing follow-up is wrong. In immunocompetent hosts it does not convert to invasive mucormycosis, so lifelong amphotericin is unwarranted. It is a benign allergic process, not a malignancy, so radiation is inappropriate.

pearls: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis affects atopic, immunocompetent patients with nasal polyps and thick eosinophilic 'peanut-butter' allergic mucin—no tissue invasion.

CT shows heterogeneous hyperattenuating, expansile sinus contents that may remodel bone; MRI often shows a central signal void; pathology shows eosinophilic mucin with Charcot-Leyden crystals and non-invasive hyphae.
Distinguish sharply from invasive mucormycosis (immunocompromised host, necrosis, angioinvasion) which needs amphotericin and aggressive debridement.
Treat with endoscopic surgery to clear mucin/polyps plus postoperative corticosteroids and allergy control; recurrence is common, so surveillance is essential.

cases.title: Recurrent Nosebleeds in a Teenager
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent nosebleeds, with another episode that started 30 minutes ago and would not stop at home.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother for a nosebleed that began spontaneously while he was watching television and has continued for about 30 minutes despite tilting his head back. He reports several similar episodes over the past two months, usually from the right nostril, often after blowing his nose or during dry winter weather. He admits to frequent nose-picking and describes a chronically dry, crusty right nostril. He uses no nasal sprays. There is no history of easy bruising, gum bleeding, prolonged bleeding after dental work, or bloody stools. He takes no medications, denies aspirin or NSAID use, and has no known allergies. He has no chronic illnesses. Family history is negative for bleeding disorders; no relatives require transfusions. He plays soccer, denies tobacco, alcohol, or intranasal drug use, and reports no recent facial trauma. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no fevers, weight loss, or unilateral facial pain.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress, sitting upright with blood-stained tissues. There is active oozing of bright red blood from the anteroinferior right nasal septum. Anterior rhinoscopy after gentle suction reveals a prominent, friable vascular plexus on the anterior cartilaginous septum with adjacent excoriation and crusting; no posterior pooling is seen in the oropharynx. The nasal mucosa is otherwise pink. There is no septal mass, no skin telangiectasias on the lips or fingers, and no petechiae or ecchymoses on the trunk or extremities. The remainder of the head and neck exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in managing this actively bleeding patient?
options: Have him lean forward and pinch the soft cartilaginous part of the nose continuously for 10-15 minutes
Lay him supine with his head tilted back to slow the bleeding
Immediately place a posterior nasal balloon catheter
Order a CT angiogram of the face to localize the bleeding vessel
stages.explanation: Sustained firm pressure over the anterior septum, with the patient sitting up and leaning forward, is the correct first-aid maneuver: pinching the soft lower nose compresses Kiesselbach's plexus, and leaning forward prevents blood from being swallowed or aspirated. Tilting the head back is a common error—it causes swallowed blood, nausea, and aspiration risk without controlling bleeding. A posterior balloon is reserved for posterior bleeds refractory to anterior measures, not a stable anterior bleed. CT angiography is unnecessary for a typical anterior nosebleed and exposes a child to radiation without changing initial management.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: From which anatomic site is this anterior epistaxis most likely originating?
options: Sphenopalatine artery in the posterior nasal cavity
Woodruff's plexus on the lateral nasopharyngeal wall
Ethmoidal arteries at the skull base
Kiesselbach's plexus (Little's area) on the anterior septum
stages.explanation: The vast majority of anterior nosebleeds—especially in children—arise from Kiesselbach's plexus in Little's area, an anastomotic network on the anteroinferior septum fed by the anterior ethmoidal, sphenopalatine, greater palatine, and superior labial arteries. Its superficial location makes it vulnerable to digital trauma and dry air, fitting this boy's picture. The sphenopalatine artery and Woodruff's plexus are posterior sources causing brisk, harder-to-control bleeds usually in older adults. The ethmoidal arteries are deeper skull-base vessels implicated in trauma or surgery, not typical recurrent pediatric anterior epistaxis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL
Platelets 265 ×10³/µL
PT 12.5 s
INR 1.0
aPTT 30 s
Bleeding workup unremarkable; no coagulopathy identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Direct pressure for 15 minutes slows but does not fully stop the oozing, and a discrete bleeding point is visible on the anterior septum. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Cauterize both sides of the septum at the same site to ensure hemostasis
Apply a topical vasoconstrictor (oxymetazoline) and then perform silver nitrate chemical cautery of the visible vessel
Place bilateral anterior nasal packing and discharge without further treatment
Transfuse packed red blood cells
stages.explanation: For a localized anterior bleeding point that is visible after pressure, topical decongestant to vasoconstrict followed by focal silver nitrate cautery is the standard next step; the decongestant often slows bleeding enough to allow precise cautery. Cauterizing both sides of the septum at the same level risks septal perforation from devascularizing both mucosal surfaces and should be avoided. Packing alone is reasonable when no point is seen, but cautery is preferred when a discrete vessel is identified. Transfusion is unwarranted—he is hemodynamically stable with normal vitals and no signs of significant blood loss.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful cautery, what is the most appropriate discharge counseling to prevent recurrence?
options: Start daily aspirin to keep the vessels healthy
Begin a 10-day course of oral antibiotics
Apply petrolatum or saline nasal gel to the septum, humidify the air, and avoid nose-picking and forceful blowing
Avoid all physical activity for one month
stages.explanation: Recurrent anterior epistaxis in children is largely preventable with mucosal hydration—topical petrolatum or saline gel, a bedroom humidifier, and behavioral measures to stop digital trauma and forceful nose-blowing. These directly address the dry, excoriated mucosa over Kiesselbach's plexus. Aspirin is contraindicated as it impairs platelet function and would worsen bleeding (and risks Reye syndrome in children). Routine oral antibiotics are not indicated for simple anterior epistaxis or after silver nitrate cautery. Blanket activity restriction is excessive and unnecessary; normal play is fine once bleeding is controlled.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent anterior epistaxis from Kiesselbach's plexus (Little's area)
pearls: Anterior epistaxis is the most common type and usually arises from Kiesselbach's plexus in Little's area on the anteroinferior septum.
First aid: sit up, lean forward, and pinch the soft part of the nose for 10-15 minutes—never tilt the head back.
Treat a visible anterior bleeding point with topical vasoconstrictor then focal silver nitrate cautery; avoid cauterizing both sides of the septum to prevent perforation.
Prevent recurrence with mucosal moisturization (saline/petrolatum), humidification, and stopping nose-picking.

cases.title: Heavy Nosebleed in an Older Man on Blood Thinners
cases.chiefComplaint: Profuse nosebleed for over an hour that keeps running down the back of his throat.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with a brisk nosebleed that began spontaneously 75 minutes ago. He has been spitting and swallowing large amounts of blood and feels it dripping down the back of his throat despite pinching his nose. He has hypertension and atrial fibrillation, for which he takes apixaban 5 mg twice daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He took his apixaban this morning. He denies trauma, nasal sprays, recent nasal surgery, and intranasal drug use. He has no prior major bleeding and no known bleeding disorder. He reports mild lightheadedness when standing. He has no chest pain or dyspnea. Allergies: none. Social history: former 30-pack-year smoker, quit 10 years ago; occasional alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. On further questioning, he notes his home blood pressure has been poorly controlled recently because he ran out of pills last week. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no melena reported.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious and pale, with blood-stained clothing. Anterior rhinoscopy after suction and topical decongestant shows no discrete anterior septal bleeding source, but blood is seen welling from deep within the nasal cavity. Examination of the oropharynx reveals a steady stream of fresh blood trickling down the posterior pharyngeal wall. There is no anterior septal mass or telangiectasia. The remainder of the head and neck exam is limited by ongoing bleeding but shows no facial deformity or trauma. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm; lungs are clear. There is no peripheral edema and no abdominal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Along with applying a topical vasoconstrictor and attempting anterior packing, what immediate intervention is most appropriate?
options: Establish IV access, send a type and screen, CBC, and coagulation studies, and apply direct anterior measures while monitoring hemodynamics
Aggressively lower the blood pressure to normal with IV nitroprusside before doing anything else
Give a unit of platelets empirically
Discharge with outpatient ENT follow-up given that nosebleeds are usually benign
stages.explanation: A brisk nosebleed in an elderly anticoagulated patient with hypertension and tachycardia can cause significant blood loss, so resuscitative groundwork—IV access, CBC, type and screen, and coagulation panel—must accompany local control and continuous monitoring. Abruptly normalizing BP with nitroprusside risks hypoperfusion and is not the immediate priority; gentle BP control comes after volume status is addressed. Empiric platelets are not indicated—his defect is factor Xa inhibition, not thrombocytopenia. Discharge is unsafe given active brisk bleeding, anticoagulation, and unstable vitals.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL
Hematocrit 33%
Platelets 210 ×10³/µL
INR 1.2
aPTT 32 s
BUN 24 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Anterior nasal pack placed bilaterally; blood continues to drip down the posterior pharynx

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Despite well-placed bilateral anterior packing, blood continues to stream down the posterior pharynx. What is the most likely source of bleeding?
options: Anterior ethmoidal artery on the septum
Kiesselbach's plexus that was missed
Bleeding gastric ulcer producing hematemesis
Posterior bleed from the sphenopalatine artery / Woodruff's plexus region
stages.explanation: Bleeding that persists down the posterior pharynx after adequate bilateral anterior packing indicates a posterior epistaxis, typically from the sphenopalatine artery and the Woodruff's plexus area; this pattern is classic in older, hypertensive, anticoagulated patients. A septal anterior ethmoidal source or missed Kiesselbach's plexus would be controlled by anterior packing and would bleed anteriorly. A gastric ulcer causes hematemesis and melena, not blood actively descending from the nasopharynx with a clear nasal source, and does not fit the local exam findings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a posterior bleed failing anterior packing, what is the most appropriate next step to achieve hemostasis?
options: Remove all packing and observe
Immediately take the patient for emergency external carotid artery ligation
Place a posterior nasal pack (balloon catheter or gauze pack) in addition to anterior packing, then admit for monitoring
Repeat the same anterior pack with more gauze
stages.explanation: For posterior epistaxis refractory to anterior measures, the next step is a posterior pack—an inflatable balloon catheter or traditional posterior gauze pack—combined with anterior packing to tamponade the nasopharyngeal source; these patients require admission and monitoring. Removing packing would allow exsanguinating bleeding. Emergency carotid ligation is far too aggressive as an initial maneuver; if packing fails, the escalation is endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation or embolization, not external carotid ligation. Simply adding more anterior gauze will not reach or control a posterior source.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After the posterior pack controls bleeding and the patient is admitted, which complication of posterior packing most warrants continuous cardiopulmonary monitoring?
options: Permanent anosmia
Hypoxia and bradycardia/hypotension from the nasopulmonary (nasovagal) reflex, plus airway compromise and pressure necrosis
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Hyperthyroid crisis
stages.explanation: Posterior packs can trigger the nasopulmonary reflex causing hypoventilation, hypoxia, and bradycardia/hypotension, and can compromise the airway or cause pressure necrosis of the septum/columella—hence admission with continuous pulse oximetry and cardiac monitoring. Anosmia is not a recognized complication of posterior packing. Acute angle-closure glaucoma and thyroid storm are unrelated to nasal packing and have no mechanistic link to this scenario. Managing his anticoagulation and elevated BP also remains important during admission.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior epistaxis on apixaban, controlled with posterior nasal packing
pearls: Bleeding that continues down the posterior pharynx despite good bilateral anterior packing signals a posterior source (sphenopalatine artery / Woodruff's plexus).
Posterior epistaxis is more common in older, hypertensive, anticoagulated patients and can cause significant blood loss—resuscitate and monitor.
Posterior packs require admission with continuous monitoring because of the nasopulmonary reflex (hypoxia, bradycardia) and airway/pressure-necrosis risks.
If packing fails, escalate to endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation or angioembolization rather than blind external carotid ligation.

cases.title: One-Sided Stuffy Nose After an Old Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent difficulty breathing through the left side of his nose for years.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man presents to the otolaryngology clinic with chronic left-sided nasal obstruction that has bothered him for several years and worsened gradually. He breathes well through the right nostril but feels the left is 'always blocked,' especially when lying on his left side or exercising. He recalls being struck in the nose during a recreational hockey game in his early twenties, after which his nose looked slightly crooked; he never sought care. He denies facial pain, anosmia, nosebleeds, clear rhinorrhea, or snoring with witnessed apneas. He has tried over-the-counter oxymetazoline spray with only transient relief and worries about overusing it. He has no history of allergies, asthma, or sinus infections, and seasonal symptoms are absent. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no regular medications. He has no drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, social alcohol, works as an accountant. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, epistaxis, and unilateral facial numbness.
cases.exam: External nasal exam shows a subtle dorsal deviation of the nose toward the left. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals the cartilaginous and bony septum deviated convexly into the left nasal cavity, markedly narrowing the left airway, with a compensatory enlargement of the right inferior turbinate. The mucosa is pink and non-edematous, with no purulent discharge, polyps, or masses. After topical decongestant, the left airway improves modestly at the level of the turbinate but the bony septal deviation persists. There is no septal perforation, no boggy swelling, and no tenderness. The oropharynx and neck exam are unremarkable, with no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What finding best explains why this patient's obstruction is unilateral and only partially relieved by decongestant?
options: Bilateral allergic turbinate hypertrophy
Nasal polyposis filling the left nostril
Acute bacterial sinusitis
A fixed structural deviation of the nasal septum into the left cavity
stages.explanation: A fixed bony/cartilaginous septal deviation produces a static, unilateral narrowing that a decongestant (which only shrinks vascular mucosa/turbinates) cannot relieve—matching this man's history of trauma, a crooked nose, and persistent left obstruction. Bilateral allergic turbinate hypertrophy typically causes alternating or bilateral congestion and responds better to decongestant/steroids. Polyposis would show grayish translucent masses on exam, which are absent. Acute bacterial sinusitis presents with purulence, facial pain, and a shorter, often febrile course—none of which are present here.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before considering surgery, what is the most appropriate initial management for his nasal obstruction?
options: A trial of intranasal corticosteroid spray (and saline) to treat any reversible mucosal component, with reassessment
Proceed directly to septoplasty at this visit
Long-term daily oxymetazoline to keep the airway open
Start broad-spectrum oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: Even with a structural deviation, an intranasal corticosteroid (with saline irrigation) trial addresses any reversible mucosal/turbinate contribution and is the appropriate first step before surgery; many patients improve enough to defer an operation. Going straight to septoplasty skips reversible causes and conservative therapy. Chronic daily oxymetazoline causes rhinitis medicamentosa and rebound congestion and must be avoided. Antibiotics are inappropriate—there is no infection; symptoms are mechanical, not infectious.

stages.label: CLINIC FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
Nasal endoscopy: persistent left bony-cartilaginous septal deviation
Mucosa pink, no polyps or purulence after 8-week intranasal steroid trial
Peak nasal inspiratory flow reduced on the left
No septal perforation; no masses

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He returns after 8 weeks of intranasal steroids and saline with persistent, bothersome left-sided obstruction. He asks about septoplasty. Which statement best describes appropriate septoplasty candidacy?
options: Septoplasty is contraindicated in anyone with prior nasal trauma
He should only have a cosmetic rhinoplasty and leave the septum alone
Septoplasty is indicated because he has a symptomatic septal deviation refractory to medical therapy that correlates with his exam
Septoplasty is purely cosmetic and not covered for functional complaints
stages.explanation: The ideal septoplasty candidate has a symptomatic deviated septum that correlates with exam findings and has failed an adequate trial of medical therapy—exactly this patient.
