Prior trauma is not a contraindication; in fact, post-traumatic deviation is a common indication. Isolated cosmetic rhinoplasty would not address his functional airway obstruction from the septum. Septoplasty for symptomatic obstruction is a functional, not purely cosmetic, procedure and is a recognized medical indication; cosmetic concerns can be addressed concurrently via septorhinoplasty if desired.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: When counseling him about septoplasty risks, which complication should be specifically mentioned because it can cause a saddle-nose deformity or whistling?
options: Permanent loss of taste
Septal perforation
Facial nerve palsy
Cervical spine injury
stages.explanation: Septal perforation—from bilateral opposing mucosal tears or overaggressive cartilage removal—is a key septoplasty risk and can cause crusting, whistling, bleeding, and, with loss of dorsal/caudal support, a saddle-nose deformity. Permanent loss of taste is not a typical septoplasty complication. Facial nerve palsy is unrelated—the facial nerve is not in the surgical field of a septoplasty. Cervical spine injury is not a recognized complication of intranasal septal surgery. Other counseled risks include bleeding, infection, persistent obstruction, and the small chance of needing revision.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic post-traumatic nasal septal deviation, septoplasty candidate after failed medical therapy
pearls: A fixed septal deviation causes unilateral obstruction that does not fully resolve with decongestants, unlike reversible mucosal/turbinate causes.
Trial intranasal corticosteroids and saline first; reserve septoplasty for symptomatic deviation refractory to medical therapy that correlates with exam.
Contralateral compensatory inferior turbinate hypertrophy often accompanies a deviated septum.
Counsel on septoplasty risks including septal perforation (crusting, whistling, possible saddle-nose), bleeding, and need for revision.

cases.title: Swollen, Tender Nose After a Soccer Collision
cases.chiefComplaint: Nasal pain and worsening stuffiness after being hit in the nose during a soccer game earlier today.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the ED after colliding head-to-head with another player during a soccer game about 4 hours ago. He had a brief nosebleed at the time that stopped on its own. Since then his nose has become increasingly painful, swollen, and progressively more blocked on both sides, and he says he 'can't breathe through his nose at all' now. There was no loss of consciousness, no vomiting, no clear nasal drainage, and no vision changes. He has no headache or neck pain. Past medical history is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He has no bleeding history and no prior nasal injuries or surgery. Social history: lives with parents, attends fourth grade, very active in sports. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems, he denies fever, ear pain, and double vision. His mother is worried because the swelling 'seems to be getting worse, not better,' since the injury.
cases.exam: There is mild external nasal swelling and tenderness without gross deformity or crepitus; the bony nasal pyramid feels stable and midline. There is no clear rhinorrhea and no halo sign on filter paper. Anterior rhinoscopy after suction reveals bilateral bluish, boggy, fluctuant swellings of the nasal septum that bulge into both nasal cavities and nearly occlude the airway; gentle palpation with a cotton applicator shows the swelling is soft and compressible rather than firm cartilage. There is no active bleeding. The eyes show no periorbital ecchymosis, and extraocular movements are intact. The remainder of the head and neck exam, including the ears and oropharynx, is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What does the bilateral bluish, boggy, fluctuant swelling of the septum most likely represent?
options: A nasal septal hematoma
Simple nasal mucosal edema from the trauma
A deviated nasal septum
A nasal polyp
stages.explanation: A boggy, fluctuant, often bluish bilateral swelling of the septum after nasal trauma is the classic appearance of a septal hematoma—blood collecting between the perichondrium and the cartilage. Simple mucosal edema is diffuse and firm, not a compressible discrete septal collection bulging bilaterally. A deviated septum is a fixed, firm cartilage/bone displacement, not soft or fluctuant. A nasal polyp is a pale, translucent, painless mucosal growth unrelated to acute trauma and would not appear acutely after a collision with progressive swelling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why does this finding require urgent intervention rather than observation?
options: It will resolve only if antihistamines are given promptly
It indicates an underlying skull fracture requiring neurosurgery
It is contagious to household contacts
Untreated, it can devascularize and infect the septal cartilage, leading to cartilage necrosis and saddle-nose deformity
stages.explanation: A septal hematoma strips the perichondrium from the septal cartilage, cutting off its blood supply; if not drained promptly, avascular necrosis and/or abscess formation ensues within days, destroying the cartilage and producing a saddle-nose deformity (and rarely intracranial infection). Antihistamines have no role—this is a blood collection, not an allergic process. It does not itself indicate a skull fracture needing neurosurgery; the concern is local cartilage loss. It is not contagious. Time-sensitive drainage is what prevents the dreaded cosmetic and structural complications.

stages.label: BEDSIDE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Bedside inspection: bilateral fluctuant bluish septal swelling, soft on palpation
No CSF rhinorrhea (negative halo/beta-2 transferrin not indicated)
CBC: Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, Platelets 290 ×10³/µL
No nasal bony step-off or instability on palpation

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management in the ED?
options: Prescribe oral antibiotics and arrange ENT follow-up in 2 weeks
Incision and drainage of the hematoma with packing/quilting to prevent reaccumulation, plus antibiotic coverage
Apply ice and discharge with reassurance that it will reabsorb
Obtain an MRI of the brain before doing anything
stages.explanation: A septal hematoma must be incised and drained promptly to evacuate the collection and restore perichondrial apposition to the cartilage; the cavity is then obliterated with packing or quilting sutures to prevent reaccumulation, and antibiotics (covering S. aureus) are given because of the abscess/toxic-shock risk from packing. Delayed follow-up with antibiotics alone risks cartilage necrosis. Ice and reassurance is inadequate—these do not reliably reabsorb. An MRI is unnecessary and dangerously delays drainage; the diagnosis is clinical.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful drainage and packing, what is the most appropriate follow-up plan?
options: No follow-up needed once drained
Routine follow-up in 6 months
Close ENT follow-up within 24-72 hours to check for reaccumulation, infection, and to manage packing, with return precautions
Refer only to plastic surgery for cosmetic concerns
stages.explanation: Even after drainage, septal hematomas can reaccumulate or progress to abscess, so close ENT reevaluation within a few days is essential to inspect the septum, manage or remove packing, and confirm there is no recollection or infection; parents must be given return precautions for fever, worsening pain, or swelling. No follow-up is unsafe given the reaccumulation and abscess risk. A 6-month interval is far too long to catch early complications. Referring solely for cosmetic concerns ignores the urgent need to monitor cartilage viability and infection in the acute phase.

pearls: Always inspect the septum in any nasal trauma—a boggy, fluctuant septal swelling is a hematoma, an emergency.
Undrained septal hematoma causes cartilage avascular necrosis/abscess and saddle-nose deformity within days.
Treatment is prompt incision and drainage with packing/quilting to prevent reaccumulation, plus anti-staphylococcal antibiotics.
Arrange close (24-72 hour) ENT follow-up to detect reaccumulation or infection.

cases.title: Crooked, Painful Nose After a Fight
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful, visibly crooked nose after being punched in the face last night.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man presents to the ED about 12 hours after being punched in the nose during an altercation outside a bar. He had immediate epistaxis that has since stopped and notes his nose now looks deviated to one side. He complains of pain, swelling, and difficulty breathing through the nose, which he attributes to swelling. He denies loss of consciousness, vision changes, double vision, clear nasal drainage, malocclusion, or numbness of the cheek or upper lip. He has no neck pain. Past medical history is unremarkable; no prior nasal fractures or surgery. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. Social history: drinks alcohol on weekends, smokes occasionally, denies illicit drug use; he is a college student. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he reports only nasal symptoms and denies headache, ear bleeding, or jaw pain. He is concerned both about the appearance of his nose and whether it will need to be 'reset.'
cases.exam: There is moderate swelling and ecchymosis over the nasal dorsum with tenderness and palpable crepitus/mobility of the nasal bones; the nose is deviated to the right. There is dried blood at both nares but no active bleeding. Anterior rhinoscopy after suction shows a midline septum without boggy swelling, fluctuance, or bluish discoloration. There is no clear rhinorrhea and no halo sign. The eyes have normal extraocular movements without diplopia, no enophthalmos, and intact infraorbital sensation; there is no step-off of the orbital rims. The maxillary occlusion is intact and the midface is stable. The remainder of the head and neck exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination step is most critical to perform before focusing on the nasal bone fracture itself?
options: Carefully inspect the nasal septum to exclude a septal hematoma
Order a CT of the facial bones for every nasal injury
Obtain plain nasal bone X-rays to confirm the fracture
Test smell with scratch-and-sniff cards
stages.explanation: In any nasal trauma, the most important immediate step is to inspect the septum for a hematoma, because an undrained septal hematoma causes cartilage necrosis and saddle-nose deformity and is a true emergency. Routine CT is not needed for isolated nasal fractures—it is reserved for suspected additional facial/orbital fractures or other concerns. Plain nasal X-rays are notoriously unreliable and rarely change management; diagnosis is clinical. Formal smell testing is not relevant to acute nasal fracture triage and would not alter management.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The septum is midline without hematoma. How is an isolated nasal bone fracture best diagnosed in this setting?
options: Only by MRI of the face
Only after a confirmatory plain radiograph
By measuring serum inflammatory markers
Clinically, based on history and exam (deformity, crepitus, mobility, tenderness)
stages.explanation: An isolated nasal bone fracture is a clinical diagnosis—history of trauma plus deformity, crepitus, mobility, and focal tenderness are sufficient, and imaging is generally unnecessary. MRI has no role for acute bony nasal injury. Plain radiographs have poor sensitivity/specificity (old fracture lines, nasal suture lines) and do not reliably confirm or change management, so they are not required. Inflammatory markers are irrelevant to a traumatic fracture. Imaging is reserved when concomitant facial, orbital, or skull-base injury is suspected.

stages.label: EXAM FINDINGS
stages.content:
Septum midline; no septal hematoma, fluctuance, or bluish swelling
No CSF rhinorrhea; negative halo sign
Intact infraorbital sensation; no diplopia or enophthalmos
No orbital rim step-off; midface stable, occlusion intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding timing of closed reduction of a displaced nasal fracture in an adult, what is the most appropriate plan?
options: Reduce immediately tonight regardless of swelling
Wait at least 6 weeks before any reduction
Allow swelling to subside and perform closed reduction within about 5-10 days (before the bones set, roughly 2 weeks)
No reduction is ever needed for nasal fractures
stages.explanation: Displaced nasal fractures are best reduced once edema settles enough to judge alignment, generally within about 5-10 days in adults (children heal faster, so sooner), and before the fragments become fixed at roughly 2-3 weeks. Reducing immediately through marked swelling makes alignment hard to assess. Waiting 6 weeks means the bones have already healed in a malunited position, necessitating later osteotomies/rhinoplasty. Saying reduction is never needed is wrong—displaced fractures with cosmetic or functional deformity benefit from timely reduction.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What discharge instruction is most appropriate for this patient?
options: Resume contact sports tomorrow

Return immediately if he develops increasing nasal swelling/pain or a sensation of nasal blockage that could indicate a developing septal hematoma; follow up with ENT in several days for reduction assessment
Start prophylactic anticoagulation
Apply heat to the nose continuously to reduce swelling
stages.explanation: Because a septal hematoma can develop or be missed early, the patient must be told to return urgently for worsening swelling, pain, or new nasal obstruction, and to follow up with ENT within several days for reassessment and possible closed reduction once swelling subsides. Resuming contact sports immediately risks re-injury of an unhealed fracture. Anticoagulation is contraindicated—it would promote bleeding/hematoma. Continuous heat worsens swelling acutely; ice and head elevation are appropriate early measures instead.

cases.diagnosis: Isolated nasal bone fracture (displaced), septal hematoma excluded
pearls: Always exclude a septal hematoma in nasal trauma—it is the true emergency and requires immediate drainage.
Nasal bone fracture is a clinical diagnosis; plain films are unreliable and CT is reserved for suspected concomitant facial/orbital injury.
Closed reduction of a displaced nasal fracture is best done after swelling subsides but before the bones set—about 5-10 days in adults (sooner in children).
Give return precautions for developing septal hematoma and avoid contact sports until healed.

cases.title: Foul-Smelling Discharge from One Nostril in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Foul-smelling, blood-tinged discharge from the right nostril for two days.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her father for two days of malodorous, blood-tinged drainage from the right nostril. The discharge is unilateral; the left nostril is clear. The father initially thought she had a cold, but the smell prompted concern. She has been fussy and has been rubbing her nose. He recalls she was playing with small beads and toy parts earlier in the week. She has had no fever, cough, or ear pulling, and is eating and drinking normally. Past medical history is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. She lives with both parents and an older sibling; no smokers in the home; she attends daycare. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems there is no respiratory distress, no facial swelling, and no drooling. The father did not witness her placing anything in her nose but notes she 'puts everything in her mouth and nose.'
cases.exam: The child is interactive and in no respiratory distress. There is purulent, foul-smelling, blood-tinged discharge at the right naris with mild surrounding skin excoriation. After gentle suction, anterior rhinoscopy reveals a firm foreign object lodged in the right nasal cavity with surrounding mucosal edema and a small amount of mucopurulent material; the object has a shiny, rounded metallic edge. The left nasal cavity is clear with normal mucosa. There is no septal swelling or hematoma. The oropharynx is clear without posterior drainage of concern, the tympanic membranes are normal, and there is no facial cellulitis, periorbital swelling, or cervical lymphadenopathy. The airway is patent.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this toddler's unilateral foul nasal discharge?
options: Acute bacterial sinusitis
Allergic rhinitis
Adenoiditis
A nasal foreign body
stages.explanation: Unilateral, foul-smelling, often blood-tinged nasal discharge in a young child is a nasal foreign body until proven otherwise, and direct visualization here confirms it. Acute bacterial sinusitis typically causes bilateral symptoms with fever and is uncommon as an isolated unilateral malodorous discharge in toddlers. Allergic rhinitis causes bilateral clear rhinorrhea, sneezing, and itching—not unilateral purulent foul drainage. Adenoiditis causes bilateral nasal obstruction and postnasal drainage, not a one-sided fetid discharge with a visible object. The unilateral fetor is the classic clue.

stages.label: BEDSIDE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Direct visualization: firm object with shiny metallic, rounded (button-battery) edge in right nasal cavity
Surrounding mucosal edema and mucopurulent debris
No radiograph obtained yet; if uncertain, plain film shows a double-ring/halo sign of a button battery
Left nasal cavity and septum normal

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The visualized object has a shiny, rounded metallic edge raising concern for a button battery. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Treat as an emergency and remove the suspected button battery immediately (or arrange emergent ENT removal)
Schedule routine ENT removal in the next available outpatient slot in a week
Prescribe nasal saline and have the parents flush it out at home over several days
Reassure and observe, as most nasal foreign bodies pass on their own
stages.explanation: A button (disc) battery in the nose is a true emergency: contact with moist mucosa generates a local electrical current and liquefactive necrosis, causing septal perforation and tissue destruction within hours, so it must be removed emergently. Routine outpatient removal in a week risks devastating tissue injury. Home saline flushing is ineffective and dangerous, prolonging mucosal contact. Observation is wrong—nasal foreign bodies, especially batteries, do not safely 'pass,' and batteries require immediate extraction followed by irrigation and inspection for injury.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While preparing for removal in a cooperative-enough child, which technique is a reasonable first attempt for an anterior, graspable object before resorting to sedation/OR?
options: Blindly irrigate the nose with copious water
Push the object posteriorly into the nasopharynx with a probe
Positive-pressure 'parent's kiss' or direct instrument/balloon-catheter removal under direct visualization
Wait for the child to sneeze it out spontaneously
stages.explanation: For an anterior, visualized object, direct removal under visualization with forceps/hook or a balloon catheter, or positive-pressure techniques like the 'parent's kiss' (occlude the contralateral nostril and blow into the mouth), are reasonable first-line maneuvers in a cooperative child; failure or a poorly cooperative child warrants ENT and possible sedation/OR. Copious irrigation is contraindicated with a battery (worsens current/necrosis) and risks aspiration. Pushing it posteriorly risks airway aspiration. Waiting for a sneeze is unreliable and unsafe, particularly with a battery requiring urgent removal.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful removal of a button battery, what is the most appropriate immediate care and follow-up?
options: Discharge with no follow-up since the object is out
Irrigate the area, inspect for mucosal/septal injury, and arrange close ENT follow-up to monitor for septal perforation and synechiae
Start systemic corticosteroids for one month
Obtain an emergent brain MRI
stages.explanation: After battery removal, the nasal cavity should be irrigated to dilute residual alkali, inspected for the extent of mucosal/septal injury, and the child needs close ENT follow-up because delayed septal perforation, synechiae (adhesions), and saddle-nose deformity can develop even after removal. Discharge without follow-up misses these progressive complications. Routine month-long systemic steroids are not standard care. A brain MRI is unnecessary for an isolated nasal battery without neurologic findings or skull-base concern. Ongoing surveillance of the injured mucosa is the priority.

pearls: Unilateral foul-smelling, blood-tinged nasal discharge in a child is a nasal foreign body until proven otherwise.
A button (disc) battery in the nose is an emergency—it causes liquefactive necrosis and septal perforation within hours and needs immediate removal.
Do not irrigate or push a battery posteriorly; remove under direct visualization (parent's kiss, forceps, or balloon catheter), escalating to ENT/OR if needed.
After battery removal, irrigate, inspect for injury, and arrange close follow-up for delayed perforation, synechiae, or saddle-nose deformity.

cases.title: Recurrent Nosebleeds and Blockage in a Teenage Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of recurrent right-sided nosebleeds and progressive nasal blockage.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy is referred to the otolaryngology clinic for four months of recurrent, sometimes profuse right-sided epistaxis and progressive right-sided nasal obstruction. The nosebleeds occur spontaneously, occasionally requiring local pressure for prolonged periods. He also reports right-sided nasal congestion that does not improve with decongestants, intermittent right-sided facial fullness, and recently a 'plugged' right ear. He denies anosmia, vision changes, diplopia, headache, and weight loss. Past medical history is unremarkable; no bleeding disorder, no anticoagulants, no nasal trauma or surgery. He takes no medications and has no allergies. Social history: high-school student, nonsmoker, no drug use, lives with parents. Family history is negative for bleeding disorders and for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia. On review of systems he notes mild right-sided hearing changes and occasional postnasal drainage but no fevers or night sweats. His pediatrician noted a mass-like fullness in the back of the nose on a recent exam, prompting the referral.
cases.exam: Nasal endoscopy reveals a smooth, lobulated, reddish-pink vascular mass filling the right posterior nasal cavity and extending toward the nasopharynx, arising near the sphenopalatine foramen / posterior choana; the mass is firm and appears highly vascular. There is no active bleeding at rest but the surface is friable. The left nasal cavity is patent with normal mucosa. There is no septal hematoma or telangiectasia of the lips/skin. Otoscopy shows a right serous middle-ear effusion (retracted, dull tympanic membrane with fluid) consistent with eustachian tube obstruction. There is no proptosis, no cranial-nerve deficit, no facial numbness, and no cervical lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given an adolescent male with recurrent epistaxis, nasal obstruction, and a vascular posterior nasal mass at the sphenopalatine region, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
Nasal polyp
Inverted papilloma
Rhabdomyosarcoma
stages.explanation: The classic triad of an adolescent boy with recurrent epistaxis, unilateral nasal obstruction, and a vascular nasopharyngeal mass arising near the sphenopalatine foramen defines juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA)—a benign but locally aggressive, highly vascular tumor that is almost exclusive to adolescent males. A nasal polyp is pale, avascular, and does not cause profuse bleeding. Inverted papilloma occurs in older adults, usually off the lateral nasal wall. Rhabdomyosarcoma is possible in children but typically presents as a rapidly enlarging mass with cranial-nerve or orbital involvement rather than this hallmark vascular JNA picture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: In-office incisional biopsy of the mass to obtain tissue
Empiric intranasal corticosteroids for 6 weeks
Fine-needle aspiration of the nasopharyngeal mass
Contrast-enhanced CT and/or MRI of the sinuses and skull base (with consideration of angiography)
stages.explanation: When JNA is suspected, the next step is cross-sectional imaging (contrast CT and/or MRI) to characterize the mass, assess extent and skull-base/pterygopalatine fossa involvement, and demonstrate the avid vascular enhancement; angiography is used to map feeders and enable preoperative embolization. Biopsy and FNA are contraindicated because of the high risk of torrential hemorrhage—diagnosis is made by imaging, not tissue sampling. Intranasal steroids do not treat a vascular tumor and would dangerously delay definitive evaluation of a bleeding, expansile mass.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Contrast-enhanced CT: avidly enhancing soft-tissue mass centered at the right sphenopalatine foramen with widening of the pterygopalatine fossa
Anterior bowing of the posterior maxillary sinus wall (Holman-Miller sign)
Extension into the nasopharynx and posterior nasal cavity; no intracranial extension
MRI: flow voids within the mass indicating high vascularity
Angiography: primary blood supply from the internal maxillary (sphenopalatine) artery

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms a vascular tumor fed by the internal maxillary artery with pterygopalatine fossa widening. What is the most appropriate definitive management approach?
options: Observation alone since the lesion is benign
Preoperative angiographic embolization followed by surgical resection (commonly endoscopic)
Primary chemotherapy
In-office cauterization of the mass surface
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of JNA is surgical resection (frequently endoscopic for appropriately staged tumors), typically preceded by angiographic embolization of feeding vessels 24-48 hours prior to reduce intraoperative blood loss. Observation is inappropriate because JNA, though histologically benign, is locally aggressive and bleeds, eroding bone and extending toward the skull base/orbit. Primary chemotherapy is not standard for a benign vascular tumor. In-office surface cauterization cannot eradicate a deep, vascular, expansile tumor and risks massive hemorrhage. Radiotherapy is reserved for unresectable or intracranial extensive disease.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature best explains why a biopsy of this lesion is specifically avoided?
options: It seeds malignant cells along the needle tract
Biopsy would cause permanent anosmia
Its extreme vascularity makes biopsy prone to life-threatening hemorrhage, and imaging is diagnostic without tissue
Biopsy invariably causes a CSF leak
stages.explanation: JNA is extraordinarily vascular, so even an in-office biopsy can provoke profuse, life-threatening epistaxis; because the imaging appearance (avid enhancement, pterygopalatine fossa widening, Holman-Miller sign, internal maxillary artery supply) is diagnostic, tissue sampling is unnecessary and is deliberately avoided. Needle-tract seeding describes malignant tumors—JNA is benign. Anosmia is not the reason biopsy is avoided. A CSF leak is a possible surgical complication only with skull-base extension, not an inevitable consequence of biopsy. The core teaching point is hemorrhage risk plus diagnostic imaging.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful embolization and endoscopic resection of the angiofibroma, what is the most appropriate long-term plan?
options: Surveillance with serial endoscopy and imaging (e.g., MRI) to detect recurrence/residual tumor
No follow-up is needed because the lesion is benign
Lifelong chemotherapy
Immediate prophylactic radiation to the whole nasopharynx
stages.explanation: Even after gross-total resection, juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma can recur or leave residual tumor (especially with skull-base or pterygopalatine fossa extension), so postoperative surveillance with serial nasal endoscopy and MRI is appropriate. No follow-up ignores the documented recurrence risk of this locally aggressive lesion. Lifelong chemotherapy is not part of management for this benign tumor. Prophylactic whole-nasopharynx radiation is not indicated after complete resection and carries long-term risks in an adolescent; radiotherapy is reserved for unresectable or intracranially extensive/residual disease.

pearls: Suspect juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma in any adolescent male with recurrent epistaxis and a vascular nasopharyngeal mass.
Diagnose with imaging (contrast CT/MRI showing avid enhancement, pterygopalatine fossa widening, Holman-Miller sign); do NOT biopsy due to hemorrhage risk.
Blood supply is typically the internal maxillary (sphenopalatine) artery—preoperative embolization reduces surgical bleeding.
Treatment is surgical resection (often endoscopic) after embolization; radiotherapy is reserved for unresectable/intracranial disease.

cases.title: One-Sided Nasal Blockage with Recurrent Polyp-Like Growth
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive one-sided nasal obstruction and a persistent 'polyp' on the left.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man is referred for a year of progressive left-sided nasal obstruction and intermittent left-sided epistaxis. He was treated previously for presumed nasal polyps with steroid sprays without lasting benefit, and a small polypoid lesion was removed in another clinic but the obstruction recurred. He denies anosmia loss that is bilateral but notes decreased smell on the left, mild left facial pressure, and occasional blood-streaked mucus. He has no diplopia, no facial numbness, and no weight loss. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily. He has no asthma or aspirin sensitivity. No known drug allergies. Social history: 25-pack-year smoking history, social alcohol, works in construction. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he denies fevers, night sweats, and dysphagia. He is frustrated that the growth 'keeps coming back on just one side,' which prompted specialty referral and imaging.
cases.exam: Nasal endoscopy reveals a unilateral, firm, lobulated, mulberry-like (papillary, somewhat translucent-to-fleshy) mass arising from the left lateral nasal wall near the middle meatus, distinct from the typical pale, soft, bilateral inflammatory polyps. The mass partially obstructs the left nasal cavity and bleeds slightly on contact. The right nasal cavity is normal with no polyps. The septum is intact without hematoma. There is no proptosis, the extraocular movements are full, infraorbital sensation is intact bilaterally, and there is no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the head and neck exam, including cranial nerves, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A unilateral nasal mass that mimics a polyp but is one-sided and recurrent should prompt what general principle?
options: It is almost certainly a benign inflammatory polyp; continue steroid sprays
It is an infection; treat with antibiotics
It is a foreign body; remove at bedside and reassure
Unilateral nasal masses require investigation (imaging and tissue) to exclude neoplasm—they are not simple inflammatory polyps
stages.explanation: A truly unilateral nasal mass is a red flag: unlike bilateral inflammatory polyps, it must be worked up with imaging and biopsy to exclude neoplasms such as inverted papilloma, juvenile angiofibroma, or malignancy. Assuming a benign inflammatory polyp and continuing sprays risks missing a tumor—the recurrence and unilaterality argue against simple polyposis. Empiric antibiotics do not address a neoplastic mass. Treating it as a foreign body is inappropriate in an adult with a fleshy, recurrent lateral-wall growth and no foreign-body history. Unilateral equals investigate.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging is obtained and a biopsy of the lateral-wall mass shows hyperplastic epithelium growing inward into the underlying stroma. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Inflammatory (allergic) nasal polyp
Antrochoanal polyp
Inverted (Schneiderian) papilloma
Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
stages.explanation: A unilateral, recurrent, lateral-nasal-wall mass in a middle-aged adult with histology showing epithelium inverting (growing endophytically) into the stroma is the hallmark of an inverted (Schneiderian) papilloma. Inflammatory polyps are bilateral, pale, edematous, and show benign edematous stroma, not endophytic epithelial growth. An antrochoanal polyp is a solitary benign polyp arising from the maxillary sinus, more common in younger patients, without inverted histology or malignant potential. JNA occurs in adolescent males as a highly vascular nasopharyngeal mass—the wrong demographic and histology.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT sinuses: unilateral soft-tissue mass in the left middle meatus/maxillary sinus
Focal hyperostosis at the lateral nasal wall (possible tumor origin)
Bony remodeling/expansion of the maxillary sinus wall without aggressive bony destruction
MRI: convoluted cerebriform pattern on T2/contrast imaging
No orbital or intracranial invasion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the imaging and histology, what is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Long-term intranasal corticosteroids and observation
Complete surgical resection (commonly endoscopic) including the tumor attachment site, with long-term surveillance
Primary radiation therapy
Repeated office debulking as needed without removing the base
stages.explanation: Inverted papilloma is treated with complete surgical excision—frequently endoscopic—removing the entire tumor along with its attachment/origin (often drilling the focal hyperostosis), because incomplete removal leads to high recurrence; long-term surveillance is required given recurrence and malignant-transformation risk. Steroids and observation do not treat a neoplasm and risk progression. Primary radiation is not standard for benign inverted papilloma and is reserved for malignant transformation or unresectable disease. Piecemeal office debulking without addressing the base virtually guarantees recurrence and is inadequate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most important reason for long-term surveillance after resection of this lesion?
options: Risk of converting into an allergic polyp
Risk of spontaneous resolution requiring documentation
Risk of transmitting infection to family members
Risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma (and local recurrence)
stages.explanation: Inverted papilloma carries a meaningful risk of harboring or transforming into squamous cell carcinoma (often cited around 5-10%), and it also recurs locally if incompletely excised—hence the need for complete resection and long-term endoscopic surveillance. It does not 'convert into an allergic polyp'—these are unrelated entities. Spontaneous resolution is not expected for this neoplasm. It is not an infectious, transmissible disease. The malignant-transformation and recurrence risks are the defining reasons for ongoing follow-up.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Final pathology after complete resection shows inverted papilloma with no invasive carcinoma. What follow-up is most appropriate?
options: Long-term endoscopic surveillance (with imaging as indicated) for recurrence and possible malignant transformation
Discharge with no further surveillance
Routine annual chest X-ray only
Begin adjuvant chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Inverted papilloma requires prolonged endoscopic surveillance (with imaging when there is concern) because it recurs locally—often from incompletely removed attachment sites—and can harbor or develop into squamous cell carcinoma over time. Discharge without surveillance misses recurrence and transformation. A routine chest X-ray does not monitor the local sinonasal site where recurrence occurs and is not the relevant surveillance. Adjuvant chemotherapy is not indicated for a completely resected benign inverted papilloma without invasive carcinoma; treatment of any malignant component would be guided separately.

cases.diagnosis: Inverted (Schneiderian) papilloma of the lateral nasal wall
pearls: A unilateral, recurrent nasal mass is a neoplasm until proven otherwise—work it up with imaging and biopsy, do not treat as a simple polyp.
Inverted papilloma arises from the lateral nasal wall; histology shows epithelium inverting into the stroma, and CT may show focal hyperostosis at the origin.
Treatment is complete surgical resection (often endoscopic) including the attachment site to minimize recurrence.
Long-term surveillance is essential because of recurrence and the risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma.

cases.title: Nasal Blockage and Cheek Numbness in a Woodworker
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of right nasal obstruction with bloody discharge and a numb right cheek.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents with about five months of progressive right-sided nasal obstruction, recurrent blood-tinged nasal discharge, and, more recently, numbness over the right cheek and upper lip. He has noticed intermittent right facial pressure and some loosening sensation he attributes to dental issues. He denies bilateral symptoms, has had no response to antibiotics or nasal steroids, and reports a 5 kg unintentional weight loss. He has no diplopia currently but notes occasional right-eye watering. Past medical history: hypertension and type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily). No known drug allergies. Social history: 40-pack-year smoker, worked for 30 years as a furniture maker with chronic hardwood-dust exposure and occasional exposure to wood finishes; moderate alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is notable for the weight loss and facial numbness but negative for fever, night sweats, and headache. He sought care because the cheek numbness 'is new and scary.'
cases.exam: Nasal endoscopy shows a friable, ulcerated, fungating mass filling the right nasal cavity and extending laterally toward the maxillary sinus/middle meatus, with contact bleeding. The left nasal cavity is normal. There is hypoesthesia in the right infraorbital (V2) distribution over the cheek and upper lip, indicating infraorbital nerve involvement. There is mild fullness of the right cheek without overt skin involvement; the eye shows no proptosis and extraocular movements are intact. The oral cavity shows no palatal mass but mild fullness of the right gingivobuccal sulcus. There is a single firm, mobile right level II cervical lymph node, ~1.5 cm. The remainder of the cranial-nerve exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features in this patient most strongly raises concern for a sinonasal malignancy rather than a benign process?
options: Bilateral clear rhinorrhea and sneezing
Seasonal symptoms responsive to antihistamines
Unilateral mass with epistaxis, V2 (infraorbital) numbness, weight loss, and a hardwood-dust occupational history
Acute onset fever and facial pain over a few days
stages.explanation: A unilateral fungating mass with epistaxis, cranial-nerve (V2/infraorbital) sensory loss signifying perineural/bony invasion, unintentional weight loss, and an occupational hardwood-dust exposure together strongly suggest sinonasal malignancy (here squamous cell carcinoma; hardwood dust is classically linked to adenocarcinoma but raises overall sinonasal cancer risk and smoking adds SCC risk). Bilateral clear rhinorrhea and sneezing and seasonal antihistamine-responsive symptoms point to allergic rhinitis. Acute fever and facial pain over days suggest acute sinusitis. Numbness and weight loss are red-flag features that benign processes do not produce.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in the workup of this suspicious sinonasal mass?
options: A trial of oral antibiotics and reassessment in 6 weeks
Cross-sectional imaging (CT and MRI) of the sinuses/skull base followed by biopsy of the mass
Immediate wide surgical excision without imaging or tissue diagnosis
Allergy testing and immunotherapy
stages.explanation: A suspicious unilateral sinonasal mass requires imaging (CT for bony detail, MRI for soft-tissue/skull-base/perineural extent) and then biopsy for tissue diagnosis to guide staging and treatment. Unlike a vascular JNA, a friable sinonasal carcinoma is appropriate to biopsy. Antibiotics and reassessment waste time on a likely malignancy with red-flag features. Operating without imaging or a tissue diagnosis is unsafe and precludes proper staging and planning. Allergy testing/immunotherapy is irrelevant to a fungating, nerve-invading mass with weight loss.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT sinuses: unilateral soft-tissue mass in the right nasal cavity/maxillary sinus with bony destruction of the medial maxillary wall and erosion toward the infraorbital canal
MRI: enhancing mass with perineural extension along V2; no intracranial extension
Biopsy: invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma
Neck ultrasound: right level II node, FNA positive for metastatic SCC
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma with bony destruction, perineural spread along V2, and a positive cervical node. What is the most appropriate management framework?
options: Multidisciplinary, multimodality treatment—typically surgical resection with adjuvant radiation ± chemotherapy and management of the neck
Palliative care only, as cure is impossible
Single-agent oral chemotherapy at home
Radiation alone to the primary, ignoring the neck node
stages.explanation: Locally advanced sinonasal SCC with bony invasion, perineural spread, and nodal metastasis is managed by a multidisciplinary head-and-neck team with multimodality therapy—generally surgical resection plus adjuvant radiotherapy with or without chemotherapy, and treatment of the involved neck. Palliative care only is premature; even advanced local disease without distant metastasis can be treated with curative intent. Home single-agent oral chemo is not standard curative therapy. Radiating the primary while ignoring a biopsy-proven positive node undertreats the disease. Coordinated, stage-appropriate combined therapy is the standard.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which occupational exposure in this patient's history is the classically tested risk factor for sinonasal carcinoma?
options: Loud noise exposure
Prolonged sun exposure
Cold, dry air
Hardwood/wood dust (and related industries such as leather and nickel)
stages.explanation: Chronic hardwood/wood-dust exposure—along with nickel, chromium, and leather-tanning industries—is the classic occupational risk factor for sinonasal carcinoma (wood dust especially with adenocarcinoma; overall sinonasal cancer risk is elevated), and tobacco use compounds the risk of squamous cell carcinoma. Loud noise causes hearing loss, not sinonasal cancer. Sun exposure is linked to cutaneous, not sinonasal, malignancies. Cold, dry air provokes rhinitis/epistaxis but is not a carcinogen. The woodworking history is the high-yield exam association here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The infraorbital (V2) numbness in this patient is best explained by which mechanism, important for surgical and radiation planning?
options: Allergic mucosal swelling compressing the nerve reversibly
Tumor invasion of bone with perineural spread along the maxillary division (V2)/infraorbital nerve
A simple viral neuritis unrelated to the tumor
Referred pain from the cervical lymph node
stages.explanation: Cheek and upper-lip numbness reflects tumor invasion of bone and perineural spread along the infraorbital nerve (V2), confirmed on MRI; recognizing perineural involvement changes resection margins and radiation field planning. Reversible allergic mucosal swelling does not cause a fixed V2 sensory deficit. Viral neuritis is unrelated and would not coexist with a destructive enhancing mass tracking along V2. Referred pain from a cervical node does not produce a discrete dermatomal sensory loss in the infraorbital distribution. Perineural spread is a hallmark of aggressive sinonasal malignancy.

cases.diagnosis: Sinonasal squamous cell carcinoma (maxillary/nasal cavity) with perineural and nodal spread
pearls: Unilateral nasal mass with epistaxis plus cheek/upper-lip numbness (V2) signals bony/perineural invasion by a sinonasal malignancy.
Red flags (unilateral mass, cranial-nerve deficits, weight loss) mandate CT + MRI and biopsy—unlike vascular JNA, sinonasal carcinoma is biopsied.
Hardwood/wood dust, nickel, chromium, and leather exposures are classic occupational risk factors for sinonasal carcinoma; smoking adds SCC risk.
Treat with multidisciplinary, multimodality therapy (surgery plus adjuvant radiation ± chemotherapy) and address the neck.

cases.title: Loss of Smell and a Growing Nasal Mass
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive loss of smell and one-sided nasal obstruction over several months.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with about six months of progressive loss of smell and gradually worsening left-sided nasal obstruction, with intermittent blood-tinged nasal discharge and recent dull frontal headache. She reports the anosmia came on insidiously and is now nearly complete. She denies fevers, weight loss, and visual changes, though she notes occasional left-eye watering. There is no history of head trauma or prior sinus surgery. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes only a daily multivitamin and occasional ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as a teacher, no occupational dust exposures. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she denies seizures, clear watery rhinorrhea suggesting CSF, neck masses, and limb weakness. She initially attributed her symptoms to 'bad allergies,' but the persistent anosmia and a visible mass high in the nose prompted referral. She is anxious about the loss of smell, which has affected her appetite.
cases.exam: Nasal endoscopy reveals a reddish-gray, polypoid, vascular mass high in the left nasal vault arising near the cribriform plate / superior nasal cavity (olfactory cleft region), with contact bleeding. The mass extends inferiorly into the nasal cavity. The right nasal cavity is patent. Formal testing confirms anosmia. There is no proptosis, extraocular movements are intact, and visual acuity is grossly normal; infraorbital sensation is intact. There is no septal hematoma. Cranial-nerve exam is otherwise normal, with no facial weakness. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the head and neck and neurologic exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most specifically localizes the lesion to the olfactory region at the skull base?
options: Bilateral nasal congestion
A tympanic-membrane effusion
Early, prominent anosmia with a mass at the cribriform plate / superior nasal vault
Cheek numbness in the V2 distribution
stages.explanation: Early, prominent anosmia combined with a mass located high in the nasal vault at the cribriform plate points to a tumor arising from the olfactory neuroepithelium—anatomically explaining the smell loss. Bilateral congestion is nonspecific and suggests inflammatory disease, not skull-base localization. A middle-ear effusion points toward nasopharyngeal/eustachian-tube pathology, not the olfactory cleft. V2 cheek numbness localizes to the maxillary/infraorbital region (more typical of maxillary sinus malignancy), not the cribriform/olfactory area. The anosmia plus cribriform-region mass is the localizing combination.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: MRI and CT of the sinuses and skull base, followed by biopsy
Empiric antihistamines and nasal steroids for presumed allergies
Reassurance and repeat exam in 6 months
Olfactory training alone without imaging
stages.explanation: A vascular mass at the cribriform plate with anosmia requires both MRI (soft-tissue, dural/intracranial extension, brain involvement) and CT (bony skull-base detail) of the sinuses/skull base, followed by biopsy for histologic diagnosis. Empiric antihistamines/steroids treat allergic rhinitis and would dangerously delay diagnosis of a skull-base tumor. Reassurance with delayed follow-up is inappropriate for a bleeding, expansile mass causing progressive anosmia. Olfactory training alone addresses post-viral smell loss, not a structural neoplasm, and ignores the visible mass. Imaging then biopsy is the correct pathway.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
CT: soft-tissue mass in the superior nasal cavity with erosion of the cribriform plate
MRI: enhancing mass spanning the cribriform plate with intracranial/intradural extension above and a nasal component below, giving a dumbbell (hourglass) shape across the skull base
Cysts at the tumor-brain interface noted on MRI
Biopsy: small round blue cells in lobules with neurofibrillary stroma and rosettes; immunostains positive for neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a cribriform-plate mass with a dumbbell intracranial extension and small round blue cells with rosettes positive for neuroendocrine markers, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Sinonasal squamous cell carcinoma
Inverted papilloma
Nasal polyposis
Esthesioneuroblastoma (olfactory neuroblastoma)
stages.explanation: A tumor arising at the cribriform plate from olfactory neuroepithelium, causing anosmia, with a dumbbell/hourglass configuration straddling the skull base and histology of small round blue cells, Homer Wright rosettes, neurofibrillary stroma, and neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin/chromogranin) is esthesioneuroblastoma (olfactory neuroblastoma). Squamous cell carcinoma shows keratinizing squamous histology, not neuroendocrine rosettes. Inverted papilloma is a benign lateral-wall lesion with endophytic epithelium, not a cribriform neuroendocrine tumor. Nasal polyposis is bilateral inflammatory tissue without rosettes, neuroendocrine staining, or skull-base erosion. The histology and location are diagnostic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With intracranial/intradural extension across the cribriform plate, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment approach?
options: Antibiotics and observation
Chemotherapy alone as the sole modality for all cases
Surgical resection of the cribriform plate and tumor (craniofacial or endoscopic-assisted anterior skull-base resection) with adjuvant radiation therapy
Radiofrequency ablation in the office
stages.explanation: Esthesioneuroblastoma with skull-base/intracranial involvement is treated with surgical resection encompassing the cribriform plate and dura as needed—via craniofacial or endoscopic-assisted anterior skull-base approaches—followed by adjuvant radiotherapy, with chemotherapy added for higher-grade or advanced disease. Antibiotics and observation do nothing for a malignant skull-base tumor. Chemotherapy alone for all cases is not the standard primary modality; it is adjunctive/neoadjuvant in selected cases. Office radiofrequency ablation cannot achieve oncologic resection of an intracranial-extending tumor and is inappropriate. Surgery plus radiation is the backbone of curative treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which staging system is classically used to describe the extent of this tumor and guide prognosis?
options: TNM staging only, identical to skin cancer
The Kadish staging system (with the Hyams histologic grade)
The Glasgow Coma Scale
The Ann Arbor staging system
stages.explanation: Esthesioneuroblastoma is classically described by the Kadish staging system (A: confined to nasal cavity; B: nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses; C: extension beyond, e.g., intracranial/orbital), complemented by the Hyams histologic grade—both informing prognosis and treatment intensity. Standard cutaneous-type TNM alone is not the classic system taught for this tumor. The Glasgow Coma Scale assesses consciousness, not tumor extent. The Ann Arbor system stages lymphoma, not esthesioneuroblastoma. This patient's intracranial extension corresponds to advanced (Kadish C) disease.

pearls: Esthesioneuroblastoma arises from olfactory neuroepithelium at the cribriform plate—suspect it with early anosmia plus a high nasal-vault mass.
MRI/CT classically show a dumbbell (hourglass) mass straddling the skull base, often with cysts at the tumor-brain interface.
Histology: small round blue cells with Homer Wright rosettes, neurofibrillary stroma, and neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin).
Treatment is anterior skull-base resection (craniofacial or endoscopic-assisted) plus adjuvant radiotherapy, with chemotherapy in advanced/high-grade disease.

cases.title: A Swollen, Red Eye After a Bad Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Right eye swelling, redness, and pain for 1 day.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother for progressive right eye swelling. He had a 10-day history of nasal congestion, green nasal discharge, and low-grade fevers that his pediatrician treated as a 'sinus infection' with oral amoxicillin started 4 days ago. Yesterday his mother noticed his right upper eyelid becoming puffy and red; this morning the eye was nearly swollen shut and he complained the eye 'hurts when I look around.' She also reports the eye appears to be 'bulging out.' He has had decreased appetite and a higher fever today (39.1 °C at home). No prior eye problems, no trauma, no contact lens use. PMH is notable only for recurrent ear infections as a toddler. Vaccinations are up to date including Hib and pneumococcal conjugate. Medications: amoxicillin 45 mg/kg/day divided BID. No known drug allergies. He lives with both parents and a younger sibling; attends third grade. No family history of immunodeficiency or bleeding disorders.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing but interactive. Right periorbital region: marked eyelid edema and erythema, warm and tender. Globe is proptotic with lateral and inferior displacement. Conjunctival chemosis present. Pain on attempted upgaze and lateral gaze with restricted extraocular movements (ophthalmoplegia). Visual acuity grossly intact but cooperation limited; pupils equal and reactive, no relative afferent pupillary defect. Left eye normal. Tenderness over the right medial canthus and ethmoid region. Nasal mucosa edematous with purulent right middle meatus discharge. Neck supple, no nuchal rigidity. Remainder of cranial nerve and neurologic exam nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exam finding most strongly distinguishes orbital (postseptal) cellulitis from preseptal cellulitis?
options: Eyelid erythema and edema
Fever above 39 °C
Proptosis with painful, restricted extraocular movements
Tenderness to palpation of the eyelid
stages.explanation: Proptosis, pain with eye movement, and ophthalmoplegia indicate involvement posterior to the orbital septum (orbital/postseptal cellulitis), a sight- and life-threatening infection. These signs reflect inflammation and mass effect within the orbit itself. Eyelid erythema/edema and eyelid tenderness occur in both preseptal and orbital cellulitis and do not localize the process. Fever is nonspecific and can accompany either entity. The presence of chemosis, proptosis, and ophthalmoplegia mandates urgent imaging and admission, whereas isolated preseptal disease without these findings can sometimes be managed with oral antibiotics and close follow-up.
stages.hint: Think about what lies anterior versus posterior to the orbital septum.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits and sinuses
Plain radiographs of the facial bones
Bedside ocular ultrasound only
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits and paranasal sinuses is the standard imaging to confirm orbital cellulitis, define the source (most commonly ethmoid sinusitis), and detect a subperiosteal or orbital abscess that may require drainage. Plain radiographs lack sensitivity for orbital and soft-tissue detail. Bedside ultrasound cannot reliably evaluate the orbital apex or intracranial extension. Lumbar puncture is not indicated unless meningitis is suspected and would be premature and potentially dangerous before imaging. CT findings guide both the antibiotic plan and the decision for surgical intervention.
stages.hint: You need to see the orbit, the sinuses, and any drainable collection.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 17.8 ×10³/µL (82% neutrophils)
CRP 9.4 mg/dL
Blood cultures: pending
CT orbits/sinuses with contrast: Opacification of right ethmoid and maxillary sinuses. Medial subperiosteal abscess measuring 11 mm displacing the medial rectus and proptosing the globe. Preseptal and intraconal fat stranding. No cavernous sinus or intracranial abnormality.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides ophthalmology and ENT consultation, what is the most appropriate initial antibiotic regimen?
options: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate, continued outpatient
IV vancomycin plus ceftriaxone (with metronidazole)
IV acyclovir
Topical fluoroquinolone eye drops alone
stages.explanation: Orbital cellulitis with subperiosteal abscess requires admission and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA, and respiratory/anaerobic flora from the sinuses. IV vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone), often with metronidazole for anaerobic coverage, is appropriate. Oral therapy is inadequate for postseptal disease, and this child already failed oral amoxicillin. Acyclovir treats herpetic disease, not bacterial orbital infection. Topical drops do not penetrate the orbit. IV antibiotics plus monitoring of vision are the cornerstone; surgery is added if there are specific indications.
stages.hint: Cover MRSA, strep, and sinus anaerobes intravenously.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly indicate the need for urgent surgical drainage of the abscess rather than a trial of IV antibiotics alone?
options: Persistent fever at 24 hours on IV antibiotics
Decreasing visual acuity with a new afferent pupillary defect
Eyelid edema that has not fully resolved
WBC still mildly elevated at 12 ×10³/µL
stages.explanation: Deteriorating vision, an afferent pupillary defect, or progressive ophthalmoplegia signals optic nerve compromise and is an absolute indication for urgent surgical drainage. Small medial subperiosteal abscesses in young children can sometimes resolve with IV antibiotics alone, but visual deterioration cannot wait. Persistent fever at 24 hours and slowly improving eyelid edema are expected early and warrant continued therapy, not immediate surgery. A mildly elevated WBC is nonspecific. Other surgical indications include large abscess, no improvement after 24-48 hours, or intracranial extension.
stages.hint: The eye that is losing vision cannot wait for antibiotics to work.

cases.diagnosis: Orbital cellulitis with medial subperiosteal abscess secondary to acute ethmoid sinusitis
pearls: Pain with eye movement, proptosis, and ophthalmoplegia separate orbital (postseptal) from preseptal cellulitis.
Ethmoid sinusitis is the most common source of pediatric orbital cellulitis because the lamina papyracea is thin and porous.
Contrast CT of the orbits and sinuses is the imaging of choice and detects subperiosteal abscess.
Vision loss, afferent pupillary defect, large abscess, or no response to 24-48 h of IV antibiotics are indications for surgical drainage.

cases.title: A Soft Lump on the Forehead
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening forehead swelling and headache for several days.
cases.history: A 16-year-old boy presents to the ED with a 5-day history of a tender, doughy swelling over the center of his forehead and a worsening frontal headache. He reports about 3 weeks of nasal congestion and facial pressure that he attributed to a lingering cold; over the past week he developed fevers to 38.8 °C, and 2 days ago the forehead swelling appeared and has enlarged. Today his mother notes he seems 'off,' more drowsy and irritable than usual, and he vomited once this morning without nausea beforehand. No seizures, no visual changes, no neck stiffness reported by the patient. No trauma to the head. PMH unremarkable; he plays high-school basketball. He took ibuprofen and an OTC decongestant nasal spray with minimal relief. No known drug allergies. He does not smoke or use drugs; occasional alcohol. Immunizations up to date. Family history noncontributory. He has missed several days of school due to the headache and fevers.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable, oriented to person and place, slow to answer. A fluctuant, tender, warm swelling approximately 4 cm overlies the central frontal bone with overlying erythema and edema ('puffy' forehead). Frontal sinus region tender to percussion. Purulent discharge in both middle meatuses on anterior rhinoscopy. Mild photophobia. No nuchal rigidity, Kernig and Brudzinski negative. Cranial nerves intact, pupils equal and reactive. No focal motor deficit; gait not tested due to drowsiness. No papilledema appreciated on limited fundoscopy. Remainder of exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The fluctuant frontal swelling overlying the frontal bone in this patient with chronic sinusitis most likely represents what underlying process?
options: A sebaceous cyst of the forehead skin
Subgaleal hematoma from unrecognized trauma
Frontal bone osteomyelitis with subperiosteal abscess (Pott puffy tumor)
Allergic contact dermatitis
stages.explanation: A soft, doughy, fluctuant forehead swelling over the frontal bone in the setting of frontal sinusitis is the classic Pott puffy tumor: osteomyelitis of the frontal bone with an associated subperiosteal abscess. It develops when frontal sinus infection erodes through the anterior table. A sebaceous cyst is not warm, fluctuant, or associated with sinusitis and systemic illness. A subgaleal hematoma requires trauma, which is absent. Contact dermatitis causes pruritic surface changes, not a fluctuant collection with fever and altered mental status. The accompanying drowsiness and vomiting raise concern for intracranial extension.
stages.hint: Frontal sinusitis plus a doughy forehead swelling has an eponymous name.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his drowsiness and vomiting, what is the most appropriate imaging study?
options: Plain skull radiographs
Contrast-enhanced CT and/or MRI of the head and sinuses with contrast
Non-contrast CT of the head only
Carotid Doppler ultrasound
stages.explanation: Pott puffy tumor frequently extends intracranially (epidural abscess, subdural empyema, meningitis, or venous sinus thrombosis). With altered mental status and vomiting, contrast-enhanced CT defines the bone and sinus disease, and contrast-enhanced MRI best characterizes intracranial complications and the brain parenchyma. Plain radiographs are insensitive for both osteomyelitis early on and intracranial collections. Non-contrast CT alone can miss small empyemas and abscess rims. Carotid Doppler evaluates the carotid arteries, irrelevant here. Imaging both the bone/sinus and the intracranial compartment is essential before treatment decisions.
stages.hint: His mental status changes mean you must image inside the skull, with contrast.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.6 ×10³/µL (left shift)
CRP 16.2 mg/dL
ESR 78 mm/hr
Blood cultures: pending
CT/MRI head with contrast: Opacified frontal sinuses with erosion of the anterior table and a subperiosteal (forehead) abscess. Erosion of the posterior table with an adjacent epidural abscess (1.2 cm) and overlying frontal bone osteomyelitis. No midline shift. Patent dural venous sinuses.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate empiric IV antibiotic regimen while arranging surgery?
options: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone (or cefepime) + metronidazole
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
Ceftriaxone monotherapy
Azithromycin + doxycycline
stages.explanation: Pott puffy tumor with intracranial extension requires broad IV coverage that penetrates the CNS and addresses the polymicrobial sinus flora: streptococci (including S. anginosus group), staphylococci including MRSA, gram-negatives, and anaerobes. Vancomycin (MRSA), a CNS-penetrating third/fourth-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefepime, plus metronidazole (anaerobes) is appropriate. Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate is inadequate for an intracranial infection. Ceftriaxone monotherapy misses MRSA and anaerobic coverage. Azithromycin plus doxycycline targets atypicals and does not reliably cover this polymicrobial CNS infection. Antibiotics are adjunctive to surgical drainage.
stages.hint: Cover MRSA, anaerobes, and gram-negatives, all with CNS penetration.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the definitive management in addition to prolonged IV antibiotics?
options: Discharge home once afebrile on oral antibiotics
Hyperbaric oxygen as primary therapy
Urgent neurosurgical and ENT surgical drainage of the abscesses and sinus
Observation with repeat imaging in 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Definitive management of Pott puffy tumor with epidural abscess is combined ENT and neurosurgical drainage: drainage/debridement of the frontal sinus and osteomyelitic bone (often endoscopic sinus surgery with or without external approach) and neurosurgical evacuation of the intracranial collection, plus a prolonged course (typically 6+ weeks) of IV antibiotics. Discharge on oral antibiotics is unsafe with an intracranial abscess. Hyperbaric oxygen is at most adjunctive, never primary. Observation risks neurologic deterioration, herniation, and death. Source control plus prolonged IV therapy is the standard of care.
stages.hint: An intracranial abscess plus osteomyelitis is a surgical disease.

cases.diagnosis: Pott puffy tumor (frontal osteomyelitis with subperiosteal abscess) complicating frontal sinusitis, with epidural abscess
pearls: Pott puffy tumor = frontal bone osteomyelitis with a subperiosteal abscess presenting as a doughy forehead swelling over a frontal sinus infection.
It is classically a disease of adolescents because the frontal sinuses are well developed and the diploic veins permit spread.
Always image for intracranial complications (epidural/subdural abscess, meningitis, venous sinus thrombosis) with contrast CT/MRI.
Treatment is combined ENT/neurosurgical drainage plus prolonged broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering MRSA and anaerobes.

cases.title: Both Eyes Bulging in a Critically Ill Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache, double vision, and progressive swelling around both eyes.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after presenting with 4 days of worsening headache, high fevers, and a 'splitting' retro-orbital pain that started on the right and spread to both sides. He had untreated symptoms of a severe sinus infection for about 2 weeks (facial pain, purulent nasal discharge, nasal obstruction). Over the past 24 hours he developed double vision, drooping eyelids, and swelling around both eyes, and he became confused and lethargic, prompting ICU admission. PMH: poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 10.2%); takes metformin 1000 mg BID, inconsistently. No known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack per day, drinks socially. Works as a warehouse supervisor. No recent travel. Family history of diabetes. On review of systems he reports no neck stiffness initially but increasing photophobia. He has not sought care until now because he 'thought it was just a bad sinus infection.'
cases.exam: Lethargic, oriented to person only. Bilateral proptosis with periorbital edema and chemosis, right greater than left. Bilateral ophthalmoplegia: limited abduction, adduction, elevation, and depression in both eyes; bilateral ptosis. Pupils sluggishly reactive; visual acuity reduced bilaterally. Decreased sensation over the V1 and V2 distributions on the right (corneal reflex diminished). Purulent discharge in both nasal cavities, foul-smelling. Neck mildly stiff. No focal limb weakness; reflexes symmetric. Fundoscopy shows early disc edema. Skin warm; no necrotic nasal eschar visualized.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of bilateral proptosis, multidirectional ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, and V1/V2 sensory loss localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: The optic chiasm
The cavernous sinus
The cerebellopontine angle
The jugular foramen
stages.explanation: The cavernous sinus transmits cranial nerves III, IV, V1, V2, and VI plus the internal carotid artery. Involvement here produces ophthalmoplegia from CN III/IV/VI dysfunction, ptosis (CN III/sympathetics), and facial sensory loss in V1/V2 distributions, with proptosis and chemosis from impaired venous drainage. Bilateral findings strongly suggest cavernous sinus pathology because the two sinuses communicate. The optic chiasm causes visual field defects, not ophthalmoplegia. The cerebellopontine angle affects CN V, VII, VIII. The jugular foramen affects CN IX, X, XI. The clinical pattern is classic for cavernous sinus involvement.
stages.hint: Which single structure carries CN III, IV, V1, V2, and VI together?

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Non-contrast head CT
MRI brain with MR venography (or CT venography)
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Plain sinus radiographs
stages.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is best confirmed with contrast-enhanced MRI brain plus MR venography (MRV); CT venography is an acceptable alternative. These demonstrate filling defects, expansion of the cavernous sinus, and abnormal flow, and they characterize the sinus source and any intracranial spread. Non-contrast CT is insensitive and may appear normal early. Carotid duplex evaluates the cervical carotids, not the cavernous sinus. Plain sinus films cannot assess venous thrombosis or intracranial structures. MRV is the key study because it directly visualizes the thrombosed venous sinus.
stages.hint: You need to image the venous outflow, not just the brain parenchyma.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 21.4 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
Glucose 318 mg/dL
HbA1c 10.4%
Blood cultures x2: pending
MRI brain + MRV: Lack of normal flow void with a filling defect in both cavernous sinuses; convex lateral walls. Sphenoid and ethmoid sinusitis. Engorged superior ophthalmic veins bilaterally. No parenchymal abscess; no infarct.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Along with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (including MRSA and anaerobic coverage), which adjunctive therapy is generally recommended in septic cavernous sinus thrombosis?
options: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin
High-dose aspirin alone
Immediate thrombolysis with tPA
Antiplatelet therapy with clopidogrel
stages.explanation: Anticoagulation with IV unfractionated heparin (or LMWH) is commonly recommended in septic cavernous sinus thrombosis to limit thrombus propagation and may reduce morbidity, provided there is no major hemorrhage; evidence is observational but generally favorable. Aspirin and clopidogrel are antiplatelet agents, not the standard for venous sinus thrombosis. Systemic thrombolysis carries a high hemorrhage risk and is not standard initial therapy. The mainstays remain prolonged IV antibiotics and source control of the underlying sinusitis; anticoagulation is the typical adjunct after weighing bleeding risk.
stages.hint: It is a venous thrombosis — think about preventing clot propagation, carefully.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which organism is the most common cause of septic cavernous sinus thrombosis arising from sinusitis?
options: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Staphylococcus aureus
Escherichia coli
Cryptococcus neoformans
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the single most common organism in septic cavernous sinus thrombosis, reflecting spread from the face and paranasal sinuses; streptococci and anaerobes are also frequent, and the infection is often polymicrobial when sinus-derived. Pseudomonas is uncommon in this setting except in specific hosts. E. coli is a urinary/abdominal pathogen, not typical here. Cryptococcus causes meningitis in immunocompromised patients but is not a cause of acute septic cavernous sinus thrombosis. Empiric therapy must include anti-staphylococcal coverage with MRSA activity, plus agents for streptococci, gram-negatives, and anaerobes.
stages.hint: It spreads from the face and sinuses — think of the most common skin/sinus pathogen.

cases.diagnosis: Septic cavernous sinus thrombosis secondary to sphenoethmoid sinusitis
pearls: Bilateral findings (proptosis, multidirectional ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, V1/V2 sensory loss) suggest cavernous sinus disease because the two sinuses communicate.
CN VI lies free within the cavernous sinus and is often affected early; together CN III, IV, V1, V2, VI and the carotid traverse the sinus.
Diagnose with contrast MRI plus MR venography (or CT venography).
Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen; treat with prolonged broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, source control, and usually anticoagulation.

cases.title: A Persistent Clear Drip From One Nostril
cases.chiefComplaint: Watery clear fluid dripping from the right nostril for 6 weeks.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman presents to the otolaryngology clinic with 6 weeks of clear, watery fluid dripping from her right nostril. It is unilateral, worse when she leans forward or strains, and sometimes runs down the back of her throat with a salty taste. She denies fever, but had two episodes of headache and one of low-grade neck stiffness. She has chronic daily headaches and was told months ago she has 'high pressure in the head'; she is overweight and has noticed transient visual blurring. No recent trauma, but she did have a minor fall onto her face about 2 months ago that she dismissed. No prior sinus surgery. PMH: obesity (BMI 36), migraines, irregular menses. Medications: topiramate (recently stopped), OTC ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. Nonsmoker, social alcohol, works as an accountant. Family history noncontributory. She is worried because antihistamines and intranasal steroids prescribed for presumed 'allergies' have not helped at all.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, obese woman. Anterior rhinoscopy: clear watery discharge from the right nasal cavity; mucosa pink and non-edematous, no purulence or polyps. The drip increases with Valsalva and when she leans her head forward. A drop placed on tissue paper produces a clear central area surrounded by a ring ('halo'/double-ring sign). No nasal crusting or septal lesion. Tympanic membranes normal. Cranial nerves intact; visual fields grossly full but optic discs show mild blurring of margins. No meningismus today. Remainder of head and neck exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Unilateral clear rhinorrhea that worsens with leaning forward and Valsalva and produces a 'halo' ring on tissue should raise suspicion for what?
options: Allergic rhinitis
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leak/rhinorrhea
Vasomotor rhinitis
Acute viral rhinitis
stages.explanation: Unilateral, clear, watery rhinorrhea that increases with positional/postural maneuvers and Valsalva, often with a salty/metallic postnasal taste and a halo sign on tissue paper, is the classic presentation of CSF rhinorrhea from a skull-base defect. Allergic rhinitis is typically bilateral with sneezing, itching, and other allergic features and responds to antihistamines/steroids — which failed here. Vasomotor rhinitis is bilateral, trigger-related, and lacks positional CSF features. Viral rhinitis is self-limited over days, not 6 weeks, and is bilateral with systemic symptoms. The history of trauma and possible elevated intracranial pressure further supports a CSF leak.
stages.hint: Clear unilateral drip, positional, with a salty taste and a ring sign — what fluid leaks from the skull base?

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most specific laboratory test to confirm that the fluid is cerebrospinal fluid?
options: Fluid glucose by bedside glucometer
Beta-2 transferrin assay of the fluid
Fluid culture and Gram stain
Nasal smear for eosinophils
stages.explanation: Beta-2 transferrin is found almost exclusively in CSF (and perilymph and aqueous/vitreous humor) and is the gold-standard, highly specific confirmatory test for CSF rhinorrhea; only a small fluid sample is needed. Bedside glucometer testing is unreliable because nasal secretions and contamination produce false results, and it is nonspecific. Culture/Gram stain evaluates for infection, not the nature of the fluid. Nasal eosinophil smear supports allergic/eosinophilic rhinitis, not a CSF leak. Beta-2 transferrin (and beta-trace protein where available) confirms the diagnosis before localization imaging.
stages.hint: A protein nearly unique to CSF gives the definitive answer.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Fluid beta-2 transferrin: POSITIVE
Fluid glucose: 48 mg/dL
High-resolution CT skull base: 2 mm bony dehiscence of the right cribriform plate/ethmoid roof
MRI/CT cisternography: meningoencephalocele protruding through the defect
Opening pressure (LP, later): 28 cm H2O

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming and localizing the leak, what is the preferred definitive treatment for a persistent anterior skull-base CSF leak?
options: Indefinite antihistamines and decongestants
Endoscopic endonasal repair of the skull-base defect
Bilateral nasal packing for 6 weeks
Empiric long-term prophylactic antibiotics alone
stages.explanation: A persistent (or high-flow/traumatic with encephalocele) anterior skull-base CSF leak is definitively treated with endoscopic endonasal repair of the defect, which has high success rates and low morbidity. Medical therapy with antihistamines/decongestants does nothing for a structural dural defect. Nasal packing does not seal a skull-base leak and risks ascending infection. Prophylactic antibiotics alone do not close the leak and are not a substitute for repair; the ongoing communication with the subarachnoid space carries meningitis risk. Elevated intracranial pressure should also be addressed to prevent recurrence.
stages.hint: A structural hole at the skull base usually needs to be surgically closed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
