stages.question: Given the elevated opening pressure and her body habitus, what underlying condition likely contributed to this spontaneous leak and must be managed to prevent recurrence?
options: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH)
Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Chronic allergic rhinitis
Obstructive sleep apnea alone
stages.explanation: Spontaneous CSF leaks in an obese woman of childbearing age with headaches, transient visual obscurations, papilledema, and elevated opening pressure (28 cm H2O) strongly suggest idiopathic intracranial hypertension; chronically elevated CSF pressure erodes the skull base and drives spontaneous leaks and high recurrence after repair unless the pressure is controlled (weight loss, acetazolamide, sometimes CSF diversion). Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with the gait/cognitive/incontinence triad and normal-to-low pressure in older patients. Allergic rhinitis is unrelated to skull-base erosion. OSA may coexist but does not directly cause CSF leaks. Recognizing and treating IIH is essential to durable repair.
stages.hint: Obese young woman, headaches, papilledema, opening pressure 28 — what raises CSF pressure?

cases.diagnosis: Spontaneous CSF rhinorrhea from a cribriform plate defect (meningoencephalocele) associated with idiopathic intracranial hypertension
pearls: Unilateral clear positional rhinorrhea with a salty taste and a halo/double-ring sign on tissue should prompt evaluation for a CSF leak.
Beta-2 transferrin is the highly specific confirmatory test; bedside glucose testing is unreliable.
High-resolution CT plus CT/MR cisternography localizes the skull-base defect for endoscopic repair.
Spontaneous leaks in obese women often reflect idiopathic intracranial hypertension; control the pressure to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: A Runny Nose Set Off by Cold Air and Perfume
cases.chiefComplaint: Years of clear, watery runny nose triggered by weather changes and strong smells.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents to clinic for chronic, bothersome rhinorrhea present for several years. She describes a clear, watery drip from both nostrils that is set off within minutes by cold air, going from a warm room to outdoors, eating spicy food, and strong odors such as perfume, cleaning products, and cigarette smoke. She also has nasal congestion but notably denies itchy or watery eyes, sneezing fits, or palatal itching. Symptoms occur year-round with no clear seasonal pattern and no relationship to pets or dust exposure. She has tried OTC oral antihistamines (loratadine, then cetirizine) without benefit, which frustrates her. PMH: hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; GERD on omeprazole. No known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, lives with her husband and a cat she has had for 12 years, works as a schoolteacher. No family history of asthma or eczema. She has no pet-related worsening and no improvement when away on vacation.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing. Anterior rhinoscopy: boggy, pink-to-erythematous nasal mucosa with clear watery secretions bilaterally; no nasal polyps; septum midline without perforation or crusting. No pale or bluish 'allergic' mucosa, no cobblestoning of the posterior pharynx. Eyes without conjunctival injection, no allergic shiners or Dennie-Morgan lines. No sinus tenderness. Lungs clear to auscultation without wheeze. No periorbital edema. Remainder of head and neck exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most argues against allergic rhinitis and toward a nonallergic cause in this patient?
options: Bilateral clear rhinorrhea
Symptoms triggered by temperature change and odors without ocular itching, sneezing, or seasonality
Nasal congestion
Boggy nasal mucosa on exam
stages.explanation: Triggering by nonspecific physical/chemical stimuli (cold air, odors, spicy food) in the absence of itching, paroxysmal sneezing, ocular symptoms, seasonality, or clear allergen relationship points to vasomotor (nonallergic) rhinitis, which reflects autonomic hyperreactivity rather than IgE-mediated allergy. Bilateral clear rhinorrhea and nasal congestion occur in both allergic and nonallergic rhinitis and do not distinguish them. Boggy mucosa is also nonspecific. The lack of ocular/itch/sneeze symptoms and the failure of antihistamines are the key discriminators favoring a nonallergic process.
stages.hint: Allergic patients itch, sneeze, and have eye symptoms; this patient reacts to triggers without those.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to help confirm the diagnosis?
options: Allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE) to exclude allergic rhinitis
Sinus CT scan
Beta-2 transferrin of the nasal fluid
Total serum IgE alone
stages.explanation: Vasomotor (nonallergic) rhinitis is largely a clinical diagnosis of exclusion; documenting negative allergy testing (skin prick or serum specific IgE) to relevant aeroallergens supports it by ruling out allergic rhinitis. A sinus CT is not needed without features of chronic rhinosinusitis (purulence, facial pain, polyps). Beta-2 transferrin is for suspected CSF leak, not relevant to bilateral trigger-related rhinorrhea. Total serum IgE alone is nonspecific and cannot identify specific sensitizations or confidently exclude allergy. Negative specific allergy testing in the right clinical context confirms the nonallergic mechanism.
stages.hint: To call it nonallergic, you should first show there is no allergy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Skin prick testing: negative to dust mite, cat, dog, molds, tree/grass/weed pollens
Serum specific IgE panel: negative
Nasal cytology: no significant eosinophilia
CBC: within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For a patient whose predominant symptom is watery rhinorrhea from nonallergic rhinitis, which intranasal medication is most specifically effective?
options: Intranasal ipratropium bromide
Oral pseudoephedrine
Oral montelukast
Intranasal oxymetazoline daily
stages.explanation: Intranasal ipratropium, an anticholinergic, directly reduces watery rhinorrhea and is especially useful when rhinorrhea is the dominant complaint, including the gustatory and cold-air-triggered varieties. Intranasal corticosteroids and intranasal antihistamines (e.g., azelastine) are also first-line for nonallergic rhinitis overall. Oral pseudoephedrine is a decongestant that helps congestion but not rhinorrhea and worsens hypertension. Montelukast is used in allergic disease/asthma, not nonallergic rhinitis. Daily intranasal oxymetazoline causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa) and must be avoided for chronic use. Ipratropium targets the secretory component directly.
stages.hint: An anticholinergic spray dries up the watery drip.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If congestion and rhinorrhea persist despite ipratropium, which is an appropriate next-line intranasal therapy for nonallergic rhinitis?
options: Add an intranasal antihistamine (azelastine) and/or intranasal corticosteroid
Start long-term daily oral antihistamine
Begin allergen immunotherapy
Recommend daily oxymetazoline for control
stages.explanation: Intranasal antihistamines such as azelastine and intranasal corticosteroids are effective in nonallergic rhinitis and can be combined with ipratropium for broader symptom control; intranasal antihistamines work in nonallergic rhinitis even though oral antihistamines often do not. Daily oral antihistamines are typically ineffective here, as this patient already experienced. Allergen immunotherapy is reserved for confirmed IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis, which she does not have. Daily oxymetazoline leads to rhinitis medicamentosa and is contraindicated for maintenance therapy. Step-up combination intranasal therapy is the rational next step.
stages.hint: Topical (intranasal) antihistamines work where oral ones failed.

pearls: Vasomotor rhinitis is triggered by nonspecific stimuli (cold air, odors, spicy food) without itching, sneezing, ocular symptoms, or seasonality.
It is a diagnosis of exclusion supported by negative allergy testing.
Intranasal ipratropium specifically targets watery rhinorrhea; intranasal corticosteroids and intranasal antihistamines (azelastine) are also first-line.
Oral antihistamines are typically ineffective, and chronic topical decongestants must be avoided to prevent rhinitis medicamentosa.

cases.title: Worsening Stuffy Nose Despite the Spray
cases.chiefComplaint: Constant nasal congestion that keeps getting worse despite using nasal spray several times a day.
cases.history: A 29-year-old man presents with severe, constant nasal congestion for the past 3 months. It began with a cold, after which he started using an OTC nasal decongestant spray (oxymetazoline) that worked great at first. Over time he found he needed it more frequently — now using it 4 to 6 times daily in both nostrils — and the relief lasts only an hour or two before the congestion 'comes back worse than before.' He feels he 'can't breathe through his nose without the spray' and has been waking at night to use it. He denies facial pain, fever, purulent discharge, or itchy/watery eyes. No anosmia. PMH: none. Medications: oxymetazoline nasal spray (multiple bottles), occasional ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. Nonsmoker, drinks socially, works in software. No pets. No family history of allergies or asthma. He is frustrated and asks whether he needs a stronger spray.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, breathing through his mouth. Anterior rhinoscopy: markedly swollen, erythematous (beefy red) nasal turbinates bilaterally nearly obstructing the airway, with scant clear secretions. No purulence, no polyps, septum midline without perforation. No pale boggy 'allergic' mucosa, no crusting. Posterior pharynx without cobblestoning. No sinus tenderness. Eyes without conjunctival injection. Lungs clear. Remainder of head and neck exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given chronic topical decongestant use with worsening rebound congestion?
options: Allergic rhinitis
Rhinitis medicamentosa
Chronic bacterial sinusitis
Nasal polyposis
stages.explanation: Rhinitis medicamentosa is rebound nasal congestion caused by prolonged use (typically beyond 3-5 days) of topical decongestant sprays such as oxymetazoline. Tachyphylaxis and rebound vasodilation produce beefy-red, swollen turbinates and a cycle of escalating use, exactly as described. Allergic rhinitis features itching, sneezing, ocular symptoms, and pale boggy mucosa, which are absent. Chronic bacterial sinusitis would have purulence, facial pain/pressure, and often anosmia. Nasal polyps appear as pale, glistening grape-like masses, not seen here. The clear temporal link to overuse of the spray makes rhinitis medicamentosa the diagnosis.
stages.hint: What happens when you use a decongestant spray for months instead of days?

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is the diagnosis of rhinitis medicamentosa best established?
options: Sinus CT scan
Clinical history of topical decongestant overuse with characteristic exam
Allergy skin testing
Nasal culture
stages.explanation: Rhinitis medicamentosa is a clinical diagnosis based on a history of prolonged topical decongestant (or cocaine) use combined with the characteristic exam of beefy-red, edematous turbinates and rebound congestion. No specialized testing is required. A sinus CT is unnecessary without features of rhinosinusitis. Allergy testing would be appropriate only if allergic rhinitis were suspected, which the history does not support. Nasal culture is for suspected bacterial infection. Recognizing the medication history is the diagnostic key, sparing the patient unnecessary workup.
stages.hint: The diagnosis is in the bottle of spray he keeps using.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment?
options: Switch to a stronger topical decongestant
Discontinue the topical decongestant and start an intranasal corticosteroid
Begin long-term oral antibiotics
Refer immediately for turbinate surgery
stages.explanation: The cornerstone is cessation of the offending topical decongestant; an intranasal corticosteroid is started to reduce mucosal inflammation and ease the rebound congestion during withdrawal, and a short oral steroid taper or short course of an oral decongestant may help the transition. Switching to a stronger topical decongestant perpetuates and worsens the cycle. Long-term oral antibiotics are not indicated for this noninfectious condition. Turbinate surgery is reserved for refractory structural hypertrophy after medical therapy fails, not first-line. Stopping the spray is essential; symptoms typically improve over days to a few weeks.
stages.hint: The fix starts with stopping the very thing causing it.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What should you counsel the patient to expect and to avoid going forward?
options: Congestion will worsen transiently after stopping, but resolves; avoid using topical decongestants beyond 3-5 days
He should resume the spray as needed indefinitely
He will require lifelong oral steroids
He should avoid all intranasal sprays permanently, including steroids
stages.explanation: Patients should be counseled that congestion often worsens transiently when the spray is stopped but improves over days to a few weeks, and that topical decongestants should be limited to no more than 3-5 consecutive days to prevent recurrence. Resuming the spray indefinitely restarts the cycle. Lifelong oral steroids are inappropriate and carry systemic toxicity. Avoiding all intranasal sprays including corticosteroids is wrong, since intranasal steroids are precisely the helpful treatment; it is the decongestant sprays that must be limited. Clear expectations improve adherence during the difficult withdrawal phase.
stages.hint: Set expectations for short-term worsening and teach the 3-5 day rule.

cases.diagnosis: Rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion from chronic oxymetazoline use)
pearls: Topical decongestants (oxymetazoline) should be limited to 3-5 days; longer use causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).
Exam shows beefy-red, swollen turbinates with an escalating-use history; it is a clinical diagnosis.
Treatment is stopping the decongestant plus an intranasal corticosteroid, sometimes with a short steroid taper.
Counsel patients that congestion worsens transiently after cessation but resolves within days to weeks.

cases.title: Crusting Nose, a Collapsing Bridge, and Bloody Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of nasal crusting, recurrent nosebleeds, and a change in the shape of her nose.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents with 4 months of persistent nasal crusting, recurrent epistaxis, nasal obstruction, and foul nasal discharge unresponsive to multiple courses of antibiotics and saline rinses. She has noticed her nasal bridge 'sinking in' over the last few weeks. Review of systems reveals fatigue, arthralgias, a 6 kg weight loss, intermittent low-grade fevers, and a recent 'cold' with a cough productive of blood-tinged sputum. She also reports that her urine looked dark/tea-colored twice this week. She has decreased hearing in the right ear. PMH: previously healthy. Medications: none chronic; recent amoxicillin then doxycycline. No known drug allergies. Nonsmoker, no illicit drug use (specifically denies intranasal cocaine), drinks rarely. Works as a nurse. Family history noncontributory. She is concerned because nothing has helped and she feels increasingly unwell.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing. Nasal exam: extensive mucosal crusting, friable granular mucosa, a septal perforation, and early saddle-nose deformity from cartilage destruction. Bloody crusts in both nasal cavities. Right serous otitis media with conductive hearing loss. Oral cavity without lesions. Lungs: scattered crackles at the right base. No focal neuro deficit. Skin: a few palpable purpuric lesions on the lower legs. No synovitis on joint exam today. No lymphadenopathy. Peripheral edema absent.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of upper airway disease (crusting, septal perforation, saddle nose), lung involvement, and renal disease most suggests which diagnosis?
options: Sarcoidosis
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)
Intranasal cocaine-induced midline destructive lesion
Chronic invasive fungal sinusitis
stages.explanation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis classically affects the upper respiratory tract (crusting, epistaxis, septal perforation, saddle-nose deformity, serous otitis media), the lungs (nodules, hemoptysis), and the kidneys (pauci-immune glomerulonephritis with hematuria). This ENT-lung-kidney pattern with constitutional symptoms and purpura is characteristic. Sarcoidosis causes granulomas but rarely produces this destructive necrotizing nasal disease with glomerulonephritis. Cocaine-induced midline destruction is in the differential for septal/palatal destruction but the patient denies use and lacks the systemic lung/renal/vasculitic features. Invasive fungal sinusitis typically occurs in immunocompromised/diabetic hosts and does not explain the renal disease and purpura.
stages.hint: Think of the classic ENT + lung + kidney vasculitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which serologic test is most associated with this diagnosis?
options: p-ANCA (anti-MPO)
c-ANCA (anti-proteinase 3 / PR3)
Anti-dsDNA
Anti-glomerular basement membrane antibody
stages.explanation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis is most strongly associated with c-ANCA directed against proteinase 3 (PR3-ANCA), present in the large majority of patients with generalized disease. p-ANCA/anti-MPO is more typical of microscopic polyangiitis and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Anti-dsDNA indicates systemic lupus erythematosus. Anti-GBM antibody defines anti-GBM (Goodpasture) disease, which causes pulmonary-renal syndrome but not the destructive upper-airway granulomatous disease seen here. A positive c-ANCA/PR3 in this clinical context strongly supports GPA, though biopsy provides definitive confirmation.
stages.hint: The cytoplasmic ANCA pattern targeting PR3 is the hallmark.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
c-ANCA (anti-PR3): strongly positive
Creatinine 2.3 mg/dL (baseline normal)
Urinalysis: 3+ blood, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, proteinuria
ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 14 mg/dL
Chest CT: bilateral pulmonary nodules, two with cavitation
Sinus mucosal biopsy: necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with vasculitis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this patient with organ-threatening (renal and pulmonary) GPA, what is the appropriate induction therapy?
options: High-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab (or cyclophosphamide)
Methotrexate monotherapy
Long-term broad-spectrum antibiotics
Intranasal corticosteroids and saline rinses only
stages.explanation: Severe, organ-threatening GPA (glomerulonephritis, pulmonary involvement) is treated with remission-induction using high-dose glucocorticoids combined with either rituximab or cyclophosphamide; plasma exchange may be considered in severe renal disease or alveolar hemorrhage. Methotrexate is reserved for limited/non-organ-threatening disease, not severe renal involvement. Antibiotics such as trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole have a role in limited upper-airway disease and PCP prophylaxis but are not induction therapy for severe vasculitis. Topical nasal therapy addresses only local symptoms and would be dangerously inadequate here. Prompt immunosuppression preserves renal function and prevents death.
stages.hint: Organ-threatening vasculitis needs steroids plus a potent immunosuppressant, not antibiotics.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is most important to prevent or monitor for once immunosuppressive induction is started?
options: Opportunistic infection (e.g., Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia)
Hyperthyroidism
Deep vein thrombosis from the medications
Gout flare
stages.explanation: High-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide cause profound immunosuppression, so opportunistic infection, especially Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, is a major risk; prophylaxis (commonly trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is standard during induction. Hyperthyroidism is unrelated to this regimen. While glucocorticoids and active vasculitis modestly raise thrombosis risk, DVT is not the principal preventable complication that drives routine prophylaxis here. Gout is not a primary concern of induction therapy. Anticipating and preventing opportunistic infection is a core part of safe management of severe GPA.
stages.hint: Powerful immunosuppression opens the door to a specific pneumonia you can prevent.

cases.diagnosis: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener) with sinonasal, pulmonary, and renal involvement
pearls: GPA classically combines upper-airway disease (crusting, septal perforation, saddle nose, serous otitis), lung nodules/hemoptysis, and pauci-immune glomerulonephritis.
c-ANCA against PR3 is the characteristic serology; biopsy shows necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis.
Cocaine-induced midline destruction is a key mimic — ask about intranasal drug use and check ANCA.
Organ-threatening disease is induced with glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide, with PCP prophylaxis.

cases.title: Sore Throat and Fever in a Schoolgirl
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden sore throat and fever for 2 days.
cases.history: A 12-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother for 2 days of sore throat, fever, and difficulty swallowing. The sore throat began abruptly and she has had fevers measured to 38.9 °C at home. She has pain on swallowing, decreased appetite, and a headache. Importantly, she has NO cough, no runny nose, no hoarseness, and no conjunctivitis. A classmate was recently diagnosed with 'strep throat.' She also complained of a brief episode of abdominal pain. PMH: healthy, immunizations up to date. Medications: acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. She lives with her parents and one sibling; attends 7th grade. No recent travel. No known sick contacts other than the classmate. No rash noted by mother, though she did mention the girl's tongue looked 'bumpy.' Family history noncontributory. She has not been able to attend school for 2 days.
cases.exam: Mildly uncomfortable but non-toxic, well-hydrated. Oropharynx: erythematous tonsils enlarged with patchy white-yellow exudate; no asymmetry, no uvular deviation, no trismus. Tender, enlarged anterior cervical lymph nodes bilaterally. No cough during the visit, no rhinorrhea, no conjunctival injection. Voice normal (no hot-potato voice). Tympanic membranes normal. Neck supple. Skin without rash today; tongue with prominent papillae. Lungs clear; abdomen soft with no hepatosplenomegaly. No posterior cervical adenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Using the Centor (McIsaac) criteria, which combination of this patient's findings supports Group A streptococcal pharyngitis?
options: Cough present, tonsillar exudate, tender nodes
Fever, tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical nodes, absence of cough, age 12
Rhinorrhea, conjunctivitis, fever
Hoarseness, exudate, posterior cervical nodes
stages.explanation: Centor/McIsaac criteria award points for fever (>38 °C), tonsillar exudates, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, absence of cough, and age 3-14 (+1). This patient meets all of these, yielding a high score and a higher pretest probability of GAS pharyngitis. Cough present actually argues against GAS and reduces the score. Rhinorrhea and conjunctivitis are viral features that argue against strep. Hoarseness and posterior cervical adenopathy suggest viral causes (and posterior nodes are more typical of mononucleosis). The viral-feature absence plus exudate, fever, anterior nodes, and young age fit GAS.
stages.hint: Strep favors fever, exudate, tender anterior nodes, young age, and NO cough.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis in a child?
options: Treat empirically with antibiotics without testing
Rapid antigen detection test, and if negative, confirm with throat culture
Monospot (heterophile antibody) test
No testing; symptomatic care only
stages.explanation: In children, a rapid antigen detection test (RADT) is performed, and because RADT sensitivity is imperfect, a negative result should be confirmed with a throat culture (the back-up culture is recommended in children/adolescents). Empiric antibiotics without testing is discouraged because clinical criteria alone overtreat viral pharyngitis. A Monospot is for suspected mononucleosis, suggested by posterior adenopathy, splenomegaly, and marked fatigue, not this presentation. No testing at all risks missing GAS and its complications. Confirming GAS guides appropriate, targeted antibiotic use.
stages.hint: Do the rapid test, but in kids back up a negative with culture.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Rapid antigen detection test (RADT): POSITIVE for Group A Streptococcus
Throat culture: not required given positive RADT
CBC: not obtained

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line antibiotic for confirmed Group A streptococcal pharyngitis in a child without penicillin allergy?
options: Oral penicillin V (or amoxicillin)
Azithromycin
Ciprofloxacin
Vancomycin
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for GAS pharyngitis is oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days; there has been no documented penicillin resistance in GAS, and treatment prevents acute rheumatic fever and suppurative complications. Azithromycin is reserved for penicillin-allergic patients and faces rising macrolide resistance. Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone with no role in routine GAS pharyngitis and is generally avoided in children. Vancomycin is unnecessary and inappropriate for this susceptible organism. Completing the full course is important to eradicate the organism and prevent rheumatic fever.
stages.hint: GAS has never developed resistance to this oldest antibiotic class.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Adequate antibiotic treatment of GAS pharyngitis primarily prevents which complication?
options: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Acute rheumatic fever
Reactive arthritis
Dental caries
stages.explanation: Timely antibiotic treatment of GAS pharyngitis prevents acute rheumatic fever, a major nonsuppurative complication; it also reduces suppurative complications (peritonsillar abscess) and shortens symptoms/transmission. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is generally NOT prevented by antibiotic treatment of pharyngitis. Reactive arthritis is associated with other infections (e.g., enteric/GU) and is not the primary target here. Dental caries is unrelated. The key teaching point is that antibiotics for strep throat prevent rheumatic fever but do not reliably prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
stages.hint: Treatment prevents the heart-threatening sequela, not the kidney one.

pearls: Centor/McIsaac criteria: fever, tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical nodes, absence of cough, age 3-14 — higher scores raise GAS probability.
In children, confirm a negative rapid antigen test with a throat culture; avoid purely empiric treatment.
First-line therapy is penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days; GAS remains universally penicillin-susceptible.
Antibiotics prevent acute rheumatic fever but do not reliably prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

cases.title: Muffled Voice and a Jaw That Won't Open
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe one-sided sore throat, trouble opening his mouth, and a muffled voice.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student presents to the ED with 4 days of worsening sore throat, now severe and localized to the left side. For the last day he has had difficulty opening his mouth, painful swallowing with drooling, a muffled 'hot-potato' voice, and pain radiating to the left ear. He started a sore throat with fevers earlier in the week; his roommate said he had 'strep' and he took a few leftover antibiotics without improvement. He has had decreased oral intake due to pain. He denies difficulty breathing or stridor but says swallowing his own saliva hurts. PMH: recurrent tonsillitis as a teenager. Medications: a few doses of leftover amoxicillin. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends, vapes nicotine, no other drugs. Lives in a dormitory. No recent travel. Family history noncontributory. He is anxious and uncomfortable, leaning forward and spitting into a cup.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, febrile, with audible muffled voice and pooling of saliva. Trismus limits mouth opening to ~2 cm. Oropharynx: marked swelling of the left soft palate and peritonsillar region with the left tonsil displaced inferomedially; the uvula is deviated to the right. Tender, enlarged left cervical lymph nodes. No tongue protrusion deviation, no floor-of-mouth induration, no neck crepitus. No respiratory distress, no stridor; lungs clear. Tympanic membranes normal. Neck supple aside from tender adenopathy. No drooling-related airway compromise at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings is most specific for a peritonsillar abscess rather than uncomplicated tonsillitis?
options: Bilateral tonsillar exudate and fever
Trismus, uvular deviation, and a muffled 'hot-potato' voice
Cough and rhinorrhea
Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy
stages.explanation: Peritonsillar abscess characteristically produces trismus (from pterygoid muscle irritation), deviation of the uvula away from the affected side, inferomedial displacement of the tonsil, and a muffled 'hot-potato' voice, often with unilateral severe pain and drooling. Bilateral exudate with fever describes typical pharyngitis/tonsillitis without abscess localization. Cough and rhinorrhea suggest a viral upper respiratory infection. Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy is more typical of mononucleosis. The unilateral peritonsillar bulge with uvular deviation and trismus is the hallmark of an abscess.
stages.hint: Look for the one-sided bulge pushing the uvula across and the jaw that won't open.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: In a cooperative patient with classic findings, how is the diagnosis of peritonsillar abscess typically made?
options: Clinical examination, with imaging (CT or intraoral ultrasound) reserved for unclear cases
Routine contrast CT of the neck for all patients
Lateral neck radiograph
Throat culture alone
stages.explanation: Peritonsillar abscess is usually a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic unilateral findings; imaging such as contrast CT or intraoral ultrasound is reserved for atypical presentations, when the exam is limited by trismus, when deeper neck space infection is suspected, or to distinguish abscess from cellulitis (peritonsillitis). Routine CT for all is unnecessary and exposes patients to radiation/cost when the exam is classic. A lateral neck radiograph is more useful for epiglottitis or retropharyngeal processes. Throat culture alone does not establish an abscess. Confident clinical diagnosis allows prompt drainage.
stages.hint: The classic exam usually suffices; imaging is for the ambiguous cases.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of this peritonsillar abscess?
options: Oral antibiotics and discharge with follow-up
Drainage (needle aspiration or incision and drainage) plus antibiotics covering streptococci and oral anaerobes
Immediate tonsillectomy under general anesthesia for all cases
IV corticosteroids alone
stages.explanation: Peritonsillar abscess is managed with drainage, either needle aspiration or incision and drainage (both are acceptable first-line options), combined with antibiotics targeting streptococci and oral anaerobes (e.g., ampicillin-sulbactam or a penicillin plus metronidazole; clindamycin if penicillin-allergic). Oral antibiotics with discharge alone are inadequate without source control of a true abscess. Immediate ('quinsy') tonsillectomy is reserved for select cases (failed drainage, recurrent disease, complications, or young children needing general anesthesia), not all patients. IV steroids alone may reduce edema/pain adjunctively but do not drain pus. Drainage plus appropriate antibiotics is the standard.
stages.hint: Pus needs to come out; pair drainage with anaerobe-active antibiotics.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most increase concern for spread to a deep neck space (e.g., parapharyngeal) requiring CT and possible surgical drainage?
options: Improvement of trismus after drainage
Neck swelling with torticollis, worsening neck pain, and signs of airway compromise
Mild residual sore throat after aspiration
Low-grade fever resolving on antibiotics
stages.explanation: Extension into deep neck spaces is suggested by lateral neck swelling, torticollis, worsening or new neck pain, trismus that does not improve, and any airway compromise; these warrant contrast CT of the neck and possible operative drainage, with attention to complications like Lemierre syndrome or mediastinitis. Improving trismus after drainage indicates a favorable response. Mild residual sore throat and a resolving low-grade fever are expected during recovery. Recognizing deterioration and deep-space spread is essential because these infections can rapidly threaten the airway and life.
stages.hint: Worsening neck signs and airway trouble after drainage mean the infection may have spread deeper.

pearls: Peritonsillar abscess presents with trismus, uvular deviation away from the lesion, inferomedial tonsil displacement, and a hot-potato voice.
Diagnosis is usually clinical; reserve CT or intraoral ultrasound for atypical or exam-limited cases.
Treat with drainage (needle aspiration or I&D) plus antibiotics covering streptococci and oral anaerobes.
Watch for deep neck space spread, airway compromise, and Lemierre syndrome.

cases.title: Exhausting Sore Throat That Won't Quit
cases.chiefComplaint: A week of severe sore throat, swollen glands, and overwhelming fatigue.
cases.history: A 17-year-old high-school student presents with 7 days of sore throat, low-grade fevers, and profound fatigue that keeps her in bed most of the day. She has swollen, tender glands in her neck and a poor appetite. A clinician 3 days ago diagnosed 'strep,' prescribed amoxicillin, and yesterday she broke out in a diffuse, itchy maculopapular rash. She reports mild left upper abdominal discomfort. She denies cough, rhinorrhea, or shortness of breath. She is on the varsity soccer team and asks when she can return to play. PMH: healthy, fully immunized. Medications: amoxicillin (started 3 days ago), acetaminophen. No known drug allergies (this is her first amoxicillin rash). She has a steady boyfriend; she drinks occasionally at parties. Lives with her parents. No recent travel. Family history noncontributory. She is concerned about missing school and the upcoming championship game.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing but non-toxic. Oropharynx: symmetrically enlarged tonsils with grayish-white exudate; palatal petechiae. Tender, enlarged posterior AND anterior cervical lymph nodes; also mild bilateral lymphadenopathy. A faint, diffuse blanching maculopapular rash over the trunk and arms. Mild splenomegaly palpable below the left costal margin; no marked tenderness. Mild hepatomegaly. No jaundice. No respiratory distress; lungs clear. No periorbital edema. No focal neuro deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A maculopapular rash after amoxicillin in a teenager with exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly most suggests which diagnosis?
options: True penicillin (IgE-mediated) allergy
Infectious mononucleosis (EBV)
Scarlet fever
Acute HIV seroconversion
stages.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis from Epstein-Barr virus classically causes exudative pharyngitis, posterior (and anterior) cervical lymphadenopathy, marked fatigue, and splenomegaly; administration of amoxicillin/ampicillin commonly triggers a non-allergic morbilliform rash, exactly as seen here. A true IgE-mediated penicillin allergy typically causes urticaria/anaphylaxis and is not what the EBV-associated maculopapular rash represents. Scarlet fever produces a sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and lacks splenomegaly and posterior adenopathy. Acute HIV can mimic mono but the amoxicillin-rash association plus splenomegaly and posterior nodes point to EBV. The rash does not indicate a lifelong penicillin allergy.
stages.hint: Why would a 'strep' patient get a rash specifically after amoxicillin?

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best supports the diagnosis of infectious mononucleosis?
options: Heterophile antibody (Monospot) test, often with CBC showing atypical lymphocytes
Rapid strep antigen test
Throat culture
Blood culture
stages.explanation: The heterophile antibody (Monospot) test is the usual initial test for infectious mononucleosis and is supported by a CBC showing lymphocytosis with atypical (reactive) lymphocytes; EBV-specific serologies (VCA IgM/IgG, EBNA) can confirm when the heterophile test is negative, especially early or in young children. A rapid strep antigen test and throat culture evaluate for GAS, which can coexist but does not explain splenomegaly, posterior adenopathy, and the amoxicillin rash. Blood culture is for bacteremia and is not indicated here. Note the Monospot may be falsely negative in the first week, prompting EBV serology if suspicion remains.
stages.hint: Look for the heterophile antibody and reactive lymphocytes on the smear.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Heterophile (Monospot) antibody: POSITIVE
CBC: WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL with lymphocytosis; >10% atypical lymphocytes
AST 78 U/L, ALT 92 U/L (mild transaminitis)
Rapid strep antigen: negative
Ultrasound abdomen: spleen mildly enlarged (13 cm)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for uncomplicated infectious mononucleosis?
options: Supportive care (rest, fluids, analgesics) and avoid contact sports until spleen recovery
A 10-day course of amoxicillin
Start acyclovir to shorten the illness
Oral corticosteroids for all patients
stages.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis is managed supportively with rest, hydration, and analgesics/antipyretics; patients must avoid contact and strenuous sports for at least 3-4 weeks (and until clinically recovered) because of the risk of splenic rupture. Continuing amoxicillin is wrong: it is unnecessary for a viral illness and caused the rash. Acyclovir reduces viral shedding but does not meaningfully improve clinical outcomes and is not routinely recommended. Corticosteroids are reserved for specific complications such as impending airway obstruction from tonsillar hypertrophy, severe hemolytic anemia, or thrombocytopenia, not routine cases. Activity restriction to protect the spleen is the critical counseling point.
stages.hint: It's viral — support, rest, and protect that enlarged spleen.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most important counseling point regarding her return to soccer?
options: She may return to play immediately once the fever resolves
She must avoid contact/strenuous sports for at least 3-4 weeks due to splenic rupture risk
She should avoid sports for a full year regardless of recovery
She can play if she takes prophylactic antibiotics
stages.explanation: Because splenomegaly predisposes to splenic rupture, patients should avoid contact and strenuous sports for at least 3-4 weeks from symptom onset, returning only after clinical recovery (and per some protocols, documented resolution of splenomegaly for high-contact athletes). Immediate return once afebrile ignores the persistent splenic enlargement and rupture risk. A blanket one-year restriction is excessive and not evidence-based. Prophylactic antibiotics do nothing for a viral illness or splenic risk and are inappropriate. Clear activity counseling prevents a potentially life-threatening complication in this young athlete.
stages.hint: The spleen, not the fever, dictates when she can safely return to contact play.

cases.diagnosis: Infectious mononucleosis (Epstein-Barr virus) with amoxicillin-induced rash
pearls: EBV mononucleosis: exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, profound fatigue, and splenomegaly.
Amoxicillin/ampicillin commonly triggers a non-allergic morbilliform rash in EBV infection — it does not mean true penicillin allergy.
Diagnose with a heterophile (Monospot) test and CBC showing atypical lymphocytes; use EBV serology if early/negative.
Management is supportive; avoid contact/strenuous sports for at least 3-4 weeks to prevent splenic rupture.

cases.title: Toddler Who Won't Move His Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: Neck stiffness, drooling, and refusal to eat for two days.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his parents for two days of progressively worsening neck stiffness, fever, and refusal to eat or drink. He had an upper respiratory infection with sore throat about a week ago that seemed to be improving. Over the last day he has been holding his neck rigidly, cries when his mother tries to turn his head, and has begun drooling because swallowing hurts. His voice sounds muffled. He is up to date on immunizations, including Hib and PCV13. Past medical history is unremarkable; he was born at term with no birth complications. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He attends daycare three days a week, where several children have had colds. He lives with both parents and an older sibling, neither of whom is ill. Family history is noncontributory. Parents deny any choking episode, trauma, or foreign-body ingestion, and report no rash or recent dental work. Oral intake over the past 12 hours has been minimal, and his last wet diaper was roughly 8 hours ago.
cases.exam: The child is ill-appearing, irritable, and sitting upright holding his neck in slight extension with limited rotation. There is tender bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy and mild nuchal rigidity localized to the neck rather than meningismus. Oropharynx shows midline-to-mild bulging of the posterior pharyngeal wall without unilateral tonsillar deviation; tonsils are symmetric. There is pooling of secretions and drooling. Voice is muffled but he is not in respiratory distress, with no stridor at rest and clear breath sounds bilaterally. Trismus is minimal. No skin rash, no Kernig or Brudzinski sign, and pupils are equal and reactive.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial step in evaluating this child?
options: Forcefully examine the posterior pharynx with a tongue depressor to look for an abscess
Obtain a lateral soft-tissue neck radiograph in inspiration with the neck extended
Perform an immediate lumbar puncture to rule out meningitis
Discharge with oral amoxicillin and reassurance for presumed viral pharyngitis
stages.explanation: A lateral soft-tissue neck radiograph (taken in inspiration with neck extended to avoid pseudothickening) is a quick, low-risk screen for widening of the prevertebral/retropharyngeal space, the classic sign of retropharyngeal abscess (RPA). It guides the need for CT and surgical consultation. Aggressive instrumentation of the pharynx risks abscess rupture, aspiration, and airway compromise and should be avoided. Lumbar puncture is wrong: nuchal rigidity here reflects neck-space inflammation, not meningitis, and there are no meningeal signs. Discharge on oral antibiotics is unsafe given the toxic appearance, drooling, and high fever, which point to a deep neck-space infection requiring inpatient evaluation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Lateral soft-tissue neck radiograph (neck extended, inspiration): prevertebral soft-tissue width at C2 = 12 mm (normal <7 mm), with anterior displacement of the airway
No radiopaque foreign body identified
Chest radiograph: clear lung fields, no widened mediastinum
Lateral film also notes loss of normal cervical lordosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study best confirms the diagnosis and defines whether a drainable collection is present?
options: Plain anteroposterior neck radiograph
Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck
Ultrasound of the thyroid
MRI brain with and without contrast
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck is the gold standard for retropharyngeal abscess: it distinguishes a rim-enhancing, organized abscess (which usually needs drainage) from retropharyngeal cellulitis/phlegmon (often managed with IV antibiotics alone) and delineates spread toward the mediastinum or great vessels. The AP radiograph adds little beyond the lateral film and cannot characterize a fluid collection. Thyroid ultrasound evaluates the wrong structure and is poor for deep retropharyngeal spaces in a crying child. MRI brain does not image the neck soft tissues of interest and is impractical/slow in an acutely ill toddler who may need airway control.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
CRP 92 mg/L
Na 137 mEq/L
Contrast-enhanced CT neck: 2.2 cm rim-enhancing low-density collection in the retropharyngeal space from C2–C4 with surrounding edema; airway patent but mildly narrowed; no mediastinal extension; carotid sheath uninvolved

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides ENT consultation for possible drainage, what is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?
options: Oral amoxicillin alone
IV ampicillin-sulbactam (or clindamycin) covering streptococci, oral anaerobes, and S. aureus
IV acyclovir for presumed herpetic infection
IV vancomycin monotherapy
stages.explanation: Retropharyngeal abscesses are typically polymicrobial, involving Group A Streptococcus, Staphylococcus aureus, and oral anaerobes. IV ampicillin-sulbactam (or clindamycin in penicillin-allergic patients), with MRSA coverage such as vancomycin added if local prevalence or severity warrants, provides appropriate empiric coverage. Oral amoxicillin is inadequate for a deep neck-space abscess and misses anaerobes and staph. Acyclovir treats viral infection, which is not the pathophysiology here. Vancomycin monotherapy covers MRSA but leaves anaerobes and many streptococci/gram-negatives undertreated, so it is insufficient as a single agent.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication of an untreated or spreading retropharyngeal abscess is most immediately life-threatening?
options: Cervical osteomyelitis
Descending necrotizing mediastinitis with airway compromise
Chronic cervical lymphadenopathy
Recurrent otitis media
stages.explanation: The retropharyngeal space communicates inferiorly with the 'danger space' and mediastinum, so infection can descend to cause necrotizing mediastinitis, a rapidly fatal complication; concurrent airway compromise from mass effect and edema is the other immediate threat. Cervical osteomyelitis can occur but evolves over a longer time course and is not the acute killer. Chronic lymphadenopathy is a benign, non-emergent finding. Recurrent otitis media is unrelated to deep neck-space infection. Recognizing the mediastinal/airway danger drives early imaging, airway readiness, and prompt drainage.

pearls: Lateral neck film showing prevertebral soft tissue >7 mm at C2 (or >14 mm at C6) suggests retropharyngeal space widening; obtain in extension/inspiration to avoid false positives.
Contrast CT distinguishes a drainable abscess from cellulitis/phlegmon and screens for mediastinal/vascular extension.
Avoid blind, forceful pharyngeal instrumentation, which can rupture the abscess and precipitate aspiration or airway crisis.
RPA is most common in children under 5 and is typically polymicrobial; treat with IV anaerobe- and staph-active antibiotics plus surgical drainage when organized.

cases.title: Swollen Mouth Floor After a Toothache
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly worsening swelling under the jaw and trouble swallowing his own saliva.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man presents to the ED with two days of worsening pain and swelling in the floor of his mouth and under both sides of his jaw. It began as a left lower molar toothache he had been meaning to get fixed for weeks. Over the last 12 hours the swelling has spread to both submandibular regions, his tongue feels pushed up, and he now has difficulty swallowing and a 'hot potato' voice. He reports fever and chills. He has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 9.4%) and takes metformin 1000 mg twice daily. He has no known drug allergies. He has not seen a dentist in several years and has visibly poor dentition. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and drinks alcohol socially. He denies trauma, foreign body, or prior neck infections. Family history is noncontributory. He is sitting forward, anxious, and reluctant to lie flat. He has not been able to eat since yesterday and is now spitting saliva into a cup because swallowing is too painful.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious and sitting upright, leaning slightly forward, with audible 'hot potato' voice and pooling secretions. There is tense, tender, brawny bilateral submandibular and submental swelling that is firm to palpation (woody induration) without fluctuance. The floor of the mouth is elevated and erythematous, displacing the tongue upward and posteriorly. Trismus is present, limiting mouth opening. A carious, tender left mandibular molar is noted. No stridor at rest, but he becomes more dyspneic when supine. Trachea is midline; no overt crepitus. Lungs are clear bilaterally. No facial palsy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important priority in the immediate management of this patient?
options: Order a contrast-enhanced CT of the neck before any other intervention
Assess and secure the airway, anticipating a difficult airway and involving anesthesia/ENT early
Incise and drain the submandibular swelling at the bedside
Start oral clindamycin and arrange outpatient dental follow-up
stages.explanation: Ludwig angina is a rapidly progressive, bilateral cellulitis of the submandibular/sublingual/submental spaces that kills by airway obstruction from posterosuperior tongue displacement. The first priority is airway assessment and securing it early, ideally awake fiberoptic intubation or a surgical airway with ENT/anesthesia present, because the airway can deteriorate suddenly. Sending an unstable patient to CT before airway control risks losing the airway in the scanner. Bedside I&D is usually unhelpful early because this is a cellulitis without organized pus and may worsen things. Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up is dangerously inadequate for an airway-threatening deep-space infection.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly distinguishes Ludwig angina from a simple dental abscess or peritonsillar abscess?
options: Unilateral tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation
Bilateral woody induration of the floor of mouth with tongue elevation, without fluctuance
Isolated fluctuant swelling over a single carious tooth
Posterior pharyngeal wall bulging on the lateral neck film
stages.explanation: Ludwig angina is defined by bilateral, brawny (woody) induration of the submandibular and sublingual spaces with elevation and posterior displacement of the tongue and floor of mouth; it is a spreading cellulitis, so it characteristically lacks fluctuance early. Unilateral tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation describes peritonsillar abscess. An isolated fluctuant swelling over one tooth is a localized dental/periapical abscess, not the diffuse bilateral process of Ludwig. Posterior pharyngeal wall bulging on a lateral film suggests retropharyngeal abscess, a different deep-space infection. The bilateral, non-fluctuant, floor-of-mouth process is the hallmark.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 21.4 ×10³/µL (left shift)
Glucose 318 mg/dL
HbA1c 9.4%
Lactate 2.6 mmol/L
Contrast-enhanced CT neck (after airway secured): diffuse cellulitis and edema of bilateral submandibular and sublingual spaces with a small developing collection near the left molar; gas locules in soft tissue; airway narrowed but patent

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After the airway is secured, what is the most appropriate antimicrobial and source-control strategy?
options: Oral amoxicillin and discharge once the airway looks stable
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering streptococci, anaerobes, and gram-positives, plus surgical drainage and dental source control
IV antifungal therapy alone
Antiviral therapy and supportive care only
stages.explanation: Ludwig angina is polymicrobial (viridans streptococci, anaerobes, and staphylococci from odontogenic flora). After airway control, treat with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics such as ampicillin-sulbactam or a combination like ceftriaxone plus metronidazole (add MRSA coverage as indicated), and pursue source control: surgical decompression/drainage of any collection and removal/treatment of the offending tooth. Oral amoxicillin with discharge is grossly inadequate and unsafe for an airway-threatening infection. Antifungals alone target the wrong pathogens. Antivirals with supportive care ignore the bacterial etiology and the need for source control. Glycemic control is also essential in this diabetic patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient after initial stabilization?
options: Discharge home with oral antibiotics and next-day dental appointment
Admit to a general medical floor for observation without airway monitoring
Admit to the ICU with a secured/monitored airway and serial reassessment
Transfer to a psychiatric unit for anxiety management
stages.explanation: A patient with Ludwig angina and a tenuous, recently secured airway requires ICU-level monitoring for airway protection, frequent reassessment, IV antibiotics, glucose control, and coordination of surgical/dental source control. Discharge home is unsafe given the airway risk and severity. A general floor without airway monitoring is inappropriate because deterioration can be abrupt and lethal. A psychiatric unit misattributes his anxiety, which is driven by air hunger and a genuine airway threat rather than a primary psychiatric problem. ICU admission ensures the patient is in the safest setting for ongoing airway vigilance.

pearls: Ludwig angina is a bilateral submandibular/sublingual cellulitis, usually odontogenic from a lower molar; airway loss from tongue elevation is the main killer.
Airway first: prepare for a difficult airway with awake fiberoptic intubation and surgical backup before imaging.
It is typically a non-fluctuant cellulitis early, so treatment centers on IV broad-spectrum antibiotics plus source control (drainage and dental extraction).
Diabetes and immunocompromise increase risk and severity; aggressive glycemic control supports recovery.

cases.title: Loud Snoring and Falling Asleep at His Desk
cases.chiefComplaint: Daytime sleepiness and loud snoring with breathing pauses noticed by his wife.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man comes to clinic at his wife's insistence because of loud, habitual snoring and episodes where she has watched him stop breathing and then gasp awake during the night. He reports waking unrefreshed, morning headaches, and falling asleep during meetings and once while stopped at a red light. He has gained about 20 pounds over three years. Past medical history includes hypertension (on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily, still not at goal) and prediabetes. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two or three beers most evenings and does not smoke. He works as an accountant and rarely exercises. Family history includes a brother with sleep apnea and a father with early coronary disease. He denies cataplexy, sleep paralysis, or hypnagogic hallucinations. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 15. He has noticed decreased concentration and irritability and is concerned about driving safety. Caffeine intake is high to compensate for fatigue.
cases.exam: Obese man (BMI 35 kg/m²) with a neck circumference of 44 cm. Oropharyngeal exam shows a crowded airway with a low-lying soft palate and a Mallampati class IV view; tonsils are small (1+). There is mild retrognathia and a large tongue base. Nasal passages are patent with mild septal deviation. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable with regular rhythm and clear lungs; there is no jugular venous distension or peripheral edema. No focal neurologic deficits and normal mood on brief screen. No goiter or facial plethora.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Start empiric modafinil for daytime sleepiness without further testing
Order in-laboratory polysomnography (or validated home sleep apnea testing) to confirm the diagnosis and quantify severity
Obtain a daytime arterial blood gas as the primary diagnostic test
Refer directly for uvulopalatopharyngoplasty
stages.explanation: The triad of habitual snoring, witnessed apneas, and daytime hypersomnolence with obesity and a crowded airway strongly suggests obstructive sleep apnea. The confirmatory test is polysomnography, which measures the apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) and severity; home sleep apnea testing is acceptable in uncomplicated, high-pretest-probability adults. Empiric modafinil treats sleepiness without diagnosing or treating the underlying obstruction and could mask a dangerous condition. A daytime ABG is insensitive and does not diagnose OSA; it may be considered if hypoventilation is suspected. Jumping to surgery is premature before the diagnosis is confirmed and severity defined.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDY
stages.content:
Attended polysomnography:
Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) 38 events/hour (predominantly obstructive)
Lowest oxygen saturation (nadir) 81%
Arousal index elevated; sleep efficiency reduced
No features of central sleep apnea or periodic limb movements

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is the severity of this patient's obstructive sleep apnea best classified?
options: Mild OSA
Moderate OSA
Severe OSA
Normal study, no OSA
stages.explanation: OSA severity is graded by the AHI: mild = 5–14, moderate = 15–29, and severe = 30 or more events per hour. This patient's AHI of 38, with a desaturation nadir of 81%, places him firmly in the severe category. 'Mild' and 'moderate' understate his disease and would misguide urgency of therapy. 'Normal' is incorrect because an AHI of 38 is far above the threshold of 5. Accurate severity classification matters because severe OSA carries higher cardiovascular and accident risk and strengthens the indication for prompt, effective therapy such as CPAP.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for this patient with severe obstructive sleep apnea?
options: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) plus weight loss counseling
Oral appliance (mandibular advancement device) as sole therapy
Supplemental nighttime oxygen alone
Tracheostomy
stages.explanation: CPAP is the first-line, most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe OSA; it pneumatically splints the upper airway open and reliably lowers the AHI, improves sleepiness, and helps blood pressure. It should be paired with weight loss, alcohol reduction, and positional measures. An oral appliance is better suited to mild-to-moderate OSA or CPAP-intolerant patients and is less effective for severe disease as monotherapy. Nighttime oxygen alone does not relieve the mechanical obstruction and can prolong apneas. Tracheostomy is a definitive but highly invasive last resort reserved for severe, refractory cases, not first-line therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term complication is most strongly associated with untreated severe obstructive sleep apnea?
options: Resistant hypertension and increased cardiovascular/stroke risk
Peptic ulcer disease
Primary hypothyroidism
Glaucoma
stages.explanation: Untreated OSA causes intermittent hypoxia, sympathetic surges, and sleep fragmentation that drive resistant hypertension and raise the risk of atrial fibrillation, coronary disease, stroke, and heart failure; it also contributes to metabolic dysregulation and motor-vehicle accidents from sleepiness. His difficult-to-control blood pressure despite two agents is a classic clue. Peptic ulcer disease is unrelated to OSA pathophysiology. Hypothyroidism can worsen OSA but is not a consequence of it. Glaucoma has only weak, inconsistent associations and is not the principal long-term risk. Effective therapy mitigates these cardiovascular and accident risks.

pearls: Suspect OSA with the triad of snoring, witnessed apneas, and daytime hypersomnolence, especially with obesity, large neck circumference, and a crowded oropharynx.
Polysomnography confirms the diagnosis; AHI grades severity (mild 5–14, moderate 15–29, severe ≥30).
CPAP is first-line for moderate-to-severe OSA; oral appliances suit mild-to-moderate or CPAP-intolerant patients.
Untreated OSA drives resistant hypertension, atrial fibrillation, stroke, and motor-vehicle accidents — counsel on driving safety.

cases.title: A Mouth-Breathing Boy Who Can't Sit Still
cases.chiefComplaint: Chronic snoring, mouth breathing, and recent behavioral and school problems.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for loud snoring nearly every night, persistent mouth breathing, and restless sleep with occasional gasping. He frequently wakes unrefreshed, is difficult to rouse for school, and his teacher reports inattention, hyperactivity, and declining performance that the school suspects may be ADHD. He has a chronically open-mouth posture and a hyponasal voice and gets recurrent sore throats. He is otherwise healthy, born full term, fully immunized, and on no daily medications. He has no known drug allergies. He has had several episodes of tonsillitis over the past two years but no prior surgery. He lives with both parents and a younger sibling; no household smokers. Family history includes a father who snores. His mother reports she sometimes sees him pause his breathing during sleep and that he is a very restless sleeper who sweats at night. Growth has been along the lower percentiles recently after previously tracking higher.
cases.exam: Alert, fidgety child breathing through his mouth with an 'adenoid facies' (open-mouth posture, long midface). Oropharyngeal exam shows markedly enlarged, nearly touching tonsils (3+ to 4+/kissing tonsils) without exudate. Voice is hyponasal. Nasal exam suggests posterior obstruction with mouth breathing; no acute purulent rhinorrhea. Tympanic membranes are normal. Neck has small, soft, mobile lymph nodes without significant tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with no murmur, and there is no clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. No stridor at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely underlying cause of this child's snoring, mouth breathing, and behavioral changes?
options: Primary attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder
Adenotonsillar hypertrophy causing pediatric obstructive sleep apnea
Allergic rhinitis alone
Hypothyroidism
stages.explanation: In children, the most common cause of obstructive sleep-disordered breathing is adenotonsillar hypertrophy, and pediatric OSA classically presents with snoring, mouth breathing, restless sleep, witnessed pauses, and daytime neurobehavioral changes (hyperactivity, inattention, poor school performance) rather than pure sleepiness. Kissing tonsils and adenoid facies support this. Primary ADHD can mimic the behavior but does not explain the snoring, apneas, and obstructive exam findings, which must be addressed first. Allergic rhinitis may contribute but does not account for kissing tonsils and witnessed apneas. Hypothyroidism is far less likely given the normal vitals and classic obstructive findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the gold-standard test to confirm obstructive sleep apnea in this child if there is diagnostic uncertainty or before surgery in select cases?
options: Lateral neck radiograph
Overnight polysomnography (sleep study)
Throat culture
Daytime pulse oximetry spot check
stages.explanation: Overnight polysomnography is the gold standard for confirming and grading pediatric OSA, distinguishing it from primary snoring and quantifying severity; it is especially useful when the diagnosis is uncertain or in children with comorbidities. A lateral neck radiograph can show adenoid size but does not diagnose OSA or measure obstruction during sleep. A throat culture evaluates for streptococcal pharyngitis, not sleep-disordered breathing. A single daytime oximetry check is insensitive because obstructive events occur during sleep and may be missed entirely. In many otherwise-healthy children with classic findings and obvious hypertrophy, treatment may proceed without PSG, but PSG remains the confirmatory standard.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDY
stages.content:
Pediatric polysomnography:
Obstructive Apnea-Hypopnea Index (oAHI) 9 events/hour (normal in children <1–1.5)
Oxygen saturation nadir 88%
Frequent obstructive events with arousals
No central apneas

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment for this child with adenotonsillar hypertrophy and confirmed obstructive sleep apnea?
options: Adenotonsillectomy
Lifelong CPAP as first-line therapy
Stimulant medication for presumed ADHD
Watchful waiting with no intervention for at least two years
stages.explanation: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for pediatric OSA caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy and frequently resolves snoring, normalizes sleep, and improves the neurobehavioral and growth disturbances. CPAP is reserved for children who are poor surgical candidates, have residual OSA after surgery, or have non-adenotonsillar causes; it is not first-line here. Starting a stimulant for presumed ADHD treats a symptom while ignoring the treatable airway obstruction and may be unnecessary once OSA is corrected. Indefinite watchful waiting is inappropriate given an oAHI of 9 with desaturations and clear daytime consequences; brief observation is only reasonable for very mild disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate postoperative counseling point for the family after adenotonsillectomy?
options: Watch for postoperative bleeding and dehydration, and expect improvement in snoring and behavior over the following weeks
The child will require lifelong nightly CPAP regardless of outcome
Antibiotics for six weeks are mandatory to prevent recurrence
Tonsils always grow back, so repeat surgery is expected within a year
stages.explanation: After adenotonsillectomy, families should be counseled to watch for postoperative hemorrhage (primary within 24 hours, secondary around days 5–10) and to ensure adequate hydration and analgesia, while expecting gradual improvement in snoring, sleep quality, and daytime behavior/attention over the following weeks. Mandatory lifelong CPAP is incorrect because surgery is usually curative for adenotonsillar OSA. A mandatory six-week antibiotic course is not standard and does not prevent recurrence. The claim that tonsils always regrow requiring yearly surgery is false; significant regrowth needing reoperation is uncommon. Realistic expectations and bleeding precautions are the key teaching points.

cases.diagnosis: Adenotonsillar hypertrophy with pediatric obstructive sleep apnea
pearls: Pediatric OSA from adenotonsillar hypertrophy presents with snoring, mouth breathing, and neurobehavioral problems (hyperactivity, inattention) — not just daytime sleepiness.
Polysomnography is the gold standard, but treatment can proceed in classic cases with obvious hypertrophy.
Adenotonsillectomy is first-line and often resolves symptoms; CPAP is reserved for residual or non-adenotonsillar OSA.
Untreated pediatric OSA can impair growth, behavior, and learning and may mimic ADHD.

cases.title: Bleeding From the Throat a Week After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Spitting up bright red blood seven days after a tonsillectomy.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl is brought urgently to the ED after she began spitting up and vomiting bright red blood at home this evening. She underwent tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy for recurrent tonsillitis and obstructive sleep-disordered breathing seven days ago. Her recovery had been typical, with throat pain managed by acetaminophen and ibuprofen, but she had been eating and drinking poorly for the last two days. Tonight her mother noticed her swallowing repeatedly, then she coughed up a clot followed by fresh red blood. There was no trauma. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable, with no known bleeding disorder; she takes no chronic medications and has no known drug allergies. She is fully immunized. Family history is negative for bleeding diatheses, and she has had no prior surgeries with abnormal bleeding. Her mother estimates she has lost 'a few mouthfuls' of blood. The child appears frightened and is anxious, repeatedly swallowing. There is no fever reported at home, and she has had decreased oral intake with fewer wet diapers than usual today.
cases.exam: An anxious child who is repeatedly swallowing, a clue to ongoing posterior bleeding. Oropharyngeal exam shows a clot adherent to the right tonsillar fossa with a small amount of fresh oozing; mucosa elsewhere shows expected post-tonsillectomy slough (white-gray eschar). Mucous membranes are slightly dry. She is tachycardic but normotensive with capillary refill of about 2 seconds. Lungs are clear with no stridor or distress at rest; airway is patent. There is no neck swelling, no petechiae or bruising on the skin, and no lymphadenopathy. She is alert and oriented to her age.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this episode of post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage best classified?
options: Primary (within 24 hours) hemorrhage
Secondary (delayed) hemorrhage, typically from eschar separation around days 5–10
Intraoperative hemorrhage
Coagulopathy-induced hemorrhage unrelated to the surgery
stages.explanation: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage is classified by timing: primary occurs within the first 24 hours (usually a surgical/hemostatic issue), while secondary (delayed) hemorrhage occurs after 24 hours, classically between postoperative days 5 and 10 when the protective fibrin eschar sloughs. This patient is on day 7 with eschar slough visible, so this is a secondary hemorrhage. Intraoperative bleeding by definition happens during surgery, not a week later. While a coagulopathy can contribute, the timing and visible eschar separation make typical secondary hemorrhage the best classification, and there is no personal or family bleeding history to suggest a primary clotting disorder.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority in the ED?
options: Send the child home with reassurance because most bleeds stop on their own
Secure IV access, send a type and screen and CBC, and prepare for possible operative control while protecting the airway
Give oral aspirin to reduce throat inflammation
Perform an immediate lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Active post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage is a potential airway and hemorrhagic emergency. The priorities are establishing IV access, obtaining a CBC and type and screen/crossmatch, beginning fluid resuscitation as needed, keeping the child upright with suction available, and notifying ENT and anesthesia for possible operative control under general anesthesia. Discharging the child is unsafe because bleeding can recur briskly and the airway is at risk from aspiration. Aspirin impairs platelet function and would worsen bleeding, in addition to Reye-syndrome concerns in children. A lumbar puncture is irrelevant to this surgical bleeding problem.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL
Hematocrit 32%
Platelets 280 ×10³/µL
PT/INR and aPTT within normal limits
Type and screen sent; blood available on hold
The child has another episode of brisk bleeding from the right fossa while in the ED

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given recurrent brisk bleeding from the tonsillar fossa, what is the definitive next step?
options: Continued observation with cold compresses to the neck only
Operative control of hemorrhage in the OR under general anesthesia with a secured airway
Discharge once bleeding briefly slows, with outpatient follow-up
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics as the sole intervention
stages.explanation: Recurrent or brisk post-tonsillectomy bleeding that does not stop with conservative measures requires operative control in the OR, where general anesthesia with a secured (often rapid-sequence) airway allows safe cautery or suture ligation of the bleeding vessel and full visualization. Observation with cold compresses alone is inadequate for active, recurrent bleeding and ignores aspiration risk. Discharging the child after a brief slowdown is dangerous because rebleeding is common and can be catastrophic. Antibiotics alone do not control hemorrhage. Securing the airway during operative management is essential because blood in the pharynx threatens aspiration and obscures the field.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During airway management for this bleeding child, what is the single greatest anesthetic concern?
options: Malignant hyperthermia from the surgical lights
Aspiration of blood and a full stomach of swallowed blood, requiring rapid-sequence induction
Latex allergy from the gloves
Hypothermia from the cold compresses
stages.explanation: The greatest concern when intubating a child with active oropharyngeal bleeding is aspiration: the stomach is typically full of swallowed blood and the airway is soiled, so rapid-sequence induction with suction immediately available and the patient considered 'full stomach' is standard to minimize aspiration and allow rapid airway protection. Malignant hyperthermia relates to specific triggering anesthetics, not surgical lights, and is not the primary issue here. Latex allergy is a general perioperative consideration but not the dominant risk in this scenario. Hypothermia from compresses is minor compared with the immediate aspiration threat from blood in the airway and stomach.

pearls: Primary post-tonsillectomy bleeding occurs within 24 hours (hemostatic/surgical); secondary bleeding peaks at days 5–10 from eschar separation.
Frequent swallowing in a postoperative child is a red flag for ongoing posterior bleeding that may be hidden.
Resuscitate, get IV access, CBC and type and screen, involve ENT/anesthesia early, and avoid antiplatelet agents like aspirin.
Treat active/recurrent bleeding operatively with a secured airway; assume a full stomach of swallowed blood and use rapid-sequence induction.

cases.title: Sudden Severe Sore Throat and Drooling in an Adult
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly worsening sore throat, painful swallowing, and drooling over several hours.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents to the ED with a sore throat that began this morning and has worsened dramatically over the last several hours, now with severe odynophagia, an inability to swallow his saliva, and a muffled voice. He has fever and feels very unwell. He reports the throat pain is far worse than the visible findings would suggest and he is anxious and leaning forward. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily; he is unsure of his last tetanus or other vaccinations and does not recall receiving Hib vaccine. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes a pack of cigarettes daily and drinks alcohol on weekends. He works as a warehouse supervisor and denies recent travel, sick contacts, or foreign-body ingestion. Family history is noncontributory. Over the past hour his breathing has felt slightly harder, and he prefers to sit upright. He has not eaten since last night and is now spitting saliva into a tissue. He denies chest pain or rash.
cases.exam: The patient is toxic-appearing, anxious, sitting in a tripod position, leaning forward and drooling, with a muffled 'hot potato' voice and inspiratory stridor that worsens when he lies back. Oropharynx on limited inspection appears relatively unremarkable, with no tonsillar exudate or peritonsillar bulge that would explain the severity, and there is exquisite anterior neck tenderness over the hyoid with pain on gentle movement of the larynx. There is no trismus and no neck mass. Lungs have transmitted upper-airway sounds but are otherwise clear. No skin rash, no facial edema, and trachea is midline.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this toxic patient with severe odynophagia, drooling, and stridor, what is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Forcefully depress the tongue with a blade to fully visualize the epiglottis
Keep the patient calm and upright and arrange controlled airway management in a setting prepared for emergent intubation or surgical airway, with ENT/anesthesia present
Send the patient to CT immediately while he lies supine
Discharge with oral antibiotics for presumed viral pharyngitis
stages.explanation: The clinical picture (rapid sore throat, drooling, muffled voice, tripod posture, stridor, pain out of proportion to oropharyngeal findings) is classic for epiglottitis (supraglottitis). The priority is airway: keep the patient calm and upright, avoid agitation, and arrange definitive airway control in a controlled environment (OR or equipped resuscitation area) with ENT and anesthesia ready for fiberoptic intubation or surgical airway. Aggressive tongue-blade examination can precipitate laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction. Sending an unstable patient supine to CT (that option here is another option) risks airway loss away from help. Discharge on oral antibiotics is dangerous and ignores impending airway obstruction.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Once the airway is secured, which diagnostic finding most directly confirms epiglottitis?
options: A 'steeple sign' (subglottic narrowing) on anteroposterior neck film
A swollen, cherry-red epiglottis seen on careful laryngoscopy, or a 'thumbprint sign' on lateral neck radiograph
Bilateral peritonsillar abscesses on CT
Vesicular lesions on the soft palate
stages.explanation: Epiglottitis is confirmed by direct visualization of a swollen, erythematous ('cherry-red') epiglottis on careful laryngoscopy, ideally performed in a controlled setting; the supportive radiographic finding is the lateral neck 'thumbprint sign' of an enlarged epiglottis. The 'steeple sign' indicates subglottic narrowing in croup, a different entity in young children. Bilateral peritonsillar abscesses describe a different deep-space infection and do not explain the epiglottic findings. Vesicular palatal lesions suggest herpangina or viral enanthem, not epiglottitis. Visualization should generally occur only when airway control is available, since manipulation can provoke obstruction.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 17.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Glucose 244 mg/dL
Blood cultures drawn (pending)
