Many stones pass spontaneously with these measures. Gland excision is reserved for recurrent, refractory disease or large/proximal stones, not first-line for a small palpable distal stone. IV antibiotics and admission are unnecessary without infection. Radiation has no role in benign stone disease. If conservative therapy fails, options include transoral stone removal, sialendoscopy, or lithotripsy.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Conservative measures over several weeks fail; symptoms recur with each meal
Intraoral occlusal radiograph: radiopaque calculus in the distal left Wharton duct
Non-contrast CT face/neck: solitary 6 mm calculus in the distal submandibular duct near the papilla; gland mildly enlarged, no abscess
Ultrasound: corresponding shadowing stone with proximal ductal dilation

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Imaging confirms a palpable 6 mm stone in the distal Wharton duct after failed conservative therapy. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Primary submandibular gland excision via a neck incision
Transoral duct surgery (sialolithotomy) and/or sialendoscopic retrieval to remove the stone and preserve the gland
External-beam radiation to dissolve the stone
Indefinite continuation of the same conservative measures
stages.explanation: For a palpable, accessible distal duct stone that has failed conservative therapy, the gland-preserving approach is preferred: transoral duct surgery (sialolithotomy, sometimes with marsupialization of the duct) and/or sialendoscopic stone retrieval directly removes the calculus while sparing the functioning gland. Primary gland excision is more invasive—carrying risks to the lingual, hypoglossal, and marginal mandibular nerves—and is reserved for proximal/intraglandular stones or recurrent disease with a chronically damaged gland, not an accessible distal stone. Radiation has no role in benign salivary calculi. Continuing the same failed conservative measures leaves the obstruction unaddressed and risks recurrent infection. Minimally invasive, gland-sparing removal is the standard.

pearls: Mealtime gland swelling and pain that resolves between meals is the hallmark of an obstructing salivary stone.
80-90% of salivary calculi form in the submandibular gland/Wharton duct due to mucoid alkaline saliva and an uphill, tortuous duct.
First-line therapy is conservative: hydration, warm compresses, massage, sialogogues, and NSAIDs—many stones pass.
Refractory or proximal/large stones need imaging (non-contrast CT/ultrasound) and sialendoscopy or surgical removal; gland excision is last resort.

cases.title: A Swollen, Painful Cheek After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: New painful swelling of the cheek and fever on postoperative day three.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman on the surgical ward, three days after an open abdominal hysterectomy for fibroids, develops acute pain and swelling over the right cheek and in front of the ear, along with a low-grade fever. She has been kept NPO with slow advancement of diet, has had limited oral intake, and nursing notes document poor fluid intake and a dry mouth. She reports the cheek is tender and feels worse when she tries to eat. She denies sore throat, ear discharge, or trismus initially, though jaw opening is now uncomfortable. PMH: type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic constipation. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily (held perioperatively), amlodipine 10 mg daily, oxycodone PRN for postoperative pain, and an anticholinergic that was started for bladder spasm. No known drug allergies. She is a nonsmoker. Family history is noncontributory. The combination of advanced age, dehydration, NPO status, diabetes, and a recently added anticholinergic medication has reduced her salivary flow, setting the stage for an ascending ductal infection.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing elderly woman with dry mucous membranes. There is a tender, warm, erythematous, indurated swelling over the right parotid region extending in front of and below the ear, with mild overlying skin erythema and trismus on attempted wide opening. Intraorally, massaging the right parotid gland expresses turbid, purulent material from the Stensen duct opposite the upper second molar. The facial nerve appears intact (symmetric facial movement). No fluctuant abscess is clearly palpable. Contralateral parotid and the submandibular glands are normal. No stridor and the airway is patent. Cervical nodes are mildly tender on the right.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute bacterial (suppurative) parotitis
Mumps (viral parotitis)
Pleomorphic adenoma with infection
Temporomandibular joint dysfunction
stages.explanation: An acutely painful, swollen, erythematous parotid with fever and frank pus expressed from the Stensen duct, arising in a dehydrated, NPO, debilitated postoperative elderly patient on anticholinergics, is classic acute bacterial (suppurative) parotitis. Reduced salivary flow allows retrograde bacterial ascent up the duct. Mumps is a bilateral viral parotitis typically in unvaccinated younger patients without purulent ductal discharge. Pleomorphic adenoma is a chronic, painless, slow-growing mass, not an acute febrile suppurative process. TMJ dysfunction causes jaw pain but no fever, erythema, gland swelling, or pus. The purulent Stensen duct discharge is the clinching finding.

stages.label: MICROBIOLOGY
stages.question: Which organism is the most common cause of acute bacterial parotitis?
options: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Escherichia coli
Candida albicans
Staphylococcus aureus
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen in acute suppurative parotitis, followed by streptococci and oral anaerobes; mixed flora is common, and methicillin-resistant strains must be considered. Empiric antibiotics should target S. aureus and oral anaerobes. Pseudomonas is uncommon except in immunocompromised or hospitalized patients and is not the usual cause. E. coli is a gut/urinary organism, not the typical parotid pathogen. Candida causes oral thrush and rarely a suppurative gland infection in immunocompetent patients. Knowing S. aureus predominance guides antibiotic selection while cultures of the expressed pus are pending.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil-predominant)
Glucose 212 mg/dL
Na 134 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Gram stain of Stensen duct pus: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Contrast CT face/neck: enlarged, enhancing right parotid with ductal dilation; no drainable abscess; no calculus

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT shows diffuse parotid inflammation without a drainable abscess. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate superficial parotidectomy
Aggressive hydration, IV antistaphylococcal antibiotics, sialogogues, warm compresses, gland massage, and oral care
Antiviral therapy for mumps
Discharge home with reassurance and no antibiotics
stages.explanation: Acute bacterial parotitis without an organized abscess is managed medically: rehydration to restore salivary flow, empiric IV antibiotics covering S. aureus (including MRSA risk) and oral anaerobes, sialogogues (sour candy/lemon) and gland massage to promote ductal drainage, warm compresses, meticulous oral hygiene, and stopping or minimizing anticholinergics. Most cases resolve with these measures plus correcting the underlying dehydration. Surgery is reserved for abscess formation or failure to improve, not diffuse inflammation. Antivirals are wrong—this is bacterial, with gram-positive cocci in clusters on Gram stain. Withholding antibiotics is unsafe in a septic-appearing diabetic patient. Reverse the predisposing factors and treat the infection.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite appropriate antibiotics and hydration, she remains febrile at 48 hours with increasing localized swelling and a now fluctuant area. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge
Begin antifungal therapy
Repeat imaging to confirm abscess and perform surgical incision and drainage if an abscess is present
Continue current therapy unchanged for another week
stages.explanation: Failure to improve with developing fluctuance signals abscess formation, which does not resolve with antibiotics alone. The correct step is to confirm a drainable collection (ultrasound or repeat CT) and perform incision and drainage, taking care to spare the facial nerve branches that course through the parotid. Switching to oral antibiotics and discharging abandons a patient who is failing therapy. Antifungals are inappropriate—the pathogen is staphylococcal. Continuing unchanged for a week risks progression to deeper neck space infection, facial nerve injury, or sepsis. Source control via drainage is essential once an abscess forms.

pearls: Dehydration, NPO status, advanced age, diabetes, and anticholinergics reduce salivary flow and predispose to ascending bacterial parotitis.
Purulent discharge from the Stensen duct with a tender, erythematous parotid is the diagnostic hallmark.
Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen; empiric coverage should include anaerobes and consider MRSA.
Treat medically with hydration, antibiotics, sialogogues, and massage; reserve incision and drainage (sparing the facial nerve) for abscess or failure.

cases.title: A Slow-Growing Bump by the Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump in front of the ear that has slowly enlarged over a year.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents with a lump just in front of and below her left ear that she first noticed about a year ago. It has grown very gradually and is completely painless. She denies facial weakness, numbness, difficulty smiling or closing the eye, trismus, skin changes, weight loss, night sweats, or fevers. There is no rapid growth and no overlying skin ulceration. She reports no problems chewing or swallowing. She has no history of head and neck radiation. She is a never-smoker and drinks alcohol rarely. PMH includes hypothyroidism and mild osteoarthritis. Medications: levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, occasional ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. Family history: mother had breast cancer; no salivary or head-and-neck tumors. She works as a librarian. She is anxious about whether the lump is cancerous and whether she will need her 'face nerve cut.' The slow, painless growth of a discrete mass in the parotid region, without facial nerve involvement, is the central feature of her presentation.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. In the left preauricular/parotid region there is a single, firm, well-circumscribed, mobile, nontender mass approximately 2.5 cm, without overlying skin changes, warmth, or fluctuance. The mass is not fixed to skin or deep structures. Facial nerve function is fully intact—symmetric forehead wrinkling, eye closure, and smile (House-Brackmann I). There is no trismus, no intraoral parapharyngeal bulge, and no expressible pus from the Stensen duct. No cervical lymphadenopathy. The contralateral parotid and the submandibular glands are normal. The remainder of the head and neck exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What clinical feature is most reassuring against malignancy in this parotid mass?
options: The mass is firm
The patient is over 50 years old
Intact facial nerve function with a mobile, painless, slowly growing mass
The mass is located in the parotid region
stages.explanation: Features favoring a benign parotid tumor include slow, painless growth; a mobile, well-circumscribed mass; and—critically—normal facial nerve function. Facial nerve weakness, pain, rapid growth, fixation, skin involvement, or cervical lymphadenopathy raise concern for malignancy because the facial nerve runs through the parotid and is invaded by aggressive tumors. A firm consistency is nonspecific—many benign tumors are firm. Age over 50 does not by itself favor benignity. Location in the parotid is where both benign and malignant tumors occur and is not reassuring on its own. The intact facial nerve plus indolent, mobile mass is the most reassuring combination.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to evaluate this parotid mass?
options: Incisional (open) biopsy of the mass in clinic
Fine-needle aspiration, typically with ultrasound guidance, and often MRI/CT imaging
Empiric antibiotics and reassessment in 6 months
Immediate total parotidectomy without prior tissue sampling
stages.explanation: The standard workup of a parotid mass is fine-needle aspiration (often ultrasound-guided), which is safe, accurate, and avoids the tumor seeding and facial nerve injury risks of open biopsy; cross-sectional imaging (MRI preferred, or CT) characterizes extent and deep-lobe involvement. Incisional/open biopsy is contraindicated for parotid masses because it risks tumor spillage and nerve injury. Antibiotics are inappropriate for a noninfectious, slow-growing mass and delay diagnosis. Proceeding directly to total parotidectomy without tissue sampling is excessive and may be the wrong operation; FNA guides whether superficial parotidectomy or a more extensive resection is appropriate. FNA plus imaging is the evidence-based pathway.

stages.label: FNA & IMAGING
stages.content:
Ultrasound-guided FNA cytology: benign biphasic tumor with epithelial (ductal) cells and chondromyxoid (myxoid/cartilage-like) stroma
Interpretation consistent with pleomorphic adenoma
MRI: 2.6 cm well-defined mass confined to the superficial lobe of the left parotid; no deep-lobe extension; no suspicious cervical nodes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: FNA confirms pleomorphic adenoma confined to the superficial lobe. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Observation alone, since these tumors never cause problems
Simple enucleation of the tumor through a small incision
Primary radiation therapy
Superficial (or partial) parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation, removing the tumor with a cuff of normal tissue
stages.explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma is treated with surgical excision via superficial or partial parotidectomy that removes the tumor with a margin of normal parotid tissue while identifying and preserving the facial nerve. Adequate margins are essential because simple enucleation leaves microscopic pseudopodia of tumor behind, causing high recurrence rates. Observation is inappropriate—although benign, these tumors grow and carry a real long-term risk of malignant transformation (carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma). Primary radiation is not first-line for a resectable benign tumor and carries its own risks; radiation is reserved for select malignant or unresectable/recurrent cases. Complete excision with nerve preservation is standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Why is complete excision with adequate margins emphasized for pleomorphic adenoma rather than simple enucleation?
options: To prevent the tumor from metastasizing to the lungs immediately
Because incomplete removal leads to high recurrence and the tumor can undergo malignant transformation over time
Because the tumor is highly radiosensitive once cut
To shorten the operation and avoid the facial nerve entirely
stages.explanation: Pleomorphic adenomas have finger-like extensions through their pseudocapsule; enucleation leaves residual tumor and is associated with high recurrence, and recurrent disease is often multinodular and harder to treat. Importantly, long-standing or recurrent pleomorphic adenomas can undergo malignant transformation to carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma, so complete excision with a tissue cuff reduces both recurrence and that risk. That option is wrong—this benign tumor does not immediately metastasize. Another option is incorrect; radiosensitivity is not the rationale, and surgery is the mainstay. A third option is backwards—proper surgery requires careful identification and preservation of the facial nerve, not avoidance of it. Margin-adequate resection is the key principle.

cases.diagnosis: Pleomorphic adenoma of the parotid gland
pearls: Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common salivary gland tumor and the most common parotid tumor—slow, painless, mobile, with intact facial nerve.
Facial nerve weakness, pain, rapid growth, fixation, or adenopathy suggest malignancy and warrant urgent evaluation.
Work up parotid masses with FNA (often ultrasound-guided) and MRI/CT; never perform open incisional biopsy due to seeding/nerve risk.
Treat with superficial/partial parotidectomy preserving the facial nerve; avoid enucleation—incomplete removal recurs and can transform to carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma.

cases.title: Lumps on Both Sides of the Jaw
cases.chiefComplaint: Soft, painless lumps near both ears noticed over several months.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with painless lumps in front of and below both ears that he has noticed over the past several months. They are soft and have not caused pain, facial weakness, or trouble eating. He reports the lumps seem to fluctuate slightly in size but have not rapidly enlarged. He denies fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or skin changes. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. He drinks beer on weekends. PMH includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No known drug allergies. He has no history of head and neck radiation and no family history of salivary tumors. He works as a machinist. He is concerned because there are now lumps 'on both sides,' which he fears means cancer that has spread. Bilateral, soft, painless parotid masses in an older male smoker—with intact facial nerve function—are the defining features and point strongly toward a specific benign salivary neoplasm.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress with the smell of tobacco. In both parotid (preauricular/infra-auricular) regions there are soft, somewhat cystic, mobile, nontender masses—approximately 3 cm on the right and 2 cm on the left—without overlying skin changes, warmth, or fluctuant pus. Facial nerve function is fully intact bilaterally (symmetric forehead, eye closure, and smile). No trismus and no parapharyngeal bulge intraorally; the Stensen ducts express clear saliva without pus. There is no cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy, and no hepatosplenomegaly. The bilaterality, soft cystic quality, smoking history, and preserved facial nerve are the salient findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given bilateral soft parotid masses in an older male smoker, which benign salivary tumor is most likely?
options: Pleomorphic adenoma
Warthin tumor (papillary cystadenoma lymphomatosum)
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
Adenoid cystic carcinoma
stages.explanation: Warthin tumor is the salivary neoplasm most strongly associated with smoking, characteristically arises in the parotid of older men, and is the benign salivary tumor most likely to be bilateral or multifocal—matching this presentation of soft, cystic, painless bilateral parotid masses with an intact facial nerve. Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common salivary tumor overall but is typically solitary, firmer, and not specifically linked to smoking or bilaterality. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma is the most common salivary malignancy but usually presents as a solitary mass and may involve the nerve. Adenoid cystic carcinoma is malignant, often painful with perineural invasion. The smoking + bilateral + male profile fits Warthin tumor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating these bilateral parotid masses?
options: Reassure and discharge without any testing
Open excisional biopsy of one mass in clinic
Fine-needle aspiration with imaging (ultrasound and/or CT/MRI)
Start empiric antibiotics for presumed bilateral sialadenitis
stages.explanation: As with any salivary neoplasm, the evaluation is fine-needle aspiration (commonly ultrasound-guided) plus cross-sectional imaging to characterize the masses, confirm cystic morphology, assess deep-lobe involvement, and document bilaterality. FNA of Warthin tumor classically shows oncocytic epithelial cells in a background of lymphocytes and debris. Reassurance without testing is inappropriate—tissue diagnosis is needed even for likely-benign masses, especially in a smoker. Open excisional biopsy in clinic is contraindicated for parotid masses due to facial nerve injury and tumor seeding risk. Empiric antibiotics are wrong—these are painless, afebrile, non-purulent masses, not infection. FNA plus imaging is the standard.

stages.label: FNA & IMAGING
stages.content:
Ultrasound: bilateral well-defined cystic-solid parotid masses in the superficial lobes
Ultrasound-guided FNA cytology: oncocytic (eosinophilic) epithelial cells with abundant background lymphocytes and proteinaceous/cystic debris
Interpretation: consistent with Warthin tumor
MRI: bilateral superficial-lobe masses, no deep-lobe extension, no pathologic cervical adenopathy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: FNA confirms bilateral Warthin tumors with no concerning features. What is a reasonable management approach to discuss with this patient?
options: Bilateral total parotidectomy is mandatory immediately
High-dose radiation therapy to both glands
Lifelong systemic chemotherapy
Either surgical excision (superficial/partial parotidectomy) or, for confirmed Warthin tumors, active observation may be reasonable, plus smoking cessation
stages.explanation: Warthin tumors are benign with very low malignant potential, so management can be individualized: surgical excision via superficial/partial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation is curative and definitive, but observation is a reasonable option for asymptomatic, cytologically confirmed Warthin tumors—particularly when bilateral—balancing surgical risk against benefit. Smoking cessation is strongly advised given the etiologic link. Mandatory immediate bilateral total parotidectomy is overly aggressive and risks bilateral facial nerve injury for a benign condition. Radiation and chemotherapy have no role in benign Warthin tumor. Shared decision-making between surgery and surveillance, plus quitting smoking, is appropriate.

stages.label: PATIENT COUNSELING
stages.question: The patient asks how his smoking relates to these tumors. What is the most accurate statement?
options: Smoking is strongly associated with Warthin tumor, so quitting is recommended and may reduce risk of new lesions
Smoking has no known relationship to Warthin tumors
Smoking causes Warthin tumors to become malignant within weeks
Only secondhand smoke, not active smoking, is linked to these tumors
stages.explanation: Warthin tumor has one of the strongest associations with cigarette smoking among salivary neoplasms; smokers have a markedly higher incidence, and the tumors are more often bilateral/multifocal in smokers. Counseling should emphasize smoking cessation, which is good general health advice and is reasonable to reduce risk of additional lesions. That option is false—the link is well established. Another option overstates risk—Warthin tumors are benign with only rare malignant transformation, not rapid weeks-long malignancy. A third option is incorrect; it is active smoking that carries the strong association. Accurate counseling reinforces cessation while reassuring the patient that the lesions themselves are benign.

cases.diagnosis: Warthin tumor (papillary cystadenoma lymphomatosum) of the parotid glands, bilateral
pearls: Warthin tumor is the benign salivary tumor most associated with smoking and the one most likely to be bilateral/multifocal, typically in older men.
FNA shows oncocytic epithelial cells in a lymphoid (lymphocyte-rich) background with cystic debris.
Almost exclusively parotid; malignant transformation is rare, so observation can be an option alongside surgical excision.
Preserve the facial nerve during any parotid surgery, and counsel strongly on smoking cessation.

cases.title: A Slow-Growing Cheek Lump and a Drooping Smile
cases.chiefComplaint: A lump in front of the left ear that has slowly enlarged over eight months and a recent droop at the corner of his mouth.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with a left preauricular mass first noticed about eight months ago. It has slowly grown and over the past three weeks he has noticed his smile looks uneven and food collects in his left cheek. He denies pain, fevers, or weight loss. He reports occasional left ear fullness but no otorrhea or hearing loss. He has no trismus or dysphagia. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. No prior head and neck irradiation. He has no drug allergies. He worked as an electrician and smoked half a pack daily for 20 years, quitting at age 45; he drinks two beers on weekends. Family history is notable for colon cancer in his father. He has had no prior salivary problems, no recurrent swelling with meals, and no skin cancers. Review of systems is otherwise negative including no numbness of the face and no neck masses he has felt.
cases.exam: A firm, fixed, nontender 2.5 cm mass occupies the left parotid tail in the preauricular region. The overlying skin is intact and mobile. There is House-Brackmann grade III left facial weakness: incomplete eye closure, flattened nasolabial fold, and asymmetric smile. No cervical lymphadenopathy is palpable. The external auditory canal and tympanic membrane are normal. Oropharynx is clear; no parapharyngeal bulge. The contralateral parotid and submandibular glands are normal. Sensation of the face is intact and there is no trismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this parotid mass is most concerning for malignancy?
options: Slow growth over eight months
Location in the parotid tail
Associated facial nerve weakness
Occasional ipsilateral ear fullness
stages.explanation: Facial nerve dysfunction in the setting of a parotid mass is a hallmark red flag for malignancy, since the facial nerve courses through the gland and benign tumors almost never invade it. Slow growth is more typical of benign lesions like pleomorphic adenoma, though it does not exclude a low-grade cancer. The parotid tail is simply a common location for both benign and malignant tumors and is not itself worrisome. Ear fullness is nonspecific and can result from any preauricular mass effect. Other malignant red flags include pain, fixation to deep structures or skin, rapid growth, and cervical adenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Incisional biopsy of the mass
Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration biopsy
Empiric superficial parotidectomy without tissue diagnosis
Two-week course of antibiotics and reassess
stages.explanation: Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration (FNA) is the standard first-line tissue diagnosis for a parotid mass; it is safe, avoids violating tumor planes, and helps distinguish benign from malignant and guides surgical planning. Incisional (open) biopsy is contraindicated for parotid tumors because it risks tumor seeding and facial nerve injury and complicates definitive resection. Proceeding straight to parotidectomy without any tissue diagnosis is not ideal when imaging and FNA can refine consent and the extent of surgery. Antibiotics are inappropriate—there is no evidence of infection (no pain, fever, or erythema), and delay would be harmful given the malignant red flag of facial weakness.

stages.label: IMAGING & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
MRI face/neck with contrast: 2.6 cm heterogeneous, irregularly marginated mass in the left superficial and deep parotid lobes, abutting the stylomastoid foramen; no skull base erosion
Ipsilateral level II node 1.4 cm, rounded, with loss of fatty hilum
Ultrasound-guided FNA: cytology shows a mixture of mucous-secreting cells, intermediate cells, and squamoid epidermoid cells
Mucicarmine stain: positive for intracytoplasmic mucin

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These cytologic findings are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pleomorphic adenoma
Warthin tumor
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
Acinic cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: The combination of mucous (goblet) cells, epidermoid/squamoid cells, and intermediate cells with mucicarmine-positive mucin is the defining picture of mucoepidermoid carcinoma, the most common salivary gland malignancy overall. Pleomorphic adenoma shows a mix of epithelial and myxochondroid stromal elements, not mucin-producing and squamoid cells, and rarely causes facial palsy. Warthin tumor shows oncocytic epithelium over a lymphoid stroma and is typically benign, often bilateral, and FDG-avid but not mucinous. Acinic cell carcinoma shows serous acinar cells with zymogen granules and lacks the squamoid/mucous biphasic pattern. The MAML2 (CRTC1-MAML2) translocation supports mucoepidermoid carcinoma when molecular testing is performed.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the facial weakness, suspicious node, and high-grade features, what is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Total parotidectomy with facial nerve sacrifice as needed, neck dissection, and postoperative radiotherapy
Superficial parotidectomy alone with facial nerve preservation
Definitive radiotherapy without surgery
Observation with serial MRI every six months
stages.explanation: A high-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma involving the facial nerve with a suspicious regional node requires aggressive surgery: total parotidectomy, resection of involved facial nerve segments (with grafting when feasible), an ipsilateral neck dissection for the clinically positive node, and adjuvant radiotherapy for high-grade histology, positive margins, perineural invasion, or nodal disease. Superficial parotidectomy alone is inadequate when the deep lobe and nerve are involved and a node is positive. Definitive radiotherapy alone is reserved for unresectable disease or poor surgical candidates; salivary cancers are best treated surgically. Observation is unacceptable for an aggressive malignancy with nerve involvement.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If the facial nerve is sacrificed during resection, which complication should be specifically anticipated and addressed to protect the eye?
options: Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating)
Inability to close the eye, risking exposure keratopathy
First-bite syndrome
Greater auricular nerve numbness of the earlobe
stages.explanation: Sacrificing the facial nerve paralyzes the orbicularis oculi, so the patient cannot close the eye, leading to exposure keratopathy and corneal ulceration if not protected; management includes lubrication, taping, and procedures such as an upper-eyelid gold/platinum weight or tarsorrhaphy, plus nerve grafting and facial reanimation. Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating from aberrant reinnervation of sweat glands by parasympathetic fibers) is a real parotidectomy complication but does not threaten the eye. First-bite syndrome causes pain in the parotid region with the first bite of a meal and is uncomfortable but not sight-threatening. Greater auricular nerve injury causes earlobe numbness and is a minor sensory deficit, not an ocular emergency.

cases.diagnosis: High-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma of the parotid gland with facial nerve involvement and regional nodal metastasis
pearls: Facial nerve weakness with a parotid mass is malignant until proven otherwise—benign tumors essentially never paralyze the nerve.
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma is the most common salivary gland malignancy; its hallmark is mucous, epidermoid, and intermediate cells with mucicarmine-positive mucin.
FNA, not open biopsy, is the diagnostic procedure of choice for parotid masses to avoid tumor seeding and nerve injury.
High-grade histology, positive margins, perineural invasion, or nodal disease are indications for adjuvant radiotherapy after salivary gland cancer resection.

cases.title: A Persistent Aching Lump on the Palate
cases.chiefComplaint: A firm swelling on the roof of her mouth that has been present for several months and has become persistently painful.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with a slowly enlarging swelling of the hard-soft palate junction noticed roughly six months ago. Unlike most oral lumps she has had, this one aches constantly and the pain has crept toward her cheek and upper teeth; she also describes intermittent numbness of the left upper gum. She denies bleeding, weight loss, or difficulty swallowing. She wears no dentures. Past medical history includes well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 88 mcg daily. She has no drug allergies. She has never smoked and drinks rarely. She worked as a schoolteacher. Family history is unremarkable for head and neck cancer. She has no prior radiation exposure. Review of systems is notable for the facial discomfort and gum numbness but negative for trismus, otalgia, epistaxis, or visual changes. She initially attributed the lump to a 'sore from spicy food' but it never resolved, prompting evaluation.
cases.exam: A firm, submucosal 2 cm mass is present at the junction of the hard and soft palate on the left, with intact but slightly stretched overlying mucosa and no ulceration. The mass is mildly tender and fixed to the deep tissue. There is decreased sensation in the distribution of the left greater palatine and superior alveolar nerves. No trismus. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, including extraocular movements and facial sensation over V1-V2 elsewhere. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Dentition is intact; the remaining oral cavity, oropharynx, and contralateral palate are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A firm submucosal palatal mass at the hard-soft palate junction most likely arises from which structure?
options: Squamous mucosal epithelium
Minor salivary glands
Maxillary alveolar bone
Adjacent tonsillar lymphoid tissue
stages.explanation: The palate—especially the junction of the hard and soft palate—is the most common site for minor salivary gland tumors, which classically present as firm submucosal masses with intact overlying mucosa. The lack of ulceration argues against a primary squamous cell carcinoma of the mucosa, which usually ulcerates. Alveolar bone lesions present with dental or bony findings and are far less common at this soft-tissue submucosal location. Tonsillar lymphoid tissue is located in the oropharynx laterally, not the palatal vault, and lymphoid lesions are typically softer. Pain and nerve involvement here further point toward an infiltrative salivary malignancy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical feature most strongly raises concern for a malignant rather than benign minor salivary gland tumor?
options: Submucosal location with intact mucosa
Slow growth over months
Pain with regional numbness (nerve involvement)
Size of approximately 2 cm
stages.explanation: Pain and sensory loss reflect perineural invasion, a feature strongly associated with malignancy—particularly adenoid cystic carcinoma, which is notorious for tracking along nerves. Submucosal location with intact mucosa is common to both benign (e.g., pleomorphic adenoma) and malignant salivary tumors and is not discriminating. Slow growth is typical of both benign tumors and the indolent course of adenoid cystic carcinoma, so it does not reassure. A 2 cm size alone is not a reliable malignancy marker. The triad of a palatal mass with pain and nerve symptoms should prompt urgent imaging and biopsy for suspected salivary malignancy.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
MRI face with contrast: 2.1 cm enhancing left palatal mass with enhancement and enlargement tracking along the left greater palatine canal toward the pterygopalatine fossa
No cervical lymphadenopathy; no bone destruction of the hard palate cortex
Incisional biopsy: nests and cords of basaloid cells in a cribriform 'Swiss-cheese' pattern surrounding pseudocystic spaces
Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells positive for c-KIT (CD117); abundant perineural invasion identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The cribriform 'Swiss-cheese' architecture with perineural invasion and c-KIT positivity is characteristic of which tumor?
options: Pleomorphic adenoma
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
Adenoid cystic carcinoma
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
stages.explanation: Adenoid cystic carcinoma classically shows a cribriform ('Swiss-cheese') pattern of basaloid cells, marked perineural invasion, and frequent c-KIT (CD117) expression; it is the most common malignancy of minor salivary glands. Pleomorphic adenoma is benign with a mixed epithelial-myxochondroid stroma and lacks perineural infiltration. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma shows mucous, epidermoid, and intermediate cells with mucin, not a cribriform basaloid pattern. Polymorphous adenocarcinoma also favors the palate and can show perineural spread, but it has architecturally diverse ('polymorphous') growth, single-file streaming, and lacks the classic Swiss-cheese cribriform pattern and strong c-KIT positivity. The pterygopalatine fossa tracking on MRI reflects characteristic perineural spread.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management given perineural invasion extending toward the skull base?
options: Wide surgical resection followed by postoperative radiotherapy
Surgical resection alone with close margins
Chemotherapy alone with cisplatin
Observation given the slow-growing nature of the tumor
stages.explanation: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is treated with wide surgical excision plus adjuvant radiotherapy; radiation is strongly indicated because of the high rate of perineural invasion and named-nerve/skull-base spread, which makes negative margins difficult to achieve. Surgery alone is insufficient here given extensive perineural invasion tracking toward the skull base. Chemotherapy is not curative for adenoid cystic carcinoma and is generally reserved for palliation of unresectable or metastatic disease; it is not first-line. Observation is inappropriate for a malignancy with active perineural spread—although indolent, adenoid cystic carcinoma is relentless, with late local recurrences and characteristic distant metastases to the lung years after treatment.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: When counseling this patient on prognosis and surveillance, which statement is most accurate?
options: Cure is essentially guaranteed once margins are negative
Recurrence and distant metastasis can occur many years later, so prolonged surveillance is required
Cervical lymph node metastasis is the dominant route of spread
Five-year survival reliably predicts long-term cure
stages.explanation: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is defined by an indolent but persistent course with late local recurrences and distant metastases (classically to the lung) appearing 5-20 years after treatment, so long-term surveillance is essential and short-term success is misleading. Negative margins do not guarantee cure because microscopic perineural spread is common. Unlike most head and neck cancers, adenoid cystic carcinoma spreads more by hematogenous and perineural routes than by lymphatics, so nodal metastasis is relatively uncommon. Because relapse occurs late, five-year survival overestimates true cure; ten- and fifteen-year survival drop substantially, underscoring the need for extended monitoring including periodic chest imaging.

cases.diagnosis: Adenoid cystic carcinoma of the minor salivary glands of the palate with perineural invasion
pearls: A firm, submucosal palatal mass with intact mucosa, especially at the hard-soft palate junction, should raise suspicion for a minor salivary gland tumor.
Pain and numbness signal perineural invasion—the hallmark of adenoid cystic carcinoma, which tracks along named nerves toward the skull base.
Adenoid cystic carcinoma is treated with wide resection plus adjuvant radiotherapy because of its propensity for perineural spread.
Beware late recurrence: adenoid cystic carcinoma metastasizes hematogenously (classically to lung) years to decades later, so five-year survival overstates cure.

cases.title: Gritty Eyes, a Parched Mouth, and Swollen Cheeks
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of dry, gritty eyes and a persistently dry mouth along with intermittent swelling of both cheeks.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman reports about a year of progressively dry eyes that feel gritty, as if there is sand under her lids, and a dry mouth that makes swallowing crackers difficult and forces her to sip water through meals. She has needed three dental fillings in the past year despite good hygiene. Over recent months both cheeks intermittently swell, sometimes tender, without clear relation to eating. She has noticed mild joint stiffness in her hands in the mornings and occasional fatigue. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She takes artificial tears as needed. She has no drug allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks wine occasionally, and works as an accountant. Family history includes a sister with lupus and a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems is negative for rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, Raynaud phenomenon, fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. She denies a hard, fixed neck mass but is worried about the persistent cheek fullness.
cases.exam: Both parotid glands are diffusely enlarged, soft, and mildly tender without overlying erythema; no discrete dominant nodule. The oral mucosa is dry with scant pooled saliva, and the tongue is mildly fissured. Dental caries are present. Eyes show diminished tear film with mild conjunctival injection. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy and no firm focal salivary mass. Small joints of the hands have mild tenderness without synovitis or deformity. No malar rash, no oral ulcers, and the skin shows no sclerodactyly. The remainder of the head and neck exam, including the submandibular glands, is otherwise unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of dry eyes, dry mouth, and bilateral parotid enlargement most strongly suggests which category of disease?
options: An obstructive sialolithiasis
An autoimmune exocrine gland disorder
A bacterial parotitis
A primary parotid neoplasm
stages.explanation: Sicca symptoms (keratoconjunctivitis sicca and xerostomia) with bilateral, diffuse parotid enlargement point to an autoimmune exocrine gland disorder—most often Sjögren syndrome—where lymphocytic infiltration destroys lacrimal and salivary glands. Sialolithiasis typically causes unilateral, mealtime-related painful swelling from ductal obstruction, not bilateral sicca. Bacterial (suppurative) parotitis is acute, unilateral, with fever, erythema, and purulent ductal discharge—not a chronic bilateral picture. A primary neoplasm presents as a discrete, often unilateral mass rather than diffuse bilateral enlargement with sicca. The associated arthralgias and family history of autoimmune disease further support an autoimmune etiology.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of antibodies is most specifically associated with this condition?
options: Anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith
Anti-Ro (SSA) and anti-La (SSB)
Anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere
Anti-Jo-1 and anti-Mi-2
stages.explanation: Anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB are the antibodies most characteristic of Sjögren syndrome and are part of the classification criteria. Anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus. Anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere are markers of systemic sclerosis (diffuse and limited forms, respectively). Anti-Jo-1 and anti-Mi-2 are associated with inflammatory myopathies such as polymyositis and dermatomyositis. While ANA and rheumatoid factor are frequently positive in Sjögren syndrome, they are not specific; anti-Ro/SSA is the most useful serologic marker and, when positive, also carries implications for neonatal lupus risk in pregnancy.

stages.label: LABS & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
ANA 1:640 (speckled pattern)
Anti-Ro/SSA positive; anti-La/SSB positive
Rheumatoid factor positive; anti-CCP negative
ESR 42 mm/hr
Schirmer test: 4 mm wetting at 5 minutes (reduced)
Minor (labial) salivary gland biopsy: focal lymphocytic sialadenitis with a focus score of 2 foci/4 mm²

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate initial management for her sicca symptoms?
options: Systemic high-dose corticosteroids
Saliva and tear substitutes plus a muscarinic agonist such as pilocarpine, with meticulous dental and ocular care
Rituximab as first-line therapy
Broad-spectrum oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: First-line management of Sjögren-related sicca is symptomatic: artificial tears and saliva substitutes, secretagogues (muscarinic agonists like pilocarpine or cevimeline) to stimulate residual gland function, and aggressive dental/ocular care to prevent caries and corneal damage. Systemic high-dose corticosteroids are reserved for significant extraglandular/systemic involvement, not routine sicca, because of their side-effect burden. Rituximab and other immunomodulators are considered for refractory systemic disease, not initial sicca control. Antibiotics have no role absent superimposed bacterial infection. Hydroxychloroquine may help fatigue and arthralgias. Patient education about reduced caries risk and the importance of frequent dental visits is essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient later develops a new, firm, persistently enlarging unilateral parotid mass, which complication must be excluded?
options: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
Pleomorphic adenoma
MALT (marginal zone) B-cell lymphoma
Sialolithiasis
stages.explanation: Patients with Sjögren syndrome have a markedly increased risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, classically extranodal marginal zone (MALT) B-cell lymphoma arising in chronically inflamed salivary tissue. A new firm, persistent, asymmetric parotid mass—especially with persistent parotid swelling, lymphadenopathy, cryoglobulins, low C4, or a monoclonal gammopathy—warrants biopsy to exclude lymphomatous transformation. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma and pleomorphic adenoma are salivary neoplasms but are not the characteristic Sjögren-associated malignancy. Sialolithiasis causes obstructive, mealtime-related swelling and does not explain a fixed enlarging mass. Vigilance for lymphoma is a key part of long-term Sjögren follow-up, and any dominant or growing mass should prompt tissue sampling.

cases.diagnosis: Sjögren syndrome (primary) with bilateral parotid enlargement and elevated MALT lymphoma risk
pearls: Keratoconjunctivitis sicca plus xerostomia plus bilateral parotid enlargement is the classic triad of Sjögren syndrome.
Anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB are the characteristic antibodies; labial salivary gland biopsy showing focal lymphocytic sialadenitis (focus score ≥1) supports the diagnosis.
First-line therapy is symptomatic—tear/saliva substitutes, muscarinic agonists, and rigorous dental and ocular care.
A new firm, enlarging unilateral parotid mass in Sjögren syndrome should prompt biopsy to exclude MALT lymphoma.

cases.title: An Incidental Lump in the Front of the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump in the lower front of her neck found incidentally while applying a necklace.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman noticed a painless lump in the lower front of her neck two months ago while fastening a necklace. It does not hurt and she has not noticed it growing rapidly. She denies hoarseness, dysphagia, dyspnea, or a sensation of choking. She reports no palpitations, heat or cold intolerance, weight change, or tremor, and her menstrual cycles are regular. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only a daily multivitamin and has no drug allergies. As a child she underwent two head and neck CT scans after a sports injury; she recalls no therapeutic radiation. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, exercises regularly, and works as a graphic designer. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with 'thyroid cancer' diagnosed in her 40s; no family history of pheochromocytoma or parathyroid disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative including no fevers, night sweats, or unexplained weight loss, and no prior neck masses.
cases.exam: A firm, nontender 1.8 cm nodule is palpable in the right thyroid lobe that moves with swallowing. The remainder of the thyroid is normal in size without diffuse enlargement or bruit. There is no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy in the central or lateral compartments. The voice is normal and there is no stridor. There are no signs of hyper- or hypothyroidism: no exophthalmos, no lid lag, no tremor, warm and dry skin without diaphoresis, and normal reflexes. The remainder of the head and neck examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first set of tests for this newly discovered thyroid nodule?
options: Serum TSH and thyroid (neck) ultrasound
Radioactive iodine uptake scan as the initial study
CT neck with iodinated contrast
Immediate fine-needle aspiration without imaging
stages.explanation: Initial evaluation of a thyroid nodule begins with a serum TSH and a dedicated thyroid/neck ultrasound. TSH screens for autonomous function, and ultrasound characterizes the nodule and assesses lymph nodes to guide whether FNA is needed. A radioiodine uptake scan is only indicated if TSH is low (to identify a hyperfunctioning 'hot' nodule, which is rarely malignant); it is not the first test when TSH is unknown or normal. CT with iodinated contrast is not first-line and the iodine load can interfere with subsequent radioiodine therapy if cancer is found. FNA should follow, not precede, ultrasound risk stratification, since sonographic features and size determine the need for biopsy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
Thyroid ultrasound: 1.8 cm solid hypoechoic right-lobe nodule, taller-than-wide, with irregular margins and punctate microcalcifications
No abnormal cervical lymph nodes identified
ULTRASOUND RISK CATEGORY: high suspicion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a 1.8 cm solid, hypoechoic nodule with microcalcifications and irregular margins, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat ultrasound in 12 months
Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration biopsy
Radioactive iodine ablation
Levothyroxine suppression therapy
stages.explanation: A solid hypoechoic nodule that is taller-than-wide with irregular margins and microcalcifications is sonographically high-suspicion; FNA is recommended for high-suspicion nodules at ≥1 cm, so ultrasound-guided FNA is the correct next step. Repeating ultrasound in 12 months would inappropriately delay diagnosis of a high-risk nodule of this size. Radioactive iodine ablation has no role before a tissue diagnosis and is not used as a diagnostic maneuver. Levothyroxine suppression does not shrink suspicious nodules meaningfully and is not appropriate; it would also delay needed cytologic evaluation. Microcalcifications correspond histologically to psammoma bodies and are a worrisome feature for papillary thyroid carcinoma.

stages.label: CYTOPATHOLOGY
stages.content:
FNA cytology (Bethesda category VI — malignant)
Cells show enlarged, overlapping nuclei with nuclear grooves and intranuclear pseudoinclusions
Occasional psammoma bodies identified
Nuclear clearing ('Orphan Annie eye') nuclei noted

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The cytologic features—nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, psammoma bodies, and 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei—are diagnostic of which cancer?
options: Follicular thyroid carcinoma
Medullary thyroid carcinoma
Papillary thyroid carcinoma
Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
stages.explanation: These nuclear features—overlapping cells with grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, optically clear 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei, and psammoma bodies—are pathognomonic for papillary thyroid carcinoma, the most common thyroid malignancy. Follicular carcinoma cannot be diagnosed on FNA alone because the distinction from a benign adenoma requires demonstration of capsular or vascular invasion on histology. Medullary carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells and shows amyloid stroma and calcitonin positivity, not these nuclear features. Anaplastic carcinoma is a rapidly growing, often fixed mass in older patients with highly pleomorphic, undifferentiated cells. Papillary carcinoma spreads via lymphatics, generally carries an excellent prognosis, and is the expected diagnosis here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive treatment for this 1.8 cm papillary thyroid carcinoma without clinically apparent nodal disease?
options: Thyroid lobectomy (or total thyroidectomy), with risk-adapted radioactive iodine and TSH suppression as indicated
External beam radiotherapy to the neck
Systemic chemotherapy with doxorubicin
Observation with serial ultrasound only
stages.explanation: Surgery is the cornerstone of treatment for papillary thyroid carcinoma. For a 1-4 cm intrathyroidal tumor without nodal disease, either lobectomy or total thyroidectomy is acceptable, with the extent guided by risk factors; postoperative radioactive iodine and TSH-suppressive levothyroxine are applied in a risk-adapted fashion. External beam radiotherapy is reserved for unresectable or aggressive disease and is not first-line for differentiated thyroid cancer. Cytotoxic chemotherapy is ineffective for differentiated thyroid cancer and is not used. Active surveillance is generally reserved for very small (subcentimeter) low-risk papillary microcarcinomas, not a 1.8 cm high-suspicion lesion, so observation alone is inappropriate here.

cases.diagnosis: Papillary thyroid carcinoma of the right thyroid lobe
pearls: Work up a thyroid nodule with serum TSH plus neck ultrasound; reserve the radioiodine uptake scan for a suppressed TSH.
High-suspicion sonographic features—solid hypoechoic, taller-than-wide, irregular margins, microcalcifications—warrant FNA at ≥1 cm.
Papillary thyroid carcinoma shows nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, psammoma bodies, and 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei and spreads via lymphatics.
Treatment is surgical (lobectomy vs total thyroidectomy by risk), with radioactive iodine and TSH suppression applied in a risk-adapted manner.

cases.title: A Neck Nodule and Surprising Flushing Episodes
cases.chiefComplaint: A firm thyroid lump noticed a few months ago, along with episodes of facial flushing and loose stools.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with a firm nodule in the front of his neck noticed three months ago. He also describes intermittent facial flushing and several months of loose, watery stools that he attributed to diet. He denies hoarseness, dysphagia, or dyspnea. He reports occasional pounding headaches and episodic palpitations with sweating, which he assumed were anxiety. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no drug allergies. He is a nonsmoker and drinks socially; he works as a contractor. Family history is striking: his father died suddenly at 45 of a 'heart problem,' and a paternal uncle had thyroid surgery in his 30s and 'adrenal tumors.' Review of systems is negative for weight loss attributable to thyroid dysfunction, but he notes the flushing and diarrhea and the episodic spells. He has never had a thyroid problem before and has felt no other neck masses.
cases.exam: A firm, nontender 2.5 cm nodule is palpable in the left thyroid lobe, moving with swallowing. There is a single firm 1 cm left level III cervical lymph node. The voice is normal without stridor. There is no exophthalmos, lid lag, or tremor, and no diffuse goiter or bruit. The skin shows no characteristic mucosal neuromas of the lips or tongue, and the body habitus is not marfanoid. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular tachycardic rhythm without murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender without masses. The remainder of the head and neck and neurologic examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A firm thyroid nodule accompanied by flushing and chronic diarrhea should raise suspicion for which thyroid cancer, prompting which tumor marker?
options: Papillary carcinoma — measure thyroglobulin
Follicular carcinoma — measure free T4
Medullary carcinoma — measure serum calcitonin
Anaplastic carcinoma — measure lactate dehydrogenase
stages.explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells and secretes calcitonin; high levels can cause flushing and secretory diarrhea, so the appropriate marker is serum calcitonin (CEA is also useful). Papillary carcinoma is followed with thyroglobulin after thyroidectomy, but thyroglobulin is not a diagnostic marker for an intact gland and papillary cancer does not cause flushing/diarrhea. Follicular carcinoma is also a differentiated, thyroglobulin-secreting cancer and free T4 reflects gland hormone output, not malignancy. Anaplastic carcinoma is an aggressive undifferentiated tumor of older patients and LDH is nonspecific. The paraneoplastic flushing and diarrhea here are classic clues to a calcitonin-secreting medullary carcinoma.

stages.label: LABS & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
Serum calcitonin 820 pg/mL (markedly elevated; normal <10)
CEA 38 ng/mL (elevated)
TSH 1.6 mIU/L (normal)
FNA of the nodule: nests of polygonal/spindled cells in an amyloid stroma; Congo red stain positive; immunostains positive for calcitonin and chromogranin

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the family history of sudden cardiac death and a relative with thyroid and adrenal tumors, which inherited syndrome and genetic test are most relevant?
options: MEN1 — test the MEN1 (menin) gene
MEN2 — test the RET proto-oncogene
Von Hippel-Lindau — test the VHL gene
Familial adenomatous polyposis — test the APC gene
stages.explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma with a family history suggestive of pheochromocytoma (episodic headache, palpitations, sweating, hypertension; relative with 'adrenal tumors' and sudden death) points to MEN2, caused by germline RET proto-oncogene mutations, so RET testing is indicated. MEN1 (menin gene) involves the '3 Ps'—parathyroid, pituitary, pancreatic tumors—not medullary thyroid carcinoma. Von Hippel-Lindau causes pheochromocytoma, hemangioblastomas, and renal cell carcinoma, but not medullary thyroid cancer. FAP (APC gene) causes colonic polyposis and can associate with papillary thyroid cancer, not medullary. Identifying a RET mutation also enables screening of at-risk relatives and prophylactic thyroidectomy in carriers.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before any thyroid surgery in this patient, which evaluation is mandatory?
options: Screen for and exclude/treat pheochromocytoma (plasma or 24-hour urine metanephrines)
Begin levothyroxine suppression
Obtain a radioactive iodine uptake scan
Start systemic chemotherapy
stages.explanation: In suspected MEN2, a coexisting pheochromocytoma must be excluded with plasma free or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines before any surgery; an unrecognized pheochromocytoma can precipitate a fatal hypertensive crisis under anesthesia. If present, it is resected first after adequate alpha-blockade. His episodic headaches, palpitations, sweating, and hypertension make this especially important. Levothyroxine suppression is irrelevant for medullary carcinoma, which arises from C cells and does not respond to TSH suppression. Radioiodine uptake scanning is useless because medullary carcinoma cells do not concentrate iodine. Chemotherapy is not first-line; surgery is the mainstay once a pheochromocytoma is excluded or treated. Parathyroid status (hyperparathyroidism in MEN2A) should also be assessed.

stages.label: GENETIC & ENDOCRINE WORKUP
stages.content:
Plasma free metanephrines: elevated
Abdominal MRI: 3 cm right adrenal mass consistent with pheochromocytoma
Germline RET mutation: positive (codon 634)
Serum calcium and PTH: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the correct overall management sequence for this patient with medullary thyroid carcinoma and a pheochromocytoma in MEN2?
options: Total thyroidectomy first, then treat the pheochromocytoma
Alpha-blockade and resection of the pheochromocytoma first, then total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection
Simultaneous resection of both lesions without preoperative blockade
Radioactive iodine ablation followed by adrenalectomy
stages.explanation: The pheochromocytoma must be addressed first: adequate alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) followed by beta-blockade, then adrenalectomy, to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis. Definitive thyroid surgery—total thyroidectomy with central (level VI) compartment neck dissection (and lateral dissection for involved nodes)—follows. Doing the thyroidectomy first risks a catastrophic catecholamine surge from the unresected pheochromocytoma during anesthesia. Operating on both simultaneously without preoperative alpha-blockade is dangerous for the same reason. Radioactive iodine has no role in medullary carcinoma because C cells do not take up iodine, and it does not treat a pheochromocytoma. Postoperatively, calcitonin and CEA are followed to detect residual or recurrent disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After total thyroidectomy, what is the most appropriate way to monitor for residual or recurrent medullary thyroid carcinoma?
options: Serial serum thyroglobulin levels
Serial serum calcitonin and CEA levels (with imaging if they rise)
Annual radioactive iodine whole-body scans
Serial TSH measurements alone
stages.explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma is followed postoperatively with serial calcitonin and CEA, the tumor markers it secretes; a rising or detectable calcitonin signals residual or recurrent disease and prompts imaging to localize it (and calcitonin doubling time predicts aggressiveness). Thyroglobulin is the marker for differentiated (papillary/follicular) thyroid cancer, which arises from follicular cells, not the C cells of medullary carcinoma, so it is not useful here. Radioactive iodine scans are useless because medullary carcinoma does not concentrate iodine, making them inappropriate for both treatment and surveillance. TSH guides levothyroxine replacement dosing after thyroidectomy but does not track medullary tumor burden, so TSH alone cannot detect recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Medullary thyroid carcinoma in MEN2 (germline RET mutation) with coexisting pheochromocytoma
pearls: Flushing and secretory diarrhea with a thyroid nodule suggest calcitonin excess from medullary thyroid carcinoma; measure calcitonin and CEA.
Medullary thyroid carcinoma can be part of MEN2—test the RET proto-oncogene and screen at-risk relatives for prophylactic thyroidectomy.
Always exclude pheochromocytoma (metanephrines) before thyroid surgery in MEN2; resect the pheochromocytoma first after alpha-blockade.
Medullary carcinoma does not take up iodine, so radioactive iodine is ineffective—treatment is total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection.

cases.title: A Bump in the Middle of a Child's Neck That Moves When She Sticks Out Her Tongue
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump in the middle of the front of the neck that her parents noticed a few weeks ago.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl is brought in by her parents who noticed a small painless lump in the midline of her lower-anterior neck about three weeks ago, shortly after a cold. The lump has not visibly grown, is not painful, and she has had no difficulty swallowing, breathing, or speaking. She has had no fevers, weight loss, or night sweats since the cold resolved. She is otherwise healthy with normal growth and development. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Immunizations are up to date. There is no family history of thyroid cancer or endocrine syndromes. Her parents specifically deny any hoarseness, voice change, or signs that suggest she is hyper- or hypothyroid such as heat or cold intolerance. They are worried the lump is cancer. There is no history of recurrent neck infections, no draining sinus, and no trauma to the neck.
cases.exam: A smooth, soft, nontender 1.5 cm midline neck mass is palpable just above the level of the thyroid cartilage near the hyoid bone. The mass moves upward when the child protrudes her tongue and also rises with swallowing. There is no overlying erythema, fluctuance, or draining sinus. The thyroid gland is palpable in its normal position and is of normal size. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the head and neck examination, including the oropharynx and tympanic membranes, is normal, and the child is well-appearing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A midline neck mass that elevates with tongue protrusion and swallowing in a child is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Reactive lymph node
Thyroglossal duct cyst
Branchial cleft cyst
Dermoid cyst
stages.explanation: A midline cyst near the hyoid that moves up with tongue protrusion is the classic presentation of a thyroglossal duct cyst, which is connected to the foramen cecum at the tongue base via the tract of thyroid descent. Reactive lymph nodes are usually lateral, tender, and do not move with the tongue. Branchial cleft cysts are lateral, located along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid, not midline. Dermoid cysts can be midline but typically do not elevate with tongue protrusion because they lack the embryologic tract to the tongue base. The movement with tongue protrusion reflects the duct's attachment to the hyoid and tongue.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which study is most important to obtain before surgical planning?
options: Neck ultrasound to confirm a normal, orthotopic thyroid gland
CT neck with contrast as the initial study
Radioactive iodine scan in all cases
Fine-needle aspiration of the cyst
stages.explanation: A neck ultrasound is the key preoperative study to confirm normal, normally located (orthotopic) thyroid tissue. In rare cases the thyroglossal duct cyst contains the only functioning thyroid tissue (ectopic/lingual thyroid), and removing it could render the child hypothyroid—so documenting a normal gland is essential. CT is not the first-line study in children and adds radiation without clear benefit when ultrasound suffices. A radioactive iodine scan is not routinely needed if ultrasound shows a normal gland and is generally avoided in children when unnecessary. FNA is not required for a classic thyroglossal duct cyst and is generally avoided because the clinical and ultrasound picture is diagnostic.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Neck ultrasound: well-defined 1.5 cm anechoic midline cystic structure at the level of the hyoid bone
Normal thyroid gland present in the expected location with normal echotexture
No suspicious solid components or calcifications
No abnormal cervical lymph nodes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment of choice for a symptomatic or persistent thyroglossal duct cyst?
options: Simple cyst excision (cyst only)
Sistrunk procedure (excision of the cyst, the central hyoid bone, and the tract to the tongue base)
Needle aspiration of the cyst contents
Long-term antibiotic therapy
stages.explanation: The Sistrunk procedure—removal of the cyst together with the central portion of the hyoid bone and the tract up to the foramen cecum—is the definitive treatment and dramatically lowers recurrence compared with simple cyst excision. Simple excision leaves the tract and the mid-hyoid attachment behind, producing high recurrence rates. Needle aspiration provides only temporary decompression and does not remove the lesion, so the cyst reaccumulates and can become infected. Antibiotics treat secondary infection of an inflamed cyst but never cure the cyst itself. Surgery is typically deferred until any acute infection is controlled, then the Sistrunk procedure is performed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If the cyst becomes acutely red, warm, tender, and enlarged, what is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Proceed directly to the Sistrunk procedure
Treat the acute infection with antibiotics (and drainage if abscess), then perform definitive Sistrunk surgery after inflammation resolves
Begin chemotherapy
Observe indefinitely without any treatment
stages.explanation: An acutely infected thyroglossal duct cyst should first be treated medically with antibiotics, with incision and drainage reserved for a frank abscess; definitive Sistrunk excision is then performed once the inflammation has settled. Operating during active infection increases the risk of incomplete tract removal and recurrence because tissue planes are distorted and friable. Chemotherapy has no role—this is a benign developmental cyst, not a malignancy. Observation alone is inappropriate once the cyst is recurrently or acutely infected, as infection will recur and complicate eventual surgery. Counsel families that infection is the most common complication and that timing surgery after resolution optimizes outcomes.

pearls: A midline neck mass near the hyoid that elevates with tongue protrusion is a thyroglossal duct cyst until proven otherwise.
Always confirm a normal, orthotopic thyroid gland on ultrasound before surgery to avoid removing the only functioning thyroid tissue.
The Sistrunk procedure (cyst + central hyoid + tract) is definitive and minimizes recurrence versus simple excision.
Infection is the most common complication; treat the infection first, then perform definitive surgery once inflammation resolves.

cases.title: A Lump on the Side of the Neck That Ballooned After a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: A swelling on the left side of his neck that he had vaguely noticed before but that grew quickly after a recent cold.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man presents with a left-sided neck mass that he thinks has been faintly present for a couple of years but became obviously larger and slightly tender over the past week following an upper respiratory infection. As his cold improved, the swelling persisted. He denies fevers now, night sweats, weight loss, dysphagia, or trouble breathing. He has had no skin lesions, dental infections, or animal exposures. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He is a college student, drinks socially, does not smoke or use other tobacco, and has had no high-risk exposures. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no hoarseness, no ear pain, and no prior neck surgery or radiation. He is concerned because the lump 'came and went' before but is now noticeably bigger, and he wonders whether it is dangerous.
cases.exam: A smooth, mobile, mildly tender 3 cm cystic mass is palpable along the anterior border of the left sternocleidomastoid muscle at the upper-mid neck (near the angle of the mandible). The overlying skin is normal without a draining sinus or punctum. There is no fluctuant abscess pointing to the skin. The oropharynx, tonsils, and tympanic membranes are normal. There is no other cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. The thyroid is normal and midline. The remainder of the head and neck examination is unremarkable, and he is well-appearing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A smooth cystic mass along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid in a young adult that enlarged after a URI is most consistent with which lesion?
options: Thyroglossal duct cyst
Second branchial cleft cyst
Reactive lymph node
Carotid body tumor
stages.explanation: A second branchial cleft cyst is the classic lateral neck cyst, located along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid near the mandibular angle; it often fluctuates and can enlarge or become tender after an upper respiratory infection. A thyroglossal duct cyst is midline and moves with tongue protrusion, not lateral. A reactive lymph node is usually solid (not cystic) and tied to active infection, whereas this lesion is a discrete fluctuant cyst that predates the cold. A carotid body tumor is a pulsatile, firm, laterally mobile paraganglioma at the carotid bifurcation, not a soft cystic mass, and it does not flare with URIs. The chronic, fluctuating history is a key clue.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which imaging study is the best initial choice to characterize this lateral neck cyst in a young patient?
options: Plain neck radiograph
Neck ultrasound
Whole-body PET scan
Bone scintigraphy
stages.explanation: Ultrasound is the best initial imaging for a lateral neck cyst in a young patient: it is radiation-free, confirms the cystic nature, defines size and relationship to the carotid sheath, and helps distinguish a simple cyst from a solid or necrotic node. Plain radiographs provide little soft-tissue detail and would not characterize a cyst. Whole-body PET is inappropriate for a presumed benign developmental cyst and would expose the patient to needless radiation and false positives. Bone scintigraphy assesses skeletal pathology and is irrelevant here. If features are atypical or to map deep extent before surgery, contrast-enhanced CT or MRI may follow ultrasound, but ultrasound is the appropriate first step.

stages.label: IMAGING & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
Neck ultrasound: 3.2 cm well-circumscribed, thin-walled anechoic cystic lesion along the anterior border of the left sternocleidomastoid, deep to the platysma
No solid mural nodule or internal vascularity
No suspicious cervical lymphadenopathy
FNA: bland squamous epithelial cells with proteinaceous/cholesterol-rich fluid; no atypical cells

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An important caveat is that a cystic lateral neck mass in an OLDER adult (over ~40) can mimic a branchial cleft cyst but actually represent which entity?
options: Cystic metastasis from HPV-related oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
Pleomorphic adenoma
Lipoma
Thyroglossal duct cyst
stages.explanation: In an adult over roughly 40, a new cystic lateral neck mass that looks like a 'branchial cleft cyst' is often a cystic nodal metastasis from HPV-associated oropharyngeal (tonsil or tongue base) squamous cell carcinoma, which classically presents with cystic neck nodes. Such patients warrant a thorough mucosal exam and workup rather than assuming a benign cyst. Pleomorphic adenoma is a salivary tumor, usually parotid and solid, not a thin-walled cyst. A lipoma is a soft, fatty, non-cystic subcutaneous mass. A thyroglossal duct cyst is midline. In this 22-year-old with a long fluctuating history and benign cytology, a true branchial cleft cyst is far more likely, but the age caveat is high-yield.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After resolution of any acute inflammation, what is the definitive treatment for a symptomatic branchial cleft cyst?
options: Repeated needle aspiration
Complete surgical excision of the cyst and its tract
Long-term suppressive antibiotics
Sclerotherapy as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: Complete surgical excision of the cyst along with any epithelial tract is the definitive treatment; removing the entire tract minimizes recurrence and prevents repeated infections. Surgery is best performed when the cyst is quiescent, after any acute infection has been treated, because inflamed tissue planes raise the risk of incomplete excision and recurrence. Repeated needle aspiration only temporarily decompresses the cyst and does not cure it. Long-term antibiotics treat infection but never eliminate the cyst. Sclerotherapy is used for certain vascular and lymphatic malformations, not as standard therapy for a branchial cleft cyst. Preoperative imaging defines the tract anatomy to ensure complete removal.

pearls: A branchial cleft cyst is the classic lateral neck cyst along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid that may flare after a URI.
Ultrasound is the best initial study; complete surgical excision of the cyst and tract is definitive treatment.
Operate when the cyst is quiescent—excising during active infection raises the risk of incomplete removal and recurrence.
In adults over ~40, a 'branchial cleft cyst' may actually be a cystic nodal metastasis from HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer—evaluate the mucosa.

cases.title: A Soft Neck Swelling in a Newborn That Glows With a Light
cases.chiefComplaint: A soft swelling on the side of a newborn girl's neck noted at delivery.
cases.history: A newborn girl delivered at 38 weeks is evaluated in the NICU for a soft swelling over the left posterolateral neck noted at birth. The pregnancy was complicated by a second-trimester prenatal ultrasound that showed a cystic neck mass and increased nuchal translucency. The infant was born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery with Apgars of 8 and 9. She is feeding and breathing comfortably without stridor. There is no respiratory distress. The mass has been stable since birth. There is no family history of vascular malformations. Maternal serologies are unremarkable and there were no teratogen exposures. The infant is otherwise vigorous and pink. On parental history there were no complications of labor, and the placenta was normal. The team notes a webbed-appearing neck and asks whether a chromosomal condition should be considered given the prenatal findings and physical features.
cases.exam: A soft, compressible, nontender mass approximately 4 cm fills the left posterior triangle of the neck. It is fluctuant and brightly transilluminates when a light is applied. The overlying skin is normal in color without bruit or warmth. There is mild apparent neck webbing and a low posterior hairline. The airway is patent with no stridor and clear breath sounds bilaterally. The cardiovascular exam reveals normal first and second heart sounds; femoral pulses are noted to be slightly diminished. The remainder of the newborn examination is otherwise grossly normal, and the infant is active with good tone.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A soft, compressible posterior triangle neck mass in a newborn that brightly transilluminates is most consistent with which lesion?
options: Cystic hygroma (macrocystic lymphatic malformation)
Hemangioma
Thyroglossal duct cyst
Branchial cleft cyst
stages.explanation: A cystic hygroma (macrocystic lymphatic malformation) classically presents at or before birth as a soft, compressible mass in the posterior triangle that brightly transilluminates because it is filled with clear lymphatic fluid. A hemangioma is a vascular tumor that is typically reddish, may have a bruit, often appears or proliferates in the first weeks of life, and does not transilluminate like a fluid-filled cyst. A thyroglossal duct cyst is midline and moves with tongue protrusion, not in the posterior triangle. A branchial cleft cyst lies along the anterior sternocleidomastoid border and usually presents in older children or young adults, not as a posterior triangle mass at birth.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the prenatal increased nuchal translucency, neck webbing, low hairline, and diminished femoral pulses, which chromosomal condition is most associated and what is the confirmatory test?
