Soft signs (e.g., minor stable hematoma, dysphonia without distress, minor hemoptysis) may permit CT angiography and selective management, but they do not apply when hard signs are present. These are emphatically not normal findings. Hard signs apply across all neck zones; the modern selective-management approach reserves immediate exploration specifically for patients with hard signs like these.

stages.label: INTRAOP & LABS
stages.content:
Airway secured via early endotracheal intubation; surgical airway kit at bedside
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
Type and crossmatch sent; 2 units PRBC to OR
Neck exploration: laceration of the right internal jugular vein with adjacent carotid sheath hematoma; no esophageal injury on intraoperative evaluation
Trachea intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given hard signs in this Zone II injury, what is the definitive management approach?
options: Discharge with wound care instructions
Immediate surgical neck exploration in the operating room
CT angiography then send home if negative
Observation on the floor with serial exams only
stages.explanation: When hard signs of vascular or aerodigestive injury are present, the definitive management is immediate surgical neck exploration in the operating room to control hemorrhage and repair injured structures—exactly what revealed and addressed the internal jugular vein laceration here. Discharge is unsafe with a penetrating injury and hard signs. CT angiography is the pathway for hemodynamically stable patients with soft signs or no signs (selective management), not for patients with hard signs requiring the OR. Floor observation with serial exams is inadequate for an actively bleeding, airway-threatening injury. Hard signs short-circuit imaging and go straight to operative control.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Intraoperatively, which additional structures must be specifically evaluated in a Zone II penetrating injury?
options: Only the skin and subcutaneous tissue
The aerodigestive tract (pharynx/esophagus and larynx/trachea) in addition to vascular structures
The brachial plexus only
The thoracic duct only
stages.explanation: Zone II penetrating neck injuries can damage vascular structures (carotid, jugular) and the aerodigestive tract; the pharynx/esophagus and larynx/trachea must be specifically evaluated because missed hypopharyngeal or esophageal injuries lead to deep neck infection and mediastinitis. Evaluating only skin and subcutaneous tissue would miss life-threatening deep injuries. The brachial plexus and thoracic duct are more relevant to Zone I (thoracic inlet) injuries and are not the primary focus of Zone II exploration. A thorough Zone II exploration systematically inspects vessels and the aerodigestive tract to avoid catastrophic missed injuries.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For a hemodynamically stable patient with a Zone II neck stab wound and only soft signs, what is the modern management approach?
options: Mandatory operative exploration for every penetrating Zone II injury
CT angiography with selective management based on findings
Immediate discharge without imaging
Blind wound probing to determine trajectory
stages.explanation: Modern management of stable penetrating neck injuries with soft or no signs is CT angiography with selective intervention based on findings, rather than mandatory exploration of all Zone II wounds—an outdated practice that led to many negative explorations. Routine mandatory exploration is no longer recommended for stable patients without hard signs. Immediate discharge without imaging is unsafe because vascular and aerodigestive injuries can be occult. Blind probing risks dislodging a clot and provoking hemorrhage and is contraindicated. The selective, imaging-driven pathway applies only when hard signs (which mandate the OR) are absent.

cases.diagnosis: Penetrating Zone II neck trauma with vascular (internal jugular) injury
pearls: Airway first: an expanding neck hematoma can obstruct fast—intubate early with surgical airway backup.
Hard signs (expanding/pulsatile hematoma, thrill/bruit, active bleeding, airway compromise) mandate immediate OR exploration.
Hemodynamically stable patients with soft or no signs are candidates for CT angiography and selective management.
Never blindly probe a neck wound; in Zone II, evaluate both vascular and aerodigestive structures.

cases.title: A Toddler Drooling and Refusing to Eat
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden drooling and refusal to eat or drink.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because he suddenly began drooling and refusing to eat or drink about three hours ago. He had been playing on the floor near his older sibling's piggy bank of coins. There was a brief episode of gagging and coughing that resolved, and since then he has been fussy, pointing to his throat/chest, and spitting out saliva. He has no fever, no stridor at rest, and no cyanosis. He has no significant past medical history, is fully immunized, takes no medications, and has no known allergies. He was born full-term without complications. There are no sick contacts and no recent illness. His mother is unsure exactly what he may have swallowed but notes some coins are missing. Review of systems is otherwise negative for vomiting, abdominal distension, and respiratory distress. He is consolable in his mother's arms but distressed when offered fluids.
cases.exam: Alert, fussy toddler in his mother's lap, drooling onto a bib. He is in no acute respiratory distress and there is no stridor, retractions, or cyanosis at rest. The oropharynx is clear with no visible foreign body and moist mucous membranes (saliva pooling). Neck is supple without swelling or crepitus. Lungs are clear bilaterally with good air entry and no wheeze. Cardiac exam shows mild tachycardia. The abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended without masses. He gags and refuses when offered a sip of water. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic test?
options: Barium swallow study
Plain radiographs (neck/chest, including frontal view) to localize a radiopaque foreign body
Upper GI endoscopy without any imaging
CT of the abdomen with contrast
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs (AP and lateral neck/chest, often including abdomen) are the appropriate first test to localize a suspected radiopaque foreign body like a coin and to confirm its position; a coin lodged in the esophagus classically appears en face (flat/circular) on the frontal film, whereas one in the trachea is more often oriented sagittally. A barium swallow is avoided because contrast can obscure endoscopic visualization and risks aspiration. Endoscopy is the therapeutic step but should follow localization imaging. CT of the abdomen is unnecessary radiation for a problem readily diagnosed by plain films and would not capture the likely esophageal location at the thoracic inlet.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
AP neck/chest radiograph: round radiopaque disc seen en face at the level of the thoracic inlet (C7–T1)
Lateral view: single thin disc, no second object, airway patent
No subcutaneous emphysema or free air
No signs of perforation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The coin is at the thoracic inlet and the child is drooling but breathing comfortably. Where is the coin most likely lodged and why?
options: In the trachea, because of the cough at onset
In the esophagus at the level of the upper esophageal sphincter (thoracic inlet), explaining drooling and dysphagia
In the stomach, given the lack of distress
In the larynx, given the drooling
stages.explanation: Drooling, refusal to eat/drink, and an en face coin at the thoracic inlet (C7–T1) point to esophageal impaction at the upper esophageal sphincter, the narrowest and most common site of esophageal coin lodgment in children. The en face (flat/circular) orientation on the frontal film favors esophagus over trachea, where coins more often lie sagittally. A tracheal/laryngeal foreign body would cause respiratory symptoms (stridor, distress), which are absent. A coin in the stomach would typically be asymptomatic and would be managed expectantly, not consistent with active drooling. The clinical and radiographic picture localizes it to the esophagus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of this esophageal coin?
options: Immediate emergent surgery (thoracotomy)
Endoscopic removal (rigid or flexible esophagoscopy); a brief period of observation may be reasonable for a smooth coin if the child is stable
Discharge home and let it pass on its own indefinitely
Induce vomiting with ipecac
stages.explanation: An esophageal coin in a symptomatic child (drooling, unable to handle secretions) is removed by endoscopy (rigid or flexible esophagoscopy). For a smooth, blunt coin in an asymptomatic, stable child, a short observation period to see if it passes into the stomach can be reasonable, but symptomatic impaction warrants removal and should not be left indefinitely. Emergent thoracotomy is reserved for perforation or failed endoscopic retrieval, not first-line. Discharging the child to let a symptomatic esophageal coin sit risks mucosal injury, pressure necrosis, and aspiration. Inducing vomiting with ipecac is contraindicated and dangerous, risking aspiration and injury.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Distinguishing a coin from a button battery on imaging is critical because a button battery requires what?
options: No urgency; both are managed identically
Emergent removal due to risk of rapid caustic mucosal injury (battery shows a double-ring/halo sign)
Routine outpatient follow-up only
Observation for one week
stages.explanation: A button battery lodged in the esophagus is a true emergency requiring immediate removal because it causes rapid liquefactive (caustic) tissue injury within hours, leading to perforation, fistula, or major vessel erosion. On radiographs, a button battery shows a double-ring or halo sign (and a step-off on the lateral view), distinguishing it from a single-density coin—this distinction changes the urgency entirely. Treating both identically or with non-urgent follow-up could be fatal if a battery is present. Observation for a week is dangerous for a battery. Always scrutinize a round esophageal foreign body for the halo sign to avoid mistaking a battery for a benign coin.

cases.diagnosis: Esophageal foreign body (coin) at the thoracic inlet
pearls: Drooling and refusal to eat after a witnessed/suspected ingestion suggest an esophageal foreign body—get plain films first.
A coin in the esophagus lies en face (flat) on the AP film; in the trachea it tends to lie sagittally.
The upper esophageal sphincter (thoracic inlet) is the most common site of esophageal coin impaction in children.
Always rule out a button battery (double-ring/halo sign)—it causes rapid caustic injury and demands emergent removal.
